https://www.ready.gov/floods.name: Flash Flood Response
steps: Move to high ground IMMEDIATELY. Do not wait to gather belongings.
Never cross flowing water deeper than your ankles.
If swept away: float on your back with feet downstream.
If in a vehicle: abandon it if water starts rising around it.

https://www.ready.gov/floods.name: Post-Flood Survival
steps: Do NOT drink floodwater or any water contaminated by flooding.
Avoid walking through standing floodwater.
Discard all food that contacted floodwater.
Watch for structural collapse in buildings.

https://www.ready.gov/floods.name: Extreme Flood: Rooftop Escape
steps: Move to the highest floor immediately. Take an axe or heavy tool.
NEVER enter a closed attic space without an axe or saw.
If trapped in the attic, chop a hole at the highest point securely.

https://www.ready.gov/floods.name: Post-Flood: Waterborne Disease Prevention
steps: Treat all floodwater as heavily contaminated raw sewage.
Clean all open wounds immediately with clean water and soap/iodine.
Do NOT consume food or drink that touched floodwater.

https://www.ready.gov/floods.name: Post-Flood Water Safety
steps: Assume all tap water is contaminated.
Sanitize all wounds instantly.
Boil water dynamically.

https://www.ready.gov/floods.name: Flood: Pathogen & HAZMAT Protocol
steps: Assume Class 3 Biohazard status.
Strip and isolate clothing.
Sanitize with extreme dilution.

https://www.ready.gov/floods.name: Finding & Eating Grubs
steps: Look inside rotting logs and under bark for beetle larvae (grubs).
Squeeze grubs gently to expel gut contents (optional but improves taste).
Roast on a stick near fire, or skewer and fry. Cook thoroughly.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Ocean Survival (Adrift)
steps: Conserve energy above all else. Float, don't swim.
Protect yourself from sun and cold water.
Collect rainwater immediately when it falls.
Fish near floating debris — it attracts marine life.
Signal constantly — use anything reflective or bright.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Open Water Survival
steps: Stay with the vessel or debris as long as possible.
HELP position: Heat Escape Lessening Position.
Signal with mirror, dye marker, whistle, or bright clothing.
Never drink seawater. Collect rain at every opportunity.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Coastal & Tidal Survival
steps: Learn tide cycle—changes every ~6 hours.
Rip current: swim PARALLEL to shore, not against it.
Forage at low tide: tidal pools have shellfish, crabs, seaweed.
Signal from headlands—most visible from sea and air.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Rip Current Escape
steps: DO NOT fight the current by swimming directly toward shore. You will exhaust yourself and drown.
Swim PARALLEL to the shore — to either side. Rip currents are narrow. You'll escape within 50-100 feet.
If too exhausted to swim: float on your back. Rip currents do NOT pull underwater — they move you outward.
Signal for help: wave one arm while floating. Lifeguards are trained to spot this signal.
Identify rip currents BEFORE entering water: look for channels of churning water, different water color, or gaps in breaking waves.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Cold Water Immersion Survival
steps: The first 1-3 minutes: GASP REFLEX. You will involuntarily gasp and hyperventilate. Focus on controlling breathing.
Minutes 3-30: SWIM FAILURE. Cold numbs your muscles. Self-rescue must happen NOW before you lose strength.
HELP position: if you can't get out, assume the Heat Escape Lessening Position.
If in a group: huddle together side by side, arms around each other, children in the center.
Hypothermia sets in after 30+ minutes. You will become confused, drowsy, and stop shivering. Do NOT fall asleep.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Tsunami Vertical Evacuation
steps: Recognize the signs: a massive, prolonged earthquake near the coast, or the ocean suddenly and rapidly receding, exposing the sea floor.
Immediately abandon all vehicles and run on foot.
Seek natural high ground above 100 feet (30 meters) elevation, as far inland as possible.
If trapped on the flats, perform Vertical Evacuation.
Do NOT return when the water recedes.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Life Raft Survival Protocol
steps: Immediately upon boarding the raft: administer seasickness medication if available.
Collect rainwater using every available surface.
Shade yourself during the day.
Fish using improvised hooks, lures, or a shirt dragged behind the raft as a net.
Signal constantly.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Shark Deterrent Techniques
steps: Do not urinate or defecate in the water if sharks are present.
Do not trail hands or feet over the edge of a raft.
If a shark bumps or circles you, face it and maintain eye contact.
Deploy sea dye marker if available.
Avoid splashing or erratic movements.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Seasickness Acupressure
steps: Locate the Nei-Kuan point (P6): 3 finger-widths below the wrist crease, between the two central tendons.
Apply steady, firm pressure for 2-3 minutes.
Commercial Sea-Bands use this same principle with a plastic bead.
Fix your gaze on the horizon.
If vomiting occurs, do so over the DOWNWIND side of the raft.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Life Raft Repair
steps: Locate the leak by listening for hissing or smearing soapy water (if available) over the surface.
Use the repair kit included in the raft's survival kit.
If no repair kit: chewing gum, duct tape, or melted rubber from shoes can serve as improvised sealant.
Keep the raft fully inflated by topping off with the hand pump every 2-4 hours.
If a major chamber fails, redistribute weight to the intact chambers.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Raft Water Discipline Protocol
steps: Do NOT drink any water for the first 24 hours.
After 24 hours, ration water to 500ml (1 pint) per day per person.
NEVER drink seawater.
Collect every drop of rain using the raft canopy, tarps, and clothing.
Reduce water loss: stay in shade, minimize movement, keep skin covered.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Edible Barnacle Harvesting
steps: Goose barnacles attach to the underside of rafts and floating debris within days of deployment.
Scrape them off carefully with a knife or sharp edge.
Crack open the shell and eat the stalk (neck) portion raw.
Small crabs and fish often hide among barnacle clusters.
Do not eat barnacles if you have no freshwater.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Signal Fire Maintenance
steps: Pre-build 3 signal fire platforms in a triangle, 100 feet apart.
Cover each platform with a waterproof layer (bark, tarp, or leaves).
Prepare green branches or oil-soaked material for smoke production.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: CD Signal Mirror
steps: Use the shiny side of a CD or DVD.
Look through the center hole at your target (plane/boat).
Flash the sunlight across the target repeatedly.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Ocean Swell Reading
steps: Polynesian navigators used swell direction as a primary compass. Swells are ocean waves generated by distant storms, unaffected by local wind.
Observe the ocean surface. Ignore the chaotic top layer of waves. Look for the underlying, rhythmic rise and fall of the water.
Identify the dominant swell. The dominant swell is usually the largest and most consistent. Note its direction relative to your boat.
Check for a secondary swell crossing the primary swell. This creates an interference pattern (a 'grid' on the water).
Watch for swell refraction around islands. Swells bend around landmasses.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Signal Fire
steps: Build 3 fires in a triangle, 100 feet apart.
Prepare each fire with dry tinder and kindling, ready to light.
Add green branches or wet material for heavy smoke.
At night, flames are more visible than smoke.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Signal Mirror
steps: Any reflective surface works: mirror, CD, phone screen, metal.
Hold the mirror near your eye and extend your other hand toward the target.
Reflect sunlight onto your extended hand, then move hand to align flash with target.
Flash in sets of three (SOS pattern) — three short, three long, three short.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Ground-to-Air Signals
steps: Create symbols at least 10 feet tall, 3 feet wide.
Standard signals: V = need assistance, X = need medical help, → = traveling this direction.
Place in open areas visible from above — clearings, hilltops, beaches.
Add shadow-casting height for visibility from low angles.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Whistle Signal
steps: Three sharp blasts = universal distress signal.
Blow in short, sharp bursts — not long continuous blows.
Point the whistle toward open areas, valleys, or potential rescuer direction.
Conserve energy: blow every 5 minutes, not continuously.
If no whistle: use two rocks struck together or a metal object.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: International Ground Signal Codes
steps: V = Need Help. X = Need Medical. I = Need Supplies.
Arrow = traveling this direction. Y = Yes. N = No.
Build with rocks, logs, clothing, or trenches.
Minimum 10 feet long, 3 feet wide. Bigger is better.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Colored Smoke Signals
steps: Build a standard fire and get it burning hot.
add GREEN vegetation for WHITE smoke.
Add OIL, RUBBER, or PLASTIC for BLACK smoke.
Create 3 distinct puffs (cover/uncover) for SOS.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Night Signaling Techniques
steps: Maintain a bright fire on the highest accessible point.
Use flashlight or phone flash in SOS pattern.
Create a torch from resinous wood or cloth soaked in fat.
Use reflective material to catch any light source.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Vehicle-Based Signaling
steps: Stay with the vehicle — it is more visible than you are.
Open hood and trunk — this signals distress to aircraft.
Use mirrors, headlights, horn for active signaling.
Burn oil or tires for thick black smoke (extreme emergency only).

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Improvised Flag Signaling
steps: Tie the brightest fabric you have to a long pole or branch.
Wave in large, slow arcs — both arms overhead means 'need help.'
Plant the flag on the highest visible point when not actively waving.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Cliff and Canyon Signaling
steps: Use sound amplification — canyon walls reflect and amplify whistles.
Create signals on the canyon rim if possible, not the floor.
Use smoke — it rises out of canyons and is visible from above.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Ground-to-Air Signal Panel
steps: Create a large V (need help), X (need medical), or arrow (traveling this direction).
Make signals at least 10 feet long using contrasting materials.
Place in open areas — meadows, beaches, ridgetops. Away from tree canopy.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Survival Whistle Signaling
steps: Three blasts = universal distress signal. Pause. Repeat.
Two blasts = 'come to me' or acknowledgment from rescuers.
One long blast = 'attention' or 'where are you?'

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Vehicle Emergency Signaling
steps: Stay with the vehicle — it's much easier to spot than a person.
Use mirrors, hood, CDs, or any reflective surface to signal aircraft.
If safe, burn the spare tire for black smoke signal (extreme last resort).

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Smoke Signal Timing & Technique
steps: Build three fires in a triangle — three smoke columns = distress signal.
Use green vegetation for white smoke on dark backgrounds, tires/oil for dark smoke on light backgrounds.
Keep signal fires prepared but unlit. Light when you see or hear aircraft.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Signal Kite Construction
steps: Build a diamond kite frame from two crossed sticks.
Cover with plastic bag, poncho, or large leaves tied to the frame.
Attach a long line and fly in open area. Tie bright streamers to the line.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Water Dye Marker
steps: Commercial sea dye markers create bright green/yellow patches visible from aircraft. Use if available.
Improvised: motor oil creates a visible sheen on water. Pour around the raft or swim area.
Fluorescein (if available in first aid kits) creates an extremely bright green marker visible from miles.
At night on water: chemical light sticks in the water create a glowing marker visible to surface and air rescuers.
Spread any brightly colored fabric flat on the water surface around the raft.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Drowning Rescue (Reach-Throw-Row-Go)
steps: REACH: If the victim is close to the edge, extend a stick, paddle, branch, or towel from a secure position.
THROW: If they are out of reach, throw anything that floats — a cooler, life ring, jug, or rope.
ROW: Use a boat, kayak, or improvised raft to paddle to the victim.
GO (Only if trained): Swimming to a panicked victim is the absolute last resort and highly dangerous.
If grabbed: dive straight downward underwater. The victim wants to stay on the surface and will let go.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Sea Dye Marker Deployment
steps: Keep the dye marker attached to your life vest via the lanyard.
Do NOT deploy the dye until you hear or see an aircraft.
Tear open the packet and empty the fluorescent uranine powder into the water downwind from you.
Agitate the water gently to help it spread into a massive slick.
Retain the empty packet.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Aerial Parachute Flare
steps: Assess the wind direction.
Wait until a vessel or aircraft is genuinely within visual range.
Hold the flare tube firmly in your non-dominant hand, pointed skyward at a 75 to 80-degree angle.
Pull the firing chain or strike the cap, keeping your face turned entirely away from the tube.
Once it fires, immediately drop the spent tube in the water.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Man Overboard Response
steps: SHOUT 'MAN OVERBOARD!' immediately and point continuously at the person in the water.
Throw flotation immediately.
Activate the MOB button on GPS or drop a floating GPS marker.
Maneuver the vessel to approach the victim FROM DOWNWIND.
Get them aboard via the lowest point of the vessel.

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: H.E.L.P. & Huddle Positions
steps: Understand the cold shock response.
Minimize movement.
Assume the H.E.L.P. position (Solo).
Form a Huddle (Group).

https://www.uscgboating.org.name: Seawater Distillation
steps: NEVER drink seawater directly. The salt concentration (3.5%) accelerates dehydration and causes kidney failure.
Boil seawater in a pot or metal container.
Capture the steam.
Channel the condensed droplets into a catch container.
The collected water is pure H2O, free of salt and pathogens.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Coastal & Tidal Hazards
steps: Rip current: SWIM PARALLEL to shore, never against the current.
Tidal cut-offs: check tide tables before exploring coastal areas.
Storm surge: evacuate to high ground before the storm arrives.
Tsunami warning signs: rapidly receding water, unusual roar from sea.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Jungle Travel Planning
steps: Travel in the early morning (6-10 AM) and late afternoon (3-6 PM).
Use ridgelines when possible — they're above the undergrowth.
Mark your trail to prevent circling.
Camp early — set up shelter before dark (5 PM in tropics).

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Jungle Travel
steps: Follow streams downstream — they lead to rivers, rivers lead to people.
Travel on ridgelines when possible — less vegetation, better visibility.
Mark your path — the jungle looks identical in every direction.
Watch your feet and where you grab — snakes and insects are everywhere.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Mountain Altitude Management
steps: Above 8,000 ft: ascend no more than 1,000 ft per day.
Climb high, sleep low.
Hydrate aggressively — 3-4 liters per day at altitude.
Golden rule: if symptoms worsen, descend immediately.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Quicksand Escape
steps: Stop moving. Do NOT struggle or kick.
Drop pack and heavy items.
Lean back slowly. Float on your back.
Wiggle legs to break suction. Pull out one at a time.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: High Altitude Sickness Management
steps: Recognize symptoms: headache, nausea, fatigue, dizziness above 8,000 feet.
Descend immediately if symptoms worsen or confusion develops.
Climb high, sleep low. Ascend no more than 1,000 feet per day above 10,000 feet.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Coastal Storm Shelter Strategy
steps: Get to high ground immediately. Storm surge kills more than wind.
Shelter in the strongest structure available. Interior rooms, lowest floor.
After the storm: beware downed power lines, contaminated water, and unstable structures.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Blizzard Survival
steps: Shelter immediately. Do NOT try to walk for help in whiteout.
If in car: run engine 10 mins/hour only. Clear exhaust pipe.
Exercise extremities to keep blood flowing. Don't sleep if shivering stops.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Alligator/Croc Evasion
steps: Stay 15ft from water edge. Don't wash/drink at same spot.
Run in straight line away. Do NOT zigzag.
If bitten: attack eyes and snout. Don't pull.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Bow Drill Fire
steps: Assemble 4 components: fireboard, spindle, bow, handhold.
Carve a notch in the fireboard to collect ember dust.
Wrap the bowstring around the spindle once.
Apply downward pressure with handhold and saw the bow back and forth.
When heavy smoke pours from the notch, stop. The ember is born.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Dakota Fire Hole
steps: Dig a hole 1 foot wide and 1 foot deep.
Dig a second hole 1 foot away, connected by a tunnel.
The air tunnel should face the prevailing wind.
Build fire in the main hole. The forced air creates intense heat.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Potassium Permanganate Fire
steps: Place a small pile of potassium permanganate on dry surface.
Add a few drops of glycerin (antifreeze works too).
Stand back—the mixture will spontaneously ignite.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Fire Piston
steps: Place a small piece of charcloth or tinder fungus in the piston cup.
Ram the piston into the cylinder with one sharp stroke.
Quickly withdraw the piston. Transfer glowing ember to tinder bundle.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Waterproof Tinder Kit
steps: Coat cotton balls in petroleum jelly (Vaseline).
Dip wooden matches in melted wax for waterproofing.
Store charcloth in a sealed container with your ferro rod.
Add a birthday candle — guaranteed flame for 60 seconds.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Fatwood Identification
steps: Look for dead pine/spruce stumps with resin-saturated heartwood.
Split the stump to find the resin-saturated core.
Shave thin curls for tinder. Fatwood ignites with a single spark.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Keyhole Fire (Cooking Fire)
steps: Dig or build a keyhole shape: circle for main fire, narrow channel for cooking.
Build main fire in the circular area. Let it produce coals.
Rake hot coals into the narrow channel for controlled cooking.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Fire in High Wind
steps: Build a windbreak from rocks, logs, or earth on the windward side.
Use a top-down fire lay: large logs on bottom, smaller fuel on top.
Feed fire from the downwind side. Keep fuel low profile.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Bamboo Fire Saw
steps: Split a dry bamboo section in half lengthwise.
Cut a notch in the flat side of the hearth half. Place tinder beneath.
Rapidly saw the edge of the saw half back and forth across the notch.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Fire Fuel Stage Management
steps: Gather tinder: pencil-lead thin, bone dry, catches from spark.
Gather kindling: pencil to finger thickness, catches from tinder flame.
Gather fuel: wrist thickness and larger, sustains the fire.
Progress through stages: tinder → kindling → fuel. Never skip.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Fire Bundle Transport
steps: Wrap a glowing coal in dry grass, bark, or punky wood.
Enclose the wrapped coal in green bark or large green leaves.
Blow gently on the bundle every 15-20 minutes to keep the ember alive.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Pump Drill Fire
steps: Carve a spindle 12-18 inches long, pointed at the bottom, with a hole drilled through the top.
Create a flywheel — a flat, heavy disc (stone or hardwood) drilled through the center and threaded onto the spindle.
Attach a crossbar with cordage wound around the spindle above the flywheel.
Prepare a fireboard with a socket and notch, exactly like a bow drill setup.
Pump the crossbar rhythmically. Increase speed gradually until ember forms in the notch.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Pine Resin Collection
steps: Find coniferous trees (pine, spruce, fir) with visible resin deposits — look for amber-colored lumps on bark.
Scrape resin off the bark using a knife blade, stick, or flat rock. Collect on a leaf or bark piece.
Mix crumbled resin with dry materials: bark fiber, cotton, dryer lint, or fine wood shavings.
Resin also works as a standalone fire extender — press lumps directly onto burning tinder.
Store extra resin in a bark pouch or wrap. It stays effective indefinitely when dry.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Char Material Preparation
steps: Cut 100% cotton cloth into 2-inch squares. Pack loosely into a small metal tin with a tight lid.
Punch a small hole in the tin lid — this allows gas to escape without letting oxygen in.
Place the tin on hot coals or near flames. Smoke will vent from the hole.
When smoke stops, remove from heat. DO NOT open until completely cool.
Good char cloth is jet black, holds its shape, and catches a spark instantly.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Wet Wood Processing
steps: Never use wood from the ground in wet conditions — it absorbs moisture like a sponge.
Split wet wood to expose the dry interior. Even soaked logs have dry centers.
Shave feather sticks from the dry inner wood — thin curls that catch fire easily.
Collect birch bark — it contains oils that burn even when wet.
Use a 'fire sandwich': lay wet wood near the fire to dry while burning dry wood. Rotate as wood dries.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Coal-Burned Container
steps: Select a log section of appropriate size — green or slightly dry hardwood works best.
Place a hot coal from your fire on the surface where you want to hollow.
Blow gently on the coal to maintain heat. Add new coals as needed.
Scrape away charred wood with a sharp stone or shell. Repeat: burn, scrape, burn, scrape.
Control the burn area with wet clay or mud paste around the edges of the desired shape.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Pine Pitch Torch
steps: Find a fatwood source — the resin-saturated heartwood of dead pine stumps.
Split fatwood into a bundle of sticks about 12 inches long and finger-thickness.
Alternatively: wrap cloth around a green stick and soak with melted pine resin.
Light the torch — fatwood ignites from a match, ferro rod, or existing fire. Burns with a bright, smoky flame.
To extinguish: press the burning end into dirt or sand. Do NOT stomp — this scatters burning material.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Two-Person Bow Drill
steps: Person 1 holds the bearing block on top of the spindle and provides downward pressure.
Person 2 operates the bow with long, full-length strokes.
Communication: call 'more pressure' or 'faster' as smoke increases.
When thick smoke pours from the notch, Person 2 increases to maximum speed for 10 final strokes.
Person 1 carefully lifts the spindle. Person 2 gently fans the coal in the notch, then transfers to tinder bundle.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Fire-Proof Cement
steps: Mix clay soil with grass, straw, or animal hair in a 3:1 ratio (clay to fiber).
Add water until the mixture has a thick, workable consistency — like cookie dough.
Apply the mixture as mortar between rocks in a fire ring or chimney structure.
Let it air-dry for 24 hours before exposing to fire. Slow drying prevents cracking.
A small initial fire 'fires' the clay like pottery, making it permanent and waterproof.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Steel Wool & Battery Fire
steps: Pull apart fine-grade steel wool (0000 grade) into a loose, airy ball about fist-sized.
Use a 9V battery (ideal) or any battery 3V+. Touch both terminals to the steel wool simultaneously.
The steel wool glows orange and burns quickly. Have your tinder bundle ready beneath.
No steel wool? Any fine metal strand works: picture wire, wire brush strands, metal scrubbing pads.
This method works in rain and wind — the chemical reaction doesn't require the spark to catch. Just contact.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Magnifying Glass Fire
steps: Any convex lens works: magnifying glass, binocular lens, eyeglasses (farsighted only), camera lens, water-filled bag.
Prepare the finest, darkest tinder available. Char cloth is ideal. Dark materials absorb more energy.
Angle the lens between the sun and the tinder. Move lens closer/farther until the light dot is smallest.
Hold the focal point still on the tinder. Within 10-60 seconds, it will begin to smoke and glow.
Once glowing, transfer the ember to a tinder bundle and blow gently into flame.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Birch Bark Fire Uses
steps: Birch bark contains betulin oil — naturally waterproof and highly flammable even when wet.
Peel thin layers from dead or fallen birch — NEVER strip bark from a living tree (it kills the tree).
Thin sheets: light directly as tinder. They curl while burning, creating excellent fire starters.
Thick rolls: create birch bark torches by rolling tightly and binding with cordage.
Birch bark also works as container, roofing, writing material, and waterproof wrapping.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Making Char Cloth
steps: Char cloth is 100% natural cotton (like an old t-shirt or denim) that has been baked in the absence of oxygen.
Cut your 100% cotton fabric into 2-inch squares.
Place the tin directly onto the glowing coal bed of an established fire.
Wait until the smoke completely stops jetting out of the hole (about 5-10 minutes).
Let the tin cool completely to the touch.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Traditional Flint & Steel
steps: Obtain a piece of high-carbon steel (an old file, the spine of a carbon steel knife, or a dedicated striker).
Find a hard, sharp-edged stone: flint, chert, jasper, agate, or quartz.
Place a piece of char cloth directly on top of the sharp edge of the flint.
Strike the steel downward against the sharp edge of the flint at a glancing 30-degree angle.
When a spark catches on the char cloth (it will glow orange), transfer it to a tinder bundle and blow.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Fire Carry with Fungus
steps: Amadou (horse hoof fungus, Fomes fomentarius) is the premier fire-carrying material.
Cut the fungus in half and extract the inner trama layer.
Touch a glowing ember to the amadou sheet.
Wrap the smoldering amadou inside dry grass or bark to insulate it.
To restart your fire: unwrap the amadou, blow on it until it glows, place it in a tinder bundle.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Coal Extender Bundle
steps: Select a large, solid hardwood coal from your campfire.
Wrap the coal in a thick layer of green leaves (non-toxic species).
Wrap the leaf bundle in a thick layer of dry grass or bark.
Wrap the entire package in wet clay or mud.
The bundle can keep a coal alive for 6-12 hours.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Improvised Hook & Line
steps: Make hooks from thorns, bones, safety pins, or carved wood.
Use any strong thin material for line.
Bait with insects, worms, berries, or bright material.
Set multiple passive lines to maximize chance.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Meat Preservation (Smoking/Drying)
steps: Cut meat into thin strips — 1/4 inch thick.
For smoking: build a low smoky fire in an enclosed space.
For drying: hang strips in direct sun and wind.
Meat is done when it cracks but doesn't break when bent.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Pemmican
steps: Pound dried meat into powder.
Mix meat powder with rendered fat (50/50 ratio).
Add dried berries or nuts if available.
Form into bars or balls and let cool.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Ojibwe Bird Snare
steps: Find a flexible sapling near bird feeding areas.
Bend the sapling down and secure it with a trigger mechanism.
Attach a snare loop made from cordage to the trigger.
Place bait (berries, seeds, insects) around the trigger.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Pemmican Making
steps: Dry lean meat into jerky, then pound into powder.
Render fat by slowly heating it until it's liquid.
Mix powdered meat with warm rendered fat at a 1:1 ratio.
Press into bars. Stores for months without refrigeration.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Seaweed & Kelp Harvesting
steps: Harvest seaweed from clean water, attached to rocks (not free-floating).
Rinse thoroughly and dry in sun or near fire to preserve.
Avoid blue-green algae (cyanobacteria) — it's toxic.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Bird Snare Construction
steps: Build an Ojibwa bird pole: tall pole with perch and snare.
Place in open area near water or food sources where birds congregate.
Check snares frequently. Process caught birds immediately.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Small Game Field Dressing
steps: Make a small cut through the belly skin (avoid puncturing organs).
Remove intestines, stomach, and bladder. Keep liver, heart, and kidneys.
Skin by pulling hide off like removing a sock.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Wild Garlic & Onion Identification
steps: Look for grass-like leaves with a strong garlic or onion smell when crushed.
Dig up the bulb. It should look like a small onion or garlic clove.
Eat raw, add to cooking, or dry for preservation.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Wild Fruit Identification
steps: Use the Universal Edibility Test: test ONE plant part at a time.
Avoid: white/yellow berries, umbrella-shaped flowers, milky sap.
Safe bets: blackberries, raspberries, blueberries, rose hips.
Smell test: crush a small amount and smell. Reject if bitter or almond-like.
Skin test: rub on inner wrist, wait 15 minutes for reaction.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Acorn Leaching Methods
steps: Crack acorn shells with a rock. Remove meats and discard any with insect holes or dark spots.
Cold water method (best): crush meats, submerge in cold flowing water (stream) for 24-48 hours.
Hot water method (faster): boil crushed acorn meat, pour off brown water, add fresh water, repeat 5-7 times.
Taste test: a small piece should be mild and nutty with no bitterness. If bitter, continue leaching.
Use leached acorn meal to make flatbread, porridge, or thickener for stews. Dry for long-term storage.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Tree Sap Collection
steps: Tappable trees: maple, birch, walnut, sycamore. Sap flows in late winter/early spring when nights are freezing and days thaw.
Drill or cut a 1-2 inch deep hole at a slight upward angle, 3 feet from the ground.
Insert a spile — a hollowed-out elderberry or sumac branch, or carved wooden spout.
Hang a container below the spout. A birch bark container, bottle, or pot works.
Drink sap directly — it is mildly sweet and hydrating. Boil down for syrup (40:1 ratio for maple).

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Rock Boiling
steps: Create a watertight container: birch bark folded box, hollowed log, animal stomach, or depression in rock.
Heat rocks in your fire for 30+ minutes until they glow or hiss when sprinkled with water.
Use wooden tongs (split green sticks) to transfer hot rocks from fire to water container.
Each rock heats the water significantly. 3-4 rocks will bring a quart to a rolling boil.
Rock boiling can cook stews, soups, render fat, sterilize water, and process plant foods.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Improvised Flour Sources
steps: Cattail root: harvest thick rhizomes, peel, and dry. Pound into starchy flour.
Acorns: shell, leach tannins by soaking/boiling, dry, and grind into a rich flour.
Pine cambium: dry inner bark strips, then grind into fibrous flour.
Dried grass seeds: many grass species produce small seeds that can be collected and ground.
Mix any flour with water to make dough. Cook as flatbread on hot stones, wrap around sticks for twist bread.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Primitive Grill Construction
steps: Cut 4-6 green (living) sticks about 2 feet long, finger-thickness.
Drive two forked sticks into the ground on opposite sides of the fire pit, 18 inches tall.
Lay a crossbar between the forks. Arrange remaining sticks perpendicular to the crossbar, creating a grate.
Position the grate 6-8 inches above the coals — not the flames.
Grill meat, fish, roots, and mushrooms directly. Rotate food for even cooking. Replace charred grate sticks.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Fat Rendering Techniques
steps: Collect all fat from the carcass: kidney fat (suet), belly fat, and fat trimmed from meat.
Cut fat into small pieces (1/2 inch cubes) or grind for faster rendering.
Heat slowly in a pot or on a rock near the fire. Do NOT let it boil or smoke.
Strain the liquid fat through cloth into a clean container. Discard the solid cracklings (or eat them).
Clean rendered fat solidifies into white, odorless tallow that stores for months.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Paiute Deadfall Trap
steps: The Paiute deadfall is faster and more sensitive than a Figure-4, making it better for fast mice and rats.
Drive the Y-shaped vertical stick firmly into the ground.
Tie the cordage to the outward end of the lever stick.
Wrap the cordage halfway around the vertical Y-stick.
Bait the end of the bait stick under the rock.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Pine Inner Bark (Cambium)
steps: Identify a healthy Pine, Birch, or Spruce tree.
Chop away the rough, dark outer bark using a knife or axe.
Scrape off the soft, wet, white layer (the cambium) underneath.
Eat it raw, boil it into a broth, or dry it and grind it into flour.
Pine needles can also be chopped and steeped in hot (not boiling) water for a massive Vitamin C boost.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Broadleaf Plantain (Plantago)
steps: Identify Broadleaf Plantain (Plantago major) in lawns, trails, and disturbed dirt.
Check the distinct rubbery veins.
Harvest the smallest, youngest leaves from the center.
Boil the older leaves.
Use crushed leaves medicinally.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Cattail Root Foraging
steps: Locate cattails in shallow water or muddy shorelines.
Wade in and dig deep into the mud around the base of the stalk.
Pull up the thick, ropey rhizome.
Peel the outer rind.
Extract the starch.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Purslane Foraging
steps: Identify Purslane (Portulaca oleracea) growing flat on the ground in sunny, dry areas, sidewalks, or gardens.
Examine the leaves: They are fleshy, smooth, paddle-shaped, and succulent (thick).
Purslane is a survival superfood, containing more Omega-3 fatty acids than any other leafy green plant.
Harvest the entire plant by pulling it up.
Eat it raw or boiled.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Complete Dandelion Use
steps: Identify dandelions by their jagged, tooth-shaped leaves ('dent de lion') growing in a rosette from a central taproot.
Harvest young leaves before the plant flowers.
Eat leaves raw in a salad or boil to reduce bitterness.
Roast the taproot, grind it, and steep in hot water.
Flower heads can be eaten raw, battered and fried, or steeped into tea.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Reflector Oven Baking
steps: Construct a V-shaped reflector from aluminum foil, flat bark, or sheet metal.
Place a flat cooking surface (rock, metal sheet) inside the V.
The reflector focuses the fire's radiant heat onto the food from multiple angles.
Adjust distance from fire to control temperature.
Rotate the food periodically for even cooking.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Underground Rock Oven
steps: Dig a pit 2 feet deep and 2 feet wide.
Build a large fire in the pit and burn it for 2 hours.
Rake out the coals and ash.
Layer food (wrapped in non-toxic leaves like banana, burdock, or corn husks) on the hot rocks.
Cook for 2-4 hours.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Tripod Cooking Setup
steps: Lash three sturdy poles together at the top using a tripod lashing.
Spread the legs and set the tripod over your fire.
Adjust cooking temperature by raising or lowering the pot.
The tripod is the most stable camp cooking system.
Remove the pot using a sturdy stick through the bail handle.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Spit Roasting Technique
steps: Drive two forked stakes into the ground on either side of the fire.
Skewer the meat lengthwise on a green, hardwood spit (avoid resinous pine).
Rest the spit in the forks. Let gravity rotate it or turn it manually every 10 minutes.
Cook over COALS, not flames.
Meat is done when the juices run clear, not pink.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Channel Deadfall Trap
steps: Find or create a heavy, flat stone (20+ pounds).
Build two low brush walls creating a narrow channel leading to the trap.
Prop the stone on a Figure-4 trigger.
Bait the end of the bait stick.
Place the trap on a hard surface (flat rock) for maximum impact.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Steel Wool & 9V Battery
steps: Fluff up a piece of fine steel wool (Grade 0000 is best).
Touch the 9V battery terminals to the steel wool.
Blow on the glowing wool and add tinder.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Tampon Wound Dressing
steps: Open tampon package. Fluff out the cotton material.
Push into deep wounds or tape over surface cuts.
Secure with duct tape or fabric strip.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Duct Tape Butterfly Stitch
steps: Cut two small strips of duct tape. Twist the middle of one strip.
Stick one end to skin, pull wound closed, then stick other end.
Method 2: Cut 'H' or Butterfly shape from tape.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Finding North Star (Polaris)
steps: Find the Big Dipper (7 bright stars in a ladle shape).
Follow the two 'pointer stars' at the end of the cup upward.
Polaris is at the end of the Little Dipper's handle.
Face Polaris. You are facing TRUE north.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Southern Cross Navigation
steps: Find the Southern Cross — 4 bright stars in a cross/kite shape.
Extend the long axis of the cross 4.5× its length.
Draw a perpendicular line from the midpoint of the pointer stars.
Where the two lines intersect is roughly south.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Sun Position Navigation
steps: The sun rises in the EAST and sets in the WEST.
At solar noon (highest point), the sun is due SOUTH (Northern Hemisphere).
Estimate time from sun position for rough direction.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Moon Navigation
steps: If the moon is a crescent, draw a line through the two horns.
Where the line meets the horizon is roughly south (Northern Hemisphere).
Full moon rises in east, sets in west—same as the sun.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: River Following Strategy
steps: Follow rivers DOWNSTREAM — they lead to towns.
Stay on the bank, not in the water.
Watch for signs: fences, bridges, roads, logging.
If river becomes impassable, climb above and continue.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Altimeter Navigation
steps: Read your altitude from altimeter watch, phone GPS, or barometric altimeter.
Find the contour line matching your altitude on the topographic map.
Combine with one compass bearing to a landmark for exact position.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Urban Emergency Navigation
steps: Use the sun for cardinal directions — same methods work in cities.
Satellite dishes point south (Northern Hemisphere) — instant compass.
Follow major roads — they connect population centers.
Look for railroad tracks — they always lead to stations and towns.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Pace Bead Systems
steps: Make a ranger bead system: 9 beads in one group, 4 in another on a cord.
Pull down one lower bead for every 100 meters walked.
At 1000 meters (1 km), reset lower beads and pull one upper bead.
Alternative: transfer pebbles between pockets for each 100 meters.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Snow Environment Navigation
steps: In whiteout: STOP. Moving blindly in whiteout conditions is often fatal.
Use sastrugi (wind-carved snow ridges) to maintain direction.
If you must move in low visibility, probe ahead with a ski pole or stick.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Handrail Navigation
steps: Identify a linear feature running roughly parallel to your desired direction.
Keep the handrail feature on your left or right as you travel.
When you reach your cross-feature (catching feature), leave the handrail.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Deliberate Offset Navigation
steps: When aiming for a target on a linear feature, deliberately aim to one side.
Aim 10-20 degrees to the LEFT of your target intentionally.
When you reach the linear feature, turn in the direction of your target.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Vegetation Direction Indicators
steps: In the Northern Hemisphere, heavier growth is typically on the south side of trees.
Moss grows on the SHADED side — NOT always north.
Combine multiple vegetation indicators for better accuracy.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Lichen Growth Direction
steps: In the Northern Hemisphere, lichen grows thickest on the north side of trees and rocks.
This is opposite in the Southern Hemisphere — thickest lichen growth on the south side.
Check multiple trees and rocks to establish a pattern — individual specimens can be unreliable.
Algae (green) on tree trunks follows the same pattern as lichen — thicker on the shaded pole-facing side.
Lichen direction-finding is a supplementary method — cross-reference with sun, stars, or wind indicators.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Snow Cornice Reading
steps: Observe the ridgelines of mountains or the tops of massive boulders.
Identify which way the cornice overhangs.
Cross-reference with local knowledge of prevailing winds.
Use sastrugi on flat terrain.
Combine snow clues with vegetation.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: 5-4-3-2-1 Grounding
steps: Name 5 things you can SEE.
Name 4 things you can TOUCH.
Name 3 things you can HEAR.
Name 2 things you can SMELL.
Name 1 thing you can TASTE.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Positive Self-Talk Script
steps: Replace 'I can't' with 'I haven't figured out how yet.'
Break the task into the very next single step.
Acknowledge fear, then redirect: 'I'm scared AND I can still act.'
Repeat: 'I have survived every bad day so far. 100% success rate.'

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Keeping Children Calm
steps: Children mirror adult emotions. Control yours first.
Give them jobs — simple, achievable tasks.
Be honest at an age-appropriate level.
Physical comfort: stay close, maintain physical contact.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Pain Management Without Meds
steps: Focus breathing: slow, deep—4 in, 8 out.
Mental distraction: count backward from 1000 by 7s.
Cold water on wrists and face triggers dive reflex.
Accept the pain—resistance amplifies it.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Field Sleep Hygiene
steps: Establish a consistent sleep schedule even without a clock.
Create a pre-sleep ritual: secure camp, bank fire, check water.
Insulate from below — ground steals heat 30x faster than air.
Eat something before sleeping — digestion generates internal heat.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Managing Children in Survival
steps: Control YOUR reaction first — children mirror adult emotions exactly.
Give age-appropriate honest information. No false promises.
Assign tasks — gathering sticks, counting supplies, watching for planes.
Maintain routines — meals, stories, bedtime. Normalcy is medicine.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Managing Night Anxiety
steps: Accept that nighttime anxiety is universal and biological.
Maintain a fire — even a small flame resets primal fear circuits.
Identify every sound. Named threats feel manageable; unknown ones terrify.
Breathe slowly: 4 counts in, 7 counts hold, 8 counts out.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Survival Journaling
steps: Use any surface — bark, dirt, stone, clothing, skin.
Record date, weather, food/water intake, and emotional state.
Write observations about your environment — resources, wildlife patterns, weather.
Write messages to loved ones — even if never delivered, it helps.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Sleep Discipline in Survival
steps: Prioritize sleep over almost everything except immediate danger.
Use power naps: 20-minute naps every 4-6 hours if continuous sleep is impossible.
Create sleep conditions: darkness, warmth, wind protection, elevated surface.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Establishing Normalcy & Routine
steps: Establish a daily schedule within the first 24 hours.
Assign roles and responsibilities to every group member.
Maintain personal hygiene and camp organization.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Composting
steps: Choose a spot away from water and shelter — ideally shaded.
Layer green (nitrogen) and brown (carbon) materials alternately.
Keep the pile moist like a wrung-out sponge.
Turn the pile every 1-2 weeks with a stick or improvised fork.
Compost is ready when it's dark, crumbly, and earthy-smelling.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Brain Tanning (Leather)
steps: Scrape all flesh and fat from the hide.
Mash the animal's brain in water to make a slurry.
Stretch and smoke the hide.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Maximizing Phone Battery in Emergency
steps: Turn on airplane mode immediately.
Turn off airplane mode ONLY to attempt calls/texts, then back on.
Send texts instead of calling — texts use far less power.
Emergency numbers (112, 911, 999, etc.) work on any network and often without a SIM.
Before battery dies: text your GPS coordinates to someone.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Reading & Sharing GPS Coordinates
steps: On iPhone: Open Compass app — coordinates shown at bottom.
On Android: Open Google Maps, tap the blue dot, coordinates appear.
Share coordinates as decimal degrees: e.g., 37.7749, -122.4194.
What3Words: every 3m×3m square has a unique 3-word address.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Trail Blazing Markers
steps: Break branches at shoulder height pointing your direction.
Stack 3 rocks (cairn) at every turn or decision point.
Scratch arrows on trees or dirt at junctions.
Tie bright cloth strips to branches every 50-100 feet.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Personal Locator Beacon (PLB)
steps: Deploy the antenna fully and activate the beacon.
Place beacon with clear sky view — no canopy, no overhangs.
Leave activated for minimum 24 hours. Do not turn off and on.
Stay near the beacon. Rescuers will come to the signal location.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Drone Search Awareness
steps: Maximize thermal contrast — drones often use infrared cameras.
Create large, clear ground signals visible from directly above.
Wave reflective materials — drones detect moving objects and reflections.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: River Self-Rescue Position
steps: Roll onto your back, feet pointing downstream.
Use feet to push off rocks and obstacles.
Look downstream for eddies (calm water behind rocks).
To reach shore: ferry angle at 45 degrees across current.
Avoid strainers (fallen trees, debris piles) at all costs.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: A-Frame Debris Hut
steps: Place a long ridgepole with one end on the ground and the other on a stump or tree branch.
Lean sticks against the ridgepole on both sides to form an A-frame.
Pile massive amounts of debris over the framework.
Add a final layer of bark or branches to hold the debris in place.
Stuff the interior with dry leaves or grass for bedding.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Round Debris Hut
steps: Create a tripod base by lashing three long poles together at the top.
Lean more poles against the tripod to close the gaps.
Thatch or pile debris heavily around the entire cone.
Build a small door plug from tied branches.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Tarp: A-Frame
steps: Find two anchor points 6-8 feet apart: trees, poles, or stakes.
Tie ridgeline between anchors using taut-line hitches.
Drape tarp over ridgeline evenly, with equal material on both sides.
Stake down each corner at 45-degree angle, pulling tarp taut.
Add ground insulation: leaves, pine needles, or boughs inside.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Igloo
steps: Cut firm snow blocks with a saw or knife.
Lay the first circle of blocks.
Cut a slope into the first layer.
Stack subsequent blocks in a rising spiral.
Cap the top with a final keystone block.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Emergency Mylar Wrap
steps: Wrap around torso and legs, keeping the shiny side facing inward.
Tuck edges under body and overlap seams.
Cover your head — leave only face exposed for breathing.
Get off the ground: sit on pack, logs, or pile of leaves.
Shield from wind. Get behind a tree, rock, or build a mound.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Raised Platform Shelter
steps: Find 4 trees in rectangle, or plant 4 sturdy poles.
Lash crossbeams between trees at same height.
Lay poles across beams for platform floor.
Add A-frame roof with thatched leaves or tarp.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Shelter Door & Entrance
steps: Face entrance away from prevailing wind.
Build a plug door from bundled sticks, bark, or a stuffed pack.
Create an L-shaped entrance if possible to break wind.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Shelter Ventilation Design
steps: If using fire inside shelter, create a smoke hole at the top.
Create a low air inlet opposite the smoke hole.
Monitor for condensation — it indicates insufficient ventilation.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Snow Wall Windbreak
steps: Cut or pack snow into blocks 12x12x6 inches.
Stack blocks in a crescent shape on the windward side.
Let blocks freeze together for 30 minutes before trusting the wall.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Coastal Driftwood Shelter
steps: Build ABOVE the high tide line. Look for debris marks indicating tide reach.
Stack driftwood logs into a windbreak facing away from prevailing ocean wind.
Use seagrass, kelp, or sand to fill gaps and add insulation.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Swamp Shelter
steps: Find the driest elevated ground (hummock) in the swamp.
Build a raised platform from logs lashed together.
Cover platform with thick bedding and build overhead rain cover.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Tarp: Plow Point
steps: Stake one corner of the tarp to the ground facing into the wind.
Lift the center ridgeline with a pole or ridgeline rope.
Stake remaining corners and edges taut.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Tarp Plow Point
steps: Tie one corner of the tarp to a tree at waist height.
PULL the opposite corner tight and stake it to the ground.
Stake the remaining two corners.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Debris Hut (Standard)
steps: Set a ridgepole against a tree or rock.
Line sides with 'rib' sticks.
Pile 3 feet of debris (leaves, grass) on top.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Shelter Drainage
steps: Dig a shallow trench (4-6 inches deep) around three sides of the shelter, open on the downhill side.
Angle the trench so water flows away from the entrance.
Build a slight berm (raised edge) along the shelter base using excavated dirt.
Inside: slope the floor slightly toward the entrance so any infiltration drains outward.
Create a drip line inside the entrance using a raised lip or rolled cloth to block water from flowing in.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Wind Direction Assessment
steps: Wet your finger and hold it up — the cool side faces the wind.
Drop light debris (grass, dust, fine snow) and watch which direction it drifts.
Look at tree lean and flag trees — they indicate PREVAILING (long-term) wind direction.
Check snow drift patterns, sand dunes, and cloud movement for wind direction.
Orient your shelter entrance 90° to the prevailing wind. Place the shelter's back wall into the wind.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Rainwater Roof Diversion
steps: Angle roof covering at 45° minimum — steeper is better for heavy rain.
Layer roofing material from bottom up like shingles — each layer overlaps the one below.
Create drip edges by extending roofing material 6+ inches past the shelter walls.
In heavy rain, install a bark or folded-leaf gutter to channel water for collection.
Waterproof layers: plastic sheeting > bark slabs > palm/banana leaves > layered grass > conifer boughs.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Shelter Door/Entry Insulation
steps: Build a door frame from sticks lashed into a rectangle matching the entrance size.
Weave branches, grass, or bark through the frame to create a solid panel.
Add insulating stuffing: dry leaves, moss, or grass packed between two woven layers.
Hinge the door on one side with flexible cordage or leather strips.
For deep cold: build an L-shaped entrance tunnel to create a cold air trap.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Shelter Site Drainage Check
steps: Look for water stain marks on trees and rocks — they show previous flood levels.
Check soil moisture: dig 2 inches down. If wet, water table is too high.
Pour water on the ground and watch where it flows. Your shelter should be UPHILL of the flow path.
Avoid the bottom of valleys, gullies, and ravines — these collect cold air and water.
Ideal site: mid-slope, slightly elevated, with natural drainage away from the shelter footprint.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Improvised Hammock
steps: Find two strong trees 8-12 feet apart. Test each by pushing — they should not sway.
Use a sturdy tarp, blanket, or woven bark mat as the hammock body.
Attach to trees at chest height using strong rope, paracord, or woven vine. Wrap 3x around each tree.
The hammock should sag to roughly a 30° angle — too tight causes back pain, too loose wraps around you.
Add insulation UNDERNEATH — a sleeping pad, stuffed clothes bag, or leaf-filled blanket below your body.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Brush Wall Windbreak
steps: Drive stakes 6 inches apart in a line perpendicular to the wind direction.
Weave branches horizontally through the stakes in a basket-weave pattern.
Fill remaining gaps by stuffing with leaves, grass, moss, or fern fronds.
Build the wall in a semi-circle or L-shape to block wind from multiple directions.
Place your fire between you and the windbreak for maximum reflected warmth.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Shelter Maintenance Schedule
steps: Daily: check roof for new leaks after rain. Mark leak locations and add material above them.
Every 2-3 days: add fresh insulation to walls and roof. Natural materials compress and degrade.
Weekly: inspect all lashing and structural joints. Re-tie anything loose.
After storms: immediately inspect for structural damage before re-entering.
Keep drainage channels clear — leaves and debris clog them quickly.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Solar Shelter Heating
steps: Orient the shelter opening to face south (Northern Hemisphere) or north (Southern Hemisphere).
Clear vegetation and shade from the sunny side of the shelter.
Place dark-colored rocks inside the shelter in the sun path — they absorb heat during the day.
Create a clear plastic window from a bag or sheet to create a greenhouse effect.
At night, cover the window/opening with an insulating panel to trap the stored heat.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Natural Bedding Construction
steps: Layer 1 (bottom): waterproof barrier — bark slabs, plastic sheeting, or dense packed leaves.
Layer 2: structural support — 2-inch diameter sticks laid parallel, spaced 2 inches apart.
Layer 3: bulk insulation — 12+ inches of dry leaves, grass, pine needles, or fern fronds.
Layer 4: comfort — softest materials on top: moss, fine grass, cattail fluff, or a cloth layer.
Test by lying on the bed — if you can feel the ground cold through the layers, add more insulation.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Kochanski Super Shelter
steps: The Super Shelter combines a standard lean-to with a clear plastic greenhouse front and a thermal reflector back.
Build an elevated, heavily insulated bed of pine boughs underneath a sloped log framework.
Line the sloped back wall and the ceiling of the framework with the shiny Mylar space blanket facing inward.
Drape the clear plastic sheeting completely over the front, sealing the enclosure tightly to the ground.
Build a fire 2 to 3 feet outside the clear plastic wall.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Hammock & Tarp System
steps: Select two trees 12–15 feet apart with no dead branches overhead (widow-makers).
Hang the hammock with a 30-degree sag angle.
Rig a tarp above the hammock in an A-frame or diamond configuration.
Use a structural ridgeline inside the hammock.
In cold weather, use an underquilt or reflective pad beneath you.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Improvised Containers
steps: Birch bark container: fold bark into box shape, pin with thorns.
Green bamboo section: cut between nodes for natural cup.
Stone bowl: find a concave rock and grind it deeper.
Woven basket: weave flexible branches in over-under pattern.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Camp Organization
steps: Fire pit at center, downwind from shelter.
Food storage 200 feet from camp, hung from a tree.
Latrine 200 feet from camp, downhill from water source.
Tool and supply area organized within arm's reach of shelter.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Carving a Fish Hook
steps: Find a thorn, bone splinter, safety pin, or hardwood twig.
Gorge hook: sharpen both ends of a 1-inch splinter, tie in middle.
Curved hook: heat and bend thorn/wire into J-shape with barb.
Attach to line with clinch knot. Test pull before fishing.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Net Making
steps: Make cordage first—need 50-100 feet minimum.
Tie horizontal header line between two supports.
Tie vertical lines using sheet bend knots at intervals.
Weave horizontal rows, knotting at each intersection.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Stone Axe Construction
steps: Find flat, hard stone with natural edge.
Shape edge by pecking with harder stone.
Split green wood handle, wedge stone inside.
Wrap split with wet rawhide — shrinks as it dries.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Bone Needle Making
steps: Select a splinter of dense leg bone (deer/cow).
Grind on sandstone to shape and sharpen.
Drill the eye using a sharp flint flake or metal tip.
Polish smooth to prevent fraying cordage.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Advanced Bark Container
steps: Harvest a large rectangular sheet of fresh birch, elm, or poplar bark.
Score fold lines for a box shape. Fold up the sides.
Pin corners with wooden pegs or sew with root cordage.
Seal all seams with heated pine resin mixed with charcoal.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Stone Drill (Pump Drill)
steps: Knap a small, sharp stone point from flint or obsidian.
Lash the stone point to the end of a straight, round shaft.
Attach a crossbar with string to create the pump mechanism.
Push down on the crossbar repeatedly. The string winds and unwinds, spinning the drill.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Sinew Cordage
steps: Remove tendons from along the backbone or leg of a game animal.
Dry the sinew completely — it becomes hard and brittle.
When needed, chew or soak the sinew to soften it.
Twist or braid fibers into cord. Sinew shrinks as it dries, tightening bindings.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Atlatl (Spear Thrower)
steps: Carve a flat board 18-24 inches long with a handle at one end.
Carve or attach a small spur (hook) at the far end.
Make lightweight darts 5-6 feet long with feather fletching.
Practice the overhand throwing motion — flick the wrist at the end.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Bark Cloth Processing
steps: Harvest inner bark strips from paper mulberry, basswood, or elm.
Soak the bark strips in water for 1-2 days.
Pound the soaked bark with a smooth stone or wooden mallet.
Dry the bark cloth in the sun. It becomes a durable, flexible fabric.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Bamboo Multi-Tool Construction
steps: Select a green bamboo section at least 1 inch diameter.
Split lengthwise by scoring and applying pressure. Shape the edge with a rock.
Fire-harden the edge by slowly rotating over hot coals. Do not burn.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Natural Adhesive Making
steps: Collect pine or spruce resin from tree wounds. Look for amber-colored lumps.
Melt resin over low heat. Mix with crushed charcoal (1:1 ratio) and optional plant fiber.
Apply hot to the joint. It sets in 1-2 minutes as it cools.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Torch Construction
steps: Find a green hardwood stick 2-3 feet long and 1-2 inches thick.
Pack the split top with birch bark, pine resin, cloth, or fatwood shavings.
Light from existing fire. Hold at 45-degree angle to control dripping.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Digging Stick & Pry Bar
steps: Select a hardwood branch 3-4 feet long, 1.5-2 inches diameter.
Sharpen one end to a chisel or point using a knife or sharp rock.
Fire-harden the working end by slowly rotating over coals.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Cobble Hammer & Hammerstone
steps: Find a dense, egg-shaped river cobble that fits comfortably in your hand.
For a hafted hammer: find a stone with a natural groove or pecked groove for binding.
Use different faces for different tasks: flat for pounding, edge for splitting.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Improvised Snow Goggles
steps: Select material: birch bark, cardboard, leather, or any opaque flexible sheet.
Cut a strip to cover both eyes. Cut narrow horizontal slits (1mm) at each eye position.
Attach cordage or elastic to wrap around the head. Blacken the inner surface.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Hand Drill Fire Optimization
steps: Select spindle: dead, dry, straight, pithy stem. Mullein, cattail, or elderberry ideal.
Floating hand technique: alternate hands downward while maintaining speed and pressure.
Add friction enhancers: sand in the notch, and moisture from crushed green plants on your palms.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Basic Hide Tanning
steps: Flesh the hide: scrape all meat, fat, and membrane from the inner surface.
Brain tan: mash the animal's brain and rub into the hide thoroughly.
Smoke the hide over a slow smoldering fire to set the tan.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Shell Scraper
steps: Collect large, thick-shelled bivalves: mussels, clams, or oysters from freshwater or coastal areas.
Break the shell in half along its hinge. Each half becomes a scraping/cutting tool.
Sharpen the edge by grinding on rough stone. Create a beveled edge like a chisel.
Use as: hide scraper (removing fat and membrane), fish scaler, wood shaper, and cutting edge.
Large shells also serve as containers, cups, scoops, and cooking vessels (for rock-boiling small amounts).

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Fire-Hardened Digging Stick
steps: Select a straight, green hardwood stick 3-4 feet long, 1.5-2 inches diameter.
Carve one end to a flat chisel point or a rounded spade shape using your knife.
Hold the carved end over hot coals (NOT flames) and rotate slowly until the surface darkens.
Let cool completely, then smooth by rubbing on a rock. The hardened tip resists dulling.
Uses: digging roots and tubers, making fire pits, building shelter foundations, prying loose bark.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Basic Weaving
steps: Gather long, flexible materials: willow shoots, cattail leaves, grass bundles, split roots, or bark strips.
Start with a simple over-under pattern: lay warp fibers parallel, weave weft fibers perpendicular.
For a flat mat: keep warp fibers parallel and flat. Push weft fibers tight against each other.
For a basket: splay warp fibers from a center point like wheel spokes. Weave weft in a spiral.
Finish edges by folding warp ends over and tucking them into the weave.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Gourd Canteen
steps: Harvest a mature, hard-shelled gourd — bottle gourds and dipper gourds work best.
Cut a small opening at the top. Remove all seeds and pulp with a stick or wire.
Fill with water and small pebbles. Shake vigorously to scour the interior walls.
Dry completely in the sun for several days. The interior must be fully dry to prevent mold.
Seal the opening with a carved wooden plug or corn cob stopper. Add a carrying cord through drilled holes.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Natural Adhesive (Pine Pitch Glue)
steps: Collect pine resin — scrape from wounds on coniferous trees. You need about a golf ball-sized lump.
Melt the resin in a small rock depression or bark container near heat (not over flames — it's flammable).
Mix in finely powdered charcoal (1:3 charcoal to resin ratio) while the resin is liquid.
Add a small amount of finely shredded plant fiber or animal hair for additional reinforcement.
Apply while warm and liquid. It sets as it cools into a strong, waterproof bond.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Antler Tools
steps: Find shed antlers on the ground in late winter/spring — deer, elk, and moose shed annually.
Use tine tips for pressure flaking — sharpening and shaping stone tools.
Use the base/brow tine as a wedge — drive between logs to split them.
Antler sections make excellent knife handles, toggles, buttons, and needle storage.
Antler tines can be sharpened into awls, fish hooks, and harpoon points.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Emergency Rope Making
steps: Harvest long, strong plant fibers: inner bark of basswood/linden, yucca leaves, nettle stalks, cattail leaves.
Process fibers: soak in water to soften, then split into thin, consistent strands.
Reverse-wrap technique: take 2 fiber bundles. Twist each clockwise, then wrap them around each other counter-clockwise.
Splice new fibers in: when one bundle runs short, lay a new fiber alongside it overlapping 3+ inches, and continue twisting.
Test strength progressively. Two-ply cordage from basswood bark can support 100+ pounds.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Stinging Nettle Cordage
steps: Identify tall Stinging Nettle plants in late summer or fall.
Split the long green stem vertically with your fingernail.
Peel the strong green outer skin away from the woody core.
Hold the green skin strips over a fire or lay them in the sun to dry slightly.
Perform the reverse-wrap technique.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Fire Hardening Wood
steps: Carve your tool to the desired shape while the wood is still green and workable.
Hold the carved tip over low, steady coals.
The wood should turn a deep tan to light brown color.
If the wood catches fire or turns black, it is burned and weakened.
A properly fire-hardened spear tip can penetrate thick animal hide.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Fish Bone Hook
steps: Select a thick, strong bone splinter or a large, curved thorn (like honey locust or hawthorn).
If using bone, grind it against sandstone into a J or U shape.
Carve a barb near the point by making a small cut at a 45-degree angle.
Notch the shank (top of the J) to accept a fishing line.
Bait with insects, worms, berries, or small pieces of raw meat.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Pine Pitch Glue
steps: Collect hardened pine, spruce, or fir resin from the bark of living trees.
Melt the resin over low heat in a small container (a shell, tin can, or rock depression).
Add crushed charcoal to the molten resin (approximately 1 part charcoal to 3 parts resin).
Optionally add beeswax or animal fat to make the mixture more flexible and less brittle when cooled.
Apply while warm and malleable.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Birch Bark Basket
steps: Peel a large rectangle of birch bark from a fallen or dead tree.
Score fold lines with a knife, but do NOT cut through.
Fold the bark into a box shape, creasing the corners.
Seal all seams with melted pine pitch to make the basket waterproof.
Birch bark containers can be used to boil water using hot rocks.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Brain Tanning Hide
steps: Flesh the hide: scrape all meat, fat, and membrane from the skin side.
Remove the hair by soaking the hide in a wood ash solution for 3-5 days.
Scrape the hair off once it slips easily.
Apply a paste of mashed brain tissue to the hide.
Stretch and work the hide continuously as it dries.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Rawhide Production
steps: Flesh the hide thoroughly, removing all fat and membrane.
Stretch the hide tightly in a frame made from bent branches.
Scrape both sides smooth while it dries in the frame.
Dried rawhide is extremely strong and rigid.
Rawhide shrinks as it dries.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Boiling Water
steps: Bring water to a rolling boil.
Maintain the boil for 1 minute (3 minutes above 6,500 ft).
Let cool before drinking.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Charcoal Water Filter
steps: Make charcoal from hardwood burned down to coals.
Crush charcoal into fine granules.
Build a filter: container with hole → grass → charcoal → sand → gravel.
Run water through multiple times for clearer results.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Dew Collection
steps: Wrap absorbent cloth around your ankles and walk through grass at dawn.
Wring the cloth into a container when saturated.
Purify if possible — dew from grass may contain contaminants.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Rain Collection
steps: Spread any waterproof material to maximize collection area.
Use any available containers — bottles, pots, carved wood, pits lined with plastic.
Let the first few minutes of rain wash the collection surface.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Finding Water by Vegetation
steps: Look for green vegetation in dry terrain.
Follow animal trails that converge — they lead to water.
Watch birds at dawn and dusk — grain-eating birds fly toward water.
Dig in the outside bend of dry riverbeds.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Melting Snow & Ice for Water
steps: Never eat snow directly — it lowers core body temp.
Melt snow or ice in a dark container in the sun.
Prefer ice over snow — ice yields more water per volume.
Use body heat or ambient sunlight to melt snow.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Birch Tree Tapping
steps: Find a birch tree with trunk at least 10 inches diameter.
Carve a small hole 2 inches deep, angled slightly upward.
Insert a tube or hollow stick into the hole.
Collect sap. Drinkable immediately—slightly sweet.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Snow Melting Best Practices
steps: Never eat snow directly — it lowers core body temperature dangerously.
Melt snow in a dark container in sunlight, or near (not on) fire.
Start with a small amount of water in the container, then add snow gradually.
Use ice instead of snow when available — it yields more water per volume.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Water Safety Assessment
steps: Check clarity — hold water up to light. Clear is better than cloudy.
Smell the water — chemical or sulfur odors mean contamination.
Look at the surroundings — dead animals, algae blooms, or industrial runoff.
When in doubt, purify. The cost of purification is always less than the cost of illness.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Enhanced Transpiration Bag
steps: Select a sun-facing branch with the most leafy growth.
Enclose the leafy end in a clear plastic bag. Seal airtight around the branch.
Place a small rock in the bottom corner to create a collection point.
Set up multiple bags — each yields only 60-200ml per day.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Seawater Ice Desalination
steps: In freezing conditions, collect seawater in shallow containers.
Let water freeze partially. Pour off the unfrozen brine (concentrated salt).
Melt the ice. Taste test — repeat freeze/pour if still salty.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Tarp Rain Catchment
steps: Spread tarp with edges elevated and center dipped to form a funnel.
Place a container under the lowest point of the tarp.
Pre-rinse the tarp if dirty. First flush may contain debris.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Altitude Boiling Adjustments
steps: At sea level, water boils at 212°F (100°C). At altitude, it boils at lower temperatures.
Good news: pathogens die well below boiling temperature.
Bring water to a rolling boil for 1 minute (3 minutes above 6,500 feet).

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Emergency Urban Water Sources
steps: Hot water heater: 30-80 gallons of safe, stored water.
Toilet tanks (NOT bowls): contain 1-2 gallons of clean water.
Pipe drainage: open the lowest faucet to drain remaining water from pipes.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Improvised Oral Rehydration Solution
steps: Mix: 1 liter clean water + 6 teaspoons sugar + ½ teaspoon salt.
Stir thoroughly until fully dissolved. Taste should be like tears.
Sip slowly over 4-6 hours. Do not gulp.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Tree Sap as Water Source
steps: In late winter/spring, many trees produce copious drinkable sap: maple, birch, walnut, sycamore.
Cut a V-shaped notch into the bark on the south-facing side, 3 feet from the ground.
Insert a spout: hollowed elder branch, folded bark channel, or angled stick under the cut.
Collect 1-3 liters per day per tap depending on tree size and conditions.
Drink sap raw — no purification needed. It tastes mildly sweet and clean.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Seep Well Improvement
steps: Find a natural seep: muddy ground on a hillside, moss growth in dry terrain, insect swarms over wet soil.
Dig a basin at the seep point — 12 inches deep, 18 inches wide.
Create an overflow channel on one side to prevent the basin from flooding and becoming muddy.
Line the approach path with rocks to prevent foot traffic from muddying the water source.
Cover the basin with bark or a flat rock when not in use to prevent contamination from animals and debris.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Maple & Birch Tapping
steps: Select a tree at least 10 inches diameter. Maple and birch are best — both produce abundant, drinkable sap.
Using a knife, drill, or sharpened stick, create a hole 2 inches deep, angled slightly upward.
Carve a spile from elderberry, sumac, or any wood with soft pith — hollow out or split for channel.
Hang a container from the spile or place below it. Sap flow is highest when nights freeze and days thaw.
Drink sap raw for hydration. It is sterile, mildly sweet, and contains minerals.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Safe Cactus Hydration
steps: WARNING: Most cacti are toxic and will cause severe vomiting, accelerating dehydration.
Instead of barrel cacti, look for Prickly Pear (Opuntia) fruit or pads.
If near Agave plants, you can harvest the flower stalk.
Do NOT drink milky sap.
Rely on solar stills or animal trails rather than cracking open random cacti.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Birch and Maple Tapping
steps: This method only works in late Winter or early Spring, when daytime temps are above freezing and nights are below freezing.
Identify a Sugar Maple, Silver Maple, or Birch tree.
Drill or carve a hole 2 inches deep into the trunk at a slight upward angle.
Insert a hollow spile.
Hang a container below the spile.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: Hot Rock Boiling (Wooden Bowl)
steps: Find a natural depression in a log, or carve/burn a bowl into heavy piece of wood.
Build a large fire and heat several fist-sized, dry, non-porous rocks.
Fill your wooden bowl or hide with the dirty water.
Using green wooden tongs, transfer a glowing hot rock from the fire into the water.
Rotate rocks as they cool.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97814405797.name: DIY Charcoal Water Filter
steps: Cut the bottom off a large plastic bottle or use a hollow log section.
Layer the filter materials from bottom to top: fine sand, crushed charcoal, coarse sand, gravel, grass.
The charcoal is the critical layer.
Pour dirty water into the top of the filter.
CRITICAL: This filter removes sediment and improves taste, but does NOT kill bacteria or viruses.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Tropical Disease Prevention
steps: Prevent mosquito bites: cover all skin, especially at dawn and dusk.
Sleep under a net or in a sealed shelter.
Purify ALL water in tropical environments.
Treat any cut or scratch immediately — tropical infections spread fast.
Monitor for fever, chills, severe headache — signs of tropical infection.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Desert Night Travel
steps: Travel only at night in extreme desert heat.
Navigate by stars: Polaris (north) or Southern Cross (south).
Watch for nocturnal wildlife — scorpions, snakes are active at night.
Stay warm — deserts drop 40-50°F at night.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Desert Shelter
steps: Create shade — even 15°F cooler in shade vs. full sun.
Dig a trench 18 inches deep and lie in it.
Elevate yourself off the ground surface if possible.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Finding Water in the Desert
steps: Look for green vegetation — it always indicates water nearby.
Follow animal tracks downhill — they lead to water.
Dig in the outside bend of dry riverbeds.
Make a solar still: plastic sheet over a hole with a collection container.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Jungle Movement
steps: Follow waterways downstream — they lead to civilization.
Use a stick to probe ahead and clear spider webs.
Step on logs, not over them. Check before grabbing vines.
Move slowly during the hottest hours. Pace yourself.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Arctic Shelter Building
steps: Use snow as your primary building material — it's an excellent insulator.
Build a quinzhee: pile snow 5 feet high, let it settle 2 hours, hollow it out.
