Always create a ventilation hole in the roof — CO2 can kill you.
Build a sleeping platform ABOVE the entrance — warm air rises.
Block the entrance with a pack or snow block at night.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Desert Travel Strategy
steps: Travel only at night or during dawn/dusk.
Stay covered — loose, light-colored clothing covering all skin.
Navigate by stars at night, compass in day.
Never walk without water. Ration sweat, not water.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Desert Shade Shelter
steps: Create a double-layer shade with 12-inch air gap.
Raise the shelter 18 inches off the ground.
Orient the open side away from prevailing wind.
If no materials: dig a trench and cover with any available material.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Finding Water in Desert
steps: Look for green vegetation — especially cottonwood, willow, and palm.
Dig in the outside bend of dry washes and riverbeds.
Watch for birds (especially doves) at dusk — they fly toward water.
Check rock crevices and shaded north-facing cliff bases.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Snow Shelter (Quinzhee)
steps: Pile snow into a mound 7-8 feet tall, 10 feet wide.
Let the mound settle for 1-2 hours (sintering).
Insert sticks 12 inches deep all over the mound.
Hollow from a low entrance upward. Sleep platform above entrance.
Poke a fist-sized ventilation hole in the roof.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Arctic Travel & Navigation
steps: Travel during warmest hours. Shelter during extreme cold.
Manage layers to prevent sweating.
Use snowshoes or improvised floatation to prevent post-holing.
Watch for thin ice: dark ice, snow-covered ice, and ice near moving water.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Frostbite Prevention & Treatment
steps: Recognize: white/gray waxy skin, numbness, hardness.
Rewarm in body heat: armpit, groin, stomach.
If possible, rewarm in 98-102°F water for 20-30 minutes.
Do NOT rewarm if refreezing is possible.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Jungle Movement & Navigation
steps: Follow streams and rivers — they're natural highways in dense jungle.
Use a machete or heavy stick to probe ahead. Watch for snakes.
Travel early morning and late afternoon. Rest during midday heat.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Swamp Navigation & Safety
steps: Test ground before stepping. Use a long pole to probe depth and firmness.
Follow animal trails — they use the firmest paths.
Protect against mosquitoes, leeches, and waterborne parasites continuously.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Sandstorm Shelter-in-Place
steps: Turn your back to the wind. Cover nose and mouth immediately.
Get low. Find the lee side of a large rock, dune, or vehicle.
STAY PUT until the storm passes. Mark your direction before visibility drops.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Tundra & Arctic Travel
steps: Navigate by wind direction — prevailing winds are the most reliable compass in featureless terrain.
Avoid thin ice: dark ice, snow-covered ice, ice near current or outflows.
Build snow walls or dig into snowdrifts for emergency shelter.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Lava & Pyroclastic Flow
steps: Evacuate laterally (perpendicular) to lava flow.
Avoid low-lying areas (valleys, riverbeds).
Protect against heat radiation. Cover exposed skin.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Escaping Rip Current
steps: Do NOT swim against the current (toward shore). You will tire and drown.
Swim parallel to shore (sideways) until out of the pull.
When current releases you, swim diagonally to shore.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Cougar/Mountain Lion
steps: Maintain eye contact. Do NOT turn back. Do NOT run.
Look big. Raise jacket/arms. Pick up children.
If attacked: Fight back. Protect neck.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Wolf Pack Encounter
steps: Stand ground. Do not run. Stare them down.
Back away slowly while facing them. Keep group together.
Use fire, smoke, or rocks to deter.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Moose/Elk Encounter
steps: Watch ears/hair. Ears back + hair up = charge.
If charged: RUN. Get behind a tree.
If knocked down: curl in ball. Protect head.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Tick Removal
steps: Grasp tick close to skin with tweezers.
Pull upward with steady pressure. Don't twist.
Clean area. Save tick if possible for ID.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Shark Encounter
steps: Maintain eye contact. Rotate as it circles.
Slow, rhythmic movements. Do not splash.
If attacked: hit nose, eyes, gills.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Fog Navigation
steps: In dense fog, visibility may drop below 50 feet. STOP moving if you cannot see landmarks.
Use sound for navigation: streams, wind through trees, distant traffic all provide directional cues.
Walk with one arm extended if on a trail or road to feel for obstacles.
Use a compass if available — fog completely eliminates visual references.
Mark your trail: break branches, stack rocks, drag a stick to leave a visible line. You may need to retrace.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Extreme Altitude Survival
steps: Above 15,000 feet, available oxygen drops to ~55% of sea level. Every action takes 2-3x more effort.
Recognize altitude sickness: headache, nausea, dizziness, fatigue, difficulty sleeping.
CRITICAL: if symptoms worsen (confusion, stumbling, coughing pink froth, severe headache): DESCEND IMMEDIATELY.
Hydrate aggressively — you lose 2-3x more water through breathing at altitude than at sea level.
Eat high-carbohydrate foods — carbs require less oxygen to metabolize than protein or fat.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Cave Flooding Response
steps: If rain is falling or forecast, DO NOT enter caves. Flash floods fill caves with zero warning.
If water begins rising inside a cave: EXIT IMMEDIATELY. Do not wait to see if it stops.
If the exit is already flooded: move to the highest point inside the cave. Keep your head above water.
Mark your height: scratch a line on the wall at water level. Check every few minutes to see if water is still rising or receding.
If forced to swim out an underwater passage: hyperventilate slightly (3-4 deep breaths), dive, and swim FOR the light.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Railroad Track Following
steps: Railroad tracks always lead to civilization — towns, stations, or at minimum, road crossings.
Walk BESIDE the tracks, not on them. Trains are quieter than you expect and approach fast.
Look for mile markers, crossing signs, and signals — these indicate proximity to roads and towns.
Railroad bridges and trestles: DO NOT cross on foot if there is no walkway. Find an alternative crossing.
Follow the direction with the most recent rail polishing — a shinier rail surface indicates more traffic from that direction.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Abandoned Mine Safety
steps: NEVER enter an abandoned mine unless it is your ONLY survival option. Mines kill people every year.
If you must enter: check air quality. Light a match or candle at the entrance — if it dims or goes out, the air is bad.
Stay near the entrance. Do not explore deeper. Use the mine entrance as shelter only.
Never touch old timber supports — they may be the only thing preventing collapse.
Watch for vertical shafts (winzes) in the floor — they can be hundreds of feet deep and invisible in darkness.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Desert Water Rationing Strategy
steps: NEVER ration water. Drink what you have when you are thirsty.
Ration SWEAT instead.
Stay off the ground surface.
Breathe through your nose, not your mouth.
Signs of severe dehydration: dizziness, confusion, cessation of sweating, dark urine.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Arctic Travel Protocol
steps: Regulate your temperature to avoid sweating.
Travel on frozen rivers and lakes only after testing ice.
Navigate using the wind.
Never touch metal with bare skin.
Keep water bottles inside your clothing against your body.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: River Crossing Technique
steps: Scout the river before crossing.
Never cross where the water is above your waist.
Remove your pants and socks.
Face upstream and cross diagonally, angling slightly downstream.
If you are swept off your feet, float on your back with feet downstream.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Night Movement Evasion
steps: Move only during the darkest 4 hours (typically 0100-0500).
Use the fox walk: Place the outside edge of your foot down first, roll inward, test for noise.
Navigate by stars or compass with luminous dial.
Stay in shadows and avoid silhouetting against the sky on ridgelines.
If you hear pursuers, freeze completely and remain motionless.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Anti-Tracking Techniques
steps: Walk in streams for at least 100 meters before exiting on rocky ground.
Walk backward for 50 meters, then sidestep to a hard surface.
Use the buttonhook: walk past your intended turn, continue 100m, then circle back at a right angle.
Cross hard surfaces (rock, pavement, gravel) whenever possible.
If dogs are tracking, cross through areas with strong-smelling plants (wild onion, camphor).

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Camouflage Face Paint Application
steps: The human face is highly recognizable due to its symmetry and light-dark contrast.
Darken light areas (forehead, nose, cheekbones, chin) with mud, charcoal, or dark clay.
Lighten dark areas (eye sockets, under nose, neck shadows) with lighter clay or ash.
Use irregular, diagonal stripes — never horizontal or symmetrical patterns.
Cover all exposed skin including ears, backs of hands, and neck.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Dog Team Evasion
steps: Move downwind of your trail whenever possible.
Wade through water crossings at least 200 meters long.
Move through areas with strong competing scents: farms, garbage, wild garlic patches.
Pepper spray or chili powder on your trail causes temporary nasal pain and disruption.
The most effective dog evasion is SPEED and DISTANCE.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Checkpoint Bypass
steps: Observe the checkpoint from concealment for at least 30 minutes before attempting bypass.
Bypass by routing around it through terrain that is too difficult for guards to watch.
Move during shift changes or high-traffic periods when guards are distracted.
If you must pass through, blend with local pedestrian or vehicle traffic.
Have a cover story with verifiable details prepared.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Ice Fishing Through Jigging
steps: Verify ice thickness: minimum 4 inches of clear blue ice for walking.
Chop or drill a hole 8-10 inches in diameter through the ice.
Scoop out ice chips from the hole.
Drop a baited hook or bright lure to the bottom, then lift it 6 inches off the bottom.
Fish caught through ice keep indefinitely in sub-freezing conditions.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Vapor Barrier Liner System
steps: Below 0°F, body moisture migrating outward through insulation layers freezes inside the insulation.
Wear a vapor barrier (plastic bag, rain jacket) NEXT TO YOUR SKIN, inside all insulation.
Your skin quickly reaches 100% humidity and sweating stops.
Apply the same principle to feet: wear a plastic bag between your sock liner and outer sock.
At the end of each day, turn your sleeping bag inside out and beat the frost crystals off.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Swiftwater Survival Position
steps: Do not attempt to stand up.
Roll onto your back.
Point feet downstream.
Steer using your arms.
Flip and swim only at the end.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Hurricane Shelter Protocol
steps: Shelter in an interior room on lowest floor
Do NOT go outside during the eye
Store emergency water in bathtubs
After the storm, avoid flood water

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Cold Water Swimming Survival
steps: Control breathing
HELP position if alone
Huddle if with others
Do NOT swim unless shore is within 100m

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Nuclear Fallout Shelter-in-Place
steps: Get inside the most massive structure available — concrete, brick, underground.
Seal all openings: tape windows, block vents, close all doors.
Stay sheltered for minimum 48 hours, ideally 2 weeks.
If you were outside during the blast: remove ALL clothing, shower or wipe down thoroughly.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Tornado Shelter in Open Field
steps: If you can drive away at right angles to the tornado's path, DO IT.
If trapped: find a ditch, culvert, or low depression.
Protect your head and neck with arms, bag, or any object.
DO NOT shelter under highway overpasses — wind accelerates through them.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Anti-Tracking & Evasion
steps: Walk on hard surfaces: rock, gravel, packed earth. Avoid mud and soft ground.
Brush out visible tracks behind you with a leafy branch.
Change direction at hard-surface intersections to break the trail.
Avoid breaking branches, disturbing spider webs, or crushing vegetation.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Eye Injury (Field Treatment)
steps: Foreign object: flush eye with clean water for 15+ minutes.
Impaled object: DO NOT REMOVE IT. Stabilize in place.
Chemical exposure: flush continuously for 20+ minutes.
Snow blindness: patch both eyes with dark cloth for 24 hours.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Hand Drill Fire
steps: Find a straight, dry spindle 18-24 inches long, pencil-thick.
Use the same fireboard setup as bow drill with V-notch.
Roll the spindle between your palms while pressing down.
Continue until ember forms in the notch dust.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Ferro Rod (Firesteel)
steps: Prepare a tinder bundle of dry, fine material.
Hold the rod against the tinder. Pull the striker BACK toward you.
Strike firmly with fast, controlled strokes.
Once tinder catches, gently blow and transfer to fire lay.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Solar Fire (Lens Method)
steps: Use any convex lens: glasses, binoculars, camera lens, water-filled bag.
Focus the smallest possible bright dot on dark tinder.
Hold steady. The tinder will begin to smoke, then glow.
Transfer the ember to a tinder bundle and blow to flame.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Long Fire (All-Night Fire)
steps: Find 2 green logs, 6 inches thick and as long as your body.
Lay them parallel, 6-8 inches apart.
Build a small teepee fire in the gap to ignite the logs.
Sleep parallel to the fire, 2-3 feet away.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Starting Fire in Rain
steps: Find dry tinder UNDER things: bark, overhangs, dead standing trees.
Split wet wood to expose the dry interior.
Build the fire on a platform of sticks above wet ground.
Use fatwood (resinous pine heartwood) — it burns even when wet.
Shield with your body and a covering while establishing the fire.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Fire Plow
steps: Find a flat softwood board and a hardwood shaft.
Cut a groove 8 inches long in the board.
Rub the shaft back and forth rapidly in the groove.
Fine wood dust accumulates at the end—it will ignite.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Pine Resin Torch
steps: Collect pine resin from wounds on pine or spruce trees.
Wrap resin-soaked cloth or bark around the end of a green stick.
Light the torch. Burns 20-40 minutes with bright flame.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Fire Saw Method
steps: Split a dry bamboo segment lengthwise.
Carve a small notch in the base piece. Place tinder underneath.
Saw the edge of the second piece rapidly across the notch.
Hot dust falls through notch onto tinder. Blow to flame.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Fire Reflector Wall
steps: Stack green logs or flat rocks into a wall behind your fire.
Angle the wall slightly toward your shelter for maximum heat reflection.
Sit or sleep between the fire and your shelter — heat from both sides.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Birch Bark Fire Starting
steps: Peel thin, papery bark from dead or fallen birch trees.
Shred or crumple the bark into a loose nest for tinder.
Ignite with spark or match. Bark burns hot and long enough to light kindling.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Trench Fire
steps: Dig a narrow trench 12 inches deep, 8 inches wide, oriented into the wind.
Build fire at the downwind end of the trench.
Lay sticks or a grill across the trench for cooking.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Hand Sanitizer Fire Starter
steps: Squeeze a quarter-sized amount of hand sanitizer onto your tinder bundle.
Ignite with spark, match, or lighter. The gel burns for 30-60 seconds.
Add kindling while the gel is burning.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Fire on Snow
steps: Build a platform of green logs laid side by side on the snow.
Add a second layer of logs perpendicular to the first.
Build your fire on top of the platform using dry dead wood.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Smoky Fire for Insect Repellent
steps: Build a small fire and let it burn down to coals.
Add green leaves, damp grass, or punky wood to produce thick smoke.
Use a smudge pot (fire in a can or container) for portable smoke.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Jute Twine Fire Starter
steps: Cut 6-8 inches of jute twine and unravel the strands.
Fluff the separated fibers into a bird's nest shape.
Strike a ferro rod downward into the center of the nest.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Fire Safety Perimeter
steps: Clear all flammable material in a 10-foot circle around the fire pit.
Build a ring of rocks around the fire pit.
Keep water or loose soil staged nearby for emergency extinguishing.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Fire Plough
steps: Select a soft baseboard — dead hibiscus, willow, cedar, or cottonwood. Must be bone-dry.
Carve a straight groove down the center, 12 inches long, 1/4 inch deep.
Select a hardwood rubbing stick — the tip should be rounded, not pointed.
Rub the stick back and forth in the groove with downward pressure and increasing speed.
Hot dust accumulates at the end of the groove. When it begins to smoke consistently, tip onto tinder bundle.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Camp Fire Safety
steps: Clear all leaves, needles, and dry debris in a 10-foot diameter circle around the fire.
Build a fire ring from rocks — do NOT use river rocks (they can explode from trapped moisture).
Keep the fire only as large as you need. A fist-sized fire boils water; a torso-sized fire heats a shelter.
Never leave a fire unattended. When leaving, drown it — stir — drown again — feel with hand.
In windy conditions, keep fire LOW or dig a Dakota fire hole to contain flames.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Underground Fire (Dakota Hole)
steps: Dig a main fire pit — 12 inches wide, 12 inches deep. Flare the bottom slightly wider.
Dig a ventilation tunnel angled from 12 inches upwind to the bottom of the fire pit.
Build your fire in the bottom of the main pit. Start small and build up.
Advantages: minimal smoke, low visibility, wind-resistant, concentrated heat for cooking.
Place a flat rock or green wood grill across the top for cooking. The pit concentrates heat upward.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Night Fire Management
steps: Build a large coal bed before sleeping — feed the fire heavily with hardwood 30 minutes before bedtime.
Use the long-log method: place 2-3 large logs side by side on the coal bed.
Bank the fire: push ash and coals against the logs on the sleeping side. Reduce open flames.
Set a mental alarm or buddy system to check the fire every 2-3 hours.
In morning: rake the coals apart, add fine kindling, blow gently to reignite.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Permanganate & Sugar
steps: Potassium Permanganate (purple water purification crystals) produces severe exothermic reactions when mixed with certain organic compounds or sugars.
Place one teaspoon of Potassium Permanganate crystals onto a flat, dry rock.
Add an equal amount of granulated sugar to the pile of purple crystals.
Grind the two powders together vigorously using a smooth, blunt stick.
Step back instantly as soon as it begins to smoke.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Slow Match Fire Cord
steps: Tightly twist or braid a rope from fibrous bark (cedar, tulip poplar) or cattail.
Soak the cord in a saltpeter (potassium nitrate) solution if available.
Dry the cord completely.
Light one end.
Coil the smoldering cord and carry it.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Fuel Selection: Tinder, Kindling, Fuel
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
Fire needs three sizes of fuel, used in sequence:

Tinder (catches a spark/ember):
- Dryer lint, charcloth, cotton balls in vaseline
- Birch bark (contains flammable oils — burns even wet)
- Cedar bar

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Fire-Starting Methods Compared
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
Ranked by reliability (best to worst):

1. Lighter — easiest, works thousands of times, even when empty (spark ignites tinder)
2. Ferrocerium rod — 3,000°F sparks, works wet, 10,000+ strikes
3. Matche

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Fire as a Signaling Tool
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
Fire is one of the most visible signals available to survivors.

Daytime: SMOKE
- Green branches, wet wood, and damp leaves create thick white smoke
- Rubber, oil-soaked cloth, and plastics create bla

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Fire Maintenance Through the Night
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
A fire that dies at 3 AM is a survival setback. Maintaining fire through the night is a core skill.

Banking a fire for the night:
1. Let fire burn down to a deep coal bed (1-2 hours of steady burning

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: The Science of Primitive Fire
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
Understanding WHY friction fire works makes you better at it.

The physics:
- Friction generates heat. Wood ignites at approximately 450°F (230°C).
- A bow drill concentrates friction at a single poin

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Wood Identification for Fire
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
Not all wood is equal. Knowing your fuel transforms fire-making from frustration to science.

Hardwoods (best for long-burning coals):
- Oak: king of fuel woods. Burns hot, long, makes excellent coals

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Fire in Different Biomes
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
Every environment presents unique fire challenges. Adapt your approach.

Desert:
- Fuel is scarce. Dried animal dung burns well. Dead brush and sage work.
- Wind is constant. Use trench fires or Dakot

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Chemical Fire Starters Guide
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
Modern materials that ignite easily — many are in your pocket or pack right now.

Common accelerants:
- Hand sanitizer (60-70% alcohol): gel stays in place, burns 30-60 sec
- Petroleum jelly + cotton:

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Cooking Fire Configurations
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
Cooking on a campfire is not the same as cooking on a stove. Different configurations serve different purposes.

Direct flame cooking:
- Good for: boiling water, searing meat
- Bad for: controlled tem

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Flail Chest Management
steps: Recognize flail chest: a section of ribs moves OPPOSITE to breathing (inward on inhale, outward on exhale).
Have the patient lie on the injured side, or stabilize the flail segment with your hand.
Do NOT wrap the entire chest — this restricts breathing of the good lung.
Monitor oxygen level: watch for blue lips, confusion, rapid heart rate, worsening distress.
Evacuate urgently. Keep patient semi-upright (45°) for easiest breathing.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Pelvic Fracture Management
steps: Suspect pelvic fracture if: pain in groin/hip area, inability to bear weight, leg appears shortened or rotated.
Do NOT move the patient unless in immediate danger. Stabilize where they lie.
Wrap a sheet, belt, or SAM splint tightly around the hips at the level of the greater trochanters.
Place a rolled blanket or pack between the knees and tie the legs together.
Treat for shock: keep warm, elevate legs only if no spinal injury suspected, monitor consciousness.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Basic Splinting Principles
steps: Splint in the position found. Do NOT try to straighten a deformed limb.
Splint must immobilize the joint ABOVE and BELOW the fracture.
Pad the splint generously — especially at bony prominences.
Tie splint firmly but not so tight that circulation is cut off. Check pulse, sensation, and movement below.
Materials: sticks, trekking poles, rolled magazines, SAM splints, cardboard, bark slabs, foam pads.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Traumatic Amputation Response
steps: Control bleeding on the stump FIRST. Apply direct pressure, then tourniquet if bleeding is severe.
Wrap the stump with sterile gauze or clean cloth. Apply firm pressure bandage.
Treat for shock: lay flat, elevate legs, keep warm, reassure.
Recover the severed part if safe to do so. Rinse gently with clean water.
Wrap the part in moist gauze, place in a sealed bag, then place bag on ice. Do NOT freeze or submerge in water.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Hypothermia Wrap
steps: Remove all wet clothing. Cut off if patient is shivering too hard to undress.
Lay a vapor barrier (tarp, emergency blanket, trash bags) on the ground first.
Wrap patient in dry insulation layers: sleeping bag, blankets, dry clothing. Cover head.
Add heat sources to the core: warm water bottles at neck, armpits, and groin.
Wrap the outer layer with another vapor barrier (tarp/emergency blanket). Create a 'burrito.'

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Field Blood Pressure Assessment
steps: Check radial pulse (wrist): if present, systolic blood pressure is at least 80 mmHg.
Check femoral pulse (groin): if present, systolic BP is at least 70 mmHg.
Check carotid pulse (neck): if present, systolic BP is at least 60 mmHg.
Assess capillary refill: press fingernail bed white, release, count seconds to return pink.
Mental status is the most sensitive indicator: alert → confused → verbal response only → unresponsive.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Chilblains Treatment
steps: Recognize: red or purple swollen patches on fingers, toes, ears, or nose that itch and burn.
Rewarm GRADUALLY. Move to a warm area. Do NOT apply direct heat (no fire, hot water, or heating pads).
Keep the area dry and covered. Apply gentle moisturizer to prevent cracking.
Elevate affected hands or feet to reduce swelling.
Prevention: keep extremities dry, change wet socks frequently, wear vapor barrier liners in boots.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Chemical Water Purification
steps: Iodine: add 5-10 drops of 2% tincture of iodine per liter of clear water. Double for cloudy water.
Bleach: add 2 drops of unscented household bleach (5-8% sodium hypochlorite) per liter of clear water.
Wait 30 minutes before drinking. If water is cold or cloudy, wait 60 minutes.
After treatment, water should have a slight chemical taste/smell. If not, add another dose and wait 15 more minutes.
Limitations: chemical treatment does NOT remove chemical contaminants, and may not kill Cryptosporidium.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Field Hygiene Protocol
steps: Wash your hands before eating and after defecating.
Designate a latrine area 200+ feet downhill and downstream from camp and water sources.
Change socks daily or air-dry them.
Inspect your body daily for ticks, leeches, and developing skin infections.
Boil eating utensils and cooking surfaces.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Deep Trench Latrine
steps: Dig a trench 4 feet long, 1 foot wide, and 2 feet deep.
Pile the excavated dirt beside the trench with a scoop tool.
Lay two log rails across the trench for footing.
Sprinkle wood ash into the trench periodically.
When the trench is within 1 foot of full, bury it completely and dig a new one.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Natural Insect Repellent
steps: Crush wild basil, citronella grass, or lemon balm leaves and rub them directly on exposed skin.
Burn green leaves of these plants on your campfire.
Apply mud thickly to exposed skin.
Tuck pants into socks and sleeves into gloves.
Set up camp on high, breezy ground away from standing water.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Leech Removal Protocol
steps: Do NOT yank, burn, or salt the leech while it is attached.
Slide a flat edge (fingernail, credit card) under the oral sucker (thin end).
The wound will bleed freely for some time because leech saliva contains an anticoagulant.
Clean the wound with antiseptic or clean water.
Prevent leeches by tucking pants into socks and applying insect repellent to boot tops.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Trench Foot Prevention
steps: Understand the mechanism.
Elevate and dry feet daily.
Rotate dry socks.
Never apply direct high heat.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Simple Wire Snare
steps: Make a noose loop from wire, paracord, or strong cordage.
Secure the free end to a stake, tree, or spring pole.
Set at head height on an active game trail.
Set 10-12 snares to increase your odds.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Fish Spear (Gig)
steps: Find a straight pole 6-8 feet long.
Split the tip into 4 prongs, 6-8 inches deep.
Harden the tips in fire.
Aim BELOW the fish — refraction makes fish appear higher than they are.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Universal Edibility Test
steps: Test only ONE plant part at a time (leaf, root, fruit, stem).
Rub on inner wrist for 15 minutes. Wait 45 minutes for reaction.
Touch to lip corner for 3 minutes. Then tongue tip for 15 minutes.
Chew a small piece for 15 minutes without swallowing.
If no ill effects after 8 hours, eat 1/4 cup. Wait another 8 hours.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Pine Needle Tea
steps: Gather green pine needles. Avoid yew (flat needles, red berries—toxic).
Chop or bruise needles to release oils.
Steep in hot (not boiling) water for 10-15 minutes.
5x more vitamin C than orange juice. Prevents scurvy.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Smoking Meat for Preservation
steps: Cut meat into thin strips — 1/4 inch maximum.
Build smoking rack 2-3 feet above small, smoky fire.
Use hardwood: oak, hickory, maple, apple. Never pine.
Smoke 6-12 hours until dry and dark. Should snap, not fold.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Acorn Processing & Cooking
steps: Collect fallen acorns. Discard any with holes (insect larvae inside).
Crush nutmeat and soak in running water or change water repeatedly.
Dry leached acorn meal and grind into flour for cooking.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Clay Pot Construction & Cooking
steps: Find clay near stream banks — it holds shape when squeezed and doesn't crumble.
Build pot using coil method: roll long snakes of clay and stack in circles.
Dry slowly for 24-48 hours, then fire in a hot campfire for 2-3 hours.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Pine Cambium Harvest
steps: Select a living pine tree. Cut through the outer bark to expose the creamy-white inner bark (cambium).
Peel strips of cambium using a knife blade, scraping along the wood grain.
Eat raw — it is chewy with a mild, slightly resinous flavor.
For better flavor and nutrition: dry strips and grind into flour for flatbread.
Alternatively: boil strips into a starchy noodle-like food or fry in rendered animal fat.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Organ Meat Nutrition
steps: Liver: the most nutrient-dense organ. Provides massive amounts of vitamins A, B12, iron, and protein.
Heart: lean muscle tissue, high in protein, iron, and CoQ10. Treat like a steak — slice and cook.
Kidneys: high in B12 and selenium. Soak in cold water for 30 minutes first to reduce strong flavor.
Stomach and intestines: clean thoroughly, boil for 30+ minutes. Good source of fat and protein.
Bone marrow: crack long bones and roast near fire. Marrow is calorie-dense and rich in healthy fats.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Gill Net Fishing
steps: Construct a net from paracord inner strands, fishing line, natural cordage, or unraveled fabric.
Tie mesh using a series of square knots or sheet bends. Create a net 3-4 feet tall and as wide as the stream.
Attach floats (bark, foam, sealed containers) to the top line and weights (rocks) to the bottom.
Set the net across a fish travel lane: stream narrows, inlet/outlet of a pool, near structure.
Check the net every few hours. Remove fish promptly — dead fish decay fast and deter other catches.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Trotline Fishing
steps: Tie a strong main line between two fixed points across a stream or pond.
Attach dropper lines (12-18 inches) every 3-4 feet along the main line.
Bait each hook — use insects, worms, small fish, bread, or meat scraps.
Set the trotline so hooks hang at different depths — some near surface, some mid-water, some near bottom.
Check every 2-4 hours. Re-bait empty hooks. Process caught fish immediately.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Bird Call Trap
steps: Learn to mimic distress calls — kissing the back of your hand rapidly creates a high-pitched squeaking.
Set snares around a clearing or perch before calling. Use fine cordage in slip-noose loops.
Hide in concealment 10-20 feet from the snare area. Make distress calls for 3-5 minutes, then wait 5 minutes.
For waterfowl: create a blind near water and use splashing sounds to simulate feeding activity.
Process caught birds by plucking (hot water dipping loosens feathers) and gutting. Cook thoroughly.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Hand Fishing (Noodling)
steps: Wade slowly through shallow water near undercut banks, submerged logs, and rock crevices.
Feel slowly under banks and into holes with your hands. Fish hiding in holes will not flee — they are cornered.
When you feel a fish, slide your hand forward to the gills. Grip firmly behind the gill plate.
For flatfish (catfish, flounder): pin against the bottom with both hands, then scoop upward.
Caution: be aware of snakes, turtles, and crayfish in the same holes. Feel carefully before grabbing.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Crayfish Catching by Hand
steps: Search in shallow streams and ponds — lift rocks slowly and look for crayfish underneath.
Approach from behind — crayfish escape by flipping their tail backward rapidly.
Grasp firmly behind the claws, pinching the body between thumb and finger.
Collect in a container of stream water. Keep alive until ready to cook.
Cook by boiling in water for 5-8 minutes until bright red. Eat the tail meat and claw meat.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Solar Food Drying
steps: Slice meat into thin strips (1/4 inch or thinner). Cut against the grain for easier eating.
Build a drying rack: horizontal sticks between two uprights, 3-4 feet off the ground.
Drape strips over the rack sticks with space between each strip for airflow.
Build a small smoky fire beneath the rack to repel flies and add flavor.
Meat is done when strips crack when bent but don't break completely. Store in a dry, sealed container.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Fishing Bait Collection
steps: Worms: drive a stake into moist ground and rub another stick across the top. Vibrations bring worms up.
Grubs: peel bark from dead logs and rotting stumps. Beetle larvae (white grubs) live underneath.
Insects: catch grasshoppers in the early morning when they're sluggish from cold.
Crayfish: catch small ones (1-2 inches) for bait — tail meat is irresistible to bass and catfish.
Improvised bait: cheese, bread balls, corn, berries, raw meat scraps, even soap (contains fat that attracts catfish).

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Bone Grease Rendering
steps: Crack long bones into 2-inch pieces using rocks. Smash thoroughly — expose the marrow and spongy interior.
Place crushed bones in a pot or rock-boiling container with water. Bring to a sustained boil.
Skim the fat that rises to the surface. Collect in a separate container.
Let rendered fat cool and solidify. It stores for weeks in a cool, sealed container.
Use bone grease for cooking, pemmican making, waterproofing leather, and skin protection.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Hot Stone Oven
steps: Dig a hole 2 feet deep, 2 feet wide. Line the bottom and sides with flat stones.
Build a fire in the stone-lined pit and burn for 2+ hours until the stones are extremely hot.
Rake out all coals and ash. Place food directly on the hot stones or on a bed of green leaves.
Cover with more green leaves, then a layer of dirt or sand to seal in the heat.
Cook time: 1-2 hours for root vegetables, 3-4 hours for large meat pieces. Uncover carefully — steam is scalding.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Ash Caking (Nixtamalization)
steps: Mix clean hardwood ash with water to create an alkaline solution (lye water).
Boil corn or grain kernels in the ash water for 1-2 hours until hulls begin to loosen.
Drain and rinse thoroughly — wash at least 3 times to remove all ash residue.
The resulting product (hominy/nixtamal) can be eaten as-is, dried for storage, or ground into masa flour.
This process increases calcium, iron, and niacin availability by 2-3x over unprocessed corn.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Drag Snare Set
steps: Instead of anchoring the snare to an immovable object, attach it to a heavy drag — a log or branch.
The drag should be heavy enough to slow the animal but not so heavy it can't move it at all.
The drag catches on brush and trees as the animal moves, preventing it from traveling far.
Set the snare on a known game trail with the drag positioned behind the snare, uphill if possible.
Check drag snares every few hours. Follow the drag trail to find the entangled animal.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Edible Insect Identification
steps: SAFE: crickets, grasshoppers (remove legs — spiny), ants, termites, beetle grubs, earthworms.
AVOID: brightly colored insects, hairy caterpillars, insects that smell strongly, stinging insects.
COOK all insects before eating when possible. Cooking kills parasites and improves flavor.
Where to find: under rocks and logs (grubs, worms), in rotting wood (beetle larvae), in grass (grasshoppers, crickets).
Nutritional value: 100g of crickets = 21g protein, 6g fat, plus iron, calcium, B12.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Fish Smoking Technique
steps: Gut and fillet fish. Make long, thin cuts to expose maximum surface area to smoke.
Brine in salt water for 30 minutes if salt is available. This firms the flesh and improves preservation.
Build a smoking rack: green sticks arranged as a grate over a small fire pit, enclosed with a shelter frame.
Use green wood for smoke: hickory, apple, alder, oak. Avoid resinous woods (pine, spruce) — they impart bitter resin.
Smoke for 4-8 hours at low heat (150-180°F). Fish is done when firm, golden-brown, and flakes with a fork.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Salt Extraction
steps: Coastal: boil seawater in a shallow container until all water evaporates. White crystals remain.
Inland: find salt licks — mineral deposits where animals congregate. Break off salt-crusted rock.
Plant ash method: burn hardwood, dissolve ash in water, filter, and evaporate. The residue contains potassium salts.
Blood: animal blood contains significant salt. Use fresh blood as a soup base or seasoning.
Salt is essential for electrolyte balance, food preservation, and hide tanning. Prioritize finding a source.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Deadfall Trap Variations
steps: Figure-4 deadfall: three sticks notched to interlock, supporting a heavy stone. Bait stick triggers collapse.
Paiute deadfall: uses a toggle and string trigger for more sensitivity than the figure-4.
Split-stick deadfall: a vertical stick holds the rock up. Bait is placed inside a split in the upright.
Place multiple deadfalls along game trails, near burrow openings, and at identified feeding areas.
Test the trigger before setting the weight stone. The trigger should release with a light touch.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Natural Refrigeration Methods
steps: Cold stream method: seal food in waterproof containers and submerge in a cold stream.
Evaporative cooling: wrap food in wet cloth and place in the wind or shade.
Underground cache: dig a hole 2-3 feet deep in shade. Line with rocks. Place food inside and cover.
Snow/ice cache: bury food in snow or pack in natural ice. Snow insulates at 32°F.
Hang food from a tree in shade: keeps it away from ground predators and in the cooler air above.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Pit Trap
steps: Dig a pit on an active game trail — 3 feet deep, 2 feet wide for rabbit-sized prey. Deeper for larger animals.
Drive sharpened stakes into the bottom of the pit, pointing upward.
Cover the pit with thin sticks, leaves, and debris to match the surrounding ground.
Funnel the game trail into the pit using brush barriers on either side.
Check pits every few hours. Mark pit locations clearly so YOU don't fall in.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Rabbit Processing
steps: Pinch the skin at the middle of the back and make a small cut through the skin only — not the muscle.
Insert two fingers into the cut and pull in opposite directions — the skin peels off like a shirt.
Cut off the head and feet with a knife. Make a small cut at the base of the belly to open the cavity.
Reach in and pull all organs out in one mass. Save the heart, liver, and kidneys — discard the rest.
Rinse the cavity with clean water. Cook immediately — rabbit has almost no fat and spoils quickly.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Cordage Snare Sets
steps: Make 10-20 snare loops from cordage, wire, fishing line, or inner paracord strands.
Size the loop to the target: rabbit = 4-inch diameter, squirrel = 2-inch, deer = 10-inch.
Set snares on active game trails — look for fresh droppings, tracks, disturbed vegetation.
Use guide sticks to funnel animals through the snare — narrow the trail with sticks on either side.
Anchor snares to a strong stake or tree. For larger animals, use a spring pole to lift and strangle quickly.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Animal Track Identification
steps: Deer: split heart-shaped hooves, 2-3 inches long. Walking stride 14-18 inches.
Rabbit: hind feet (long, 3-4 in) land AHEAD of front feet (small, round, 1 in) when hopping.
Turkey: 3-toed, large (4 inches). Scratchings in leaf litter indicate feeding areas.
Raccoon: hand-like prints with 5 distinct toes. Often found near water sources.
Track freshness: sharp edges = hours old. Softened edges = days old. Water-filled = recent (after last rain).

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Spring Pole Snare
steps: Find a flexible, strong sapling near an animal trail to act as your spring engine.
Bend the sapling down to the trail.
Construct a trigger mechanism (like a toggle) and drive an anchor stake into the ground.
Set the trigger and arrange the snare loop vertically over the trail.
When triggered, the sapling violently snaps upright.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Squirrel Snare Pole
steps: Find a long, thick logging pole or branch (4-6 inches diameter, 6-10 feet long).
Cut 4 to 6 wire snares. The loops should be 2.5 inches in diameter.
Attach the snares sequentially up the length of the pole.
Position each loop so it stands upright on the top face of the pole.
The squirrel gets caught and falls off the side of the pole, hanging.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Smoke Preservation of Meat
steps: Cut the meat into thin strips, no thicker than 1/4 inch.
Build a small, low-heat smoldering fire.
Construct a drying rack 2–3 feet above the smoke source.
Maintain the smoke continuously for 12–24 hours.
Properly smoked jerky can last weeks at ambient temperature.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Grub and Larva Consumption
steps: Look under rotting logs, peeling bark, and in the soil around dead trees.
Palm weevil larvae, witchetty grubs, and mealworms are all safe and highly nutritious.
Pinch the head off, as mandibles can bite your throat on the way down.
Eat raw or roasted on a hot rock near the fire.
A handful of large grubs provides more calories than a handful of berries.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Palm Heart Harvesting
steps: The heart of palm is the soft, white growing tip found inside the top of the trunk.
Fell a young palm tree (easier than a mature one).
Strip the outer layers of trunk until you reach the soft, white inner core.
Eat raw or sliced into soups.
WARNING: Harvesting kills the tree.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Coconut Water Safety
steps: Green (young) coconuts contain the most water and have thin, soft meat.
Brown (mature) coconuts have less water but thick, calorie-dense meat.
Limit coconut water intake to 3-4 coconuts per day.
Coconut meat provides fat, protein, and calories.
Use the husk fiber for cordage and the shell as a container or bowl.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Box Trap for Small Game
steps: Build a rectangular box from sticks lashed together, or use an existing container (bucket, crate).
Prop one end open with a stick connected to a trigger.
Bait the trigger with food appropriate for the target animal.
The animal enters, disturbs the trigger, and the propped end falls, trapping it inside.
Check box traps every 12 hours.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Pit Trap with Stakes
steps: Dig a pit 4 feet deep and 3 feet wide on a known game trail.
Sharpen 4-6 hardwood stakes and drive them into the pit floor, points up.
Cover the pit with thin, breakable sticks laid across the opening.
Build funnel walls (brush barriers converging toward the pit) to guide animals over it.
Mark the pit clearly so you don't fall into your own trap.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Harvesting Inner Tree Bark (Cambium)
steps: Identify an edible tree.
Score the outer bark.
Peel back to the cambium layer.
Scrape off the cambium.
Boil or fry to eat.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Figure-4 Deadfall Bait Placement
steps: Build trigger from three sticks
Place bait away from the back of the rock
Set trigger with minimal pressure
Check traps every 12 hours

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Tidal Pool Foraging
steps: Time your visit to low tide
Look for limpets, mussels, periwinkles
Check for sea anemones and crabs
Cook all tidal pool catches thoroughly

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Tree Bark Bread
steps: Harvest inner bark (cambium layer)
Dry completely
Grind to flour
Mix with water and cook on hot stone

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Snare Set Optimization
steps: Set snares on active game trails
Position loop at head height of target animal
Funnel animal into snare
Set 10+ snares to increase odds

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Earth Oven (Hangi/Imu)
steps: Dig a pit 2-3 feet deep
Heat rocks in a fire for 2+ hours
Layer: hot rocks, green leaves, food, leaves, dirt
Cook for 4-8 hours

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Improvised Crayfish Trap
steps: Cut a plastic bottle at the shoulder
Invert the top into the bottom
Bait with fish scraps or meat
Weight and submerge in stream

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Wild Greens Identification Guide
steps: Dandelion
Clover
Chickweed
Violets

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Primitive Jerky Making
steps: Slice meat into thin strips
Salt heavily if salt is available
Dry over low smoke for 6-12 hours
Test doneness by bending

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Acorn Cold Water Leaching
steps: Crack and shell acorns
Grind to coarse meal
Place in a mesh bag in running water
Test for bitterness

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Cattail Multi-Use Harvesting
steps: Root starch
Young shoots
Pollen
Fluff for insulation/tinder

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Water Hemlock Identification
steps: Grows in wet areas
Hollow stem with purple streaks
Symptoms appear in 15-30 minutes
If ingested, induce vomiting immediately

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Universal Edibility Test (Complete)
steps: Test one plant part at a time
Skin contact test (8 hours)
Lip and tongue test
Eat small amount and wait 8 hours

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Bird Snaring Techniques
steps: Identify bird feeding areas
Set multiple small snares in a grid
Bait the center of the grid
Check frequently

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Fish Stunning with Natural Compounds
steps: Identify rotenone-containing plants
Crush and release into still water
Fish float to surface stunned
This is a survival-only technique

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Chicory Root Coffee
steps: Identify: blue daisy-like flowers, basal rosette of dandelion-like toothed leaves.
Dig roots — they're long, thick taproots.
Clean, chop into small pieces, and roast until dark brown and fragrant.
Grind roasted pieces and steep in boiling water like coffee.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Burdock Root
steps: Identify: large rhubarb-like leaves, sticky burrs, thick stem.
Dig the root — it can be 2+ feet deep.
Peel and slice thinly.
Boil, roast, or eat raw. Tastes like a mild potato.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Lamb's Quarters (Wild Spinach)
steps: Identify: diamond-shaped leaves with white powdery coating underneath.
Harvest young leaves and growing tips.
Eat raw in salads or cook like spinach.
More protein, calcium, and iron than domestic spinach.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Wood Sorrel
steps: Identify: shamrock-shaped leaves (three heart-shaped leaflets), small yellow or pink flowers.
Taste a leaf — it's distinctly sour/lemony.
Eat raw. All parts are edible.
Excellent for seasoning bland survival foods and quenching thirst.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Jerusalem Artichoke (Sunchoke)
steps: Identify: tall sunflower-like plant with small yellow flowers.
Dig tubers from the root mass — looks like knobby ginger.
Scrub clean. Can eat raw (nutty/crunchy) or cooked.
High calorie density — one of the best wild calorie sources.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Deer Track Identification & Trailing
steps: Identify tracks: two-toed heart-shaped prints, 2-3 inches long.
Read trail signs: browse lines on vegetation, rubs on trees, droppings.
Follow trails to feeding areas (forest edges) and watering spots.
Set snares or blinds along trails, NOT at water sources.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Rabbit Track Identification & Habits
steps: Identify tracks: two large hind prints ahead of two small front prints in a Y pattern.
Find runs: well-worn paths under brush, along fence lines, and between cover areas.
Locate warrens and forms (resting spots) — look for clusters of droppings.
Set snares at runs using slip knots at head height (3-4 inches off ground).

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Bird Observation for Food & Weather
steps: Follow birds to water: morning flights converge toward water sources.
Watch for ground-feeding birds (quail, grouse, turkeys) to find seed and berry sources.
Read weather from bird behavior: birds going quiet = storm approaching.
Hawks circling may indicate meadows with rodent populations — set traps there.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Underground Pit Roasting
steps: Dig a pit 2 feet deep and 3 feet wide. Line bottom with flat rocks.
Build a large fire in the pit and burn for 2+ hours until rocks are superheated.
Remove excess coals. Lay wet grass/leaves on rocks, add food, more wet vegetation on top.
Cover with earth and let cook for 3-8 hours depending on quantity.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Ash Roasting (Direct Coal Cooking)
steps: Create a bed of hot coals (NOT flames) from a mature fire.
Wrap food in large green leaves or clay.
Bury wrapped food in hot coals and cover.
Cook time: fish 15-20 min, vegetables 20-30 min, game birds 45-60 min.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Fish Smoking — Field Preservation
steps: Clean and split fish — remove guts, open flat, score thick portions.
Salt heavily if salt available. If not, proceed directly to smoking.
Build a smoking rack: green stick frame 2-3 feet above a smoky fire.
Maintain steady smoke (not flames) for 4-8 hours. Fish should be firm and golden.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Spring Snare (Trigger Snare)
steps: Find a flexible sapling near an active trail.
Carve trigger: two interlocking hooks (notch-and-peg system).
Attach snare loop to the sapling end. Set the trigger under tension.
Bait around the snare or set on a funnel point of a well-used trail.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Improvised Fish Hook
steps: Select material: thorn, bone shard, safety pin, soda can tab, wire, or wood.
Shape into a hook: curve with a sharp point and a barb if possible.
Attach to line (cordage, thread, dental floss, or unraveled paracord inner strand).
Bait with insects, worms, or small pieces of meat. Set in calm water near structure.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Gill Net Construction
steps: Determine mesh size by target fish: 2-inch mesh for panfish, 4-inch for larger.
Tie net-making knots (sheet bends) using a mesh gauge stick for consistent spacing.
Build net 3-4 feet deep and as wide as you can manage.
Set across current in rivers or perpendicular to shore in lakes. Weight the bottom, float the top.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Edible Insect Field Guide
steps: Safe to eat: grasshoppers, crickets, ants, termites, grubs, beetle larvae.
ALWAYS cook insects — heat kills parasites.
Remove legs and wings from grasshoppers/crickets before eating.
Protein content: 60-70% by dry weight — higher than beef.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Earthworm Harvesting & Preparation
steps: Find in moist soil: under rocks, rotting logs, or dig in rich forest floor.
Purge: place worms in damp grass for 24 hours to empty their digestive tracts.
Kill by dropping into boiling water. Boil 3-5 minutes.
Eat whole or add to stews. 60% protein, rich in iron.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Dandelion Complete Harvest
steps: Identify: jagged-tooth leaves in a rosette, hollow stem with white sap, yellow flower.
Young leaves: eat raw in salads. Older leaves: boil to reduce bitterness.
Roots: dig, clean, roast until dark brown, grind for coffee substitute.
Flowers: eat raw, batter and fry, or make wine.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Red & White Clover Foraging
steps: Identify: three round leaflets, white or red-purple ball-shaped flowers.
Flowers: eat raw, dried for tea, or cooked.
Leaves: best cooked or dried — raw is tough to digest in quantity.
Seeds and dried flower heads can be ground into flour.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Rose Hip Harvest & Use
steps: Identify: red/orange seed pods that remain on rose bushes after petals fall.
Harvest by picking — they pull off easily when ripe.
Cut in half, scrape out seeds and fine hairs with a spoon.
Eat raw, brew into tea, or make syrup by boiling and straining.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Wild Mushroom Safety Rules
steps: NEVER eat a mushroom you cannot positively identify with 100% certainty.
Start with foolproof species: morels, chicken of the woods, giant puffball.
Deadly species to memorize: Death Cap, Destroying Angel, Galerina.
When in doubt, DON'T EAT IT. No mushroom calorie is worth the risk of liver failure.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Basket Fish Trap (Funnel Trap)
steps: Weave a cone-shaped basket from flexible branches (willow, vine).
Create a funnel entrance: a second smaller cone that fits inside the opening.
Bait with crushed insects, bread, or meat scraps inside the trap.
Set in stream facing downstream, or in still water near shore. Weight down with rocks.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Solar Food Dehydrator (Field Build)
steps: Build a frame: 3 feet wide, 4 feet long, with a mesh bottom (screen, cloth, or woven grass).
Slice food thin: 1/4 inch strips for meat, thin slices for fruit and vegetables.
Place on mesh. Cover with a second mesh layer to keep insects off.
Dry for 1-3 days depending on humidity and food type. Food is done when leathery (fruit) or brittle (meat).

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Salt Extraction from Natural Sources
steps: Seawater: boil in a wide pan until all water evaporates — salt remains.
Salt licks: scrape salt deposits from mineral deposits. Animals will lead you to them.
Hickory bark method: burn hickory bark, dissolve ash in water, evaporate water.
Salt is critical for: food preservation, electrolyte balance, hide tanning, and seasoning.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Wild Grape Identification
steps: Identify: climbing vine with tendrils, toothed heart-shaped leaves, fruit in clusters.
Check for look-alikes: moonseed has no tendrils and single seed. Canada moonseed is toxic.
Harvest ripe fruit (dark purple/blue) in late summer through fall.
Eat raw, dry as raisins, or ferment. Young leaves edible stuffed and cooked.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Pawpaw Fruit Harvest
steps: Identify: small understory tree, large tropical-looking leaves 6-12 inches long.
Fruit ripens in September — green turning yellow-brown, feels soft like a ripe avocado.
Eat the custard-like flesh raw. Discard large brown seeds.
Cannot be stored long — eat within 2-3 days of ripening.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Hickory Nut Harvest & Cracking
steps: Identify: compound leaves with 5-7 leaflets, thick-shelled round nuts in husks.
Harvest fallen nuts in autumn. Remove green husks.
Crack with a rock on a flat stone anvil. Pick out nut meat with a thorn or pin.
Eat raw or roast. Grind into nut butter. Boil shells for hickory nut milk.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Black Walnut Processing
steps: Identify: compound leaves with 15-23 leaflets, round green-hulled nuts.
Remove green hulls: stomp with boots or drive over with vehicle.
Crack dried nuts with a hammer on concrete or flat rock.
Rich, intense flavor. Use sparingly in cooking or eat straight.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Frog Hunting & Preparation
steps: Hunt at night: frogs are active and visible with any light source (firelight, flashlight).
Catch by hand: approach slowly from behind, grab firmly around the body.
Skin by cutting around the waist, pulling skin off like pants.
Cook legs simply: roast over fire or boil 5 minutes.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Field Jerky — Meat Drying Without Tools
steps: Slice meat WITH the grain into thin strips, 1/4 inch thick maximum.
Salt heavily if salt available. If not, smoke will preserve alone.
Hang strips on a drying rack in direct sun with constant airflow.
Properly dried jerky snaps when bent and has no moist spots.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Universal Edibility Test (UET)
steps: Fast for 8 hours. Test only ONE plant part at a time (leaf, stem, root separately).
Skin contact test: crush plant part, rub on inner wrist. Wait 15 minutes for reaction.
Lip test → tongue tip test → small bite (chew, don't swallow) → wait 8 hours.
If no reaction after 8 hours: eat a small handful. Wait 8 more hours. If fine: plant part is likely safe.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Bow Fishing (Improvised)
steps: Modify an arrow: attach a line to the shaft behind the point.
Aim BELOW the fish — water refraction makes fish appear higher than they are.
Shoot at fish in shallow, clear water — ideally at pools where they congregate.
Retrieve arrow by pulling line. Re-use arrows — making them is time-consuming.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Wild Berry Safety Rules
steps: White and yellow berries: 90% are poisonous. AVOID unless positively identified.
Blue and black berries: majority are safe (blueberry, blackberry, elderberry).
Red berries: 50/50 — some safe (strawberry, raspberry), some deadly (holly, yew).
Aggregate berries (like blackberry/raspberry structure) are almost always safe to eat.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Bamboo Shoot Harvesting
steps: Identify bamboo shoots: emerging spears at the base of bamboo groves, under 12 inches tall.
Peel outer sheaths layer by layer to reveal the tender inner core.
Boil in water for 20-30 minutes to remove bitter compounds (cyanogenic glycosides).
Eat boiled, stir-fried, or added to soups. Mild, slightly sweet flavor.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Wild Rice Harvesting
steps: Identify: tall grass growing in shallow water with grain heads drooping over the surface.
Harvest from a canoe or by wading: bend stalks over and knock seeds into a container.
Dry the harvested rice in sun or over low heat.
Parch in a dry pan over fire, then thresh to remove husks.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Pine Nut Harvesting
steps: Identify pine species with large cones: pinyon, stone pine, Korean pine, Swiss pine.
Harvest cones in fall when scales begin to open.
Extract seeds from between the cone scales.
Eat raw or roasted. Shell the hard outer coating to access the rich nut meat.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Field Maple Syrup Production
steps: Identify sugar maple: opposite branching, 5-lobed leaves. Can also use any maple, birch, or walnut.
Drill or carve a hole 2 inches deep, 3 feet up the trunk. Insert a tube or carved spout.
Collect sap — 1-3 gallons per day per tap. The sap is clear and slightly sweet.
Boil sap down: 40 gallons of sap = 1 gallon of syrup.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Cactus Fruit and Pad Preparation
steps: Prickly pear pads (nopales): use tongs or a stick to remove. Burn off spines over flame.
Slice pads into strips. Boil or grill until tender (10-15 min).
Prickly pear fruit (tunas): pick with gloved hands when red/purple. Peel skin.
Fruit is sweet and juicy — eat raw. Pads provide moisture and nutrition.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Rabbit Stick (Throwing Stick)
steps: Select a dense hardwood stick: 2 feet long, slightly curved, forearm-thick.
Flatten one or both ends slightly for aerodynamics.
Throw sidearm at ground-level targets from 15-30 feet.
Practice extensively before hunting — accuracy improves dramatically with repetition.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Ant Foraging as Emergency Protein
steps: Locate ant colonies: mounds, rotting logs, under rocks.
Use a stick inserted into the colony — ants climb up and can be swept into a container.
Kill by immersing in water or freezing. Some species can be eaten raw.
Roast on a flat stone for a crunchy, slightly sour snack.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Agave Heart Roasting
steps: Find mature agave — thick leaves, central stalk emerging.
Cut leaves off to expose the heart (piña).
Pit-roast for 24-48 hours until caramelized and soft.
Rich in carbohydrates and sugars when cooked.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Mesquite Pod Flour
steps: Harvest ripe (dry, rattling) pods from mesquite trees in late summer.
Dry pods completely in sun — must be crispy.
Grind in a mortar or between stones into fine flour.
35% sugar, 11% protein — makes sweet flatbread.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Barrel Cactus Water Extraction
steps: Carefully cut the top off a barrel cactus — avoid the spines.
Mash the pulp inside to release moisture into the cavity.
Strain liquid through cloth — it's bitter but drinkable.
Use only as LAST RESORT — the liquid can cause nausea and diarrhea in some species.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Yucca Root Processing
steps: Dig yucca root — thick, brown, radiating from the base.
Peel the tough outer bark to expose white starchy interior.
Boil or roast until soft — raw yucca contains saponins that cause stomach upset.
High in carbohydrates, vitamin C, and potassium.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Prickly Pear Fruit Jelly
steps: Harvest ripe prickly pear fruit (bright red/purple).
Roll on ground to remove glochids, then peel.
Boil pulp with equal weight sugar for 30 minutes.
Strain through cloth for clear jelly, or leave pulp for fruit butter.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Sassafras Root Tea
steps: Identify: mitten-shaped, three-lobed, and simple oval leaves all on the same tree.
Dig small roots — scrub clean and slice thin.
Steep in boiling water 10 minutes for aromatic tea.
Traditional root beer flavoring.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Spicebush Berry Harvest
steps: Identify: aromatic shrub with small red berries in fall.
Scratch stem — spicy smell confirms ID.
Dry berries and grind as spice — tastes like allspice.
Young leaves and twigs make aromatic tea.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Wild Persimmon Harvest
steps: Identify: gray blocky bark, oval leaves, round orange fruit.
MUST be fully ripe (soft, wrinkled) — unripe is intensely astringent.
Eat raw when soft, or dry into leather.
Very high in sugar (34%) when ripe — excellent energy food.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Elderberry Harvest & Preparation
steps: Identify: compound leaves, flat-topped clusters of tiny purple-black berries.
COOK ALL elderberries — raw berries, leaves, and stems contain cyanogenic glycosides.
Boil into syrup, jam, or wine.
Dried elderberry tea is antiviral — proven effective against influenza in clinical studies..

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Sumac Lemonade
steps: Identify STAGHORN SUMAC: fuzzy red berry clusters, compound leaves.
NOT poison sumac (white berries, swamp habitat).
Crush berry clusters in cold water — soak 30 minutes.
Strain through cloth to remove fine hairs.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Coconut Complete Utilization
steps: Green coconut: chop top for electrolyte-rich water (1 cup per nut).
Mature coconut: crack open for dense white meat (350 cal per cup).
Grate and press meat for coconut oil and cream.
Husk fiber for cordage, shell for containers and charcoal.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Breadfruit Cooking
steps: Harvest when mature (skin turns from green to yellow-green).
Roast whole over fire or in coals until skin chars and interior is soft.
Peel and eat — texture like fresh bread, taste like potato.
Can be sliced and dried for storage.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Taro Root Preparation
steps: Harvest corms (bulb-like roots) from muddy areas near water.
MUST BOIL 45+ minutes — raw taro contains calcium oxalate crystals that cause intense throat swelling.
Peeled, boiled taro tastes like mild potato.
Leaves also edible when thoroughly boiled (change water twice).

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Banana Plant Complete Utilization
steps: Fruit: eat ripe raw, cook green as starchy vegetable (like potato).
Flower: edible raw or cooked — popular in Southeast Asian cuisine.
Leaves: waterproof wrappers for cooking, plates, temporary roofing.
Trunk: contains drinkable water — chop and squeeze.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Nipa Palm Sugar Production
steps: Cut the flower stalk and collect dripping sap in a container.
Fresh sap is sweet and drinkable — like coconut water.
Boil to reduce into syrup or solid sugar blocks.
One palm produces 1-2 liters of sap per day for months.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Crowberry Foraging
steps: Identify: low evergreen shrub with tiny needle-like leaves and black berries.
Berries persist through winter under snow.
Eat raw — mild, slightly bitter, not sweet.
Mix with rendered fat for pemmican — adds vitamins and moisture.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Labrador Tea
steps: Identify: leathery, rolled-edge leaves with fuzzy brown undersides.
Grows in bogs and boreal forests.
Steep a few leaves in boiling water 5 minutes — aromatic, slightly resinous flavor.
Contains caffeine-like compounds.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Cloudberry Harvest
steps: Identify: low plant with single white flower, one raspberry-like fruit per stem.
Ripens amber-gold in late summer on tundra and bogs.
Eat raw — sweet, creamy, slightly tart.
Extremely high in vitamin C (more than oranges).

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Fireweed as Food & Tea
steps: Identify: tall pink flower spikes on cleared or burned land.
Young shoots: eat like asparagus (steam or boil 3 min).
Mature leaves: dry for tea — this is traditional Russian Ivan Chai.
Stem pith: sweet and starchy, eat raw.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Rock Tripe Lichen
steps: Identify: leathery, dark brown/black lichen growing flat on rocks.
Peel from rocks — rubber-like when wet, brittle when dry.
MUST soak in multiple changes of water, then boil 1+ hours to remove harsh acids.
Minimal flavor, gummy texture, but prevents starvation.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Chestnut Roasting & Flour
steps: Identify: spiny husks containing 1-3 glossy brown nuts with pointed tips.
Score the flat side with an X cut through the shell.
Roast in coals for 15-20 minutes or boil for 30 minutes.
Peel while warm. Eat directly or dry and grind into sweet, gluten-free flour.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Crab Trapping (Coastal)
steps: Build a trap: weighted container with bait inside and funnel entrance.
Bait with fish heads, chicken pieces, or any meat scraps.
Set in shallow water along rocky coastline. Mark with a float or landmark.
Boil crabs in seawater for 10-12 minutes. Crack shells and eat body and leg meat.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Wild Turmeric Identification
steps: Identify: large broad leaves emerging from ground, similar to ginger plant.
Harvest rhizome by digging around the base of the plant.
Peel and grate raw, or dry and grind to powder.
Add to food as seasoning or make tea for anti-inflammatory benefits.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Trap Line Management Protocol
steps: Set 10-20 traps along a 1-2 mile circuit that returns to camp.
Check ALL traps at least twice daily: dawn and dusk.
Move unproductive traps after 48 hours to new locations.
Keep a mental or physical log of catch rates by location and trap type.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Fish Weir Construction
steps: Build a V-shaped wall of stones across a stream, point downstream.
At the point of the V: create a small catch basin or cage.
Walls should extend above the water surface to prevent fish from jumping over.
Check the basin hourly during fish migration or after rain (when fish move more).

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Land Snail Foraging
steps: Collect snails after rain — they emerge in moist conditions.
Purge for 48 hours in a container with clean vegetation or cornmeal.
Boil for 10 minutes, then pick from shells with a pin or twig.
Season and eat. 80% protein by dry weight.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Freshwater Mussel Harvest
steps: Find mussels on river bottoms in clean-flowing water: feel with feet or hands.
Harvest by pulling from substrate. Check regulations — some species are protected.
Steam over fire or bake in coals until shells open (5-10 min).
Eat the orange/cream colored meat inside. Chewy texture.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Birch Sap to Syrup
steps: Collect birch sap in spring: tap multiple trees for volume.
Boil in a wide, shallow pan over sustained fire.
Continue until the liquid thickens and darkens.
Store in sealed containers. Use as sweetener, medicine, or morale boost.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Pinole (Parched Grain Flour)
steps: Parch raw seeds/grain in a dry hot pan or on a flat rock over fire.
Grind toasted seeds into fine flour using stones.
Mix with water to drink, or eat dry as trail food.
Pinole keeps indefinitely when dry. Ultra-light, ultra-compact.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Trapping vs. Hunting: Efficiency
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
Trapping is dramatically more efficient than hunting in survival situations.

Hunting:
- Requires weapon, skill, and energy expenditure
- You pursue ONE animal at a time
- Success rate for untrained h

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Fishing in Survival Situations
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
Fish are the easiest wild protein to catch with improvised gear.

Best times: Dawn and dusk. Fish feed most actively in low light.

Best locations:
- Where streams meet (confluences)
- Behind rocks in

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Poisonous Plants: Warning Signs
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
When in doubt, do NOT eat it. These signs suggest a plant may be toxic:

- Milky or discolored sap (exception: dandelion)
- Beans, bulbs, or seeds inside pods
- Bitter or soapy taste
- Spines, thorns,

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Wild Plant Basics
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
Plants are the most accessible food source but also dangerous if misidentified.

Always-safe plants worldwide:
- Dandelion (entire plant edible)
- Cattail (shoots, roots, pollen year-round)
- Pine (in

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Survival Protein Sources: Ranked by Effort
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
Not all protein sources are equal. Rank by calories gained versus calories expended to acquire them.

Best return on effort (passive methods):
1. Insects & grubs: everywhere, easy to collect, high cal

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Primitive Cooking Methods
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
Cooking improves digestibility, kills pathogens, and makes many foods edible that are toxic raw.

Direct heat:
- Spit roasting: skewer food on stick over fire. Rotate constantly. Best for small game a

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Battery & Gum Wrapper Fire
steps: Cut a gum wrapper into an hourglass shape.
Touch the foil ends to the positive and negative terminals of a battery.
Transfer the flame to your tinder bundle immediately.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Evasion & Escape
steps: Break line of sight immediately.
Change your appearance/silhouette.
Move against the flow or into complexity.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Personal Camouflage
steps: Break up the human silhouette.
Match the background texture and color.
Stay low and still.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Stick & Shadow Method
steps: Push a straight stick vertically into the ground.
Mark the tip of the shadow with a stone.
Wait 15-20 minutes. Mark the new shadow tip.
Draw a line between the two marks. This is your east-west line.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Natural Direction Indicators
steps: Spider webs are often built on the south side of trees.
Snow melts faster on south-facing slopes.
Tree growth rings are wider on the south side (Northern Hemisphere).
DO NOT rely on moss — the 'moss grows on the north side' myth is unreliable.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Lost Procedure (STOP)
steps: STOP. Do not take another step.
THINK. When was the last time you knew where you were?
OBSERVE. Climb to high ground for visibility.
PLAN. Either backtrack to your last known position or signal for help.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Terrain Association
steps: Learn to read terrain: ridges run high, valleys funnel downward.
Use handrails: features that run parallel to your direction.
Use backstops: features that cross your path and signal 'too far.'
Aim off: intentionally aim to one side of your target.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Wind Pattern Navigation
steps: Trees lean away from prevailing wind direction.
Snow drifts and sand dunes align with wind.
Combine wind with sun/stars for reliable bearing.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Coastal Navigation
steps: Follow the coastline — it always leads to human settlement.
Choose direction based on sun: populated areas are typically south-facing.
Watch for signs: fishing nets, buoys, boat tracks, tire marks on sand.
Travel at low tide for the widest, firmest walking surface.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Solar Noon Direction
steps: Plant a straight stick vertically around midday.
Mark the shadow tip every 10-15 minutes.
The shortest shadow points exactly toward true north (Northern Hemisphere).

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Cloud Reading for Weather & Direction
steps: High, thin clouds (cirrus) moving fast indicate approaching weather change.
Cumulus clouds (puffy) over land often indicate landmasses from the sea.
Cloud direction at different altitudes tells you about wind layers.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Terrain Association Navigation
steps: Before moving, study visible terrain features and memorize their relative positions.
As you travel, continuously match terrain to your mental map.
Use elevation changes and drainage patterns to confirm location.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Position Triangulation
steps: Take a compass bearing to three identifiable landmarks.
Convert each bearing to a back-bearing (add or subtract 180°).
Draw back-bearing lines from each landmark on the map. You are where they intersect.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Wind Direction by Feel
steps: Wet your finger and hold it up. The cool side faces the wind direction.
Note the wind direction relative to the time of day. Morning winds often shift from continental patterns.
Drop grass or light debris. Watch the drift direction to confirm wind observation.
Smoke from a fire shows wind direction at different heights — ground wind may differ from canopy level.
In mountains, wind typically flows upslope during the day and downslope at night.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Insect Behavior Navigation
steps: Bees: in the morning, bees fly FROM their hive toward water and flowers. In evening, they return.
Ants: follow ant trails — they often lead to water sources. Ants build mounds on specific sides.
Mosquitoes and midges: heavy swarms near sunset indicate water within 200-400 meters.
Spiders: web orientation can indicate prevailing wind. Spiders avoid building webs facing the wind.
Fireflies: common near water sources and in moist areas. Their density increases closer to streams and ponds.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Animal Trail Navigation
