steps: Game trails that converge and widen indicate a water source ahead. Multiple trails merging = high probability.
Well-worn trails with layered hoofprints indicate a regularly used, reliable path.
Trails along ridgelines provide the easiest travel and best visibility.
Downhill game trails usually lead to water. Uphill trails often lead to bedding areas or lookout points.
CAUTION: animal trails may pass through hazardous terrain that animals navigate easily but humans cannot.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Snow Drift Orientation
steps: Snow drifts build on the leeward (downwind) side of obstacles — rocks, trees, buildings.
Sastrugi (wind-sculpted snow ridges) align with the prevailing wind direction.
In the Northern Hemisphere, south-facing slopes have less snow (more sun melting).
Tree wells (deep holes around tree bases) are asymmetric — deeper on the side sheltered from wind/sun.
Combine multiple snow indicators for reliable direction-finding in featureless white terrain.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Bird Flight Pattern Navigation
steps: At dawn, most land birds fly FROM their roost toward water and feeding areas.
At dusk, birds return to their roosts — following evening flight often leads to sheltered areas.
Seabirds fly out to sea in the morning to fish and return to land at dusk.
Circling birds often indicate food below: raptors circle over prey, vultures circle over carrion or thermals.
Geese and ducks flying in V-formations indicate seasonal direction — south in fall, north in spring (Northern Hemisphere).

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Creek Following Strategy
steps: Streams merge into creeks, creeks into rivers, rivers lead to civilization. Follow water DOWNSTREAM.
Walk along the bank, not in the water. Wet feet cause blisters, trench foot, and energy loss.
When the terrain beside the creek becomes impassable (gorge, cliff, dense brush), move uphill to bypass.
Leave obvious trail markers as you follow the creek — cairns, broken branches, strips of cloth.
Watch for signs of civilization: cut stumps, fence wire, trash, bridge remnants, trail crossings.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Night Horizon Glow Navigation
steps: At night away from trees, look for light glow on the horizon — cities and towns produce visible light domes.
Climb to the highest accessible point for the widest view of the horizon.
Moving lights (headlights) indicate roads. Note the direction and plan to intersect the road.
Cell tower lights (red, blinking) indicate roads and power infrastructure nearby.
Aircraft landing lights or repeated flight paths indicate an airport — airports are surrounded by roads and infrastructure.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Shadow Board Navigation
steps: Place a straight stick vertically in flat ground. Mark the tip of its shadow with a pebble.
Wait 15-30 minutes. Mark the new shadow tip position with another pebble.
Draw a line between the two marks. This line runs approximately east-west.
A line perpendicular to the east-west line gives you north-south.
For more accuracy, mark shadow tips every 15 minutes. The shortest shadow of the day points true north (or south).

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Southern Cross Advanced (Crux)
steps: Locate the Southern Cross (Crux). It's a small but distinctive kite shape of four bright stars in the southern sky.
Find the 'Pointer Stars' (Alpha and Beta Centauri). These are two very bright stars close together, pointing toward the top of the Cross.
Draw an imaginary line extending through the long axis of the Cross (from top star through bottom star).
Draw a second imaginary line bisecting the two Pointer stars at a right angle.
Where these two imaginary lines intersect is the South Celestial Pole (due South). Drop a line straight down to the horizon.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Moon Phase Direction
steps: Determine if the moon is waxing (growing) or waning (shrinking).
If the moon rises BEFORE midnight (waxing/first quarter), the illuminated side points West.
If the moon rises AFTER midnight (waning/last quarter), the illuminated side points East.
Draw a line connecting the horns (points) of a crescent moon down to the horizon.
A full moon rises in the East around sunset, is highest in the South around midnight, and sets in the West near sunrise.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Watch Method (Southern Hemisphere)
steps: Ensure the watch is set to accurate local time. If on Daylight Saving Time, adjust back one hour.
Hold the watch completely flat (horizontal) in front of you.
Point the 12 o'clock mark directly at the sun.
Find the midpoint between the 12 o'clock mark and the hour hand.
The line drawn from the center of the watch through this midpoint points due North.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Magnetizing a Needle
steps: Obtain a ferrous metal needle or thin wire (sewing needle, straightened paperclip, safety pin).
Method 1 (Silk/Hair): rub the needle vigorously in ONE DIRECTION ONLY against silk cloth or dry hair.
Method 2 (Battery): wrap insulated wire tightly around the needle 50+ times. Connect ends to a battery.
Float the magnetized needle on still water using a leaf, piece of bark, or paper.
The needle will slowly align itself North-South. Confirm which end is North using the sun or stars.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Shadow Tip Method
steps: Find a flat, level area of dirt that is exposed to direct sunlight.
Drive a straight 3-foot stick vertically into the ground.
Wait 15 to 20 minutes.
Mark the new position of the shadow's tip with a second pebble.
Draw a straight line connecting the first mark (West) to the second mark (East).

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Pace Count Navigation
steps: Calibrate your pace count: Walk exactly 100 meters on flat ground, counting every time your LEFT foot hits the ground.
Carry a set of pace beads (Ranger beads) or use small pebbles.
Adjust your count for terrain.
Combine pace count with a compass bearing.
Pace counting is only accurate to ±10%.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Improvised Floating Compass
steps: Find a steel sewing needle or a small piece of straight steel wire.
Magnetize the needle by stroking it repeatedly in one direction against silk, hair, or a magnet.
Float the needle on a small, still body of water.
The magnetized needle will slowly rotate to align with Earth's magnetic field.
Determine which end is North using the sun.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Backtracking Protocol
steps: STOP MOVING FORWARD.
Turn around and look for your own tracks.
If you were on a trail, retrace to the last known trail marker.
Use terrain association to remember landmarks you passed.
If you cannot backtrack, stay put and signal.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Basic Map Reading
steps: Orient the map: Align the map's north arrow with actual north using a compass.
Understand contour lines: Lines close together = steep terrain. Lines far apart = flat terrain.
Identify your position using terrain association.
Understand scale.
Plan your route using the path of least resistance.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Watershed Navigation
steps: Water flows downhill and collects into larger streams
Following water downstream generally leads to people
Stay above the watercourse
Watch for trail crossings

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Box Breathing (4-4-4-4)
steps: Inhale through your nose for 4 seconds.
Hold your breath for 4 seconds.
Exhale slowly through your mouth for 4 seconds.
Hold empty for 4 seconds. Repeat 4 cycles.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: STOP Method
steps: STOP. Literally stop moving.
THINK. What do I know? What are my resources?
OBSERVE. Look around. What's my environment telling me?
PLAN. Make a rational decision based on facts, not fear.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Managing Sleep Deprivation
steps: Take 20-minute power naps whenever safe to do so.
Navy nap schedule: 20 min ON, 40 min OFF throughout the day.
Recognize cognitive decline: poor decisions, irritability, hallucinations.
Prioritize sleep as a survival task, not a luxury.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Group Survival Leadership
steps: Establish a single leader immediately.
Assign roles based on skills: fire, water, shelter, medical, signaling.
Communicate openly about resources and situation.
Monitor group morale. One panicking person can destroy the group.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Combating Decision Fatigue
steps: Recognize the signs: everything feels equally important or impossible.
Default to the survival priority list: Shelter → Water → Fire → Food.
Use the 'good enough' principle.
Eat, drink, and rest before making any major decision.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Establishing Daily Routine
steps: Wake at same time daily. Begin with a task immediately.
Set specific goals for each day—even small ones.
Maintain hygiene even when it seems pointless.
End day reviewing accomplishments and planning tomorrow.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Managing Isolation & Loneliness
steps: Talk out loud. Narrate your actions.
Create a companion: name a piece of gear.
Maintain physical appearance and camp neatness.
Journal with charcoal on bark. Record experience.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Grief Management in Crisis
steps: Acknowledge the loss — suppressing grief causes delayed breakdown.
Set a contained grief period — 10 minutes, then return to tasks.
Channel grief into purposeful action — build, signal, improve camp.
Create a memorial when safe — a cairn, carving, or moment of silence.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Combating Boredom and Apathy
steps: Create a daily schedule with specific time blocks.
Set improvement projects — always have something to build or refine.
Exercise deliberately — even simple stretching or walking circuits.
Practice skills — knot tying, fire starting, navigation drills.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Conflict Resolution in Groups
steps: Address conflict immediately — unresolved tension escalates exponentially.
Separate parties. Let each state their position uninterrupted.
Reframe as 'us vs. the situation' not 'us vs. each other.'
Establish fair resource-sharing rules BEFORE scarcity hits.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Stages of Survival Acceptance
steps: Stage 1: Denial — 'This can't be happening.' Normal. Don't fight it.
Stage 2: Anger/Blame — 'Why me?' Channel this into action.
Stage 3: Bargaining/Planning — 'If I do X, I'll survive.' This is productive.
Stage 4: Acceptance — 'This is my reality. I will work with it.'

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Leadership Succession
steps: Identify the calmest, most respected group member as backup leader.
Share all critical knowledge: water sources, fire methods, signal plans.
Delegate gradually — give increasing responsibility while you're still able.
Document the plan — scratched on bark, written in dirt, memorized by two people.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Cognitive Reframing
steps: Identify the negative thought: 'I'm going to die out here.'
Challenge it with evidence: 'People survive this. I have skills. I have resources.'
Replace with actionable thought: 'What is the next most useful thing I can do?'

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Decision Fatigue Management
steps: Make critical decisions in the morning when mental energy is highest.
Establish routines to eliminate unnecessary decisions.
When stuck, use the 'good enough' rule — don't wait for perfect.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Processing Grief & Loss
steps: Acknowledge the loss. Don't suppress it or pretend it didn't happen.
Separate grieving from decision-making. Grieve, then decide.
Channel grief into purpose: 'I will survive to honor them.'

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Group Conflict Resolution
steps: Address conflicts immediately. They escalate rapidly under stress.
Use structured discussion: each person states their view without interruption.
Focus on shared goals: 'We all want to survive and get home.'

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Teaching as a Coping Mechanism
steps: Teach another person a survival skill you know.
Let beginners practice. Mastery builds confidence.
Share knowledge generously. Hoarding skills creates dependency.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Survival Gratitude Practice
steps: Each morning and evening, identify 3 things that went right or that you're grateful for.
In a group, share these aloud at mealtimes.
Compare today to the worst day, not to normal life.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Humor as a Survival Tool
steps: Find something absurd or ironic about your situation and acknowledge it.
Share jokes and funny stories in the group, especially during downtime.
Never use humor to dismiss real problems or someone's genuine distress.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Trauma Response Phases
steps: Phase 1 — Impact: shock, disbelief, numbness. The brain shuts down non-essential functions. This is normal.
Phase 2 — Recoil: reality sets in. Emotions flood: fear, anger, grief, guilt. Physical symptoms appear.
Phase 3 — Adjustment: begins within hours to days. Problem-solving resumes. New routines form.
Phase 4 — Reconstruction: days to weeks. Long-term coping develops. Identity integrates the experience.
Key insight: allowing emotions to FLOW rather than suppressing them speeds recovery. Cry if you need to. Then act.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Acute Stress Management
steps: Recognize stress activation: racing heart, rapid breathing, tunnel vision, shaking hands.
Tactical breathing: inhale 4 counts through nose, hold 4, exhale 4 through mouth, hold 4. Repeat 4 cycles.
Ground yourself: plant your feet, squeeze your fists, name 5 things you see. Re-enter the present moment.
Create an immediate priority: 'Right now, the ONE thing I need to do is ______.'
Alternate 45 minutes of action with 15 minutes of rest. Sustained stress without breaks causes collapse.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Solo Self-Talk Strategy
steps: Talk out loud to yourself. This is not madness — it is a validated psychological survival technique.
Narrate your actions: 'I am building a fire. I need kindling. I see dry bark on that tree.'
Give yourself direct instructions: 'Stand up. Walk to the stream. Fill the bottle.'
Counter negative thoughts immediately: replace 'I'm going to die' with 'People have survived worse. I am acting now.'
End each day by verbally listing three things you accomplished. No matter how small.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Survival Priorities Recitation
steps: The Rule of 3s: 3 minutes without air, 3 hours without shelter (in harsh conditions), 3 days without water, 3 weeks without food.
Priority 1: IMMEDIATE THREATS. Stop bleeding, escape danger, get out of the water.
Priority 2: SHELTER. Protect from wind, rain, cold, or extreme heat.
Priority 3: WATER. Find, collect, or purify water.
Priority 4: FIRE, SIGNALING, FOOD — in that order. Fire provides warmth, purification, signaling, and morale.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Field Meditation
steps: Find a comfortable position: sitting, lying down, or leaning against something stable.
Focus entirely on your breath. Count each exhale: 1, 2, 3... up to 10, then restart.
Breathe naturally — do not force deep breaths. Simply observe the rhythm.
If distressing thoughts arise, label them: 'worry,' 'fear,' 'plan' — and return to the breath.
5-10 minutes of meditation reduces cortisol, lowers heart rate, and restores decision-making capacity.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Progressive Muscle Relaxation
steps: Start with your feet: squeeze all foot muscles TIGHT for 5 seconds, then release completely.
Move upward: calves (squeeze 5 sec, release), thighs, buttocks, stomach, chest.
Continue: make fists and squeeze forearms (5 sec, release), biceps, shoulders (raise to ears then drop).
Face: scrunch your entire face tightly (5 sec), then let it go completely slack.
Final: tense ENTIRE BODY at once for 10 seconds, then release everything simultaneously.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Hope Anchoring
steps: Choose your anchor: visualize a specific person, place, or future moment worth surviving for.
Make the image vivid: colors, sounds, smells, textures. The more detailed, the more powerful.
Create a physical trigger: a word, a gesture (touching a ring, pressing your thumb), a phrase you say.
Access the anchor when despair, exhaustion, or hopelessness threatens your will.
Survival studies consistently show: the will to live is the #1 differentiator between those who survive and those who don't.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Team Morale Activities
steps: Storytelling circles: each person shares a favorite memory, a joke, or a made-up story.
Skill sharing: each person teaches the group something they know — a trick, technique, or knowledge.
Planning sessions: discuss rescue scenarios, what you'll eat first when home, future plans.
Work competitions: who can process the most firewood, find the most water, build the best shelter addition.
Daily gratitude round: each person names one positive thing about the day. No skipping.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Night Anxiety Management
steps: Night anxiety is universal and evolutionary — our ancestors WERE prey in the dark. This fear is hardwired.
Fire is the #1 night anxiety reducer. Even a small fire provides light, warmth, and a psychological barrier.
Create a defensible sleeping position: back against solid object, clear sightlines, obstacles for approach noise.
Identify night sounds: most are wood settling, small animals, wind, and temperature-related cracking.
If sleep is impossible: productive night tasks reduce anxiety. Process materials, plan tomorrow, tend fire.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Survival Group Leadership
steps: Establish leadership immediately — groups without leadership dissolve into conflict within hours.
Assign roles based on strengths: fire keeper, water finder, shelter builder, lookout, medic.
Make decisions transparently. Explain reasoning. Ask for input but don't debate endlessly.
Monitor every person for: withdrawal, excessive silence, refusal to eat/drink, irrational behavior.
Never show despair to the group. Process your emotions privately, then present a calm, steady front.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Food Psychology
steps: Do NOT eat all your food on day one. Establish rationing immediately, even if rescue seems imminent.
Create a dining ritual. Eat at specific times, prepare the food deliberately, and eat it slowly.
Divide rations into physical portions representing days. Seeing the plan visually reduces anxiety about starving.
Save the best-tasting or highest-energy item for the hardest moment or the worst weather.
Forage for 'filler' — tea from pine needles, boiled roots. Hot liquids provide the psychological sensation of a meal.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: The Survival Priority Pyramid
steps: IMMEDIATE THREATS (seconds): Stop bleeding, escape fire/flood, remove from avalanche debris.
SHELTER (hours): Exposure kills faster than dehydration or starvation.
WATER (days): Dehydration kills in 3 days.
FIRE (variable): Fire provides warmth, water purification, signaling, and morale.
FOOD (weeks): Starvation takes 3+ weeks to kill a healthy adult.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: The Will to Survive
steps: Survival studies consistently show that physical fitness, gear, and training are secondary to mental attitude.
Set micro-goals.
Find a reason to survive.
Acceptance is not the same as giving up.
People who die with the ability to survive simply stopped trying.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Wood Ash Soap (Lye)
steps: Collect white wood ash (hardwood is best).
Leach the lye: Pour water over ash in a filtered container.
Mix lye water with animal fat (tallow) or oil.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Seed Saving & Storage
steps: Select only the best, healthiest plants.
Harvest seeds and dry them completely.
Store in a cool, dark, dry place (glass jar).

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Self-Rescue Decision Making
steps: Stop. Breathe. Think before acting.
Float on your back to rest and conserve energy.
Remove heavy items: shoes, backpack, heavy jacket.
Swim at 45-degree angle to current toward nearest shore.
If exhausted, switch to survival floating: face down, lift head to breathe.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Leaving a Trail for Rescuers
steps: Build rock cairns (stacked stones) at regular intervals.
Break branches at eye level pointing your direction.
Tie bright cloth strips to vegetation.
Drag a stick or mark the ground in soft terrain.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Helicopter Landing Zone Prep
steps: Clear a flat area at least 60×60 feet (20×20 meters).
Remove all overhead obstructions — trees, wires, branches.
Mark wind direction with a flag, smoke, or streamers.
Stay upwind and crouch when helicopter approaches.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Helicopter Landing Zone
steps: Clear a flat area at least 100x100 feet.
Mark with bright material. H = need helicopter. V = need help.
Indicate wind direction with smoke or flag.
Stand upwind, crouch when approaching. Never approach from rear.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: VHF Radio Distress Call
steps: Tune to Channel 16 (156.800 MHz) — monitored by Coast Guard worldwide.
Press transmit and say: MAYDAY MAYDAY MAYDAY, this is [your name/vessel].
State: position, nature of emergency, number of people, assistance needed.
Release transmit button and listen for response. Repeat every 2 minutes.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Satellite Messenger Usage
steps: Activate SOS function — press and hold the dedicated SOS button.
Send a preset message with your GPS coordinates.
Use two-way messaging to communicate situation details.
Conserve battery — turn off tracking, reduce message frequency.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Improvised Flotation Device
steps: Pants flotation: tie off leg openings, inflate by scooping air.
Use any sealed container — water bottles, dry bags, sealed coolers.
Logs, branches, and debris make effective rafts with cordage.
Survival float: face down, lift head only to breathe.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Cold Water Self-Rescue
steps: Control your breathing: fight the cold shock gasp reflex.
Float on your back. Do NOT swim unless shore is within 50 meters.
Keep your head above water at all costs.
Grab onto any floating debris or solid object.
Minimize movement. Wait for rescue or drift to shore.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Helicopter Rescue Procedures
steps: Clear a landing zone: 100x100 feet minimum, flat, no loose debris.
Signal the helicopter: both arms raised in a Y = need help. One arm up = all OK.
Approach from the FRONT and DOWNHILL side. NEVER approach from behind (tail rotor).

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Self-Evacuation Decision Making
steps: Stay put IF: someone knows your location, you filed a trip plan, you're injured, or terrain is dangerous.
Self-evacuate IF: no one knows you're missing, you know your location, and you're uninjured.
Leave clear signs at your camp indicating direction of travel.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Improvised Litter Carry Techniques
steps: Build a pole litter: two poles + fabric/jacket threaded between them.
For one-person carry: use a backpack frame as a seat carry.
Carry team rotation: switch every 200 meters to prevent exhaustion.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Ground-to-Air Body Signals
steps: Both arms raised above head, forming a Y = 'YES' / 'I need help'.
One arm raised, one down diagonally = 'NO' / 'I do not need assistance'.
Lie flat face-up with arms and legs spread (X shape) = 'Unable to proceed / need medical help'.
Wave one arm side to side while standing = 'I see you / pick me up here'.
Point both arms in one direction = 'Go this way' / indicating direction of other survivors.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Reflective Surface Signaling
steps: Any reflective surface works: mirror, glass, CD/DVD, phone screen, aluminum foil, credit card, belt buckle.
Hold the reflective surface near your eye. Extend your other hand toward the target (aircraft, ship, person).
Angle the reflective surface to catch sunlight. Move it until the bright spot appears on your outstretched hand.
Flash repeatedly — 3 quick flashes followed by a pause. Repeat. This mimics SOS pattern.
Even without a visible target, sweep the horizon periodically. Aircraft may see your flash before you see them.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Tire Fire Smoke Signal
steps: Burning rubber (tires, shoe soles, rubber mats) produces thick, black smoke visible for miles.
Build a hot base fire first with dry wood. Once burning strongly, add rubber items.
If near a vehicle: use a spare tire, floor mats, or rubber hoses. Cut into pieces for controlled burning.
Alternate rubber (black smoke) with green vegetation (white smoke) for maximum contrast.
CAUTION: burning rubber is toxic. Stand upwind. Do NOT inhale the fumes.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Clothing Ground Signals
steps: Spread brightly colored clothing flat on the ground in an open clearing.
Arrange clothing in geometric shapes that don't occur in nature: X, V, or SOS.
Make signals as large as possible — minimum 10 feet across for aerial visibility.
Weight all items with rocks to prevent wind from blowing them away.
Keep signals in place 24/7. An aircraft may overfly once — your signal must be there when it happens.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Sound Signal Patterns
steps: International distress signal: 3 blasts of any sound, followed by a 1-minute pause, then repeat.
Whistle: produces a sharp, far-carrying sound. Blow 3 long blasts (3 seconds each).
Car horn: sound SOS in Morse code — 3 short, 3 long, 3 short. Repeat every 2 minutes.
Voice: yell 'HELP' in groups of three. Project from the diaphragm, don't scream.
Bang rocks or sticks together for a sharp, carrying sound that travels through forest.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Night Light Signals
steps: Flash SOS with any light source: 3 short, 3 long, 3 short. Repeat every minute.
Build a signal fire on high ground. Bright flame is visible 5-15 miles at night.
Phone screen: set to maximum brightness, turn ON and OFF in SOS pattern. Even a cracked phone works.
Car headlights: flash high beams in SOS pattern. Visible for miles on flat terrain.
Light discipline when NOT signaling: keep your fire low and shielded if you want to avoid attention from threats.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Improvised Litter (Stretcher)
steps: Find 2 strong poles 6-7 feet long, 2-3 inches diameter. Must support body weight without bending.
Method 1 (jacket): zip/button 2-3 jackets closed, thread poles through the sleeves.
Method 2 (blanket): lay a blanket flat, place one pole 1/3 from the edge, fold the short side over, place second pole, fold remaining fabric over both.
Method 3 (poles + crossbars): lash cross-sticks every 6 inches between the two poles. Weave small branches for a platform.
Pad the litter surface. Secure the patient with strips of cloth. 4 carriers is ideal — 2 minimum.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Ground Signal Panels
steps: V = 'Require Assistance'. Lay rocks, logs, or fabric in a V shape, 10+ feet per arm.
X = 'Require Medical Help'. Build an X, 10+ feet per arm, using contrasting materials.
I = 'Need Supplies'. A single straight line, 10+ feet long.
→ (Arrow) = 'Traveling this direction'. Point the arrow the way you're heading.
Build signals from the most contrasting materials available: dark rocks on sand, white cloth on green grass.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: SOS Signal Fire Triangle
steps: Build 3 separate fire pits in an equilateral triangle pattern, 100 feet apart.
Prepare each fire pit with complete fire lay: tinder, kindling, fuel wood, and a smoke-producing layer.
For daytime: add green branches, wet leaves, or rubber to produce thick smoke.
For nighttime: keep fires burning brightly with dry hardwood for maximum flame visibility.
Maintain signal fire readiness 24/7. You may get only one chance when an aircraft passes.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Emergency Cell Phone Strategy
steps: Enable airplane mode IMMEDIATELY to preserve battery. Only disable to make a call or send a text.
Text instead of calling — texts use less power and can send on weaker signals.
Climb to the highest elevated point before attempting a call. Even 50 feet helps signal reach.
Dial emergency number (911/112) — emergency calls can connect through ANY carrier's tower, including locked phones.
Send location via text before phone dies: 'LOST [GPS coordinates or landmark description] [your name] [condition]'.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Helicopter LZ Preparation
steps: Select a flat, level area at least 100x100 feet (30x30 meters) clear of trees and power lines.
Remove all loose debris from the LZ: tents, tarps, loose branches, hats, trash.
Mark the four corners of the LZ and the wind direction.
Indicate wind direction using a windsock, smoke (outside the LZ), or standing with your back to the wind.
When the helicopter approaches: turn away, protect your eyes, and kneel down. DO NOT approach until signaled.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Ground-to-Air Symbols
steps: Select a large, open clearing visible from the sky, contrasting strongly with the ground.
Construct symbols at least 10 feet wide and 3 feet thick.
V = Require Assistance.
X = Require Medical Assistance.
Arrow (->) = Proceeding in this direction.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Throw Bag Advanced Technique
steps: Throw UPSTREAM of the victim
Brace for load
Pendulum to shore
Coil for re-throw if first throw misses

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Water Rescue — Reach-Throw-Row-Go
steps: REACH: extend a pole, branch, shirt, or any long object to the victim.
THROW: if too far to reach, throw a rope, life ring, cooler, or floating object.
ROW: if you have a boat, approach with stern toward the victim.
GO (swim rescue): LAST RESORT. Take a floatation device. Approach from behind.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Tarp: Lean-To
steps: Tie ridgeline between two trees at about 4 feet high.
Attach one long edge of tarp to the ridgeline with clips or ties.
Pull opposite edge down to ground and stake at 45-degree angle.
Build a reflector fire 6 feet in front of the opening.
Add side walls with brush or additional tarp for wind protection.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Snow Trench
steps: Dig a trench in the snow, slightly longer than your body.
Line the bottom with boughs or insulation.
Roof the trench with skis, poles, or sticks.
Cover the roof with a tarp and snow.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Quinzhee
steps: Pile snow into a large mound, 6-7 feet high.
Let the mound settle/harden for at least 2 hours.
Insert 12-inch guide sticks all over the dome.
Excavate the interior, starting from a low entrance.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Hammock Shelter
steps: Find two sturdy trees 10-15 feet apart.
Tie your hammock or improvised sheeting between them.
Rig a tarp or rain fly above the hammock.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Emergency Bivy Sack
steps: Open the bivy sack completely before getting in.
Place insulation under you before entering: leaves, grass, pack.
Climb in feet-first. Keep head opening partially shut but not sealed.
Wear all available dry clothing inside the bivy.
Draw knees up and conserve body heat.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Raised Tree Platform
steps: Select 2-4 trees growing close together with strong lower branches.
Lash cross-poles between the trees at branch level.
Lay smaller poles across the cross-beams to create the sleeping surface.
Add a roof frame and cover with large palm or banana leaves.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Bough Bed Construction
steps: Collect armloads of evergreen boughs (pine, spruce, fir).
Lay boughs in overlapping rows like roof shingles, tips pointing toward foot end.
Build up to 6+ inches for ground insulation. Test by lying on it.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Shelter Insulation Methods
steps: Add dead leaves, grass, or moss to walls — minimum 12 inches thick.
Close all gaps and drafts. Wind destroys insulation effectiveness.
Reduce shelter interior volume to minimum comfortable size.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Shelter Waterproofing
steps: Apply debris layers at 45+ degree angle so water runs off.
Apply bark shingles or large leaves over the debris layer.
Dig a drainage trench around the shelter perimeter.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Stacked Rock Windbreak
steps: Gather flat-sided rocks and stack into a crescent or straight wall.
Fill gaps between rocks with smaller stones and mud.
Place fire between you and the wall for radiant heat reflection.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Desert Shade Structure
steps: Create a double-layer shade using any available material.
Elevate the shade 2-3 feet above ground to allow air circulation beneath.
Orient shade to block the sun during hottest hours (10am-4pm).

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Camouflaged Shelter
steps: Use materials that match the surrounding environment exactly.
Keep the shelter low-profile with irregular shape.
Conceal signs of habitation: trails, fire scars, trash.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Urban Debris Shelter
steps: Identify structurally stable areas: doorframes, concrete corners, stairwells.
Use plywood, sheet metal, doors, or mattresses as shelter walls/roof.
Insulate with available soft materials: carpet, clothing, paper, cardboard.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Desert Trench Shelter
steps: Dig a trench 1-2 feet deep, large enough to lie in.
Cover with a tarp or cloth, weighting edges with sand.
At night, lower the roof or add insulation.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Swamp Bed
steps: Gather 4 sturdy forked posts or utilize smooth trees.
Lash cross-beams to the posts.
Lay a deck of poles across the frame.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Tarp Lean-To
steps: Tie ridgeline between two trees at about 4 feet high.
Attach one long edge of tarp to the ridgeline with clips or ties.
Pull opposite edge down to ground and stake at 45-degree angle.
Build a reflector fire 6 feet in front of the opening.
Add side walls with brush or additional tarp for wind protection.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Bamboo A-Frame Shelter
steps: Select 2 large bamboo poles (3-4 inch diameter, 8-10 feet) for the A-frame ridge.
Lash the tops together with cordage at a 45° angle. Spread the bases shoulder-width apart.
Lay a ridgepole across the peak. Lash smaller bamboo cross-members horizontally every 12 inches.
Cover with palm fronds, banana leaves, or layered vegetation — start from bottom, overlap upward.
Build a raised sleeping platform inside using split bamboo — keeps you off the wet ground.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Pit House (Semi-Subterranean)
steps: Choose a site with well-drained soil on a slight rise. Avoid flood-prone areas.
Dig a rectangular pit 6 feet long, 3 feet wide, 2-3 feet deep.
Place ridgepole supports at each end. Lay cross-members across the top.
Cover the frame with branches, then a thick layer of leaves/grass, then the excavated soil.
Leave an entrance on the downhill side. Build a short tunnel or covered entry to block wind.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Spider Hole (Concealed Position)
steps: Find a concealed location: thick brush, tree base, depression, or behind fallen logs.
Dig a body-sized trench — long enough to lie flat, deep enough to be below ground level.
Line the bottom with insulating material: leaves, pine needles, grass.
Create a frame of sticks across the top. Cover with vegetation that matches the surroundings.
Leave a small opening for entry/exit that can be covered from inside.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Group Shelter Design
steps: Choose a long lean-to design rather than individual shelters — shared warmth is more efficient.
Build the lean-to wall 8-12 feet long and 4 feet high at the back wall.
Build a fire reflector wall opposite the lean-to, 6 feet away. Construct from stacked logs.
Assign sleeping positions: weakest/coldest in the center, strongest on the ends.
Maintain the fire in a long trench matching the lean-to length, not one large fire at one end.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Emergency Trash Insulation
steps: Stuff crumpled newspaper between clothing layers — it traps air and insulates.
Cardboard: use as ground insulation and wind break. Double layer under your body.
Trash bags: wear as a vapor barrier, fill with crumpled material as a sleeping bag.
Styrofoam: excellent insulator. Use packing peanuts stuffed inside clothing or as ground pad.
Bubble wrap: wrap around torso and limbs under outer clothing layer.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Elevated Tree Platform
steps: Select 2-4 trees growing close together (4-6 feet apart) with strong horizontal branches at 6-8 feet.
Lash horizontal support poles between the trees using square lashings.
Lay cross-members perpendicular to the supports, spaced 4-6 inches apart.
Cover the platform with a thick layer of leaves, moss, or a ground pad for insulation.
Build a wind/rain screen on the windward side and overhead. Add a simple ladder.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Cave Shelter Safety
steps: Listen and look for animals before entering: bear scat, strong odors, scratch marks, animal bones.
Check for rockfall risk: loose rocks on ceiling, recent debris on floor, cracks in ceiling.
Check for flooding: water stains on walls, smooth polished rock, mud deposits, debris piles at entrance.
Ventilation: light a small fire at the entrance. If smoke flows steadily outward, ventilation is adequate.
Sleep near the entrance, not deep inside. Keep fire AT the entrance, not inside the cave.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Swamp Raised Bed Platform
steps: Drive 4 thick forked stakes deep into the mud to form rectangle corners.
Lay two long, heavy cross-poles into the forks lengthwise.
Lay shorter poles across the rails at close intervals.
Cover the deck with a thick layer of palm fronds, moss, or grass.
Build a simple A-frame roof above the platform using a ridgepole and thatched palm.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Improvised Body Insulation
steps: Gather large quantities of dry leaves, crumpled newspaper, dry grass, or cattail fluff.
Stuff the insulation material between your clothing layers.
Use rubber bands, cordage, or tape at your wrists and ankles to keep the insulation trapped inside.
You will look absurd. This is irrelevant.
Replace the insulation if it gets wet.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Vehicle Shelter Protocol
steps: STAY WITH THE VEHICLE.
Run the engine for heat in 15-minute intervals.
Check the tailpipe regularly.
Use floor mats, seat covers, and trunk contents as insulation.
Signal by tying bright cloth to the antenna or mirror, and keeping headlights on when the engine runs.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Kochanski Super-Shelter
steps: Build a standard lean-to.
Add a clear plastic front.
Build a raised bed.
Place a fire reflector outside.
Trap the radiant heat.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Wickiup Construction
steps: Select 12-15 poles, each 8-10 feet long
Lean poles together in a cone shape
Weave horizontal rings of branches
Thatch with leaves, grass, bark

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Advanced Lean-To with Reflector
steps: Build standard lean-to frame
Close the sides with brush walls
Build a fire reflector wall 4 feet from the opening
Place fire between you and the reflector

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Super Shelter (Kochanski)
steps: Build a lean-to frame with a clear plastic sheet
Place a reflective space blanket inside as a second wall
Build a small fire in front of the clear plastic
This system amplifies fire heat dramatically

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Hot Rock Bed
steps: Heat smooth, dry rocks in fire for 1-2 hours
Dig a shallow trench where you'll sleep
Place hot rocks in the trench and cover with 4 inches of dirt
Lay your bedding on top

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Paracord Hammock
steps: Cut 15-20 lengths of paracord, each 10 feet
Tie all lines to two end rings or loops
Hang between two trees at shoulder height
Add a ridgeline above for tarp rain cover

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Snow Block Windbreak
steps: Cut snow blocks from packed/crusted snow
Build a curved wall on the windward side
Wall should be 4-5 feet high
Build fire inside the C-curve

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Debris Insulation Depth Guide
steps: 4 inches of compressed debris = 32°F protection
8 inches = 0°F protection
12+ inches = extreme cold protection
Always insulate BELOW you, not just above

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: A-Frame Plow Point
steps: Anchor the plow point into the wind
Use trekking poles or sticks for internal frame
Stake all edges tightly
This configuration sheds both rain and wind

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Underground Shelter
steps: Find a south-facing hillside (Northern Hemisphere)
Dig horizontally into the slope
Reinforce the roof with logs
Install drainage at the entrance

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: River Crossing Campsite
steps: Camp above the high water mark
Scout crossing before dark
Camp on the departure side
Prepare dry clothes in a waterproof bag

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Log Cabin Foundation & Walls
steps: Select a dry, elevated site with drainage. Clear a level foundation area.
Lay foundation logs (sill logs) — the largest, most rot-resistant logs.
Notch each log where it crosses the one below — saddle notch is simplest.
Stack walls, alternating direction. Chink gaps with moss, mud, and sticks.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Sod House (Earth Shelter)
steps: Cut sod blocks: 4 inches thick, 12 inches wide, 18-24 inches long.
Stack blocks like bricks, staggering joints.
Walls should be 2 blocks thick (24 inches) for insulation.
Roof with poles, brush, and a soil layer. Include a chimney opening.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Tarp Shelter Configurations (8 Setups)
steps: A-frame: ridgeline between two trees, tarp draped symmetrically.
Lean-to: one edge high, opposite edge staked to ground.
Plow point: one corner staked high as peak, opposite edges staked wide.
Burrito: wrap tarp around yourself with a fold underneath to create a waterproof bivy.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Root Cellar Construction
steps: Dig into a hillside or straight down 4+ feet.
Frame the ceiling with strong logs to prevent collapse.
Line floor with gravel for drainage. Install a ventilation pipe.
Store root vegetables, dried meats, fermented foods, and dairy.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Field Chimney & Fireplace Construction
steps: Build the firebox: flat stone back wall, angled side walls creating a funnel shape.
Construct chimney from stacked stones or wattle-and-daub (mud-covered sticks).
Line the firebox with clay or mud — 2 inch thick minimum.
Build a damper: a flat stone that can cover the chimney top when fire is out.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Wattle and Daub Walls
steps: Drive posts into ground every 18 inches as the wall frame.
Weave horizontal sticks between the posts — alternating front and back.
Mix daub: clay soil + straw/grass + water into thick paste.
Press daub into wattle from both sides, filling all gaps. Smooth surface.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Vehicle as Survival Shelter
steps: Close all windows and doors except one slightly cracked for ventilation.
Insulate floor with whatever is available: floor mats, cardboard, paper, clothing.
Cover windows with reflective material (space blanket, foil) to retain heat.
Run engine 10 minutes per hour maximum. Clear exhaust pipe of snow/debris.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Survival Hammock — Off-Ground Sleeping
steps: Materials: 6x10 foot tarp, poncho, blanket, or woven bark mat + strong cordage.
Wrap each end around a strong tree at waist height and tie with a bowline or figure-8.
Insulate underneath: a pad, stuffed clothing bag, or leaves in a second fabric.
Cover with a ridgeline tarp for rain protection.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Solar Pit Shelter (Desert)
steps: Dig a trench 3 feet deep, 3 feet wide, 7 feet long in shaded area.
Cover with a tarp or vegetation canopy leaving ventilation openings.
The trench acts as a solar still if you drape clear plastic over it with a collection pot.
Sleep in the trench at night when desert temperatures can drop below freezing.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Wind-Break Debris Wall
steps: Find a natural windbreak (rock, fallen tree, bank) and enhance it.
Stack branches, brush, and debris to fill gaps and add height.
Compact with leaves, grass, or snow for density.
Sleep with the wall between you and the wind, fire on the wind side.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Brush Pile Emergency Shelter
steps: Find a large fallen tree or create a ground-level ridgepole.
Pile branches against both sides creating an A-frame shape.
Add leaves, grass, and debris 2-3 feet thick all over.
Stuff the interior with dry leaves for a sleeping mat.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Tarp Lean-To with Fire Reflector
steps: String a ridgeline between two trees at hip height.
Drape tarp BEHIND you as a heat reflector, reaching from ridgeline to ground.
Build a fire reflector wall 3-4 feet behind the fire (opposite from tarp).
Sleep between fire and tarp — you receive heat from fire AND reflected heat from behind.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Underground Survival Bunker (Long-term)
steps: Excavate a 5x8x5 foot pit in well-drained soil.
Frame the roof with strong logs — 6+ inch diameter minimum.
Layer roof: logs, branches, waterproof material (bark, plastic), then 12+ inches of soil.
Build drainage: dig a deeper sump at one end and a drainage channel leading downhill.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Igloo Construction
steps: Cut snow blocks from hard-packed snow: 24×12×6 inches each.
Lay first ring in a circle 10 feet diameter. Bevel the tops into a spiral ramp.
Each ring gets progressively smaller as walls angle inward to form the dome.
Cut entrance tunnel below floor level. Fill all gaps with loose snow. Interior stays 40-60°F warmer than outside.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Raised Swamp Platform
steps: Drive four strong poles into mud/shallow water in a rectangle.
Lash cross-pieces between poles at 2-3 feet above water level.
Lay a deck of branches, bamboo, or split logs across the cross-pieces.
Add a roof structure for rain protection. Sleep elevated, dry, and away from ground-level insects and water.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Poncho Hooch (Military Shelter)
steps: Tie a ridgeline between two trees at chest height.
Drape poncho over ridgeline. Stake corners with sticks or weight with rocks.
Adjust angle based on conditions: steep for rain, shallow for wind.
This is THE standard military field shelter — used by every branch worldwide.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Stacked Rock Wind Wall
steps: Select flat or semi-flat rocks. Dry-stack without mortar.
Build wall 3-4 feet high, as long as needed.
Chink gaps with small stones and packed earth.
A rock wall radiates stored heat at night after absorbing daytime solar energy.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Desert Subterranean Shelter
steps: Dig a trench 18 to 24 inches deep and long enough to lie in.
Pile the excavated dirt in a mound enclosing three sides of the trench.
Lay a poncho, tarp, or parachute across the top of the dirt mounds.
Suspend a second covering 6 to 12 inches below the first (inside the trench).

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Parachute/Tarp Teepee
steps: Select three sturdy poles, 10 to 12 feet long.
Stand the tripod up and spread the legs to form a base about 8 feet in diameter.
Wrap a large tarp, parachute, or overlapping boughs around the frame.
Secure the covering.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Straddle Trench Latrine
steps: Select an isolated location.
Dig a narrow trench.
Manage the excavated dirt.
Cover waste immediately.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Camp Layout: The Triad
steps: Establish the Living/Sleeping Area.
Establish the Cooking/Food Storage Area.
Establish the Latrine/Waste Area.
Maintain the invisible walls.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Site Selection Principles
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
Your shelter is only as good as its location. A perfect debris hut in a depression will become a bathtub in rain. A tarp tent under a dead branch is a death trap.

First, look up. 'Widowmakers' are de

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Insulation: Dead Air Space
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body: Insulation doesn't create heat; it traps it. The most effective insulator is 'dead air'—air that isn't moving. Your clothing, sleeping bag, and shelter materials all work by trapping pockets of air ne

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Ground Insulation
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body: The ground is an infinite heat sink. Through conduction, it will suck the heat out of your body faster than the air will. You can have a perfect roof, but if you sleep directly on the cold earth, you

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Wind Protection Orientation
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body: Wind chill strips heat away from your shelter's surface. Your primary defense is orientation. In the Northern Hemisphere, prevailing winds often come from the West, but local terrain dictates the actu

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Rain Runoff Management
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body: Water always wins gravity battles. If your shelter roof isn't steep enough (at least 45 degrees), water will soak through instead of running off. This is the difference between a waterproof roof and a

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Snow as Insulator
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
Snow is surprisingly warm. Fresh snow is 90-95% trapped air, making it an excellent insulator with a high R-value. This is why animals burrow into drifts to survive storms.

Inside a properly built sn

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Tropical Shelter Considerations
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
In the jungle, the priorities flip. Cold isn't the enemy; insects, snakes, and ground moisture are. You want to get off the ground. Hammocks are king here.

If you must build on the ground, create a r

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Desert Shelter Considerations
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
The desert has two faces: the baking sun of the day and the freezing cold of the night. Your shelter needs to handle both.

During the day, shade is survival. Build an open-sided shade shelter to allo

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Bedding Construction
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
Bedding is the most overlooked part of shelter building. In a survival situation, you spend 50% of your time in your shelter. Comfort isn't a luxury; it's sleep hygiene, which preserves sanity.

Start

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Heat Reflector Walls
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body: A fire warms your front, but your back freezes. A shelter helps, but a reflector wall turbocharges your warming setup. By placing a wall of logs or rocks behind the fire (opposite your shelter), you b

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Long-Term Camp Shelter
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
If rescue isn't coming soon, your shelter needs to evolve from emergency to homestead.

Progression timeline:
- Day 1: Emergency shelter (debris hut, tarp, whatever stops heat loss NOW)
- Days 2-3: Im

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Shelter Site Selection Science
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
Where you build matters more than how you build. The wrong site can kill you regardless of shelter quality.

Danger zones:
- Valley bottoms: cold air pools here (cold air sinks). Temperature can be 10

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Common Shelter Mistakes
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
Most shelter failures come from predictable, avoidable errors.

1. Building too big:
- Larger shelters lose more heat. Body heat alone warms a coffin-sized space but not a room-sized one
- Build only

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Long-Term Shelter Maintenance
https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.body:
Shelters deteriorate. Without maintenance, even well-built shelters fail within days.

Daily checks:
- Roof: look for new gaps, compressed debris, water penetration
- Walls: check for animal damage, w

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Field Knife Sharpening
steps: Find a smooth, fine-grained stone (river rock works).
Hold blade at 15-20 degree angle to the stone.
Sweep the blade across the stone in smooth strokes, alternating sides.
Test sharpness: slice a leaf or shave arm hair.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Natural Fiber Cordage
steps: Gather long, strong plant fibers: inner bark, yucca, nettle, dogbane.
Bundle 2-3 fibers and twist them tightly in one direction.
Fold the twisted bundle in half and let it spin back on itself.
Add new fibers by overlapping 2-3 inches before the old run out.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Throwing Sling
steps: Cut two 24-inch lengths of cordage and a leather or woven pouch.
Attach cords to each side of the pouch.
Load a round stone and swing overhead in a circular motion.
Release the knotted cord at the right moment to launch the projectile.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Bow & Arrow Making
steps: Find dead, dry hardwood stave — yew, ash, hickory, oak.
Carve belly for flex, leave flat back intact.
Notch tips. String with cordage or sinew.
Arrows: straight dry sticks with sharpened tips and feather fletching.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Fire-Hardened Spear
steps: Select a straight hardwood sapling 5-7 feet long.
Carve the tip to a sharp point using a knife or sharp stone.
Slowly rotate the tip over hot coals (NOT in flame).
The hardened point is now denser and more durable than raw wood.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Primitive Clay Pot
steps: Collect clay from riverbanks or exposed soil cuts.
Mix clay with sand or crushed shell (temper) at a 4:1 ratio.
Build the pot using coil method — roll ropes of clay and stack.
Dry slowly for 1-2 days, then fire in a pit fire for 2-3 hours.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Improvised Water Containers
steps: Birch bark container: fold bark into a box shape, secure with pins or cordage.
Animal stomach or bladder: turn inside out, rinse, and inflate to dry.
Bamboo water bottle: cut a section with one node intact as the bottom.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Charcoal Water Filter Construction
steps: Make charcoal: burn hardwood to coals, then smother. Crush into small pieces.
Layer in container: coarse gravel (bottom), fine sand, crushed charcoal, fine sand, grass (top).
Pour water through top. Collect filtered water below. STILL BOIL before drinking.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Bone Awl
steps: Select a strong leg bone from a medium-to-large animal. Deer, cow, or elk shin bones are ideal.
Break the bone at an angle to create a naturally pointed shard.
Sharpen the point by grinding on a rough rock surface — sandstone works like natural sandpaper.
Smooth the grip area to prevent hand blisters during use.
Use for: punching holes in leather/bark, sewing, basket weaving, fishing hook, and digging detail work.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Coconut Shell Tools
steps: Crack a mature coconut in half by striking around the equator with a rock or the back of a machete.
Remove the meat by prying with a knife or sharp stick. Save the meat and water for food/drink.
Smooth the interior and edges by sanding on rough rock. Fire-hardening makes it more durable.
Half-shell uses: bowl, cup, water scoop, fire starter (coconut husk fiber is excellent tinder).
The hard shell can also be carved into buttons, fishhooks, spoons, and decorative items.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Basket Fish Trap
steps: Weave a cylindrical basket 2-3 feet long and 10-12 inches diameter using flexible sticks or reeds.
Close one end completely by bending warps inward and weaving them shut.
Create a funnel entrance at the open end: weave a cone that points inward with a small opening at the tip.
Bait the trap with food scraps, crushed insects, or fish guts placed inside near the closed end.
Set in a stream with the funnel opening facing downstream. Anchor with rocks. Check every 4-6 hours.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Toggle Harpoon
steps: Carve a bone, hardwood, or stone toggle point — 2-3 inches long with a hole drilled or carved at the base.
Attach the toggle to a retrieval line (strong cordage) through the base hole.
Seat the toggle in a socket at the tip of the spear shaft. It should fit snugly but release on impact.
The toggle rotates inside the wound perpendicular to the entry angle, preventing pull-out.
Use for large fish and marine mammals where prey would pull free from a fixed-point spear.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Bola Construction
steps: Collect 2-3 smooth, heavy stones about 2 inches diameter (tennis ball-sized).
Wrap each stone tightly in leather, cloth, or woven bark. Tie securely with cordage.
Cut 3 cords of equal length — about 3 feet each. Tie all three together at one end.
Tie one wrapped stone to the free end of each cord.
Throwing: hold the center knot, spin overhead 2-3 times, release toward target. Cords wrap around legs/wings.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Bamboo Knife
steps: Select a mature bamboo section — green bamboo is easier to split but dulls faster. Dry bamboo holds an edge longer.
Split the bamboo lengthwise. Each half can be further split into knife-width strips.
Sharpen one edge by grinding at a low angle on a rough rock surface — 15-20° angle.
For a handle, wrap the base end with cordage, cloth, or leave a wider section unsharpened.
Bamboo knives are disposable — carry several. They take 5 minutes to make and are easily resharpened.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Sling Weapon
steps: Cut two equal cords, each about 2.5 feet long. Any strong cordage works — paracord, braided plant fiber.
Create a pouch in the center from leather, woven cloth, or a flat piece of bark, 3x5 inches.
Attach each cord to opposite ends of the pouch. Tie a finger loop on one cord and leave the other plain.
Load a smooth, round stone (golf ball to tennis ball size) in the pouch.
Swing overhead 1-2 times, then release the plain cord at the point aimed at the target.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Improvised Fish Scaler
steps: Nail or bind a bottle cap (teeth up) to a stick handle — the crimped edge scrapes scales efficiently.
Hold the fish by the tail. Scrape from tail toward head with firm, short strokes.
Scale both sides and the belly. Run your hand over the fish — any rough patches need more scraping.
Alternative: simply skin the fish instead of scaling. Cut behind the head, grip skin with cloth, and pull.
After scaling: gut immediately. Cut from vent to chin, remove organs, rinse cavity with clean water.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Bone Needle Crafting
steps: Select a straight, dense section of bone — a leg or wing bone from a bird or small mammal is ideal.
Shatter the bone carefully by striking it with a rock. Select a long, thin, pointed splinter.
Grind the splinter against a coarse rock (sandstone) to shape it into a smooth cylinder.
Flatten the opposite end slightly to create a surface for the eye using a smaller, harder grinding stone.
Bore the eye using a sharp stone flake (chert or flint) or the tip of a knife. Drill gently from both sides.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Improvised Compass from Razor
steps: A steel razor blade can be magnetized by stroking it in one direction against silk or hair.
Alternatively, rub the blade against a magnet if one is available.
Suspend the blade from a thread or place it on a leaf floating in still water.
One end points to magnetic North.
The magnetization weakens over hours.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Improvised Travois
steps: Cut two strong poles, each 8–10 feet long.
Lay the poles in a narrow V-shape.
Lash 3–4 crossbars between the poles to create a cargo platform.
Cover the platform with a blanket, tarp, or woven branches.
Drag the narrow (lashed) end.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Locking Wire Snare
steps: Cut 2–3 feet of brass or steel snare wire.
Form a small loop (the 'eye') at one end by twisting the wire back on itself 5–6 times.
Thread the free end of the wire through the small eye loop to form a large noose.
Secure the free end of the wire to a stake, tree, or drag log.
Set the noose vertically over a game trail, 2 inches off the ground.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Emergency Body Rappel
steps: The Dulfersitz (body rappel) requires NO hardware — only rope.
Route the rope: between the legs, over one hip, diagonally across the chest, over the opposite shoulder, and down the back to the braking hand.
Lean back, keeping feet wide apart against the rock face.
NEVER let go with your brake (lower) hand.
This method causes significant rope burn and clothing damage.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Z-Drag Mechanical Advantage
steps: The Z-drag creates a 3:1 mechanical advantage, letting you pull 3x your force.
Anchor the standing end of the rope to a strong point (tree, vehicle).
The rope forms a Z-shape when viewed from the side.
Use prussik knots as ratchets to prevent the load from sliding back.
Pull on the free end of the Z.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Tyrolean Traverse Setup
steps: Anchor a rope tightly between two points spanning a gap (river, crevasse).
Attach yourself to the rope with a carabiner or prusik loop on a harness or improvised sling.
The rope WILL sag under your weight.
Clip a second safety line to the traverse rope.
NEVER build a Tyrolean over a drop you cannot survive.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: River Highline Crossing
steps: The strongest swimmer crosses first with a rope tied to their harness.
Once across, the swimmer anchors the rope to a strong tree.
Crossing people clip into the tensioned rope with a carabiner and shuffle across.
Non-swimmers and injured persons are clipped to the rope and pulled across by haul lines.
The last person unties the far anchor and is hauled across by the group.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Improvised Water Filter Construction
steps: Use a large container with drain at bottom
Layer from bottom: gravel, sand, charcoal, sand, grass
Pour muddy water through the top
The output is CLEAR but NOT sterile

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Waterproof Container from Birch Bark
steps: Peel birch bark in large sheets — harvest from fallen or recently dead trees.
Fold bark into a box shape: sides fold up, corners fold and pin with thin sticks.
Seal seams with pine pitch or birch tar for complete waterproofing.
These containers hold water, store food dry, and can even be used to boil water over a fire.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Hardwood Ash Lye Soap
steps: Collect pure white hardwood ashes.
Leach the potassium hydroxide (Lye).
Render animal fat (Tallow).
Saponification.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Improvised Sock Filter
steps: Layer materials inside a sock or pant leg: charcoal, sand, gravel.
Pour water through slowly and collect from the bottom.
STILL PURIFY the filtered water by boiling or chemical treatment.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Solar Still
steps: Dig a hole 3 feet wide and 2 feet deep.
Place a collection container in the center of the hole.
Cover the hole with clear plastic sheeting.
Place a small rock on the plastic directly above the container.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Desalinating Seawater
steps: NEVER drink seawater directly — it accelerates dehydration.
Build a solar still using seawater as the source.
Or boil seawater and collect condensation.
Even small amounts of fresh water are worth the effort.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Bamboo Water Collection
steps: Find green bamboo with thick stems. Shake—hear water inside.
Bend a tall stalk and cut the top. Water drains out.
Alternatively, punch a hole just above each node.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Digging a Water Well
steps: Choose a location: outside bends of dry riverbeds, valley bottoms, base of hills.
Dig a hole 1-3 feet deep. Widen as you go for stability.
Wait 30 minutes for water to seep in from surrounding soil.
Filter and purify before drinking — groundwater still needs treatment.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Clay Pot Filter (Ceramic)
steps: Mix clay with fine sawdust or rice husks (60% clay, 40% organic).
Shape into a pot or bowl and let dry completely (1-2 days).
Fire in a hot fire for 6-8 hours. The organic material burns out leaving pores.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Spring Identification
steps: Look for unusually green vegetation on hillsides in dry areas.
Check the base of cliffs, rock outcrops, and where geology changes.
Test: spring water is typically cold, clear, and flowing.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Beach Well
steps: Dig above the high-tide line in a low area between sand dunes.
Dig until water begins seeping into the hole — typically 2-4 feet deep.
Taste the water. If only slightly salty, it is drinkable in an emergency.
Line the well walls with rocks or sticks to prevent sand from collapsing into the water.
If water is too salty, dig farther from the ocean or at higher elevation behind the first dunes.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Cloth Filtration Layers
steps: Fold cloth into 4-8 layers. T-shirt cotton, bandana, or sock material all work.
Pour turbid water slowly through the cloth into a clean container.
For better results, create a layered filter: cloth > sand > charcoal > cloth > gravel.
This removes sediment, large parasites, and some bacteria — but NOT viruses or dissolved chemicals.
Replace cloth filters when they become clogged. Rinse and dry in sun to partially sterilize.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Ceramic Filter Construction
steps: Mix clay soil with fine sawdust, rice hulls, or coffee grounds in a 60:40 ratio.
Form the mixture into a pot or bowl shape, 8-10 inches diameter, 1/2 inch thick walls.
Dry the formed pot completely — at least 24-48 hours in warm air.
Fire the pot in a bonfire for 6-8 hours at sustained high temperature.
Test: fill with muddy water. Clean water should slowly drip through the bottom. Flow rate: 1-2 liters per hour.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: UV Sun Purification (SODIS)
steps: Fill a clear plastic or glass bottle with water. Pre-filter if turbid — water must be clear.
Lay the bottle on a reflective surface (metal sheet, foil, light-colored rock) in full sun.
Leave in direct sunlight for minimum 6 hours. If partly cloudy, extend to 2 full days.
Shake the bottle every hour to oxygenate — the combination of UV + oxygen is more lethal to pathogens.
Water is safe to drink after the treatment period. This method kills bacteria, viruses, and protozoa.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Water Rationing Schedule
steps: NEVER ration water below 500mL (1 pint) per day — your body needs minimum water to function.
Priority 1: sip small amounts frequently rather than gulping large amounts at once.
Priority 2: minimize water loss — rest in shade during heat, breathe through your nose, avoid sweating.
Priority 3: eat less if water is scarce — digestion requires water.
Signs of dehydration: dark urine, headache, dizziness, rapid heartbeat. If these appear, increase intake immediately.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Rock Catchment Basin
steps: Find natural depressions in rock surfaces — these collect and hold rainwater.
Improve natural basins: clear debris, deepen with rock tools, create channels to direct more runoff.
Cover the basin with flat rocks or bark when not collecting to reduce evaporation.
Build artificial basins by stacking rocks with clay mortar in a natural drainage path.
Treat collected rainwater as potentially contaminated — bird droppings and debris can pollute standing rock pools.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Field Water Testing
steps: Visual test: clear water is NOT necessarily safe, but cloudy, colored, or oily water is definitely suspect.
Smell test: if water smells of chemicals, sulfur (rotten eggs), or sewage — do not drink. Odorless is better but not proof of safety.
Flow test: flowing water is generally safer than stagnant water. Spring sources are safest natural water.
Animal indicators: animals drinking from a source is a PARTIALLY positive sign, but animals tolerate pathogens humans cannot.
When in doubt: ALWAYS purify. Boiling (1 minute rolling boil) kills all biological pathogens.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Mega Rain Catch System
steps: Stretch the largest available material (tarp, poncho, emergency blanket, trash bags) between trees.
Create a funnel point at the lowest corner. Direction all water flow to one collection point.
Weight the center slightly to create a bowl shape — prevents water from sheeting off the edges.
Channel ground runoff: dig small trenches that route ground-level rainwater to a collection basin.
First few minutes of rain: let it wash the collection surface clean before collecting for drinking.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Fog Net Collection
steps: Build a frame from sticks or poles, 4-6 feet tall, oriented perpendicular to prevailing wind.
Stretch fine mesh across the frame: mosquito netting, cheesecloth, or tightly woven fabric.
Attach a gutter or channel at the bottom of the mesh to catch dripping water.
Position on a ridge, hilltop, or coastal bluff where fog is densest.
Yield: 2-10 liters per day per square meter of mesh in good fog conditions.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Vehicle Water Sources
steps: Windshield washer fluid: mostly water, but may contain methanol — NOT safe to drink.
Radiator coolant: contains ethylene glycol — LETHAL. Do NOT drink under any circumstances.
SAFE: condensation from AC system — a running AC drips clean condensate. Collect the drip.
SAFE: water from the tire — some tires contain water as ballast. Carefully puncture non-critical tires.
SAFE: solar still using clear car window or windshield glass as a collector over a dug pit.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Leaf Funnel Water Collection
steps: Find large, waxy leaves: banana, taro, elephant ear, or any smooth, broad leaf.
Fold a large leaf into a funnel shape and secure with a twig or thorn pin.
Position funnels to catch rain: lean against trees, prop on sticks, or wedge into forked branches.
For dew collection: leave funnels out overnight. Dew condenses on the waxy surface and drains to the collection point.
Replace leaves every 2-3 days as they dry out and lose their waxy, waterproof quality.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Ice Melting for Water
steps: NEVER eat snow directly — it lowers core temperature and causes dehydration (your body uses more water to melt it).
Choose ice over snow when available — ice yields more water per volume (snow is 90% air).
Start with a small amount of water in the pot, then add snow/ice gradually.
Dark-colored containers left in sunlight passively melt snow without fire.
Sea ice: old sea ice (blue, rounded, less salty) is drinkable. New sea ice (milky, sharp) is too salty.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Bamboo Water Storage
steps: Cut a bamboo section with a natural node (solid partition) at the bottom — this creates a sealed cup.
Green (fresh) bamboo segments may already contain drinkable water — shake to check before cutting.
Seal the open top with leaves, clay, or a carved wooden plug for transport.
For larger storage: don't cut — drill a small hole in the side, fill through the hole, and plug it.
Bamboo containers can also be used for boiling water by filling and placing near (not in) the fire.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Natural Spring Indicators
steps: Green vegetation in an otherwise dry area = underground water surfacing.
Seepages on hillside slopes, especially at geological layer boundaries (rock type changes).
Insect swarms (especially mosquitoes, midges) concentrated in one area indicate moisture beneath.
Animal trails converging toward a single point — especially at dawn and dusk.
Dig at the base of slopes, outside bends of dry creek beds, and near rock outcrops where seeps are likely.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Double-Boil Purification
steps: Set up: place dirty water in a pot over fire. Cover with an angled lid (metal, flat rock, or bark).
Place a clean collection container at the low edge of the lid to catch condensate drips.
Cool the lid from above with a wet cloth or cold water to improve condensation.
Bring the dirty water to a steady simmer — not a rolling boil. Excess heat wastes fuel and reduces condensation.
This produces DISTILLED water — safe from biological AND chemical contaminants.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Pit Solar Still
steps: Dig a pit roughly 3 feet wide and 2 feet deep in an area that receives full sunlight.
Place a clean catch container (cup, bottle, bowl) in the exact center of the pit.
Fill the empty space around the cup with chopped green vegetation or safely contaminated water.
Cover the pit with a large sheet of clear plastic.
Place a small rock in the center of the plastic, directly above the cup.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Solar Disinfection (SODIS)
steps: Fill a clean, clear PET plastic bottle (like a soda bottle) with water.
Lay the bottle on its side on a dark, reflective surface in direct sunlight.
Leave it exposed for a minimum of 6 hours of strong, direct sunlight.
UV-A radiation destroys the DNA of bacteria, viruses, and protozoa.
Drink directly from the bottle.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Iodine Water Purification
steps: Add 5 drops of 2% iodine tincture per liter of clear water.
If using iodine tablets (like Potable Aqua), add 2 tablets per liter.
Wait 30 minutes before drinking.
The water will taste slightly of iodine.
Pregnant women and people with thyroid conditions should avoid iodine.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Bleach Water Purification
steps: Use only unscented, plain household liquid bleach containing 5–6% sodium hypochlorite.
Add 2 drops per liter of clear water. If cloudy, add 4 drops.
Stir or shake the container and wait 30 minutes.
If there is no chlorine smell, add another 2 drops and wait 15 more minutes.
Recent CDC guidelines: if bleach concentration is 8.25% (newer bottles), use only 2 drops per gallon.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Multi-Stage Gravity Filter
steps: Stack three containers vertically
Fill top container with grass and sand
Fill middle with crushed charcoal
Collect clear water at bottom

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Emergency Well Digging
steps: Look for green vegetation in dry areas
Dig at the outside bend of a dry riverbed
Dig 2-3 feet down and wait
Let sediment settle before collecting

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Prickly Pear Cactus Water
steps: Identify Opuntia (prickly pear) cactus
Carefully remove spines
Split pads and chew the inner flesh
Do NOT eat barrel cactus flesh

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Dew Mopping Technique
steps: Wake before dawn
Tie absorbent cloth around ankles and wrists
Wring into a container
Repeat across multiple grass areas

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Advanced SODIS Technique
steps: Use clear PET bottles only
Fill 3/4, then shake vigorously
Place on reflective surface in direct sunlight
Expose for 6 hours in full sun, 48h if cloudy

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Water Vine Identification
steps: Identify thick, woody vines
Cut high first, then low
Test: clear, odorless liquid is safe
Collect from the cut end

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Clay Pot Water Purifier
steps: Find and process clean clay
Mix clay with sawdust or rice husks
Shape into a pot and sun-dry
Fire in a kiln or large fire

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Making Activated Charcoal
steps: Make hardwood charcoal
Crush charcoal to granules
Wash charcoal before use
Pack into filter container

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Efficient Snow Melting
steps: Pack snow tightly
Start with seed water
Add snow gradually
Treat melted snow as untreated water

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Elevated Solar Still
steps: Build a tripod 4-5 feet tall
Suspend a dark container with water at center
Cover tripod with clear plastic
Collect condensation at the lowest point

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Solar Desalination Still
steps: Dig a hole in sand near the waterline. Place a collection container at the center.
Pour seawater into the hole around (not into) the collection container.
Cover with clear plastic sheet, sealed at edges with sand. Place a small rock over the container.
Sun evaporates seawater; it condenses on the plastic and drips into the container.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Improvised Sand-Charcoal-Gravel Filter
steps: Cut the bottom off a plastic bottle or use a birch bark cone.
Layer (bottom to top): gravel, crushed charcoal, fine sand, gravel again.
Pour water through the top and collect from the bottom.
This filter REDUCES but does NOT eliminate all pathogens — still boil or treat the output.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Tree Bag Water Collection
