steps: Tie a clear plastic bag around a living branch with green leaves.
Seal the bag opening tightly around the branch.
Water collects in the lowest corner of the bag over several hours.
Open carefully, collect water, re-seal. Change branches every 2-3 days.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: UV Water Disinfection (SODIS Method)
steps: Fill a clear plastic (PET) bottle with reasonably clear water.
Lay bottle on a reflective surface (metal sheet, foil) in direct full sunlight.
Leave for 6 hours in full sun, or 2 days if cloudy.
WHO-endorsed method used by millions worldwide.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Improvised Seawater Distillation
steps: Boil seawater in a covered pot with a tube/hose leading from the lid.
Run the tube through a cool water bath or cool sand to condense the steam.
Collect condensed freshwater from the end of the tube.
Add a pinch of seawater back to the distilled water for mineral content.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Rock Seep Water Collection
steps: Look for wet patches, moss, or fern growth on rock faces — these indicate seeps.
Place a container directly under the drip or flow.
Channel the flow: use a trough made from bark, a curved leaf, or even a rope wick.
Seep water is often among the cleanest in nature — filtered through rock.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Fog Water Collection
steps: Build a vertical mesh screen: netting, cloth, or woven vegetation stretched between poles.
Fog droplets condense on the mesh and drip down into a collection trough.
Finer mesh catches more water but clogs faster — medium weave is optimal.
In coastal fog zones: a 10x10 foot screen can collect 5-15 gallons per day.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Hand-Drilled Well (Sandy Soil)
steps: Create a drill: attach a pointed metal or hardwood tip to a long pole.
Rotate the drill while pushing down, periodically pulling up to clear loose material.
Water seeps in when you reach the water table — typically 5-30 feet in most areas.
Install a permanent casing and create a cover to prevent contamination.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Birch Sap Collection
steps: In spring (before leaves appear): bore or cut a small hole 1-2 inches deep in a birch trunk.
Insert a tube, carved spout, or V-shaped twig to channel sap.
Collect 1-2 liters per day per tap.
Drink fresh, or boil to concentrate into syrup (like maple syrup but lighter flavor).

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Finding Natural Springs
steps: Look where the base of a hill meets a valley floor — groundwater often surfaces here.
Lush green vegetation in an otherwise dry area indicates subsurface water.
Animal trails converging toward a point often lead to water.
Dig at the base of these convergence points if no surface water is visible.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Coconut Water — Field Rehydration
steps: Select a green (immature) coconut — more water and less meat than brown ones.
Cut the top off with a machete or sharp rock.
Drink directly or pour into container.
In emergency: coconut water has been used as IV fluid — it's that close to blood plasma.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Cactus Water Extraction Methods
steps: Barrel cactus: cut the top off and mash the pulp inside to release moisture.
Prickly pear pads: peel and chew the moist interior flesh.
Agave leaves: cut and press to extract sap.
NEVER drink the liquid from a saguaro cactus — it contains alkaloids that cause vomiting and diarrhea.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Vegetation Tube Transpiration
steps: Find a healthy, heavily foliated branch on a living tree or shrub.
Place a clear plastic bag over the end of the branch.
Tie the open end of the bag tightly around the branch.
Leave exposed to direct sunlight for 24 hours.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Seep Hole Water Filtration
steps: Locate a spot 1 to 3 meters back from the edge of a muddy water source.
Dig a hole about 1 meter deep.
Bail out the first few fillings of muddy water.
Allow the hole to refill with clear water.

https://archive.org/search?query=FM+21-7.name: Procurement: Coastal Beach Seep
steps: Locate the high-tide line.
Move inland behind the first dune.
Dig a shallow hole.
Taste the seep water.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Jungle Shelter
steps: Build elevated — a platform or hammock at least 2 feet off the ground.
Roof with large overlapping leaves — banana, palm, or similar.
Clear vegetation 3 feet around your shelter — airflow deters mosquitoes.
Dig drainage channels around your shelter footprint.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Jungle Water Sources
steps: Cut green bamboo — it contains drinkable water in the stems.
Collect water from large-leaf plants early morning.
Use vine water: cut vine at the TOP first, then cut bottom to drain.
Running streams are safest — still water is highest risk.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Jungle Hammock Shelter
steps: Sleep OFF the ground. Always.
Use poncho or tarp as a rain fly above your sleeping area.
Tuck mosquito netting under your sleeping surface.
Build a smudge fire nearby to drive away insects.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Urban Disaster Survival
steps: Earthquake: DROP, COVER, HOLD ON.
After shaking stops: check for gas leaks, structural damage.
Building collapse: protect airways, tap on pipes to signal.
Civil unrest: stay indoors, fill bathtubs with water, secure doors.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Swamp & Wetland Survival
steps: Test each step with a pole before committing weight.
If stuck in mud: lean forward and swim/crawl out.
Protect against insects: mud coating, smoke, long sleeves.
Water is abundant but contaminated—always purify.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Avalanche Survival
steps: If caught: swim aggressively to stay on surface.
As it slows: cup hands over face for air pocket.
When stopped: spit to determine which way is up.
Push one arm up. Stay calm — panic uses 3x oxygen.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Volcanic Ashfall Protection
steps: Get indoors and seal windows and doors.
Wear an N95 mask or breathe through a damp cloth if outdoors.
Clear ash from roofs — wet ash can collapse structures.
Avoid driving. Ash destroys engines and blocks visibility.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Cave Shelter & Safety
steps: Check for animal inhabitants before entering. Look for tracks, droppings, bones.
Check airflow. Stale or foul air means poor ventilation — don't go deep.
Build fire near the entrance, not inside. Use cave for wind protection only.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Wildfire Entrapment Survival
steps: Move to the largest burned area (black), a road, water body, or rocky area.
If trapped: lie face-down in a depression, cover exposed skin.
After the fire passes: watch for falling trees, hidden hot spots, and unstable ground.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Volcanic Ash Protection
steps: Cover mouth/nose with N95 mask or wet cloth.
Wear goggles. Do NOT wear contact lenses.
Seal shelter. Tape doors/windows. Damp towels under doors.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Self-Rescue from Ice
steps: Control breathing. Don't panic (Cold Shock Response).
Kick feet up to float horizontal. Don't climb vertical.
Pull with elbows/picks, kick like a seal. Roll away.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Jellyfish Sting Treatment
steps: Rinse with VINEGAR (or sea water). NOT fresh water.
Remove tentacles with tweezers/stick. Don't rub.
Hot water soak (110°F) for pain.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Trapped in Vehicle (Wildfire)
steps: Park in a clear area, away from heavy vegetation.
Close all windows and vents. Turn on AC to 'recirculate'.
Lie on the floor below window level. Cover with wool blanket.
Wait for the fire front to pass. Do not exit until safe.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Urban Gray Man (Blending In)
steps: Dress in neutral, common clothing that blends with the local population.
Match the pace and behavior of those around you.
Avoid patterns: change routes, timing, and appearance regularly.
Use reflective surfaces (windows, mirrors) to check for followers.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Zip Tie / Duct Tape Escape
steps: Zip ties: tighten them yourself first, then position the locking mechanism between your wrists.
Alternative: use friction from a shoelace to saw through zip ties.
Duct tape: rotate wrists during application to create slack.
If hands are behind your back: step through your arms to bring them in front.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Fire Saw
steps: Split a dry bamboo or softwood piece lengthwise.
Cut a notch across the flat inner surface.
Saw a thin strip rapidly back and forth across the notch.
The accumulated dust ignites into an ember. Transfer to tinder.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Flint & Steel
steps: Find flint, quartz, or any hard glassy stone.
Strike the steel (knife spine) against the sharp stone edge.
Direct sparks onto charcloth or very fine tinder.
Transfer the glowing ember to tinder bundle and blow to flame.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Fire from Ice Lens
steps: Find or make a piece of clear ice 2-3 inches thick.
Shape into a lens by carving with a knife — thicker in center, thinner at edges.
Polish the surfaces smooth using your warm hands.
Focus sunlight through the ice lens onto dark tinder.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Teepee Fire Lay
steps: Place tinder bundle on the ground (dry leaves, bark shavings, grass).
Lean small twigs (pencil-lead thin) against each other in a cone over the tinder.
Add progressively larger sticks maintaining the cone shape.
Light the tinder through the opening at the base.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Log Cabin Fire Lay
steps: Lay 2 parallel sticks on the ground, 1 foot apart.
Stack 2 more sticks perpendicular on top, forming a square.
Continue stacking 3-4 layers high. Place tinder in the center hollow.
Light the center tinder. The structure burns inward and collapses into coals.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Lean-To Fire Lay
steps: Push a thick green stick into the ground at a 30° angle, pointing into the wind.
Place tinder bundle under the lean-to stick.
Lean small kindling against the support stick, covering the tinder.
Light the tinder from the open side. Add larger sticks as it grows.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Battery & Steel Wool Fire
steps: Stretch steel wool into a thin, loose pad.
Touch both terminals of a 9V battery to the steel wool.
The steel wool will glow and burn rapidly.
Transfer to tinder bundle immediately.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Carrying Fire / Ember Transport
steps: Place hot coal in bundle of dry punk wood or fungus.
Wrap in green leaves to insulate and contain.
Blow gently to feed ember when needed.
At new camp: place in tinder bundle, blow to flame.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Feather Stick Making
steps: Split a dead stick to expose dry interior.
Shave thin curls along stick — don't cut them off.
Make many curls — more surface area = better.
Light the curls. They ignite even in wet conditions.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Making Charcloth
steps: Cut 100% cotton fabric (t-shirt, jeans) into 2-inch squares.
Place cloth in a sealed metal tin with a small hole punched in the lid.
Place tin in fire. Smoke will jet from the hole. When smoke stops, remove tin.
Result: black, fragile cloth that catches the smallest spark.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Fire Plow Method
steps: Carve a groove 6-8 inches long in a softwood base board.
Use a hardwood stick to rapidly push back and forth in the groove.
Hot wood dust accumulates at the end of the groove.
Transfer ember to tinder bundle and blow to flame.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Star Fire Configuration
steps: Arrange 4-6 large logs in a star pattern with ends meeting at center.
Light a small fire at the center where the logs meet.
Push logs inward as they burn. No chopping required.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Vaseline Cotton Ball Fire Starter
steps: Work petroleum jelly thoroughly into a cotton ball.
Pull the cotton ball apart slightly to expose dry fibers.
Ignite the dry fibers. The flame will spread to the petroleum-soaked portion.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Concealed Fire (Low Signature)
steps: Use a Dakota fire hole or deep trench to hide the flame below ground.
Burn only dry hardwood to minimize smoke.
Use a dispersal cover (branches with leaves) above the fire pit.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Upside-Down Fire (Self-Feeding)
steps: Lay largest logs on the bottom in a tight row.
Stack progressively smaller wood in layers, perpendicular to the layer below.
Light the tinder on top. Fire burns downward through layers.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Chocolate & Can Fire
steps: Apply chocolate or toothpaste to the bottom of an aluminum can.
Polish in circular motions for 20-30 minutes until mirror-reflective.
Aim the polished bottom at the sun. Focus the light point onto tinder.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Fire Management in Rain
steps: Build your fire on a raised platform of green logs if ground is saturated.
Create a rain cover: lean a bark slab or tarp at an angle over the fire, high enough to avoid catching fire.
Maintain a larger fire than usual — more mass resists rain extinguishment.
Store dry firewood NEAR the fire (not on it) to keep it dry. Under a tarp or sheltered overhang.
If fire dies to coals, protect the coal bed from rain and rebuild with feather sticks and dry tinder.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Carrying Fire
steps: Create a fire bundle: wrap a glowing coal in dry grass, bark fiber, or punk wood.
Enclose the bundle in a larger layer of green leaves or bark to insulate and protect.
Carry the bundle carefully. Blow on it gently every 15-20 minutes to maintain the ember.
A hollowed-out log section or large conch shell works as a portable fire carrier.
When you arrive: unwrap the bundle, place the ember in a tinder nest, and blow into flame.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Brake Fluid & Pool Shock
steps: WARNING: This combination produces violently toxic chlorine gas before combusting.
Pool shock relies on Calcium Hypochlorite (a massive oxidizer). Brake fluid relies on Polyethylene Glycol.
Pour 2 tablespoons of Calcium Hypochlorite onto a dry piece of cardboard or wood.
Pour 1 tablespoon of DOT-3 or DOT-4 brake fluid directly onto the powder.
Run immediately upwind.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Figure-4 Deadfall Trap
steps: Find a flat heavy rock (5-20 lbs depending on target).
Carve 3 sticks: vertical post, diagonal bait stick, horizontal trigger.
Assemble under the rock: vertical stick supports horizontal trigger, diagonal holds bait.
Place bait on the end of the diagonal stick.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Ojibwa Bird Snare
steps: Drive a pole into the ground near a bird-frequented area.
Drill a hole near the top for a perch toggle.
Attach a snare loop to the toggle, hanging down.
Attach a weight or spring to the snare line.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Fish Basket Trap
steps: Weave a funnel-shaped basket from flexible branches or vines.
Create an inner funnel that points inward.
Bait with bread, berries, or fish guts inside the trap.
Set in streams facing upstream or in tidal areas.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Cooking Over Open Fire
steps: Spit roasting: skewer meat on a green stick over coals.
Rock cooking: heat flat rocks and cook directly on them.
Clay baking: coat meat in thick clay and bake in coals.
Leaf wrapping: wrap food in large non-toxic leaves and place on coals.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Stone Boiling
steps: Fill a bark container, skin bag, or hollowed log with water.
Heat rocks in a fire until glowing hot.
Transfer hot rocks to the water with sticks or tongs.
Continue adding rocks until water reaches a rolling boil.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Catching Crayfish
steps: Look under rocks in shallow streams and rivers.
Bait trap: container with small opening and bait inside.
Boil 5-7 minutes until bright red. Eat tail meat.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Cattail Harvesting
steps: Identify: tall reeds with brown cigar-shaped seed heads.
Root: dig rhizome, peel and eat raw or roast. Starchy.
Young shoots: peel outer leaves, inner core is edible raw.
Pollen: shake from flower head. High-protein flour substitute.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Animal Skinning & Butchering
steps: Shallow cut from pelvis to ribcage — do not puncture organs.
Remove organs in one mass. Save heart, liver, kidneys.
Skin by pulling hide away, cutting connective tissue.
Separate meat along muscle groups. Cook or preserve immediately.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Shellfish & Mussel Gathering
steps: Gather at low tide. Mussels on rocks, clams in sand.
Discard any that are open and don't close when tapped.
Boil until shells open, 5-10 minutes. Discard unopened.
Avoid during red tide or warm months in tropics.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Wild Onion & Garlic ID
steps: Crush leaf or bulb. MUST smell of onion or garlic.
Check for onion-like bulb with papery skin.
Leaves should be grass-like, from the base.
Eat raw, roasted, or in soups. Entire plant edible.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Frog Catching
steps: Hunt at night near water with light. Frogs freeze in light.
Grab quickly from behind. They are fast and slippery.
Avoid brightly colored frogs — many are toxic.
Remove skin, cook legs thoroughly. Discard organs.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Pine Bark Flour
steps: Strip the outer bark from a pine tree to expose the inner bark.
Scrape off the inner bark (cambium) in thin strips.
Dry the strips and grind into flour. Mix with water for porridge.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Crayfish Trapping
steps: Look for crayfish in shallow, rocky streams and pond edges.
Build a simple funnel trap from sticks or a plastic bottle.
Bait with fish scraps, meat, or strong-smelling food. Place in shallow water.
Boil crayfish in water for 5 minutes. Eat the tail meat.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Jerky Without Salt
steps: Cut meat into thin strips (1/4 inch) along the grain.
Build a drying rack over a smoky fire.
Hang strips on the rack 6-12 inches above the smoke.
Dry until strips crack when bent but don't break.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Mushroom Safety Rules
steps: NEVER eat a mushroom you cannot 100% identify. No exceptions.
Learn and focus ONLY on a few easily-identified safe species.
When in doubt, skip mushrooms. The caloric value isn't worth the risk.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Fish Spearing
steps: Create a multi-pronged spear by splitting a sapling tip into 4 prongs.
Stand still in shallow water. Wait for fish to come to you.
Aim below the fish to compensate for water refraction.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Bone Broth for Maximum Nutrition
steps: Crack or smash bones to expose marrow before boiling.
Boil cracked bones in water for 4-12 hours, keeping water level up.
Drink broth hot. Skim fat from surface for cooking or calories.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Earth Oven (Ground Pit Cooking)
steps: Dig a pit 2-3 feet deep and wide. Build a large fire in it with stones.
Layer: hot stones, wet leaves/grass, food, wet leaves/grass, more hot stones.
Cover with earth and let cook 4-8 hours. The earth insulates the heat.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Emergency Rationing Strategy
steps: Inventory EVERYTHING edible immediately.
Do NOT eat for the first 24 hours.
Eat small amounts during activity peaks.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Arapuca Bird Trap
steps: Construct a pyramid-shaped cage out of small, straight twigs.
Carve a trigger system: a long stake, a toggle stick, and a catch stick.
Prop up one edge of the pyramid cage using the catch stick.
The pyramid acts as a heavy, weighted cage.
Check the trap frequently.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: River Fish Weir
steps: Locate a shallow, narrow section of a stream or tidal inlet.
Drive heavy wooden stakes into the riverbed in a 'V' shape.
Weave smaller branches, reeds, or rocks between the stakes.
At the point of the 'V', leave a small opening and build a circular holding pen.
Wade in with a spear or net to harvest the trapped fish.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Crayfish Catching
steps: Look in shallow, rocky streams with clear water.
Slowly lift rocks and look underneath.
Grab from behind, pinching the back of the carapace (body shell).
Cook by boiling for 5 minutes.
Eat the tail meat.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Condom Water Carrier
steps: Remove lubricant if possible (wash with water/sand).
Place the condom inside a sock or shirt sleeve BEFORE filling.
Fill carefully with water and tie off the end.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Direction from Star Movement
steps: Fix a reference point: lie down and align a star with a stick or rock edge.
If the star moved up: you're facing EAST.
If it moved down: you're facing WEST.
If it moved right: you're facing SOUTH. Left: NORTH.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Dead Reckoning
steps: Calibrate your pace count: walk 100 meters and count steps.
Set your compass bearing. Count paces as you walk.
At each pace milestone, plot your position on the map.
Correct for terrain: adjust pace for slope, vegetation, and footing.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: GPS Navigation on Phone
steps: GPS works WITHOUT cell service or WiFi.
Download offline maps BEFORE your trip.
Your position shows as a blue dot on offline maps.
Mark waypoints for camp, water, and vehicle.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Magnetic Deviation Sources
steps: Remove all metal from near the compass — knives, buckles, phones.
Move away from vehicles, power lines, and metal structures.
Iron-rich rock deposits can deflect compass locally.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Wind Direction Navigation
steps: Determine wind direction at your starting point using sun or compass.
Maintain a consistent angle to the wind as you travel.
Recalibrate wind direction against other navigation cues regularly.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Star Steering (Night Travel)
steps: Select a bright star low on the horizon in your desired travel direction.
Replace your steering star every 15-20 minutes as stars move.
Use Polaris (North Star) as a constant reference — it stays fixed.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Altitude Estimation Without Tools
steps: Vegetation zones indicate approximate altitude.
Water boiling behavior indicates altitude.
Snow line and breathing difficulty provide additional altitude clues.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Weather Prediction from Nature
steps: Falling barometric pressure = incoming bad weather.
Wind shift from west to east or south often brings storms.
Red sky at morning = weather warning. Red sky at night = fair weather.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Navigating by Orion
steps: Locate Orion. It is visible from both hemispheres. Look for three bright stars in a short, straight line (Orion's Belt).
Watch Orion rise. The star Mintaka (the rightmost star in the belt when rising) rises almost exactly due East.
Watch Orion set. Mintaka sets almost exactly due West.
Use Orion's sword to find South (in the Northern Hemisphere). The sword hangs down from the belt, pointing generally South.
The belt stars point to Sirius (the dog star) in one direction, and Aldebaran (the bull's eye) in the other.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Finding North via Cassiopeia
steps: Locate Cassiopeia: a distinct 'W' or 'M' shape made of five bright stars in the northern sky.
Identify the central star of the 'W'.
Draw an imaginary line dissecting the angle made by the central star and the two stars forming the 'points' of the W.
Follow this imaginary line across the sky until you hit a moderately bright star standing alone.
Drop a vertical line from Polaris straight down to the horizon. That point is True North.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Tree Ring Navigation
steps: Find a cleanly cut tree stump that is exposed to sunlight (not in a deep, shaded ravine).
Examine the concentric growth rings. Look for the side where the rings are widest apart.
In the Northern Hemisphere, rings are typically widest on the South side. In the Southern Hemisphere, on the North side.
Conversely, the rings will be closer together on the side receiving less sunlight (North side in the Northern Hemisphere).
CAUTION: Wind patterns, slope angle, and competition from other trees can alter this. Use only as a corroborating indicator.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Finding North via Big Dipper
steps: Locate the Big Dipper (Ursa Major) in the night sky.
Identify the 'Pointer Stars'.
Draw an imaginary line straight through the two Pointer Stars.
Measure exactly 5 times the distance between the two Pointer Stars along that imaginary line.
Drop a plumb line straight down from Polaris to the horizon.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Finding South via Southern Cross
steps: Locate the Southern Cross (Crux) in the night sky.
Identify the long axis of the cross.
Locate the 'Pointer Stars'.
Follow the long axis of the cross and the perpendicular line from the Pointers until they intersect in deep space.
Drop a line straight down from that invisible junction to the horizon.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Moon Horn / Crescent Method
steps: Observe the moon when it is in a crescent phase.
Draw an imaginary line connecting the two horns of the crescent.
Mark where the line intersects the horizon line.
Understand the limitations.
Use the moon's rise and set for broader orientation.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Barometric Pressure Reading
steps: Falling pressure = deteriorating weather, rising pressure = improving weather.
Rapid pressure drop (>0.06 inHg/hr) = storm approaching within 6-12 hours.
Without instruments: Watch cloud patterns.
Towering cumulonimbus (anvil-shaped) clouds = thunderstorm within 1-3 hours.
Wind shift from steady direction to gusty and variable = front is passing through your location.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Wind Shift and Frontal Analysis
steps: Face the wind. In the Northern Hemisphere, low pressure is to your RIGHT.
A wind shifting clockwise (veering) indicates a warm front approaching.
A wind shifting counterclockwise (backing) indicates a cold front.
Sudden wind calm followed by a direction reversal = the eye of a front is passing.
Persistent winds from a single direction without change = stable weather.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Cloud Type Identification
steps: Cirrus (high, wispy, hair-like): Fair weather NOW, but may indicate front approaching in 24 hrs.
Cumulus (puffy, flat-bottomed): Fair weather clouds.
Stratus (low, gray, flat layer): Overcast, drizzle likely.
Cumulonimbus (massive tower with flat anvil top): Thunderstorm. Seek shelter.
Lenticular (lens-shaped, usually near mountains): Very high winds at altitude.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Natural Weather Indicators
steps: Red sky at night, sailor's delight. Red sky at morning, sailor's warning.
Swallows flying low = rain approaching.
Strong, sweet smells in the air = storm approaching.
Campfire smoke descending instead of rising = low pressure overhead.
Dew on the grass at dawn = fair weather.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Frost and Dew Point Prediction
steps: Frost forms when surface temperatures drop to 32°F (0°C) with sufficient moisture.
Cold air sinks.
Frost forms first on metal surfaces, then grass, then wood, then soil.
Insulate water containers overnight.
Dew point = the temperature at which your breath becomes visible.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Strengthening the Will to Live
steps: Identify your 'anchor' — the person or thing you are surviving FOR.
Set micro-goals: survive the next hour. Then the next.
Maintain routines — they create normalcy in chaos.
Stay busy. Idle hands and idle minds are your enemy.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Managing Specific Fears
steps: Name the fear specifically. Vague fear is paralyzing.
Separate real threats from perceived threats.
Take one concrete action to address the fear.
Accept residual fear as your ally — it keeps you alert.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: OODA Loop Decision Making
steps: OBSERVE: What is actually happening? Facts only.
ORIENT: What does this mean for survival?
DECIDE: Choose ONE action. Commit completely.
ACT: Execute, then loop back to OBSERVE.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Motivation Anchoring
steps: Identify your anchor — the specific person or promise pulling you home.
Visualize the reunion in vivid, sensory detail every morning.
Make a physical token — a carved symbol, a tied knot, a scratched mark.
State your commitment out loud daily: 'I am getting home because...'

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Combat Breathing (Box Breathing)
steps: Inhale through nose for 4 seconds.
Hold breath for 4 seconds. Then exhale for 4 seconds. Hold empty for 4 seconds.
Repeat until heart rate drops and thinking clears.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Positive Visualization
steps: Close your eyes and vividly imagine being rescued and reuniting with loved ones.
Visualize successfully completing your next survival task.
Practice before sleep — this sets your subconscious working on solutions.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Acceptance & Commitment
steps: Accept the reality of your situation without judgment.
Identify what you CAN control. Focus exclusively on those things.
Commit to action: 'I will do everything in my power to survive.'

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Sensory Deprivation Management
steps: Recognize the symptoms: after 24-48 hours in dark, silent environments (like a snow cave), the brain starts hallucinating.
Create artificial routine. Tap a rhythm on your leg, count seconds, recite poems or songs aloud.
Engage in detailed visualization. Mentally walk through every room of your house, naming every object.
Perform physical isometrics. Press your hands together as hard as you can. Flex and release leg muscles.
Talk aloud. Explain your situation, your plans, and your feelings to an imaginary companion or yourself.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Managing Sensory Deprivation
steps: Sensory deprivation occurs in dark caves, massive snow whiteouts, or dense jungle nights.
Ground yourself physically.
Create your own rhythm.
Focus on entirely mundane, detailed memories.
Do not attempt to navigate.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: STOP Panic Protocol
steps: S — SIT DOWN.
T — THINK.
O — OBSERVE.
P — PLAN.
Execute the single task.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Coping with Darkness and Night Fear
steps: Darkness triggers ancient predator-response circuitry in the amygdala.
Build a fire.
If you cannot build a fire, create tactile comfort.
Identify every sound deliberately.
Remind yourself: most predators avoid humans.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Charcoal Toothpaste
steps: Crush charcoal from your fire into a fine powder.
Mix with a little water (and salt/mint if available).
Scrub teeth with finger or chew stick.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Basket Weaving
steps: Collect flexible vines, willow shoots, or roots.
Create the base (spokes) first.
Weave the sides up (over-under pattern).

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Throw Bag River Rescue
steps: Yell to victim: 'GRAB THE BAG!' Get their attention first.
Open the bag, hold the end of the rope.
Throw underhanded PAST the victim, so rope drapes across them.
Once they grab the rope, brace yourself and pull.
If first throw misses: re-stuff bag quickly or throw loose coils.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Water Rescue Throw Line
steps: Throw a rope, cord, or improvised line to the person in the water.
Attach a flotation device to the line if possible.
Brace yourself firmly and pull them in slowly.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Urban Search & Rescue Marking
steps: Draw a large X on the outside of the building entrance.
Fill quadrants: top = team ID, left = date/time, right = hazards, bottom = victims found.
A single slash means search in progress. Complete X means search finished.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: PLB / inReach Activation Protocol
steps: Ensure the device has a clear, 360-degree view of the sky. Move out of deep canyons or thick canopy if possible.
Point the antenna straight up toward the sky. Do not lay the device flat or hold it horizontally.
Once activated, DO NOT turn it off. Keep it on and in place until rescuers arrive.
Stay put. Moving away from your activation point makes you harder to find.
Prepare for a long wait. Weather, terrain, and aircraft availability dictate response time.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Emergency Cell Phone Techniques
steps: Switch to Airplane Mode to conserve battery, then enable Wi-Fi only if a network is available.
Text instead of calling.
Climb to the highest accessible point to attempt a call.
Dial 911/112 even without a SIM card or service plan.
Use the phone's flashlight for signaling at night.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Lean-To Shelter
steps: Find two trees about 6 feet apart to support your crossbar.
Lash a sturdy pole between the trees at waist height.
Lean poles against the crossbar at a 45-degree angle.
Weave smaller branches or vines horizontally through the leaning poles.
Pile debris, leaves, or boughs against the wall from bottom to top.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Tarp: C-Fly
steps: Stake one side of the tarp to the ground.
Tuck 1/3 of the tarp underneath itself.
Prop up the remaining section with poles.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Tarp: Diamond
steps: Tie one corner of the tarp to a tree at head height.
Stake the opposite corner to the ground.
Stake the side corners out wide.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Wickiup
steps: Find a tripod of trees or lash three poles together.
Add many vertical poles around the frame.
Cover with mats, bark, or boughs.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Tree Well Shelter
steps: Find a large evergreen tree with low branches.
Dig out the snow around the trunk to enlarge the space.
Line the floor with boughs for insulation.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Poncho Shelter
steps: Tie ridgeline between two trees at waist height.
Drape poncho over line. Stake corners down.
Create 4-6 inch bed of leaves underneath.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Rock Overhang Improvement
steps: Find a rock overhang with at least 4 feet of ceiling clearance.
Build a wind wall across the open side with stacked rocks or logs.
Create a thick bed of insulating material on the ground.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Poncho Shelter Configurations
steps: A-frame: drape poncho over a ridgeline and stake corners.
Lean-to: tie one edge to a horizontal pole, stake the other to the ground.
Tent: support center with a stick, stake all four corners.
Ground cloth: last resort — lie on the poncho to stay off wet ground.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Parachute Tepee
steps: Create a tripod of poles 12-15 feet tall. Tie at the top.
Lean additional poles around the tripod to form a cone frame.
Drape the parachute or large tarp over the frame.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Group Survival Shelter
steps: Build a large debris shelter wide enough for the entire group.
Sleep side by side — body heat is your primary warmth source.
Assign shifts for fire maintenance and security.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Grass & Leaf Mattress
steps: Gather large quantities of dry grass, leaves, or cattail fluff.
Create a containing frame from logs or sticks on the shelter floor.
Fill the frame 8-12 inches deep. Pile extra material on top as blanket.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Storm-Proofing a Shelter
steps: Add weight to the structure with rocks or additional logs.
Lash all structural elements with cordage or vine.
Lower the profile and strengthen the windward side.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Fallen Tree Shelter
steps: Find a large fallen tree with the trunk 2-3 feet off the ground.
Lean branches against both sides of the trunk to create walls.
Cover with debris layers for insulation and waterproofing.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Elevated Food Cache
steps: Select a tree with a strong branch 10-12 feet high.
Place food in a bag and hoist it using a rope over the branch.
Tie off the rope securely. Cache should be 100+ feet from shelter.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Thermal Mass Shelter
steps: Build or dig shelter into or against earth, rock, or hillside.
Incorporate heated rocks for nighttime warmth.
Seal shelter tightly and use the thermal mass to maintain temperature.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Fire Reflector
steps: Locate spot opposite your shelter entrance, behind the fire.
Drive stakes or stack rocks/logs to create a wall.
Use mud or earth to shield wooden reflectors.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Tarp Flying V
steps: Tie a ridgeline between two trees.
Drape tarp over line diagonally.
Guy out the two hanging corners significantly.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Rock Overhang
steps: Check overhang stability and look for animal signs.
Build sidewalls with stones or branches.
Build a fire near the opening (not deep inside).

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Poncho Tent
steps: Hood must be tied off/sealed tightly.
Stake the four corners to the ground.
Use a stick in the center to prop it up (if flat pitching).

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Natural Hollow / Depression
steps: Find a natural depression, root ball hole, or fallen log.
Roof over the top with branches and debris.
Insulate the bottom heavily.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Snow Cave Insulation
steps: Smooth all interior surfaces with a gloved hand to prevent dripping.
Carve a sleeping shelf higher than the entrance — warm air rises and collects at the top.
Create a ventilation hole in the roof using a ski pole or stick. Check it regularly.
Insulate the sleeping platform: pack dense evergreen boughs, foam pad, or clothing underneath.
A single candle raises interior temperature 5-10°F. Place in a carved niche to prevent tipping.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Thermal Trap Shelter
steps: Build the smallest shelter that fits you — smaller interior = faster warming from body heat alone.
Use a sunken entrance (dig entry below floor level) to create a cold air well.
Insulate walls to at least 12 inches thick with debris, snow, or earth.
Seal all air gaps — even small drafts dramatically reduce interior temperature.
Hang a reflective surface (emergency blanket, foil) on the wall behind you to bounce heat back.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Advanced Driftwood Shelter
steps: Survey the high-tide line for structural driftwood. Look for straight, thick timbers.
Build above the highest tide line. Check seaweed debris lines to find where waves reach.
Construct an A-frame or lean-to against a large immovable object (boulder, cliff face, large log).
Layer driftwood planks as roofing, then cover with seaweed, sand, and any plastic debris found.
Build a windbreak from stacked driftwood on the windward side. Include a fire reflector wall.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Raised Swamp Bed
steps: Locate four strong, living trees forming a rectangle roughly the size of your body.
Lash two sturdy, horizontal side-poles to the trees, at least 2 feet above the high-water or mud line.
Lay smaller cross-branches tightly across the side-poles to create the platform floor.
Cover the rough cross-branches with a thick layer of soft material: palm fronds, grass, or moss.
Construct a simple A-frame roof or lean-to tarp over the platform to deflect rain.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Improvised Rain Gear
steps: Cut a head-hole in the bottom of a large trash bag.
If using a Mylar space blanket, fold it in half and cut a slit for your head.
Cinch the waist with cordage to prevent wind from billowing the material.
Wear a hat or hood underneath.
Vent periodically to prevent sweat condensation buildup.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Flint Knapping (Stone Tools)
steps: Find flint, chert, obsidian, or quartz.
Strike the edge with a harder hammerstone at a 45-degree angle.
Use the sharp flakes as-is for cutting.
Shape a larger piece into a hand axe by removing flakes around the edges.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Making a Survival Spear
steps: Find a straight hardwood pole 5-6 feet long.
Carve the tip with a knife or sharp stone into a point.
Fire-harden the tip by slowly rotating above hot coals.
For a better spear: lash a stone or metal blade to the tip.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Field Gear Repair
steps: Pine resin glue: melt pine sap and mix with charcoal dust.
Patch holes in fabric with pine resin and a fabric patch.
Splint broken tool handles with sticks and cordage lashing.
Sew tears using a bone or thorn needle and plant fiber thread.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Bark Container / Basket
steps: Cut a rectangle of flexible bark (birch, poplar, elm).
Fold the corners inward to create a tub shape.
Secure corners with split root or cordage stitches.
Seal seams with pine pitch for waterproofing.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Bone Fishhook
steps: Select a thin, flat bone fragment from a rib or shoulder blade.
Score the hook shape with a sharp stone — J-curve with a barb.
Snap away excess bone and refine the shape by grinding on stone.
Lash line to the shank using a snell knot or wrapping.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Improvised Fishing Hooks
steps: Gorge hook: carve a 1-inch sliver of bone or hardwood pointed at both ends.
Thorn hook: select a strong thorn from hawthorn, honey locust, or similar tree.
Composite hook: lash a sharp bone point to a hardwood shank at 45 degrees.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Universal Trap Trigger Mechanisms
steps: Two-stick toggle trigger: carve two sticks with matching notches that hold under tension.
Figure-4 trigger: three sticks notched to create a free-standing support.
Spring snare trigger: a peg-in-notch system attached to a bent sapling.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Improvised Cutting Tools
steps: Wire saw: twist two strands of wire together. Loop handles at each end.
Stone edge tool: find flint, obsidian, or fine-grained stone and strike flakes.
Tin can knife: cut a can in half, fold the edge over for a handle.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Improvised Fishing & Trapping Net
steps: Make cordage from plant fibers: dogbane, nettle, milkweed, or inner bark.
Tie a mesh net using sheet bend knots or overhand knots at regular intervals.
Attach weights (stones) to the bottom edge and floats (bark pieces) to the top.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Arctic Snow Goggles
steps: Snow blindness (photokeratitis) is an agonizing sunburn of the cornea caused by UV reflection off snow.
Cut a piece of soft wood or stiff bark long enough to wrap slightly around your face, and wide enough to cover your eyes.
Carve out a section on the inside back to accommodate your nose.
Cut two very narrow horizontal slits corresponding to your pupils.
Blacken the inside of the goggles with charcoal from a fire.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Iodine Treatment
steps: Add 5 drops of 2% iodine tincture per quart of clear water.
Wait 30 minutes before drinking.
Add vitamin C powder after wait time to improve taste.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Transpiration Bag
steps: Place a clear plastic bag over a leafy tree branch.
Tie the bag opening tightly around the branch.
Collect water from the lowest corner after several hours.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Water Storage & Transport
steps: Improvise containers: bamboo sections, animal stomachs, bark vessels.
Line pits with plastic, large leaves, or clay for ground storage.
Keep stored water covered and cool.
Seal containers to prevent spilling during travel.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Water Rationing Strategy
steps: Drink when thirsty. Do NOT ration your water.
Ration your SWEAT instead.
Know your needs: minimum 1 quart/day resting in shade, 1 gallon/day with activity.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Condensation Trap
steps: Find a metal surface—car hood, sheet metal, or large flat rock.
Angle the surface so condensation runs to a collection point.
Best results at dawn when temperature difference is greatest.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Rock Seepage Collection
steps: Look for wet rock faces—dark stains, moss, fern growth.
Press absorbent cloth against wet rock, wring into container.
Create channel with clay or mud to direct trickle flow.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Solar Desalination
steps: Fill a dark container with seawater. Place a smaller empty container inside.
Cover with clear plastic wrap. Seal edges tightly.
Place a small weight on the plastic above the inner container.
Place in direct sunlight. Collect fresh water from inner container.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Birch Bark Water Container
steps: Harvest a large sheet of birch bark without damaging the tree.
Fold into a box or cone shape. Secure with cordage or wooden pegs.
Seal seams with heated pine resin.
You can even boil water in birch bark directly over fire.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Water Vine Extraction
steps: Identify thick, woody vines in tropical or subtropical forest.
Cut the vine HIGH first, then cut low.
Hold the cut section over your mouth. Water drips from the bottom cut.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Fog Collection
steps: Stretch fabric or mesh between two poles at a 90° angle to prevailing wind.
Channel the bottom edge into a collection container.
Leave overnight. Fog is heaviest from midnight to dawn.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Cactus Water Extraction
steps: Identify barrel cactus (Ferocactus) — the only safe species for water.
Cut off the top of the cactus and mash the pulp inside.
Squeeze pulp through cloth to extract moisture. Drink in small amounts.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Bio-Sand Filter
steps: Fill a large container with layers: gravel (bottom), coarse sand, fine sand (top).
Create an outlet at the bottom for filtered water to exit.
Use daily for 2 weeks. A biological layer (schmutzdecke) forms and filters bacteria.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Morning Dew Collection (Cloth Drag)
steps: Before sunrise, tie absorbent cloth around your ankles and legs.
Walk through tall grass, collecting dew on the cloth.
Wring the cloth into a container. Repeat until enough water is collected.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Activated Charcoal Making
steps: Make regular charcoal by burning hardwood in a low-oxygen fire.
Crush charcoal into small granules (pea-sized to sand-sized).
Layer crushed charcoal in filter between sand layers.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Underground Seep Location
steps: Dig at the outside bend of a dry streambed.
Dig at the base of a sand dune facing the valley.
Wait for the hole to fill. Filter and purify the seep water.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Animal Indicators for Water
steps: Follow grazing animals (deer, cattle) at dawn and dusk — they head to water.
Watch birds: grain-eaters fly toward water at dusk, away at dawn.
Listen for frogs and insects at night — they indicate water nearby.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Poncho Rain Collection
steps: Tie corners of poncho to trees or stakes at waist height.
Place a small rock in the center to create a funnel point.
In light rain, maximize catchment area. Move to open ground.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Dew Trap (Advanced)
steps: Spread a large plastic sheet, tarp, or emergency blanket on the ground in the evening.
Create a slight bowl shape by raising the edges with rocks or sticks.
Place a clean container at the lowest point in the center.
In addition: wrap cloth around your ankles and walk through dewy grass at dawn. Wring cloth into a container.
Best conditions for dew: clear nights, calm wind, temperature difference between air and surface.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97800623780.name: Gypsy Well (Seep Hole)
steps: Locate a dry riverbed or a depression at the base of a slope.
Dig a hole 2 to 3 feet deep.
Let the hole sit undisturbed for 1 to 2 hours.
Line the sides of the hole with rocks or wooden stakes.
Carefully scoop the water out of the top layer.

https://www.healthline.com/health/super-.name: Superglue Wound Closure
steps: Clean and dry the wound thoroughly. Stop bleeding first.
Push wound edges together precisely.
Apply thin layer of glue ON TOP of the crack, not inside.
Hold shut for 60 seconds until dry.

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK13.name: Trapped Digit Amputation
steps: Verify impossibility of rescue. Tourniquet base of finger.
Cut skin and tendons around the bone (circumferential).
Locate the joint (knuckle). Bend finger to open joint gap.
Complete separation. Apply pressure dressing.

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK13.name: Dislocated Shoulder Reduction
steps: Confirm dislocation: the shoulder looks squared off (not rounded), arm is held away from body, intense pain with movement.
Cunningham technique (safest): have the patient sit upright. Gently massage the muscles around the shoulder.
While massaging, instruct the patient to slowly shrug their shoulders upward and squeeze their shoulder blades together.
If the Cunningham technique fails: lay patient face-down with the injured arm hanging over a ledge. Hang a weight (8-10 lbs) from the wrist.
After reduction: immobilize in a sling. Ice if available. Do NOT use the arm for overhead activities.

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK13.name: Shoulder Reduction (Field)
steps: Confirm anterior shoulder dislocation: the arm is held away from the body, the shoulder looks squared off, and the patient cannot rotate the arm inward.
Have the patient sit upright in a chair, leaning slightly forward.
Have the patient shrug their shoulders upward and backward, pinching the shoulder blades together.
Gently massage the bicep, deltoid, and trapezius muscles to release the spasm.
Once reduced, immobilize the arm in a sling against the body.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Prusik Knot
steps: Take a smaller loop of cord (prussik loop) behind the main rope.
Pass the knot end through the loop center 3 times, wrapping the main rope.
Pull the knot tight and dress the coils.
Test grip: Pull on loop to lock, push barrel to slide.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Properly Extinguishing a Fire
steps: Let the fire burn down to coals — stop adding fuel well before departure.
Sprinkle water on the coals — do NOT dump.
Stir the ashes and wet every surface.
Hold your hand over the ashes at 6 inches. If warm, repeat.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Char Rope Making
steps: Find natural fiber rope: cotton, jute, hemp, or sisal.
Place rope in a metal container with a small vent hole. Cook in fire.
Test: char rope should catch a spark and glow for extended time.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Bowline Knot
steps: Form a small loop in the standing part of the rope.
Pass the working end UP through the loop.
Go around behind the standing part.
Pass back DOWN through the same loop.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Clove Hitch
steps: Wrap the rope around the post once.
Cross over the first wrap and wrap around the post again.
Tuck the working end under the second wrap.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Taut-Line Hitch
steps: Loop rope around anchor (tree, stake).
Make 2 wraps inside the loop around the standing part.
Make 1 wrap outside the loop above the coils.
Slide the knot to adjust tension.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Square Lashing
steps: Start with a clove hitch on the vertical pole.
Weave over and under alternately around both poles.
Make 3-4 complete wrapping turns.
Frapping: wrap tightly between the poles to tighten.
Finish with a clove hitch on the horizontal pole.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Reef Knot (Square Knot)
steps: Cross right over left and tuck under.
Cross left over right and tuck under.
Pull tight. The knot should lie flat.
Verify: both free ends exit on the same side.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Constrictor Knot
steps: Wrap rope around the object once, crossing over the standing part.
Continue wrapping, passing the working end UNDER the X crossing.
Pull both ends hard to cinch down.
Trim excess rope.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Trucker's Hitch
steps: Tie one end to a fixed point.
Form a directional loop (slip knot) in the standing part.
Pass the working end around the second anchor and back UP through the loop.
Haul down hard, then lock with two half hitches.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Figure-Eight on a Bight
steps: Double the rope to form a bight.
Make a loop with the doubled rope.
Pass the bight behind the standing parts and through the loop.
Dress the knot and pull tight.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Alpine Butterfly Loop
steps: Wrap the rope around your hand twice.
Pick up the outer wrap and move it over the inner wrap to the center.
Tuck that same wrap under all strands at the palm.
Slide off your hand and dress the knot.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Sheet Bend
steps: Form a bight in the THICKER rope.
Pass the thinner rope UP through the bight.
Wrap around BOTH legs of the bight.
Tuck the thin rope under itself (but OVER the bight).

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Double Fisherman's Knot
steps: Overlap the two rope ends by 12 inches.
With end A, make two wraps around rope B toward you.
Pass end A back through both wraps.
Repeat with end B on rope A. Slide the two barrel knots together.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Cleat Hitch
steps: Take a full turn around the base of the cleat.
Cross over the top to the opposite horn, forming a figure-8.
Repeat 2-3 figure-8 crosses.
Finish with a locking hitch on the last cross.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Munter Hitch (Italian Hitch)
steps: Clip a large locking carabiner to your anchor.
Form two opposing loops in the rope.
Fold the loops together and clip both through the carabiner.
Lock the carabiner. Control descent by holding the brake strand.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Blake's Hitch
steps: Wrap the climbing line around the host rope 4 times, moving DOWN.
Bring the working end BACK UP to the top wrap.
Tuck the end between the host rope and wraps 2 and 3.
Dress tight. Weight it — it grips. Push it — it slides.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Water Knot (Tape Knot)
steps: Tie a loose overhand knot in one end of the webbing.
Feed the other webbing end into the knot, retracing the path in reverse.
Ensure the webbing lies flat throughout — no twists.
Pull all four strands tight. Leave 3-inch tails.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Anchor Hitch (Fisherman's Bend)
steps: Pass the rope through the ring twice.
Bring the working end across and under both turns.
Make a half hitch around the standing part.
Add a second half hitch for security.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Marlinspike Hitch
steps: Form a loop in the rope at the pull point.
Reach through the loop and pull the standing part through.
Insert a stick or rod through the bight.
Pull — the hitch locks around the handle. Remove handle to release.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Monkey Fist
steps: Wrap the rope around your fingers 3 times to form vertical loops.
Wrap 3 times horizontally around the vertical loops.
Wrap 3 times through the center, inside the vertical loops but outside the horizontal ones.
Slowly tighten each wrap, working your way around the ball.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Chain Sinnet (Daisy Chain)
steps: Form a slip knot near one end.
Pull a new bight through the slip knot loop.
Repeat — pull a new bight through each previous bight.
To deploy: pull the working end and the entire chain unravels instantly.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Spanish Bowline
steps: Form two side-by-side loops in the rope.
Reach through each loop and grab the opposite side rope.
Pull both grabs through to form interlocking bights.
Adjust loop sizes as needed. Each loop can be sized independently.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Klemheist Knot
steps: Wrap the cord/sling around the main rope from bottom to top.
Pass the end of the cord back through the starting loop.
Weight it — it locks. Push up — it slides.
Advantage over prusik: works with flat webbing too.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Eye Splice
steps: Unlay the last 6 inches of three-strand rope into individual strands.
Form a loop and tuck the first strand under one strand of the standing part.
Tuck the second strand under the next standing strand, and the third under the last.
Repeat for 3-5 full tucks per strand. Roll and taper.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Short Splice
steps: Unlay 8+ inches of both rope ends.
Marry the ropes — interleave the unlaid strands.
Tuck each strand over one, under one in the opposing rope.
Complete 4-5 tucks per strand, then taper and trim.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Reverse Wrap Cordage
steps: Harvest long plant fibers: inner bark, cattail leaves, nettle, yucca, or dogbane.
Bundle a pinch of fibers and fold at the center.
Twist the TOP strand away from you, then wrap it TOWARD you over the bottom strand.
Add new fibers by laying them alongside before a strand runs out.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Bark Rope
steps: Harvest inner bark from basswood, tulip poplar, mulberry, or cedar.
Soak the bark strips in water for 15+ minutes.
Separate into thin strips by pulling apart along the grain.
Reverse wrap for stronger cordage, or braid 3 strips for quick rope.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Butterfly Coil
steps: Drape the rope around the back of your neck.
Alternately wrap each side over your hands, extending arms wide.
Remove from neck, pinch the center, and wrap the remaining tail around the middle.
Tuck a bight through the top loop and pull snug.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Honda Knot
steps: Tie an overhand knot near the rope end, leaving a 6-inch tail.
Tie a second overhand knot above the first, passing the standing end through.
Thread the standing end through the honda eye.
Swing and throw. The loop tightens on what it catches.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Handcuff Knot
steps: Form two loops, one in each hand, with the rope crossing in the center.
Overlap the loops, pulling each through the other.
Pull to tighten — two adjustable loops remain.
Practical uses: hobble livestock, create twin handles on a log, or improvised restraints.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Siberian Hitch
steps: Wrap the rope around the post once.
Form a bight in the working end and tuck it under the standing part.
Pull the bight through to lock.
To release: pull the working end and everything falls free.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Bachmann Knot
steps: Clip a carabiner to a cord loop.
Place the carabiner against the main rope.
Wrap the cord through the carabiner and around the rope 4-5 times.
Weight the cord — it grips. Lift the carabiner to release.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Pile Hitch
steps: Form a bight (U-shape) in the rope near the end.
Place the bight over the top of the post.
Open the bight wide and fold it down below the rope's exit point.
Pull the standing part to cinch. It grips the post.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Adjustable Grip Hitch
steps: Wrap the rope around the anchor point and back toward the load.
Make two wraps around the standing part, moving toward the anchor.
Cross over and make one wrap on the load side.
Tuck the end and dress. Slide to adjust tension.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Girth Hitch (Lark's Foot)
steps: Pass the sling/loop around or through the object.
Thread one end of the loop through the other end.
Pull tight — the sling chokes around the object.
To release: push the choking loop back through.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Icicle Hitch
steps: Make 5-7 spiral wraps around the smooth object, moving away from the load direction.
Bring the working end back and tuck it under the first wrap.
Pull the standing end — all wraps cinch down progressively.
It holds on ice-covered posts, wet pipes, and tapered poles.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Highwayman's Hitch
steps: Pass a bight of the STANDING end around the post.
Form a bight in the WORKING end and pass it through the first bight.
Form one more bight (from the standing side) and pass it through the second bight.
Pull the free end — the entire hitch cascades free instantly.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Whipping (Common Whipping)
steps: Lay a loop of thin twine along the rope end.
Wrap tightly over the loop and rope, moving toward the end.
Thread the working end through the exposed loop.
Pull the other tail to drag the loop (and working end) under the wraps.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Double Bowline
steps: Form a long bight and tie a standard bowline with the doubled rope.
Dress the two loops to desired sizes.
The two loops distribute load across a wider area.
Used as improvised harness when no climbing gear available.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Bowline on a Bight
steps: Form a bight in the middle of the rope.
Tie a bowline using the bight as the working end.
Pull the bight loop over the entire knot.
Two fixed loops for anchor points or rescue chair.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Two Half Hitches
steps: Pass the rope through or around the anchor.
Make a half hitch around the standing part.
Make a second half hitch in the same direction.
Pull snug. Add a turn around the anchor for heavy loads.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Overhand Knot (Stopper)
steps: Form a loop near the rope end.
Pass the working end through the loop.
Pull tight.
Use as a stopper to prevent rope slipping through holes or pulleys.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Figure-Eight Stopper Knot
steps: Form a loop near the rope end.
Twist the loop one extra half-turn.
Pass the working end through the loop from behind.
The figure-8 shape makes it bulkier and easier to untie than an overhand.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Double Overhand Knot (Barrel Knot)
steps: Form a loop like a standard overhand.
Pass the working end through the loop TWICE.
Pull slowly, rotating the knot as it tightens.
Larger and more secure than a single overhand.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Slip Knot
steps: Form a bight near the working end.
Pass the bight through a loop in the standing part.
Pull the standing part to tighten around the bight.
Pull the working end to release instantly.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Cat's Paw
steps: Form two loops in the rope, one in each hand.
Twist each loop 2-3 times inward.
Place both twisted loops over the hook.
Load pulls the twists tight against the hook.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Lark's Head Tether
steps: Form a bight in the rope.
Push bight through the ring from behind.
Pass both rope ends through the bight.
Pull tight — the rope is now attached to the ring.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Water Bowline
steps: Form a bowline but start with a CLOVE HITCH instead of a simple loop.
Thread the working end up through both loops of the clove hitch.
Around the standing part and back down.
The extra friction from the clove hitch prevents the bowline from loosening when wet.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Scaffold Knot (Poacher's Knot)
steps: Form a loop in the rope.
Wrap the working end around BOTH sides of the loop 3+ times.
Pass the working end through the remaining loop opening.
Pull the standing end — the noose tightens. Pull the working end to adjust.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Fisherman's Knot
steps: Overlap the two rope ends.
Tie an overhand knot with end A around rope B.
Tie an overhand knot with end B around rope A.
Pull standing parts to slide the two knots together.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Round Turn and Two Half Hitches
steps: Take a FULL turn around the post — rope wraps completely around.
Tie two half hitches around the standing part.
Snug everything tight.
More secure than two half hitches alone. The round turn absorbs shock loads.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Masthead Knot
steps: Form three overlapping loops on a flat surface.
Weave each loop through its neighbors following the over-under pattern.
Place all three loops over a post or mast head.
Attach guy lines to each loop for three-way support.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Net Making (Sheet Bend Method)
steps: Create a starting edge: tie loops at even intervals along a cord.
Tie sheet bends connecting each loop to the next row.
Use a mesh gauge (stick cut to desired mesh size) for consistency.
Continue row by row until the net reaches desired size.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Toggle and Becket
steps: Tie a fixed loop (bowline or figure-8) in one rope end.
On the other rope, tie a toggle: a stick secured across the rope.
Push the toggle through the becket loop sideways.
Load pulls the toggle perpendicular to the loop — it cannot pull through.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Paracord Survival Bracelet
steps: Measure paracord: wrist circumference × 12 = cord length needed.
Fold cord in half, attach to one side of a buckle with a lark's head.
Measure bracelet length against wrist. Attach the other buckle end.
Cobra weave: alternating left-over-right and right-over-left square knots along the core.

https://www.animatedknots.com/.name: Field Rope Care & Inspection
steps: Inspect for cuts, abrasion, and glazed/melted spots.
Check for UV damage: faded color and stiffness in nylon/polyester.
Coil properly: never store rope in random piles.
Retire rope when: sheath is damaged, core is visible, it's been shock loaded, or it's more than 10 years old.

https://www.weather.gov/safety/lightning.name: Lightning Safety in Mountains
steps: Get below treeline and away from peaks, ridges, and isolated trees.
Assume the lightning crouch: squat on balls of feet, arms around knees.
Stay away from water, metal, and wet rock.
Groups should spread 50+ feet apart.

https://www.weather.gov/safety/lightning.name: Lightning Safety
steps: Get off ridgelines, peaks, high ground immediately.
Avoid tall trees, metal fences, water.
Lightning crouch: balls of feet, heels together, squat, ears covered.
Strike victim is safe to touch. Begin CPR if needed.

https://www.weather.gov/safety/lightning.name: Lightning Crouch Position
steps: Get off high ground, ridges, and exposed areas.
Crouch low with feet together, head tucked, hands over ears.
If in a group, spread out at least 50 feet apart.

https://www.weather.gov/safety/lightning.name: Lightning Safety Protocol
steps: 30-30 Rule: if thunder follows lightning in less than 30 seconds, seek shelter.
Avoid: tall trees, hilltops, open fields, water, metal objects.
Lightning crouch: squat low, feet together, hands on knees, head down.

https://www.weather.gov/safety/lightning.name: Lightning Distance
steps: See the Flash -> Start Counting Seconds.
Stop counting when you hear the Bang (Thunder).
Divide seconds by 5 = Distance in Miles.
If under 30 seconds: Seek shelter immediately.

https://www.weather.gov/safety/lightning.name: Lightning Strike First Aid
steps: Lightning victims do NOT carry an electrical charge — it is SAFE to touch them immediately.
Check responsiveness and breathing. Begin CPR immediately if no pulse or breathing.
Reverse triage: in a mass casualty situation, treat the 'dead-looking' patients FIRST.
Check for burns at entry and exit points — common locations: head, shoulders, feet.
Monitor for delayed complications: confusion, memory gaps, hearing loss, vision changes, muscle pain.

https://www.ready.gov/earthquakes.name: Earthquake Survival
steps: DROP to hands and knees. COVER under furniture. HOLD ON.
Outdoors: move to open area away from buildings and lines.
After shaking: expect aftershocks. Check for injuries.
Check for gas leaks, structural damage, fire hazards.

https://www.ready.gov/earthquakes.name: Earthquake: Indoor Response
steps: DROP to your hands and knees immediately.
Take COVER under a sturdy desk or table. Protect your head and neck.
HOLD ON to your shelter until the shaking stops.
After shaking stops, check for injuries and exit the building carefully.

https://www.ready.gov/earthquakes.name: Earthquake: Outdoor Response
steps: Move to an open area away from buildings, trees, and power lines.
Drop to the ground and protect your head.
If driving, pull over to a clear area. Stay in the car.

https://www.ready.gov/earthquakes.name: Trapped by Debris / Building Collapse
steps: Do NOT panic or move unnecessarily. Conserve oxygen.
Cover your mouth and nose with your shirt.
Tap on a pipe or wall in groups of 3.

https://www.ready.gov/earthquakes.name: Landslide: Void Space Survival
steps: Create an immediate dust filter.
Establish a structural void.
Conserve oxygen & signal rhythmically.
Identify spatial orientation.

https://www.weather.gov/safety/tornado.name: Tornado Survival
steps: Get to lowest floor. Basement ideal. Interior room.
Under sturdy furniture. Cover head with arms or mattress.
Outdoors: lie flat in ditch, cover head.
Vehicle: drive perpendicular to path or park, duck, seatbelt.

https://www.weather.gov/safety/tornado.name: Tornado Shelter Procedure
steps: Get to the lowest interior room away from windows.
Get under a sturdy table and cover your head and neck.
If outdoors, lie flat in the nearest ditch or low spot.
After the tornado passes, watch for downed power lines and gas leaks.

https://www.weather.gov/safety/tornado.name: Mobile Home Tornado Protocol
steps: Abandon the mobile home immediately.
Seek a storm shelter or sturdy building nearby.
If no shelter: lie flat in a low-lying ditch or depression.
Move away from the mobile home (debris hazard).

https://www.weather.gov/safety/tornado.name: Tornado Ditch Shelter
steps: Find a low spot: ditch, ravine, or depression.
Lie face down, hands covering head and neck.
Stay away from trees, cars, and poles.
Watch for flash flooding in the ditch.

https://www.weather.gov/safety/tornado.name: Driving Near Tornado
steps: Determine the tornado's path relative to you.
Drive at a right angle to the tornado's path.
If imminent impact: abandon car for a ditch.
Do NOT hide under an overpass.

https://www.nifc.gov/.name: Wildfire Survival
steps: Move downhill and into the wind.
Cover mouth with wet cloth. Stay low.
Go to water, roads, cleared areas, or burned zones.
Trapped: clear vegetation, lie face down in depression.

https://www.nifc.gov/.name: Wildfire Evacuation
steps: Evacuate EARLY. Do not wait for mandatory evacuation orders.
Drive with headlights on, windows up, vents closed.
If trapped, find a body of water, cleared area, or road.
Cover exposed skin and breathe through a wet cloth.

https://www.nifc.gov/.name: Monitoring Wildfire
steps: If fire is downwind: monitor wind direction constantly.
Clear 30 feet of flammable material around your location.
Watch for spot fires started by embers.
Be ready to evacuate instantly if smoke thickens or wind changes.

https://www.tsunami.gov/.name: Tsunami Response
steps: If you feel a strong earthquake near the coast, move to high ground immediately.
Watch for natural warning signs: ocean suddenly pulling far from shore.
Move inland or to elevation above 100 feet. Go UP, not just away.
Stay at high ground for hours. Tsunamis come in multiple waves.

https://www.tsunami.gov/.name: Tsunami Survival
steps: If you feel a quake or see water recede: RUN TO HIGH GROUND.
If caught: climb a strong concrete building or tree.
Expect multiple waves. The first is rarely the largest.

https://www.weather.gov/safety/hurricane.name: Hurricane Survival
steps: Secure all windows/doors. Create a 'safe room' in interior.
Fill bathtubs and containers with water BEFORE storm hits.
Stay inside during the 'eye' — calm is temporary.

https://www.cdc.gov/niosh/topics/heatstr.name: Extreme Heat Management
steps: Stop all activity. Find or build shade.
Wet skin/clothes if water available (not drinking water).
Elevate feet. Loosen clothing. Fan yourself.

https://www.nps.gov/subjects/bears/safet.name: Black Bear Encounter
steps: Stand your ground. Make noise. Look big.
If it charges: stand ground (bluff charge).
If it attacks: FIGHT BACK vigorously.

https://www.nps.gov/subjects/bears/safet.name: Grizzly/Brown Bear Encounter
steps: Do NOT run. Back away slowly. Speak calmly.
If it charges: STAND GROUND. Deploy spray.
If it makes contact: PLAY DEAD.

https://www.nps.gov/subjects/bears/safet.name: Polar Bear Encounter
steps: Deter immediately. Flare, warning shot, noise.
Fight for your life. Use any weapon.

https://www.nps.gov/subjects/bears/safet.name: Black Bear Avoidance
steps: Make yourself look large.
Make check noise, shout firmly: 'Hey bear! Go away!'
Back away slowly. Do NOT run.
If it follows, throw rocks/sticks and get aggressive.

https://www.nps.gov/subjects/bears/safet.name: Grizzly Avoidance
steps: Stop. Do not run. Do not yell.
Speak softly and calmly: 'Hey bear, whoa bear'.
Avoid eye contact (seen as a challenge).
Back away slowly while facing the bear.

https://www.nps.gov/subjects/bears/safet.name: Back-Bearing Triangulation
steps: Identify two or three known landmarks visible from your position.
Take a compass bearing to each landmark.
Add 180° to each bearing to get the back-bearing.
Draw back-bearing lines from each landmark on your map. You are at the intersection.

https://www.nps.gov/subjects/bears/safet.name: Back Bearing Check
steps: Every few minutes, turn around and look at where you came from.
Memorize key landmarks from the return perspective.
At decision points (trail forks, stream crossings), mark your route.

https://www.nps.gov/subjects/bears/safet.name: Emergency Straight-Line Travel
steps: Sight two objects in line with your desired direction — one near, one far.
When you reach the near object, pick a new near object aligned with the far one.
When the far object is reached or lost, select two new aligned objects.

https://www.who.int/health-topics/snakeb.name: Snake Avoidance
steps: Freeze. Locate the snake.
Identify safe escape route (away from head).
Back away slowly and deliberately.
Stomp feet as you retreat (vibrations warn others).

https://www.ready.gov/.name: Sinkhole Response
steps: If the ground starts cracking or sagging: move IMMEDIATELY away from the area.
If caught in a sinkhole: try to reach the edge. Grab roots, rocks, or ledge material.
Alert others: yell for help. Make noise continuously so rescuers can locate you.
After escaping: mark the area clearly so others avoid it. Do not approach the edge — it may continue collapsing.
Camp AWAY from sinkhole terrain: limestone, karst topography, areas with many surface depressions.

https://www.ready.gov/.name: Mudslide Survival
steps: Warning signs: increased water flow in streams, cracking ground, tilting trees, unusual sounds from uphill.
If you hear a mudslide approaching (roaring sound uphill): run LATERALLY away from the flow path, not downhill.
If caught: curl into a tight ball, protect your head, keep mouth and nose covered.
After the flow passes: extract yourself immediately. Mud creates suction — work arms free first, then legs.
Avoid sleeping in valleys, drainage areas, or below steep slopes after heavy rain.

https://www.ready.gov/.name: Ice Storm Power Outage
steps: Conserve heat: close off unused rooms. Hang blankets over doorways and windows for insulation.
Layer clothing: multiple thin layers trap air and insulate better than one thick layer.
Use the outdoors as a freezer: food in a cooler outside stays cold without electricity.
NEVER use a gas stove, charcoal grill, or generator INDOORS — carbon monoxide kills silently.
Conserve phone/device battery for emergency calls only. Keep one charged phone off as backup.

https://www.ready.gov/.name: Hazmat Evacuation
steps: Determine wind direction.
Move CROSSWIND (perpendicular to wind).
Seek higher ground.

https://www.ready.gov/.name: Vehicular Mob Evasion
steps: Keep all doors locked and windows rolled up.
Maintain slow, continuous forward momentum.
Do not turn off the engine unless immobilized or on fire.

https://www.ready.gov/.name: Barricading
steps: Block the door from the inside.
Stay away from the "fatal funnel".
Cover windows and turn off lights.

https://www.ready.gov/.name: Following Water to Civilization
steps: Find flowing water and follow it downstream.
Stay near the water but travel on the bank.
Watch for signs of civilization: fences, bridges, trash, cut stumps.
Caution: rivers can become impassable canyons.

https://www.weather.gov/.name: Dust Storm Response
steps: Signs of approaching dust storm: wall of brown/orange on the horizon, sudden wind increase, static electricity.
Seek shelter: get inside a vehicle (windows up, engine off, lights on) or behind a solid structure.
If caught in the open: get to the lee side of a large rock, vehicle, or any windbreak.
Cover ALL airways: wrap cloth around nose and mouth. Protect eyes with goggles or cloth.
Do NOT move during the storm. Visibility can drop to zero. Wait for it to pass.

https://www.weather.gov/.name: Hailstorm Shelter Response
steps: Large hail (golf ball+) can cause serious injury and even death. Get under solid cover IMMEDIATELY.
Best shelter: vehicle, building, cave, rock overhang. Worst: open field, under trees (branches break).
If no shelter: lie face-down, cover head and neck with hands, pack, or any hard object.
Protect eyes: hail shatters and fragments fly. Keep face pressed down or covered.
After hailstorm: check for injuries — hail injuries can be severe but not immediately painful due to cold/shock.

https://www.weather.gov/.name: Wildfire Smoke Protection
steps: Wildfire smoke contains fine particles (PM2.5), carbon monoxide, and volatile organic compounds.
Reduce exposure: stay inside with windows/doors closed. Breathe through a damp cloth if outdoors.
Stay low — smoke rises, so breathable air is closest to the ground.
