If driving: turn on headlights, reduce speed, close windows, recirculate cabin air.
Watch for symptoms: burning eyes, coughing, chest tightness, headache, confusion.

https://www.weather.gov/.name: Cloud Type Navigation
steps: Cumulus clouds (puffy, flat-bottomed) over open land during daytime often indicate thermal updrafts from solid ground.
Lenticular clouds (lens-shaped) indicate mountains downwind. They form on the lee side of high terrain.
Dark, flat-bottomed clouds on the horizon may indicate water beneath (reflection of water surface).
Cloud movement shows upper-level wind direction — this is more consistent than ground-level wind.
Towering cumulonimbus (thunderhead) clouds = severe weather approaching. Move perpendicular to their travel path.

https://www.weather.gov/.name: Barometric Pressure Detection
steps: Ear pressure: if your ears feel 'full' or you need to pop them, barometric pressure is dropping — bad weather coming.
Campfire smoke: smoke rising straight up = high pressure (good weather). Smoke that sinks or swirls = low pressure (storm coming).
Sound traveling farther than usual indicates low pressure and approaching weather change.
Joint pain or old injury aches intensifying can indicate dropping pressure (scientifically debated but widely reported).
Rising pressure = clearing weather, descending clouds. Falling pressure = deteriorating weather, rising clouds.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wilderness.name: Avalanche Survival (Swimming)
steps: As soon as the snow slab fractures above you, YELL to alert your party and drop your heavy gear.
Fight violently to stay on the surface.
Grab onto a sturdy tree or massive rock if one is in reach.
As the avalanche begins to slow, immediately cup both hands firmly over your mouth and nose.
Once it stops, violently thrust an arm straight up.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wilderness.name: Fear vs. Panic Protocol
steps: Acknowledge the difference: Fear is a survival tool that heightens senses. Panic is a loss of rational control.
Identify panic somatic markers: tunnel vision, hyperventilation, trembling, inability to process instructions.
Execute a physical interrupter: splash cold water on your face, bite your lip, snap a rubber band on your wrist.
Apply the STOP acronym: Stop moving. Take a breath. Observe your surroundings. Plan your next action.
Assign yourself ONE micro-task. 'I will pick up that stick.' 'I will tie my shoe.' Complete it.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wilderness.name: Sleep Deprivation Coping
steps: Understand the timeline: at 24 hours awake, cognitive impairment equals a 0.10 BAC (legally drunk).
Implement the 2-Person Rule for all critical decisions. No one decides to move, cross water, or abandon gear alone.
Use caffeine or stimulants strategically, not constantly. Save them for when physical movement is mandatory.
Take 'combat naps.' A 20-minute nap clears adenosine (sleep pressure chemical) from the brain, restoring temporary function.
Recognize microsleeps. If you catch your head nodding or realize you don't remember the last 10 seconds of walking, STOP.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wilderness.name: Survivor Guilt Management
steps: Recognize survivor guilt early. If you survived a crash or event that others didn't, the brain often defaults to self-blame.
Compartmentalize the grief until safety is secured. Box the emotions up verbally: 'I will mourn them when I am safe.'
Reframe survival as an obligation to the deceased. Their death makes your survival more important, not less.
Perform a brief, structured ritual. A 1-minute moment of silence, or placing a rock marker.
Focus obsessively on the living. Direct all your energy to helping injured or panicked survivors.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wilderness.name: Group Conflict De-escalation
steps: Recognize that fear manifests as anger.
Separate the conflicting parties physically.
Acknowledge the fear explicitly.
Never take sides in an emotional dispute.
Enforce strict routine.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wilderness.name: Immediate Grief Triage
steps: Accept the acute shock response.
Prevent irrational actions.
Focus on the living.
Assign them a mechanical, repetitive task.
Do not offer false platitudes.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_O.name: Lightning Position
steps: If caught in an exposed area during a thunderstorm with no shelter available, get to the lowest point possible.
Crouch into a ball on the balls of your feet with your heels touching.
Cover your ears, tuck your head between your knees.
NEVER lie flat on the ground.
If in a group, spread out at least 50 feet apart.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_O.name: Tornado Survival (Open Field)
steps: If you cannot reach a sturdy building or underground shelter, look for a low ditch or depression.
Lie face down in the lowest spot you can find.
If in a vehicle, do NOT try to outrun the tornado unless you can clearly see it moving perpendicular to you.
If driving and a tornado is approaching head-on, pull over, put the vehicle in park, keep your seatbelt on, and duck below the windows.
After the tornado passes, watch for downed power lines, gas leaks, and structural collapse.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_O.name: Wildfire Escape Protocol
steps: Move downhill and downwind toward already-burned areas (the black).
Look for natural firebreaks: roads, rivers, rock scree fields, lakes.
If entrapped, find a cleared or low-fuel area and lie face down.
Cover yourself with a wet blanket, dirt, or a fire shelter if available.
Stay down until the fire front passes completely.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_O.name: Bear Encounter Protocol
steps: Identify the species. Black bears are smaller with straight faces and tall ears. Grizzlies have a distinctive shoulder hump and dish-shaped face.
For both: DO NOT RUN.
GRIZZLY (defensive encounter): Play dead.
BLACK BEAR: Fight back aggressively.
Deploy bear spray at 20 feet if available.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_O.name: Snake Encounter Protocol
steps: Most snakebites occur when people try to catch or kill snakes.
Wear boots and long pants in snake country.
If bitten: remove rings, watches, and tight clothing near the bite.
Do NOT: cut the bite, suck out venom, apply ice, or apply a tourniquet.
Immobilize the bitten limb below heart level, keep the patient calm, and evacuate.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_O.name: Hypothermia Stage Assessment
steps: MILD (95–90°F / 35–32°C): Shivering, goosebumps, fumbling hands, stumbling.
MODERATE (90–82°F / 32–28°C): Violent shivering or shivering STOPS. Confusion, slurred speech, poor decisions.
SEVERE (<82°F / <28°C): No shivering. Rigid muscles. Unconscious or semi-conscious. Paradoxical undressing.
Handle severe hypothermia patients EXTREMELY gently.
Rewarm the CORE, not the extremities.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_O.name: Heat Exhaustion vs Heat Stroke
steps: HEAT EXHAUSTION: Heavy sweating, weakness, nausea, headache, dizziness, normal or slightly elevated temperature.
Treatment for heat exhaustion: Move to shade, remove excess clothing, drink cool water, apply cool wet cloths.
HEAT STROKE: Sweating STOPS. Hot, dry, red skin. Confusion or unconsciousness. Core temp above 104°F.
Heat stroke treatment: Aggressive emergency cooling.
The transition from heat exhaustion to heat stroke can happen in under 30 minutes.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_O.name: Altitude Acclimatization Schedule
steps: Above 8,000 feet (2,400m), increase sleeping altitude by no more than 1,000 feet (300m) per day.
For every 3,000 feet gained, take a rest day.
Hydrate aggressively: 3–4 liters of water per day at altitude.
Avoid alcohol and sleeping pills.
Prophylactic Acetazolamide (Diamox) accelerates acclimatization.

https://www.usgs.gov/programs/landslide-.name: Landslide (Indoors)
steps: Do NOT attempt to flee the house if the slide is already occurring.
Move to a second story or the roof immediately.
If trapped on the ground floor, curl into a tight ball under sturdy furniture.
Cover your mouth and nose with cloth.

https://www.usgs.gov/programs/landslide-.name: Landslide (Vehicle)
steps: If you see a slide approaching ahead, stop and reverse immediately.
If debris blocks your path, ABANDON THE VEHICLE instantly.
Run strictly laterally (sideways), out of the flow path.

https://www.usgs.gov/programs/landslide-.name: Landslide (Outdoors)
steps: Listen for roaring, cracking trees, or rushing water.
Run perpendicular to the slope, moving laterally out of the basin.
If escape is impossible, curl tightly into a fetal position.

https://www.rescuesource.com.name: Swiftwater Survival Swimming
steps: Assume the defensive swimming position: on your back, feet downstream, head upstream.
NEVER try to stand up in moving water deeper than your knees.
Use the ferry angle to move laterally to shore.

https://www.osha.gov/flood.name: Navigating Flood Electrocution Hazards
steps: Assume all standing water is electrically charged.
Do not enter a flooded basement if the power is still on.
If you MUST walk through suspect water, use the 'shuffle step'.

https://www.mountaineers.org/books/books.name: Rockfall Evasion
steps: Look UP instantly and identify the fall line.
Dive behind a solid barrier or large tree uphill of you.
If caught without cover, minimize your profile.

https://www.usgs.gov/special-topics/wate.name: Sinkhole Escape & Evasion
steps: If the ground gives way, spread your arms wide immediately.
Do NOT stand near the edge. The lip will continue to collapse.
If trapped inside, climb only on stable, embedded roots or solid rock.

https://www.ready.gov/car.name: Sinking Vehicle Escape
steps: Unbuckle seatbelts immediately.
Roll down the windows before the power fails.
Break the side window if it won't roll down.
Exit through the window and swim to safety.

https://www.ready.gov/car.name: Winter Vehicle Kit
steps: Pack warmth: Blankets, sleeping bag, extra hats/gloves.
Include sand/litter for traction and a shovel.
Food and water (rotate regularly).

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Sta.name: Emergency Sandbagging
steps: Fill bags only half to two-thirds full.
Lay bags flat like brickwork.
Compact each bag into place.
Build a pyramid shape if stacking high.

https://www.usgs.gov.name: Landslide Diagnostics
steps: Listen for low-frequency rumbling.
Look for new cracks in structures.
Observe water flow deviations.

https://www.iaem.org.name: Debris Flow Evacuation
steps: Do not run down the mountain.
Seek immediate massive structure.
Curl into the fetal position and cover your head.

https://www.ready.gov.name: Flood: Structural Diagnostics
steps: Check for hydrostatic bowing.
Listen for structural shearing.
Equalize water pressure (Extreme Measure).

https://rescue3.com/.name: Flood: Hydrology & Current Evasion
steps: Identify and avoid 'Strainers'.
Target 'Eddies' for refuge.
Escape 'Hydraulic jumps' (Keepers).

https://www.usgs.gov/media/images/landsl.name: Landslide: Geotech Diagnostics
steps: Look for tension cracks on slopes.
Monitor spring water clarity.
Observe 'J-root' tree lean.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: River Strainer Survival
steps: Identify the threat.
Swim aggressively forward.
Do NOT go under.
Launch yourself up.
Crawl over the obstacle.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Bear Attack Defense (Advanced)
steps: Identify bear species.
Identify attack intent.
Respond to DEFENSIVE.
Respond to PREDATORY.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Mountain Lion Encounters
steps: DO NOT RUN.
Maintain unbroken eye contact.
Make yourself massive.
Throw projectiles.
Fight back viciously if attacked.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Vehicle Rollover Bracing
steps: Cross your arms.
Tuck your chin.
Press deeply into the seat.
Exhale before impact.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Submerged Vehicle Extrication
steps: Do NOT wait for pressure to slowly equalize.
Unbuckle seatbelts.
Roll down windows fast.
Break the glass if jammed.
Swim out head first.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Avalanche Burial Survival
steps: Swim aggressively during the slide
Create an air pocket before debris settles
Determine which way is up
Conserve air

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Tsunami Survival Protocol
steps: Recognize natural warnings
Move to high ground IMMEDIATELY
If caught in water, grab floating debris
Expect multiple waves

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Volcanic Ash Fall Survival
steps: Seal your shelter
Protect your lungs
Protect your eyes
Do not drive

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Sandstorm Survival
steps: Shelter behind any windbreak
Cover nose, mouth, and eyes completely
Turn your back to the wind
Wait it out

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Flash Flood Vehicle Escape
steps: Never drive through flowing water
If water rises around your car, ESCAPE IMMEDIATELY
If water is too deep to open doors
Move to the roof of the car

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Urban Survival After Disaster
steps: Assess structural stability before entering ANY building.
Locate water: hot water heaters (40-80 gallons), toilet tanks (not bowls), pipes.
Scavenge systematically: food, water, medicine, tools, clothing, communication devices.
Move at dawn. Stay off main roads — they become choke points. Use alleys and rooftops.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Vehicle Breakdown in Desert
steps: STAY WITH THE VEHICLE. It's visible to searchers and provides shade.
Create shade: open all doors, drape blankets/space blankets over the roof.
Ration water: drink what you need, don't sip. Your body can't store small sips.
Energy budget: ALL activity in pre-dawn and post-dusk. No movement midday.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Vehicle Breakdown in Winter
steps: STAY IN THE VEHICLE unless shelter is visible within 100 yards.
Run engine 10-15 minutes per hour for heat.
Insulate: stuff seats with newspaper, floor mats on windows, huddle for warmth.
Signal: tie bright cloth to antenna, turn on hazard lights periodically, stomp SOS in snow.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Earthquake Response (During)
steps: DROP to your hands and knees immediately.
COVER your head and neck under a sturdy desk or table.
HOLD ON to your shelter until shaking stops.
After shaking stops: expect aftershocks. Check for gas leaks (smell), structural damage, and injuries.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Tsunami Warning Signs & Response
steps: Warning sign 1: earthquake you can feel while near the coast.
Warning sign 2: ocean recedes dramatically, exposing the seafloor.
RUN TO HIGH GROUND. At least 100 feet elevation or 2 miles inland.
Multiple waves will arrive over hours — the first is often NOT the largest.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Hurricane Preparation & Survival
steps: Board windows, secure outdoor items, fill bathtubs with water.
Stock 1 gallon of water per person per day for 7 days.
Shelter in an interior room on the lowest floor during the storm.
DO NOT go outside during the eye — the backside hits from the opposite direction with no warning.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Flash Flood Survival
steps: Recognize warning signs: distant rumbling, sudden rise in water level, debris in stream.
Move to high ground IMMEDIATELY. Do not try to cross flowing water.
If trapped in a vehicle: abandon it if water is rising and you can reach high ground.
After flood: avoid walking in floodwater — contains sewage, chemicals, and hidden hazards.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Snake Encounter Prevention
steps: Watch where you step and put your hands. 80% of bites are on hands and feet.
Wear boots and long pants in snake country.
At night: use a light and clear your sleeping area before lying down.
If you see a snake: freeze, then slowly back away. Most snakes strike within 1/3 of their body length.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Heat Exhaustion Emergency Treatment
steps: Recognize: heavy sweating, weakness, cold/clammy skin, nausea, fast weak pulse.
Move to shade or coolest available area immediately.
Remove excess clothing. Apply cool water to skin, fan the patient.
If patient stops sweating, becomes confused, or has hot dry skin: this is HEAT STROKE. Emergency.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Landslide & Mudflow Warning Signs
steps: Warning signs: cracks in ground, tilting trees/poles, bulging ground, new springs or seeps.
During heavy rain on steep slopes: listen for rumbling like a freight train.
Move perpendicular to the flow direction — do not run downhill ahead of it.
After: avoid the area — secondary slides are common.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Wildfire Approach Assessment
steps: Watch smoke column: leaning toward you = fire approaching. Vertical column = low wind.
Wind direction determines fire spread — fire moves with the wind.
Identify escape routes NOW before you need them — two routes minimum.
If fire is within 1 mile and approaching: evacuate immediately. Don't wait to see if it changes direction.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Crocodile/Alligator Territory Safety
steps: Assume any water body in crocodile territory contains crocodiles.
Never approach the water's edge closer than 5 meters.
Camp 50+ meters from water. Store food away from camp AND away from water.
If attacked: target the eyes and nostrils — only vulnerable points.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Volcanic Eruption Response
steps: Pyroclastic flow (superheated gas cloud) moves 400+ mph — you CANNOT outrun it.
Lava flow: generally slow (walking pace). Move perpendicular to the flow direction.
Lahar (volcanic mudflow): triggered by eruption melting glaciers. Moves 40+ mph down valleys.
Ash fall: shelter indoors. Wet ash on roofs is extremely heavy — clear it before collapse.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Spider Bite Prevention in Camp
steps: Shake out ALL clothing and boots before putting them on.
Keep sleeping area clear of ground debris. Sleep off the ground if possible.
Seal tent/shelter openings. Tuck pants into socks.
Check under logs, rocks, and debris before grabbing with bare hands.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Tundra Survival Essentials
steps: Wind is the primary killer — wind chill drops perceived temperature 40+ degrees.
Build windbreaks from snow blocks or dig into snowdrifts.
Water is everywhere but frozen — melt snow, don't eat it directly (lowers core temp).
Navigation: no trees for landmarks, use snow drifts (wind-shaped), sun, and stars.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Tropical Jungle Survival
steps: Water is abundant but contaminated — ALWAYS treat.
Movement is extremely slow: 1 mile per hour through thick jungle.
Follow streams downstream to settlements.
Insect protection is critical — malaria and dengue kill more than anything in the jungle.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Mountain Survival & Alpine Zone
steps: Altitude sickness affects everyone above 8,000 feet — ascend slowly, 1,000 feet per day max.
Weather changes in minutes — carry layers and rain protection always.
Lightning risk above treeline — descend below ridgelines during storms.
Water from snowmelt is abundant and relatively clean.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Open Ocean Survival
steps: Do NOT drink seawater — it accelerates dehydration and death.
Solar still from seawater if materials available.
Signal: mirror during day, light at night.
Fish: improvise hooks, trail bait behind raft.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Urban Post-Collapse Survival
steps: Water: hot water heaters (40-80 gal per building), toilet tanks (not bowls), swimming pools (treat first).
Food: grocery stores, restaurants, vending machines — systematically clear them.
Security: travel in groups, designated watchperson, avoid main roads at night.
Shelter: choose upper floors (flooding), interior rooms (sniper protection), buildings with clear sight lines.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Thunderstorm Safety Protocol
steps: 30/30 rule: if flash-to-bang is under 30 seconds, take shelter immediately.
Best shelter: hard-topped vehicle or substantial building.
If caught in open: get to lowest point, crouch on balls of feet, minimize contact with ground.
Stay sheltered 30 minutes after LAST thunder.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Ice Travel Safety Assessment
steps: Minimum ice thickness: 4 inches clear ice for walking. 5 inches for snowmobile. 8-12 inches for vehicles.
Test with an ice chisel or axe every 50 feet as you cross.
Carry ice picks around your neck and a throw rope.
Avoid: river bends, bridges, areas near docks, and late-season ice.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Sinkhole Recognition & Avoidance
steps: Warning signs: circular depressions, cracks in ground or pavement, leaning trees/fences.
Fresh sinkholes: bare earth in a circular pattern, slumping edges, water draining into the ground.
Camp on solid bedrock when possible — avoid karst terrain (limestone with caves).
If the ground begins to subside: move away from the center IMMEDIATELY.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Dust Storm Health Hazards
steps: Cover mouth and nose with a damp cloth — dust particles under 10 microns reach deep lung tissue.
Goggles or any eye protection: dust contains silica and fungi (Valley Fever in US Southwest).
Seal shelter openings: hang wet blankets over doors and windows.
After dust storm: clean all water containers, food surfaces, and equipment thoroughly.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Fire Ant Attack Response
steps: Brush ants off QUICKLY — they coordinate stinging in response to a chemical alarm signal.
Move away from the mound. Remove all clothing and check skin for remaining ants.
Cold compress on sting areas. Antihistamine for itching.
Watch for anaphylaxis if hundreds of stings: difficulty breathing, swelling, dizziness.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Moose Encounter Safety
steps: Moose are NOT docile — they attack more people annually than bears and wolves combined.
Warning signs: ears pinned back, hair standing up on neck, licking lips, stomping feet.
If a moose charges: run. Use a tree or vehicle as a barrier.
If knocked down: curl into a ball, protect your head.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Wasp & Hornet Nest Avoidance
steps: Scan ahead on trail: look for paper nests in trees, ground holes with wasp traffic, and hollow logs.
If wasps begin investigating you: remain still and move away slowly.
If stung once, leave the area quickly — the sting releases alarm pheromones attracting more wasps.
If swarmed: RUN. Cover your face and run in a straight line for 200+ yards.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Altitude Acclimatization Protocol
steps: Above 8,000 feet: sleep no more than 1,000 feet higher than the previous night.
Stay hydrated: 3-4 liters per day. Dehydration mimics and worsens altitude sickness.
Eat a high-carbohydrate diet at altitude.
Rest day every 3,000 feet of elevation gain.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Desert Water Rationing
steps: Do not ration your water.
Rest strictly during the day.
Travel exclusively at night.
Keep your mouth shut.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Snow Blindness Prevention
steps: Recognize the massive UV threat.
Do not squint; it is not enough.
Carve Inuit-style slit goggles.
Charcoal the ocular cavities.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Jungle River Crossing
steps: Assess the crossing.
Check for the deepest, narrowest point vs shallow, wide.
Construct a flotation aid.
Dry off immediately.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Improvised Wood Gasifier
steps: Find two nested cans.
Puncture the outer can.
Puncture the inner can.
Nest and ignite.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Ember Extension & Transport
steps: Select punk wood or cattail
Place ember in tinder bundle
Carry in a fire horn
Fan to flame at destination

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Fatwood Identification & Harvesting
steps: Locate dead standing pine or conifer
Split stump or root junction
Shave fine curls
Ignite with single spark

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Char Cloth Production
steps: Cut 100% cotton into 2-inch squares
Place in sealed tin with small hole
Place tin in fire for 15 minutes
Remove and seal hole

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Ferro Rod Technique
steps: Scrape off black coating first
Push rod away, not scraper
Direct sparks into fine tinder
Shield from wind

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Fire Piston Ignition
steps: Prepare char cloth or tinder fungus on piston tip
Insert piston into cylinder
Strike piston sharply downward
Quickly extract ember

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Wet Ground Fire Platform
steps: Lay a platform of green logs
Add a layer of bark or stones
Build fire on top of platform
Replace platform logs as needed

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Swedish Torch (Log Candle)
steps: Select a dry log 12-18 inches tall
Split into quarters with an axe
Insert tinder in the center cracks
Light from the center

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Banking a Fire for Overnight Burn
steps: Build a thick coal bed
Cover coals with ash
Place a large green log on top
Uncover and add tinder in the morning

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Signal Fire Smoke Control
steps: White smoke for dark backgrounds
Black smoke for light/snow backgrounds
Three fires in triangle = distress
Time with clear skies

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Underground Heating Flue
steps: Dig a shallow trench
Cover trench with flat stones
Build fire at intake end
Lay insulation and bedding on heated stones

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Fire from Batteries (Any Type)
steps: Find a battery and any thin conductive material: steel wool, foil gum wrapper, or wire.
Steel wool method: touch both battery terminals to fine steel wool.
Gum wrapper method: cut the foil-backed wrapper into an hourglass shape (thin center).
Car battery: touch jumper cable ends together briefly near tinder.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Upside-Down (Top-Down) Fire
steps: Lay 3-4 large logs parallel on the ground as the base.
Stack medium logs perpendicular on top, then smaller pieces, then kindling and tinder on top.
Light the tinder on TOP.
The fire feeds itself as embers fall onto the next layer — burns 3-5 hours unattended.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Bow Drill Mastery — Complete Protocol
steps: Materials: fireboard and spindle from SOFT dry wood (willow, cottonwood, cedar). They must match.
Bow: curved stick with cordage (shoelace, paracord, bark strip). Wrap once around spindle.
Carve a V-notch in the fireboard with a depression where the spindle sits. Place tinder below the notch.
Bear down with socket, saw bow rapidly. Smoke = keep going. Stop after dark brown dust pill forms in the notch.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Hand Drill Fire Starting
steps: Select a straight, dry spindle: cattail, mullein, yucca stalk, or sotol. 18 inches long.
Fireboard: flat piece of dry softwood with a depression and V-notch.
Spin the spindle between your palms, pressing down as you roll.
Key technique: 'float' your pressure — heavy at first, lighter as smoke appears.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Improvised Rocket Stove
steps: Build an L-shaped combustion chamber from rocks, bricks, cans, or clay.
Insulate the outside of the chimney with mud, earth, or double walls.
Feed small sticks into the horizontal opening. Fire burns intensely at the elbow where channels meet.
Cooking pot sits directly on top of the chimney.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Tinder Collection & Storage Guide
steps: Best tinders: birch bark, fatwood shavings, char cloth, dried grass, cattail fluff, cedar bark.
Medium tinders: dryer lint, steel wool, cotton balls with petroleum jelly, birch polypore fungus.
Collect and DRY tinder continuously — even when you don't need fire.
Wet environment: look INSIDE standing dead trees, under bark, and in bird nests.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Fire Lay Configurations
steps: Teepee: kindling leaned together in a cone shape — best for starting fires and quick heating.
Log cabin: alternating layers of parallel sticks — burns hot and produces excellent coals.
Star fire: long logs radiating from central point — push inward as they burn.
Long fire: two parallel logs with fire between — radiates heat along the entire length.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Starting Fire with Wet Wood
steps: Find the DRIEST material first: inside standing dead trees, under bark, in the center of dead branches.
Baton (split) larger pieces to expose dry interior wood.
Create feather sticks: shave thin curls along a dry stick without detaching them.
Build a platform of wet sticks to elevate your fire off the wet ground.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Traditional Flint and Steel
steps: Obtain a piece of high-carbon steel (like an old file or a dedicated striker) and a hard, sharp-edged rock (flint, chert, or quartz).
Prepare true Char Cloth or amadou fungus.
Hold the rock and the char cloth in your non-dominant hand.
Strike the edge of the rock with the steel rapidly.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Fireman's Carry Technique
steps: Roll patient face-down
Pull patient onto your shoulders
Hold their right arm and right leg
Walk with short, steady steps

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Signal Fire Platform
steps: Build a platform of dry wood, elevated off damp ground on a log base.
Cover with a waterproof layer (bark, plastic, large leaves) until needed.
When aircraft heard: remove cover, ignite from pre-positioned fire. Add green vegetation for smoke.
Three fires in a triangle = international distress signal.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Fire Arrow Ground Signal
steps: Build an arrow on the ground pointing in your direction of travel.
Make the arrow visible from the air: rocks on sand, logs on grass, dug trenches in snow.
At night: three fires in the direction you're heading.
Update the arrow and signals each time you move to a new location.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: The Quinzee (Snow Cave)
steps: Pile the snow aggressively.
Wait 90 minutes.
Excavate from the bottom up.
Punch a ventilation hole.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Swamp Bed (Raised Platform)
steps: Locate four sturdy 'corner' trees.
Lash the horizontal frame.
Lay the mattress decking.
Insulate the deck.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Improvised Reflector Oven
steps: Build an angled reflector wall
Place food between fire and reflector
Use a flat stone as a baking surface
This is the wilderness equivalent of an oven

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Ferro Rod / Fire Steel Technique
steps: Hold the ferro rod with one hand, striker/knife spine with the other.
Scrape firmly at 45° angle along the rod into your tinder bundle.
Aim sparks at the most combustible part of your tinder: char cloth, birch bark, or cotton.
Works when wet, in wind, at altitude, and in sub-zero temperatures.

https://www.harpercollins.com/products/s.name: Sea Ice Desalination
steps: Locate mature, multi-year sea ice if possible.
Identify 'old ice' by its color and shape.
Chip away the top surface and collect the ice beneath.
Melt and taste-test a small amount.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Whiteout Navigation
steps: STOP if possible
If you must move, use a rope team
Use a compass bearing and pace count
Beware of cliffs and crevasses

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Navigating Jellyfish-Infested Water
steps: Cover all exposed skin: clothing, wetsuit, even plastic bags over limbs.
Swim slowly and deliberately — don't splash.
If stung while swimming: don't panic. Exit water calmly.
Treatment after exit: vinegar for most species, hot water for Portuguese man-o-war.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Advanced Star Navigation
steps: Find Cassiopeia (M or W shape).
Find Orion's Sword.
Track a random star.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Attack Point Method
steps: Identify a large, unmistakable feature near your target
Navigate to the attack point first
From the attack point, use precise navigation
This reduces precision errors over long distances

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Contour Navigation
steps: Read the slope
Walk across the slope, not up or down
Use altimeter if available
Useful for traversing to a known drainage/ridge

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Aiming Off
steps: Identify a linear feature your target is on
Aim 5-10° to one side of the target
Turn toward your target
This eliminates the 50/50 guessing problem

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Reciprocal Bearing Check
steps: Take your forward bearing
Add or subtract 180°
Turn around and check the back bearing
If off by more than 5°, you have a deviation error

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Map Resection
steps: Identify two or three visible landmarks on your map
Take a compass bearing to each landmark
Convert to grid bearing
Draw back-bearings on the map

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Slope Aspect Direction
steps: Identify which direction a slope faces
In the Northern Hemisphere, south-facing slopes get more sun
North-facing slopes are cooler and damper
Use vegetation and snow patterns to confirm orientation

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Sun Shadow Stick Method
steps: Plant a straight stick vertically in the ground
Mark the tip of the shadow
Wait 15-30 minutes
Mark the new position

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Moss as a Direction Indicator
steps: Moss grows where conditions are moist and shaded
In dense forests, moss grows on all sides
On isolated trees in open areas, it's more useful
Use moss only to CONFIRM other navigation methods

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Urban Navigation Without GPS
steps: Satellite dishes point south (Northern Hemisphere)
Church entrances typically face west
Highway mile markers increase south-to-north/west-to-east
Cell tower sectors face specific directions

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Snow Travel Navigation
steps: Use wands or markers behind you
In whiteout, throw a snowball ahead
Travel on windward side of ridges
Avoid snow-filled gullies and ravines

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Night Travel Skills
steps: Allow 30+ minutes for night vision to develop
Lift feet higher than normal
Navigate by silhouettes
Use hearing as primary sense

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: GPS Battery Conservation
steps: Turn off GPS between fixes
Use airplane mode on phones
Lower screen brightness
Keep battery warm

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Cloud Identification for Weather Prediction
steps: Cirrus (high, wispy): fair weather NOW but can indicate a front approaching in 24-48 hours.
Cumulus (puffy, flat-bottomed): fair weather if small. Towering=thunderstorm developing.
Stratus (flat, grey layer): expect drizzle or light rain.
Cumulonimbus (anvil-shaped, dark): thunderstorm imminent. Seek shelter.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Reading Barometric Pressure Without Instruments
steps: Falling pressure (storm approaching): campfire smoke descends, scent of vegetation intensifies.
Your joints and sinuses may ache as pressure drops.
Watch animals: birds perch low, fish jump more, insects cluster near the ground.
Rising pressure (clearing): smoke rises straight, dew forms in morning, sky clears from west.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Wind Direction & Strength Assessment
steps: Wet a finger and hold it up — the cold side faces the wind.
Observe natural wind indicators: grass bending, leaf movement, smoke direction.
Mid-latitude weather generally moves west to east.
Face the wind, extend right arm 90° — it points roughly toward LOW pressure (storm center in Northern Hemisphere).

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Finding Direction at Sea
steps: Sun: rises in the east, sets in the west. Track its arc for rough cardinal directions.
Stars: Polaris (North Star) indicates true north. Southern Cross indicates south.
Swells: prevailing swells maintain consistent direction. Use them as a heading reference.
Land indicators: cumulus clouds form over islands, bird flights radiate from land in morning.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Urban Navigation Without Maps or GPS
steps: Get to high ground: rooftop, bridge, or hill for visual orientation.
Satellite dishes point toward the equator (south in Northern Hemisphere).
Follow infrastructure: power lines lead to substations, roads lead to intersections.
Street numbering systems: most US cities use grid numbering from a central point.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Shadow Stick Method
steps: Place a straight stick vertically in the ground. Mark shadow tip with a rock.
Wait 15-20 minutes. Mark the new shadow tip position.
Draw a line between the two marks — this is your east-west line.
Stand with first mark (west) on your left: you're facing north.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Using Prevailing Winds for Direction
steps: Learn your region's prevailing wind direction BEFORE you need it.
Observe bent trees: they lean away from the prevailing wind.
Windblown snow drifts accumulate on the leeward side of obstacles.
Combine with vegetation: moss grows on the side that stays moist (sheltered from drying wind).

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Night Movement & Navigation
steps: Wait 30+ minutes for full night vision adaptation. ANY bright light resets the clock.
Move slowly: feel the ground with each step before committing weight.
Navigate by silhouettes: ridge lines, tree canopy gaps, and stars visible through openings.
Mark your route: tie cord to trees, leave rock cairns, or scratch arrows for return trip.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: River Current Reading for Crossing
steps: Throw a stick in the water and pace alongside it — if you can't keep up, don't cross.
Check depth: probe with a long stick. Knee-deep is the maximum safe crossing depth in moderate current.
Cross at the widest, shallowest point — usually a riffle, not a pool.
Face upstream, cross at a 45-degree downstream angle, use a staff for third-point-of-contact.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Topographic Map Reading
steps: Contour lines connect points of equal elevation. Closer lines = steeper terrain.
Read the scale: 1:24,000 means 1 inch on map = 2,000 feet on ground.
Identify features: concentric circles = hilltop. V-shapes pointing uphill = valley.
Orient the map to terrain: rotate until mapped features align with what you see.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Magnetic Declination Adjustment
steps: Magnetic north and true north differ by 0-20+ degrees depending on your location.
Find your local declination: noted on topographic maps or use a declination table.
To convert compass bearing to map bearing: add east declination, subtract west declination.
Mark your compass with a declination arrow to avoid repeated calculation.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Dead Reckoning — Advanced Techniques
steps: Start from a KNOWN position. Take a compass bearing to your objective.
Walk on bearing using pace count. Track distance carefully.
At each obstacle (cliff, swamp): box around it maintaining cardinal direction and distance.
Use attack points: navigate to a large nearby feature first, then dead reckon the final short distance.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Telling Time by Sun Position
steps: The sun moves 15° per hour across the sky (360° / 24 hours).
At local noon, the sun is at its highest point due south.
Estimate: fist-widths between sun and horizon. Each fist at arm's length ≈ 10° ≈ 40 minutes before sunset.
3 fists from the horizon = about 2 hours until sunset.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: GPS Failure Contingency Plan
steps: BEFORE electronics fail: note your coordinates, direction of travel, and nearest landmarks.
Carry a physical compass and know how to use it — no batteries required.
Carry a paper map of your operating area. Mark your planned route.
Know at least 3 non-electronic navigation methods: sun, stars, terrain association.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Using the Moon for Direction
steps: First quarter (right half lit): the lit side faces west at sunset, south at midnight.
Full moon rises in the east at sunset and sets in the west at sunrise.
Connect the tips of a crescent moon with a line and extend to the horizon: this points roughly south (Northern Hemisphere).
Waning crescent (left side lit): the lit side faces east.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Reading a River for Navigation Clues
steps: Rivers flow DOWNHILL — always. Following downstream leads to larger water and eventually the sea.
River bends: deeper water on the outside of curves, shallow on the inside.
Debris lines on banks show flood levels — camp ABOVE the highest debris line.
Human signs along rivers: cleared banks, rope marks on trees, fishing debris, bridge abutments.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Using Vegetation for Direction
steps: In Northern Hemisphere: south-facing slopes receive more sun — vegetation is denser and greener.
Tree rings: wider on the south side (more growth from more sun).
Spider webs tend to be built on the south side of trees (warmer, more insects).
Flowers track the sun — many species face east in the morning and follow the sun west.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Orienteering — Map and Compass Integration
steps: Orient your map: rotate map until magnetic north arrow aligns with your compass needle.
Identify your position: find 2-3 features you can see that also appear on the map.
Plot your route: measure compass bearing from current position to destination.
Follow the bearing using pace count. Check terrain features against the map as you travel.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Field Sketch Map Making
steps: Find high ground with a wide view. Identify visible landmarks: hills, water, roads, structures.
Draw landmarks at approximate scale relative to each other.
Add distances between landmarks using pace count or time estimates.
Mark water sources, trails, hazards, and safe areas.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Praying Mantis Method (Phone Compass Backup)
steps: Most phones have a magnetometer that works WITHOUT cellular or GPS signal.
Open the compass app. It provides magnetic bearing even in airplane mode.
Combine with a downloaded offline map (if pre-loaded) for simple navigation.
Even without maps: knowing cardinal directions prevents walking in circles.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Dead Reckoning (Pace & Bearing)
steps: Determine your baseline 100-meter pace count.
Shoot an azimuth (bearing) to your destination using a compass.
Walk directly to the steering mark while counting paces.
Upon reaching the mark, shoot a new azimuth.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Topography: The Hill
steps: Look for concentric, closed circles.
Determine elevation gain.
Assess the slope.
Use as an orientation point.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Topography: The Saddle
steps: Identify two adjacent peaks.
Locate the hourglass shape.
Recognize navigational utility.
Identify drainage patterns.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Topography: The Valley
steps: Look for U or V shaped contour lines.
Locate the watercourse.
Determine the gradient.
Navigational hazard warning.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Topography: The Ridge
steps: Look for U or V shaped contour lines pointing down elevation.
Identify the crest.
Recognize navigational utility.
Assess exposure risks.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Topography: The Depression
steps: Look for closed contour lines with tick marks.
Differentiate from a hill.
Determine depth.
Tactical use.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Resection (2-Point)
steps: Orient the map.
Identify two prominent features.
Shoot magnetic azimuths to both.
Plot the back-azimuths.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Intersection
steps: Define the objective.
Shoot an azimuth from Position A.
Relocate laterally to Position B.
Shoot an azimuth from Position B.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Navigating by Baselines
steps: Identify a massive, linear 'Catching Feature'.
Determine the cardinal direction to the baseline.
Ignore precise points.
Commit to the direction.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Analog Watch Sun Compass
steps: Ensure the watch is set to true local time.
Point the hour hand exactly at the sun.
Bisect the angle to 12 o'clock.
The bisected line points South.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/army/fm3-25-2.name: Southern Cross Intersect
steps: Locate the Crux (Southern Cross).
Locate the Pointers.
Draw mental lines to intersect.
Drop to the horizon.

https://www.ready.gov/shelter.name: Door Barricade & Room Security
steps: Wedge a door with a belt, rope, or improvised doorstop.
Stack heavy furniture against the door.
Create an early warning system with glass or cans near the entrance.
Secure windows: block visibility and restrict entry.

https://www.ready.gov/shelter.name: Seal a Room (Shelter in Place)
steps: Choose an interior room with few windows.
Turn off all ventilation (HVAC, fans).
Tape plastic sheeting over windows, doors, and vents.

https://www.ready.gov/shelter.name: Urban Shelter in Emergencies
https://www.ready.gov/shelter.body:
Urban disasters create unique shelter challenges. Buildings may be damaged, utilities down, and normal shelter unavailable.

Immediate urban shelter options:
- Parking garages: concrete structure, win

https://www.aaa.com/.name: Emergency Vehicle Entry
steps: Break a SIDE window, never the windshield.
Use headrest metal prongs to shatter the window.
Clear all glass before reaching through the opening.
Only use this skill in genuine life-threatening emergencies.

https://www.aaa.com/.name: Vehicle Escape
steps: Unbuckle seatbelt immediately.
Open or break the window.
Exit through the window.

https://www.aaa.com/.name: Vehicle as Shelter
steps: Stay in the vehicle. It provides insulation, wind protection, visibility.
Insulate: stuff seats, floor mats, spare clothes in gaps.
Run engine 10 minutes per hour for heat if safe.
Signal: turn on hazard lights, tie bright cloth to antenna.

https://www.aaa.com/.name: Vehicle Shelter
steps: Stay with the vehicle — it is shelter, signal, and storage.
Run engine 10-15 minutes per hour for heat. Keep exhaust pipe clear of snow.
Insulate windows with floor mats, newspapers, or clothing.
Tie a bright cloth to the antenna. Turn on hazard lights periodically.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Wound Infection Management
steps: Recognize infection: redness spreading from wound, warmth, swelling, pus, red streaks.
Reopen and flush the wound with clean water — at least 1 liter under pressure.
Apply a warm, wet compress to draw out infection.
Apply natural antiseptics if no medical supplies available.
Elevate the infected limb and keep the person hydrated.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Wound Irrigation & Cleaning
steps: Wash your hands thoroughly before touching the wound.
Flush the wound with clean running water for 5 minutes.
Remove visible debris with clean tweezers.
Apply antibiotic ointment and cover with a clean bandage.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Wound Packing
steps: Identify the wound as deep and bleeding heavily despite direct pressure.
Using gauze or clean cloth, push material INTO the wound cavity.
Apply firm direct pressure on top of the packed wound for 3 minutes.
Secure with a pressure bandage. Monitor for continued bleeding.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Improvised Chest Seal
steps: Identify the wound: look for bubbling blood or sucking sound on chest.
Expose the wound completely — remove clothing around it.
Cover wound with plastic (bag, wrapper, card) taped on THREE sides.
Position person sitting upright, leaning toward injured side.
Monitor breathing closely. If breathing worsens, briefly lift seal.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Emergency Wound Closure
steps: Only close wounds that are clean, less than 6 hours old, and not from bites.
Clean the wound thoroughly with clean water under pressure.
Use butterfly strips or medical tape to close the wound edges.
Apply antibiotic ointment if available, then cover with clean bandage.
Check wound daily for infection: redness spreading, pus, fever, red streaks.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Junctional Hemorrhage Control
steps: Apply direct pressure with hemostatic gauze if available.
For junctional wounds (neck, groin, armpit): pack tightly and hold.
Maintain constant, firm pressure for minimum 3 minutes.
Apply pressure bandage over the packed wound.
Elevate the wound above heart level if possible.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Wound Infection Recognition
steps: Check wound daily. Normal: decreased redness, less pain, clear/straw-colored drainage.
Warning signs: INCREASING redness spreading outward, warmth, swelling, and pain after day 2-3.
Red streaks extending from the wound toward the heart = urgent. This is lymphangitis.
Thick, foul-smelling pus (yellow/green) indicates bacterial infection. Fever above 100.4°F confirms systemic infection.
Treatment: clean wound aggressively, apply antiseptic, change dressings frequently. Antibiotics if available.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Sucking Chest Wound Dressing
steps: Identify: bubbling or sucking sound from a chest wound on breathing. Air entering the chest cavity collapses the lung.
Cover the wound IMMEDIATELY with any airtight material: plastic wrap, credit card, duct tape, foil, or a gloved hand.
Tape the covering on THREE sides only, leaving the bottom edge open.
Check for an EXIT wound on the back — there may be two holes. Both need sealing.
Monitor breathing closely. If breathing worsens despite the seal, briefly lift one edge to release trapped air.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Nail Bed Injury Treatment
steps: If the nail is partially torn: do NOT pull it off. Trim the loose section and leave the attached portion.
If the nail is completely lost: clean the exposed nail bed gently with clean water. Do not scrub.
Apply antibiotic ointment if available. Cover with a non-stick pad secured with tape.
For a subungual hematoma (blood under the nail causing throbbing pain): heat a paperclip and melt through the nail.
Change dressings daily. A new nail will grow back in 3-6 months. Keep the area clean and protected.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Field Wound Closure
steps: ONLY close wounds that are clean, less than 6 hours old, and have smooth edges.
Butterfly strips: cut tape into narrow strips with wider ends. Apply perpendicular to the wound, pulling edges together.
Improvised sutures: use a sterilized fishhook or needle and clean thread (dental floss, inner paracord strand).
Simple interrupted suture: enter 1/4 inch from wound edge, pass through both sides, tie with square knot.
Monitor for infection daily: redness spreading from wound, increased swelling, warmth, pus, or red streaks = remove sutures immediately.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Field Infection Prevention
steps: Irrigate every wound aggressively with the cleanest water available. Pressure is key — use a squeeze bottle or syringe.
Natural antiseptics: diluted raw honey, crushed garlic juice, or sphagnum moss applied to wounds.
Keep wounds OPEN in the field — do not close dirty or animal-bite wounds. Pack loosely with clean material.
Change dressings at least twice daily. Inspect for: increasing redness, warmth, swelling, pus, or red streaks.
Elevate infected limbs above heart level. Gravity reduces swelling and slows pathogen spread.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Butterfly Closure Strips
steps: Clean the wound thoroughly with clean water under pressure.
Dry the skin on BOTH sides of the wound completely.
Apply tincture of benzoin to the skin on both sides of the wound.
Apply butterfly strips perpendicular to the wound, pulling the edges together firmly.
Cover with a sterile gauze pad and tape.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Plantain Leaf Poultice
steps: Broadleaf plantain (Plantago major) grows in lawns, paths, and disturbed soil worldwide.
Chew or crush several fresh leaves into a green paste.
Apply the paste directly to insect stings, minor cuts, or rashes.
The plantain draws out toxins, reduces inflammation, and promotes wound healing.
Plantain also soothes poison ivy rash when applied immediately after contact.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Honey Wound Dressing
steps: Raw, unprocessed honey has potent antibacterial properties.
Clean the wound thoroughly before application.
Apply a thick layer of honey directly to the wound surface.
Cover with a clean bandage.
Change the dressing every 24 hours.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Garlic as Field Antibiotic
steps: Raw garlic contains allicin, a potent antimicrobial compound.
Crush a fresh garlic clove and let it sit for 10 minutes.
For wound treatment: Apply crushed garlic to the wound periphery, NOT directly into the wound.
For internal infection: Eat 2-3 raw, crushed cloves per day.
Wild garlic grows across North America and Eurasia.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Hemostatic Wound Packing
steps: Identify source of massive bleed.
Push gauze deep into the cavity.
Pack tightly until full.
Apply direct downward pressure.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Combat Gauze Wound Packing
steps: Expose the wound completely
Pack tightly into wound
Apply direct pressure for 3 min
Apply pressure dressing over top

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Improvised Negative Pressure Wound Therapy
steps: Clean wound thoroughly
Place gauze in wound bed
Seal with plastic wrap and tape
Attach manual suction

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Stingray Wound Treatment
steps: Control bleeding with direct pressure.
Immerse wound in hot water (113°F/45°C) for 30-90 minutes.
DO NOT remove the barb if deeply embedded — stabilize and evacuate.
Wound infection risk is high — start broad-spectrum antibiotics if available.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Wound Irrigation (Pressure Irrigation)
steps: Prepare irrigation solution: cleanest water available. Boiled and cooled is ideal.
Create pressure: poke a small hole in a plastic bag or use a syringe.
Irrigate aggressively: 250ml minimum for small wounds, 1 liter for large or contaminated wounds.
Pat dry and dress. Re-irrigate every 12-24 hours if in the field.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Emergency Tourniquet (High and Tight)
steps: Place tourniquet as HIGH and TIGHT on the limb as possible.
Tighten until ALL bleeding stops and distal pulse is absent.
Note the TIME. Write 'TQ' and time on the patient's forehead.
A tourniquet can safely stay on for 6+ hours in most cases.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Sucking Chest Wound Seal
steps: Recognize: bubbling or sucking sound from chest wound during breathing.
Apply an occlusive (airtight) dressing: plastic wrapper, petroleum gauze, emergency chest seal.
Tape on THREE sides, leaving the bottom untaped as a flutter valve.
Monitor for tension pneumothorax: worsening breathing, distended neck veins.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Improvised Chest Seal from Plastic
steps: Find any non-porous material: credit card, plastic bag, duct tape, foil wrapper.
Wipe blood from skin around wound to help the seal stick.
Apply the material over the wound. Tape on THREE sides only.
Monitor for tension pneumothorax: worsening breathing, distended neck veins.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: UTI Management in the Field
steps: Recognize: burning urination, frequent urges, cloudy/bloody urine, lower abdominal pain.
Flush aggressively: 3+ liters of water per day — dilutes bacteria and flushes the tract.
Urinate frequently — do not hold it.
Cranberry if available (prevents bacteria from adhering to bladder wall).

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Wound Infection Prevention
https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.body:
In survival, infection is the slow killer. Every wound becomes a race against bacteria.

Signs of infection (usually appear 24-72 hours after injury):
- Increasing redness spreading from wound edges
-

https://www.redcross.org.uk/first-aid/le.name: Rib Fracture Management
steps: Confirm rib fracture: point tenderness on ribs, pain worse with breathing/coughing.
DO NOT wrap or bind the chest tightly.
Practice deep breathing exercises every hour despite the pain.
Position for sleep: sleep on the INJURED side.
Monitor for pneumothorax: sudden worsening, one-sided chest expansion.

https://www.redcross.org.uk/first-aid/le.name: Arm/Wrist Splint
steps: Do NOT attempt to realign the bone.
Find a rigid splint material: stick, cardboard, rolled magazine.
Pad the splint with cloth and secure with bandages or strips.
Check circulation below the splint: warmth, color, feeling, pulse.

https://www.redcross.org.uk/first-aid/le.name: Leg Splint
steps: Immobilize the leg in the position found.
Use two long rigid objects as splints — one inside, one outside the leg.
Pad between the leg and splints generously.
Secure with wide bandages at 4-5 points. Avoid tying over the break.

https://www.redcross.org.uk/first-aid/le.name: Deep Splinter & Foreign Body Removal
steps: Clean the area with soap and water before removal.
Sterilize tweezers or needle with flame or alcohol.
Grasp the splinter near the skin and pull along the angle of entry.
Squeeze the wound gently to encourage bleeding.
Clean again, apply antibiotic if available, and cover.

https://www.redcross.org.uk/first-aid/le.name: Femur Fracture Traction Splint
steps: Suspect femur fracture: severe thigh pain, inability to move leg, leg appears shortened.
Check for circulation, sensation, and movement in foot before splinting.
Create traction splint: one long pole from armpit to past foot.
Secure splint at chest, hip, above knee, and at ankle.
Apply gentle traction at ankle to realign and reduce pain.

https://www.redcross.org.uk/first-aid/le.name: Improvised Rigid Foam Splint
steps: Cut a section of a closed-cell foam sleeping pad long enough to extend past the joints above and below the fracture.
Fold the pad lengthways into a U-shape or 'gutter'.
Pad the injured limb with spare clothing before applying the foam gutter.
Place the limb in the gutter and secure it tightly with cravats, ace bandages, or duct tape.
Elevate the splinted limb.

https://www.redcross.org.uk/first-aid/le.name: Improvised Pelvic Binder
steps: Identify pelvic fracture.
Prepare a broad sheet.
Slide under the greater trochanters.
Pull tight and cross over.
Tie off or secure.

https://www.redcross.org.uk/first-aid/le.name: Improvised Pelvic Binder (Sheet)
steps: Fold sheet lengthwise
Slide under patient at hip level
Cross ends over pelvis and pull tight
Tie or clamp in place

https://www.redcross.org.uk/first-aid/le.name: Improvised Traction Splint
steps: Pad the ankle and groin
Secure two rigid poles alongside the leg
Apply traction via ankle hitch
Twist a windlass to apply steady pull

https://www.redcross.org.uk/first-aid/le.name: Open Fracture Field Management
steps: Control bleeding first
Irrigate wound with clean water
Cover with moist sterile dressing
Splint in position found

https://www.redcross.org.uk/first-aid/le.name: Jaw Dislocation Reduction
steps: Recognize: jaw locked open, patient cannot close mouth, drooling, extreme pain.
Wrap your thumbs in thick cloth for protection.
Place wrapped thumbs on the lower molars. Apply steady DOWNWARD then BACKWARD pressure.
The jaw should click back into place. Apply a bandage wrap to limit opening for 24 hours.

https://www.redcross.org.uk/first-aid/le.name: Flail Chest Stabilization
steps: Recognize: section of chest wall moves INWARD during inhalation (paradoxical motion).
Position patient with the INJURED SIDE DOWN.
Do NOT tape or strap the chest — this restricts breathing.
Support ventilation. If patient tires and breathing becomes shallow: assist ventilation with bag-valve-mask if available.

https://www.redcross.org.uk/first-aid/le.name: Finger Dislocation Reduction
steps: Confirm dislocation: finger is obviously crooked at a joint, painful, unable to bend.
Grip the dislocated segment firmly. Pull steady traction ALONG the finger axis.
As traction is applied, gently guide the tip back into normal alignment.
Buddy-tape to adjacent finger for 2-3 weeks. Ice and ibuprofen for swelling.

https://www.redcross.org.uk/first-aid/le.name: Open Fracture Management
steps: Control bleeding first.
Irrigate heavily if evacuation is delayed (>6 hours).
Dress and splint in place.
Do NOT attempt to push the bone back in.

https://www.redcross.org.uk/first-aid/le.name: Analgesic: Willow Bark Extraction
steps: Identify Salix species (Willow).
Harvest the inner bark.
Simmer, do not boil aggressively.
Drink for pain/fever relief.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Choking Response (Complete Guide)
steps: Assess: can they cough, speak, or breathe? If yes — encourage coughing.
Give 5 sharp back blows between the shoulder blades with heel of your hand.
If back blows fail: 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich maneuver).
Alternate: 5 back blows, then 5 abdominal thrusts until cleared.
For infants: 5 back blows with baby face-down on your forearm, then 5 chest thrusts.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Emergency Tourniquet
steps: Place tourniquet 2-3 inches above the wound.
Pull strap tight and secure it.
Twist the windlass rod until bleeding stops.
Lock the windlass in place and note the time.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Pressure Bandage
steps: Apply direct pressure with a clean cloth.
Elevate the wound above the heart if possible.
Wrap with elastic bandage snugly but not cutting off circulation.
If blood soaks through, add more material on top.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Impaled Object Management
steps: Do NOT remove the impaled object.
Stabilize the object so it cannot move.
Control bleeding around the object with direct pressure.
Immobilize the body part and transport carefully.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Emergency Childbirth
steps: Prepare a clean area. Wash hands and groin area.
Support the head as it crowns. Do NOT pull.
Clear baby's airway. Dry and warm immediately.
Do not cut cord until it stops pulsing (1-3 mins).

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Improvised Stretcher
steps: Find two sturdy poles 7+ feet long and a large piece of fabric or clothing.
If using jackets: zip them closed, turn inside out, and slide poles through the sleeves.
If using a blanket: lay the blanket flat, fold one-third over a pole, place the second pole on the fold, fold the remaining third over both.
Lift together on a count of three. Move in unison.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Nerve & Circulation Check
steps: Check Circulation: feel for pulse below the injury.
Check Sensation: can they feel you touching fingers/toes?
Check Movement: can they wiggle fingers/toes?
Check skin color and temperature below injury.
Document and re-check every 30 minutes.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Psychological First Aid
steps: Approach calmly. Introduce yourself. Ask if they need help.
Move to a quiet, safe location away from the scene.
Listen actively. Let them talk without interrupting or judging.
Address immediate practical needs: water, warmth, phone.
Help them identify next steps and connect with support.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Abdominal Injury Management
steps: Do NOT push organs back in if they are protruding.
Cover exposed organs with moist, clean cloth.
Position person on their back with knees bent.
Do NOT give food, water, or medication by mouth.
Treat for shock: keep warm, monitor breathing.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Wound Irrigation
steps: Gather clean water (boiled and cooled, or purified). You need at least 500mL.
Create pressure: poke a small hole in a plastic bag or use a syringe (if available).
Irrigate from the cleanest end of the wound toward the dirtiest. Flush thoroughly.
Remove visible debris with clean tweezers. Pick out gravel, splinters, plant matter.
Pat dry around the wound (not in it) and apply appropriate dressing.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Near-Drowning Aftercare
steps: Even if the person seems fine after rescue, ALL near-drowning victims need monitoring for 24+ hours.
Remove wet clothing, dry thoroughly, rewarm. Treat for hypothermia if cold water.
Position: if conscious, sit upright or in recovery position. If unconscious, recovery position.
Watch for delayed symptoms: coughing, chest pain, difficulty breathing, foamy sputum, lethargy.
If breathing deteriorates at any point, begin rescue breathing. Have a plan for evacuation.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Abdominal Assessment
steps: Have the patient lie flat with knees slightly bent to relax abdominal muscles.
Visually inspect: look for bruising, distension (swelling), asymmetry, or open wounds.
Gently palpate all four quadrants, starting AWAY from the pain. Note rigid or guarded areas.
Monitor for signs of shock: rapid pulse, pale/cool skin, confusion, falling blood pressure.
Position of comfort: left lateral (left side down) if vomiting. Keep warm. Nothing by mouth.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Femoral Artery Pressure Point
steps: Locate the femoral artery: in the crease where the leg meets the groin, midway between the hip bone and pubic bone.
Press firmly with the heel of your hand directly over the pulse point.
Maintain constant pressure while another person applies direct pressure or a tourniquet to the wound.
If alone: apply a tourniquet high and tight on the upper thigh, then release pressure point.
Monitor for shock: keep the patient warm, elevate uninjured legs, and reassure them.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Abdominal Evisceration Management
steps: DO NOT push organs back in. DO NOT touch them with bare hands.
Cover exposed organs with a moist, clean dressing — soaked in clean water or saline if available.
Cover the moist dressing with plastic wrap or a waterproof layer to retain moisture.
Position patient on their back with knees bent and supported (pillow, rolled clothing under knees).
Keep warm, monitor for shock, give nothing by mouth. Evacuate IMMEDIATELY — this is a surgical emergency.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Rapid Trauma Assessment (MARCH)
steps: M — Massive hemorrhage: scan the entire body for life-threatening bleeding. Apply tourniquet or pressure immediately.
A — Airway: is the airway open? Tilt head, lift chin. Clear blood, vomit, or debris from the mouth.
R — Respiration: is the patient breathing? Check for adequate rise and fall of the chest. Seal chest wounds.
C — Circulation: check pulse (wrist or neck). Assess skin color, temperature, and moisture for shock.
H — Hypothermia/Head: protect from heat loss (insulate from ground, cover). Assess consciousness (AVPU scale).

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: improvised Hemostatic Agents
steps: Yarrow (Achillea millefolium): crush fresh leaves and pack directly into the wound.
Spider web: collect and pack clean spider webs into minor wounds.
Wood ash (clean, white ash from hardwood): pack into wounds as a last resort.
Coffee grounds: unused, dry grounds packed into wounds promote clotting.
These are supplements to direct pressure — they do NOT replace it. Maintain firm pressure over any natural agent applied.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Airway Assessment (Chin Lift)
steps: If a person is unresponsive and not breathing, assume their tongue has fallen back and blocked their airway.
Kneel beside their head. Place one hand flat on their forehead and push backward.
Place the fingers of your other hand under the bony part of their lower jaw near the chin.
Lift the jaw upward to bring the chin forward.
If you suspect a spinal injury, use the Jaw Thrust maneuver instead.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Improvised Tourniquet
steps: A tourniquet is ONLY for life-threatening arterial bleeding that cannot be controlled by direct pressure.
Use a 2-inch-wide strip of material (belt, shirt strip, bandana). Never use narrow cords or wire.
Place the tourniquet 2 to 3 inches above the wound (between the wound and the heart).
Tie a half-knot, place a sturdy stick on top, tie a full knot over the stick.
Secure the windlass and write the time of application on the patient's forehead.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: ABCDE Primary Survey
steps: A — Airway: Is the airway open? Can the patient speak?
B — Breathing: Is the chest rising? Can you hear breath sounds on both sides?
C — Circulation: Is there a pulse? Is there major bleeding?
D — Disability: What is the level of consciousness? AVPU: Alert, Voice, Pain, Unresponsive.
E — Exposure: Expose the patient and check for hidden injuries. Then protect from hypothermia.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Secondary Survey (Head-to-Toe)
steps: Run your hands along the scalp feeling for depressions, blood, or swelling.
Palpate the cervical spine, collarbones, shoulders, and each arm.
Palpate the chest wall, pressing each rib.
Press on all 4 quadrants of the abdomen.
Check pelvis stability by pressing inward on the hip bones ONCE.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Baseline Vital Signs
steps: Heart rate: Count the pulse at the wrist (radial artery) for 15 seconds, multiply by 4.
Respiratory rate: Count breaths for 30 seconds (without telling the patient).
Skin signs: Check Color, Temperature, and Moisture (CTM).
Level of consciousness: AVPU — Alert, responds to Voice, responds to Pain, Unresponsive.
Record vitals every 15 minutes and note the TREND.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Blast Injury Assessment
steps: Primary blast injury
Secondary blast injury
Tertiary blast injury
Quaternary blast injury

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Impaled Object Stabilization
steps: NEVER remove impaled objects
Stabilize with bulky dressings
Tape or bandage to prevent movement
Shorten if necessary for transport

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Field Blood Type Identification
steps: Know your blood type in advance
O-negative is universal donor
Improvised transfusion is last resort
Monitor for transfusion reaction

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Pediatric Assessment Triangle (PAT)
steps: Appearance (TICLS)
Work of Breathing
Circulation to Skin
Combine findings

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: START Triage (Mass Casualty)
steps: Walk to designated area
Assess breathing
Assess perfusion
Assess mental status

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Emergency Field Childbirth
steps: Prepare clean area: cleanest surface available, clean cloths, string or shoelace for cord.
Support the baby's head as it emerges — DON'T PULL.
After head: body usually rotates and delivers with next contraction.
Tie cord in two places (6 and 8 inches from baby), cut between ties. Keep baby warm skin-to-skin.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Postpartum Hemorrhage Management
steps: Recognize: soaking more than one pad per hour, large clots, dizziness, rapid pulse.
Fundal massage: place hand on lower abdomen and massage the uterus firmly.
Encourage breastfeeding immediately — nipple stimulation causes uterine contraction.
If bleeding won't stop: bimanual compression — one fist inside the vagina against the uterus, other hand on abdomen.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: START Triage System
steps: Can they WALK? → MINOR (Green tag). Direct walking wounded to treatment area.
Are they BREATHING? No → reposition airway. Still no → EXPECTANT (Black tag).
Respiratory rate >30 or <10? → IMMEDIATE (Red tag).
Can they follow commands and has radial pulse? No pulse or can't follow → IMMEDIATE (Red). Both yes → DELAYED (Yellow).

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: SALT Triage System
steps: Step 1 — Global Sorting: Shout 'Everyone who can walk, move to [location].'
Step 2 — Wave assessment: prioritize patients with obvious life threats first.
Step 3 — Individual triage using SALT: does the patient have a pulse and follow commands?
Assign colored tags and direct resources to IMMEDIATE patients first.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Hemostatic Agent Application
steps: Pack the wound channel DEEP with hemostatic gauze.
Continue packing until wound is completely filled.
Apply direct pressure over the packed wound for 3+ minutes.
Cover with standard bandage, pressure bandage, or elastic wrap.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Traumatic Amputation Field Care
steps: Control bleeding from the stump: tourniquet high and tight, or direct pressure + packing.
Recover the amputated part if safe to do so.
Wrap the amputated part in moist gauze, seal in a plastic bag.
Transport patient and part together to the nearest surgical facility.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Combat Application Tourniquet (CAT)
steps: Identify massive arterial bleeding.
Apply the CAT 'High and Tight'.
Tighten the windlass until bleeding stops.
Secure the windlass and mark the time.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Scene Safety
steps: Stop and scan the area before approaching.
Listen for danger.
Ensure you have an exit route.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/fi.name: Severe Burn Treatment
steps: Stop the burning process.
Cover loosely with sterile, non-stick bandage.
Treat for shock.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dave_Cante.name: Carving a Feather Stick
steps: Select a piece of straight-grained deadwood.
Lock your knife arm.
Slice extremely thin, long curls.
Build a 'tree' of curls.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dave_Cante.name: White Birch Bark Ignition
steps: Harvest from dead/fallen trees first.
Process for sparks.
Ignite the dust.
Feed the flame.

https://www.outdoorlife.com/survival/how.name: Fire in Wet Weather
https://www.outdoorlife.com/survival/how.body:
Rain is fire's greatest enemy. Winning the battle requires knowledge and preparation.

Finding dry fuel in rain:
- Dead standing wood is drier than fallen wood (off the wet ground)
- Inner bark of dea

https://www.outdoorlife.com/survival/how.name: Fire in Wet Conditions
https://www.outdoorlife.com/survival/how.body:
Rain is the most common reason people fail at fire-making. These principles overcome it.

Finding dry fuel in rain:
- Dead standing wood: always drier than ground wood
- Inner bark of dead trees: prot

https://www.apa.org/topics/resilience.name: Fire Psychology & Morale
https://www.apa.org/topics/resilience.body:
Fire is more than heat. It is one of the most powerful psychological tools in survival.

Psychological benefits of fire:
- Comfort: fire creates a sense of home and safety
- Light: eliminates the prim

https://www.apa.org/topics/resilience.name: Shelter Psychology
https://www.apa.org/topics/resilience.body:
Shelter does more than protect your body. It protects your mind.

Psychological benefits:
- Territory: having 'your' space provides sense of control and safety
- Routine: maintaining and improving she

https://smokeybear.com/en/prevention-how.name: Fire Safety in Wilderness
https://smokeybear.com/en/prevention-how.body:
Uncontrolled fire in the backcountry can destroy thousands of acres and kill people. Fire safety is non-negotiable.

Before lighting:
- Check local fire restrictions and burn bans
- Clear a 10-foot di

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Burn Blister Management
steps: Cool the burn under cool running water for 10-20 minutes.
Do NOT pop burn blisters — the fluid protects healing skin.
Cover loosely with non-stick gauze or clean cloth.
Elevate the burned area above heart level if possible.
Watch for infection: increasing redness, pus, fever, red streaks.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Severe Burn (Third Degree)
steps: Call emergency services immediately. This is life-threatening.
Do NOT remove burnt clothing stuck to the skin.
Cover loosely with clean, dry cloth. Do NOT apply water.
Elevate burned area above heart level.
Treat for shock: lay flat, keep warm, monitor breathing.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Burn Treatment
steps: Remove from heat source. Remove clothing/jewelry from burn area.
Cool the burn with cool (not cold) running water for 20 minutes.
Cover with cling wrap or a clean non-fluffy dressing.
Do NOT pop blisters. They are sterile bandages.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Circumferential Burn Assessment
steps: Check for circumferential burns
Assess distal circulation
Elevate burned extremities
Prepare for escharotomy

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Head Trauma Assessment
steps: Keep the person still. Do NOT move them unless in immediate danger.
Check consciousness: ask their name, where they are, what happened.
Control any bleeding with gentle direct pressure.
Check for clear fluid leaking from ears or nose.
Monitor every 15 minutes: pupils, consciousness, breathing.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Concussion Management
steps: Remove person from activity. Physical and mental rest is critical.
Apply cold pack to the impact area for 20 minutes.
Monitor for worsening symptoms over 24-48 hours.
Wake the person every 2-3 hours for the first night.
No blood-thinning painkillers (aspirin/ibuprofen) for 24 hours.

https://www.cdc.gov/traumatic-brain-inju.name: Concussion Quick Guide
steps: Stop all activity. Sit them down in a safe location.
Check pupils: shine light in each eye. Both should be equal size.
Test orientation: name, location, day of the week.
Check balance: stand with eyes closed for 30 seconds.
Monitor for 24 hours. Wake every 2 hours during sleep.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Insect Sting Treatment
steps: Remove stinger immediately — scrape, don't pinch.
Wash the area with soap and water.
Apply cold pack or mud to reduce swelling and pain.
Take antihistamine if available to reduce reaction.
Monitor for allergic reaction for 30 minutes.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Choking Response (Heimlich)
steps: Ask 'Are you choking?' — if they can't speak or cough, act immediately.
Stand behind the person, wrap arms around their waist.
Make a fist, place thumb side against abdomen above navel.
Grasp fist with other hand. Thrust inward and upward sharply.
If object doesn't dislodge: alternate 5 back blows with 5 thrusts.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Heimlich Maneuver (Choking)
steps: Confirm choking — person cannot speak, cough, or breathe.
