Stand behind the person and wrap arms around their waist.
Make a fist with one hand, place thumb side against abdomen above navel.
Thrust inward and upward with quick, forceful movements.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Infant Choking Response
steps: Place infant face-down on forearm, head lower than chest.
5 firm back blows between shoulder blades.
Flip face-up. 5 chest thrusts with two fingers on breastbone.
Repeat until expelled. If unconscious, begin infant CPR.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Choking Response (Adult & Child)
steps: Determine severity: can they cough, speak, or breathe?
Stand behind, give 5 sharp back blows between shoulder blades.
If back blows fail: give 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich).
Alternate: 5 back blows, then 5 abdominal thrusts. Repeat.
If person becomes unconscious: lower to ground, begin CPR.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Choking (Heimlich Maneuver)
steps: Ask 'Are you choking?' If they cannot speak, cough, or breathe, they have a complete obstruction.
Stand behind the victim. Wrap your arms around their waist.
Grasp your fist with the other hand. Perform firm, quick, inward-and-upward thrusts.
Repeat until the object is expelled or the victim becomes unconscious.
For infants: Use back blows and chest thrusts, NOT abdominal thrusts.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Pediatric Choking (Infant < 1 year)
steps: Position infant face-down along your forearm, head lower than body.
Deliver 5 firm back blows between the shoulder blades with heel of hand.
Flip infant face-up on your other forearm. Give 5 chest thrusts.
Alternate back blows and chest thrusts until object is expelled or infant becomes unconscious.

https://www.healthline.com/health/butter.name: Wound Closure Strips
steps: Clean and dry the wound edges thoroughly.
Apply tincture of benzoin to skin on both sides of the wound.
Apply strips perpendicular to the wound, pulling edges together.
Apply reinforcing strips parallel along the strip ends.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/cp.name: CPR (Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation)
steps: Check responsiveness. Tap shoulders and shout.
Place heel of hand on center of chest, between nipples.
Compress chest 2 inches deep at 100-120 beats per minute.
Give 2 rescue breaths after every 30 compressions.
Continue 30:2 cycle without stopping until help arrives.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/cp.name: Ankle Wrap (Sprain)
steps: Apply RICE: Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation.
Start wrapping at the ball of the foot.
Wrap in a figure-8 pattern around the ankle.
Secure the bandage. Check toe circulation.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/cp.name: Sprain Treatment (RICE Protocol)
steps: Rest: stop using the injured joint immediately.
Ice: apply cold for 20 minutes every 2 hours.
Compress: wrap with elastic bandage snugly.
Elevate: keep injured area above heart level.
Test weight-bearing ability after 24 hours of RICE.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/cp.name: Needle Chest Decompression
steps: Identify tension pneumo signs
Locate insertion site
Insert 14ga needle
Secure catheter

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/cp.name: Finger Thoracostomy
steps: Identify life-threatening tension pneumo
Locate 5th ICS, anterior axillary line
Make 4cm incision through skin and muscle
Insert gloved finger and sweep

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/cp.name: Decompression Sickness (The Bends)
steps: Recognize symptoms: joint pain, skin rash, dizziness, weakness, numbness.
Lay patient flat. Administer 100% oxygen if available.
Give oral fluids: 1 liter per hour if conscious.
Evacuate to nearest recompression chamber. Do NOT fly or ascend to altitude.

https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/cp.name: Airway Management Priorities
https://www.redcross.org/take-a-class/cp.body:
Without an open airway, nothing else matters. A patient can survive massive bleeding for minutes, but complete airway obstruction kills in 4-6 minutes.

Common causes of airway obstruction:
- Tongue f

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Treating Shock
steps: Recognize shock: pale/cool/clammy skin, rapid weak pulse, confusion.
Lay the person flat and elevate legs 8-12 inches.
Maintain body temperature — cover with blankets or coats.
Do NOT give food or water. Moisten lips only.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Shock Treatment Protocol
steps: Recognize shock: pale/ashen skin, rapid weak pulse, rapid shallow breathing, confusion, anxiety.
Control the cause: stop bleeding, splint fractures, treat allergic reactions.
Lay the patient flat and elevate legs 12 inches.
Prevent heat loss aggressively.
Give small sips of water if the patient is conscious and not vomiting.

https://www.nhs.uk/conditions/first-aid/.name: Recovery Position
steps: Kneel beside the person. Extend near arm at right angle.
Place far hand against their near cheek, hold it there.
Pull the far knee up with your other hand.
Roll them toward you by pulling the raised knee.

https://www.sja.org.uk/get-advice/first-.name: Arm Sling
steps: Fold a square cloth into a triangle.
Slide the triangle under the injured arm and behind the elbow.
Tie the two upper points behind the neck.
Pin or tie the elbow point to secure it.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Chemical Burn
steps: Brush off dry chemicals before adding water.
Flush with large amounts of water for at least 20 minutes.
Remove contaminated clothing while flushing.
Do NOT try to neutralize the chemical.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Snakebite Management
steps: Move away from the snake. Do NOT try to catch or kill it.
Keep the bitten limb below heart level and immobilize it.
Do NOT cut, suck, tourniquet, or apply ice to the bite.
Mark the edge of swelling and note the time.
Evacuate to a hospital. Antivenom is the only treatment.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Snake Bite Response
steps: Move AWAY from the snake. Do NOT try to catch or kill it.
Keep the bitten limb BELOW heart level and still.
Do NOT: cut wound, suck venom, apply ice, or use tourniquet.
Clean the bite gently with water. Mark the edge of swelling with pen.
Keep calm and still. Monitor for systemic symptoms.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Spider & Scorpion Bite Treatment
steps: Clean bite with soap and water.
Apply cold pack for 10 minutes on, 10 minutes off.
Identify dangerous signs: severe pain, muscle cramps, spreading redness.
Elevate affected limb and keep still.
Draw a circle around redness and note the time.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Snake Bite Pressure Immobilization
steps: DO NOT: cut the wound, suck venom, apply ice, use a tourniquet, or apply electric shock.
Keep the victim CALM and STILL. Movement accelerates venom spread through the lymphatic system.
For neurotoxic snakes (cobras, mambas, sea snakes): apply a compression bandage starting at the bite, wrapping toward the heart.
Immobilize the bitten limb with a splint. Movement of muscles pumps lymph fluid, spreading venom.
For hemotoxic snakes (vipers, rattlesnakes): do NOT apply compression bandage. Keep limb below heart level and evacuate.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Centipede Bite Treatment
steps: Centipede bites cause intense, localized pain, swelling, and redness.
Clean the wound thoroughly.
Apply hot water (not scalding) to the bite area.
Take anti-inflammatory medication.
Watch for secondary infection over the next 48 hours.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Cone Snail Envenomation
steps: Cone snails fire a hollow, harpoon-like tooth that injects conotoxin.
Apply a pressure immobilization bandage immediately.
Immobilize the affected limb completely.
Monitor breathing constantly.
Evacuate IMMEDIATELY.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Blue-Ring Octopus Bite
steps: The blue-ringed octopus injects tetrodotoxin (TTX) — the same poison as pufferfish.
There is NO antivenom.
Apply pressure immobilization bandage to the bite site.
Begin rescue breathing as soon as the patient shows any difficulty breathing.
Continuous rescue breathing for 12-24 hours can result in full recovery.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Pit Viper Snakebite (Rattlesnake, Copperhead, Cottonmouth)
steps: Move away from the snake. Note appearance but do NOT try to catch it.
Remove jewelry and constricting clothing near the bite.
IMMOBILIZE the bitten limb at or below heart level.
Evacuate to hospital. Antivenom is the only definitive treatment.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Coral Snake Bite
steps: Identify: red-yellow-black bands. 'Red touches yellow, kills a fellow.'
Apply a pressure immobilization bandage: firm wrap starting at the bite and wrapping toward the heart.
IMMOBILIZE entirely — splint the limb.
Transport immediately. Symptoms may be delayed 12+ hours but can progress to respiratory failure.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Black Widow Envenomation Protocol
steps: Identify the bite and the spider.
Recognize systemic symptoms (Latrodectism).
Clean the bite site and apply a COLD pack.
Manage pain and evacuate.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Pit Viper Envenomation Protocol
steps: Do no further harm.
Keep the patient calm and immobile.
Splint the bitten extremity.
Mark swelling progression with a pen.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Bee/Wasp Sting Treatment
steps: Scrape the stinger out sideways with a flat edge.
Clean with soap and water. Apply cold pack for 15 minutes.
Watch for anaphylaxis: facial swelling, difficulty breathing, hives.
For mild reactions: antihistamine and pain reliever.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Anaphylaxis Response
steps: Recognize: throat tightening, facial swelling, hives, wheezing, dizziness.
Use epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) into outer thigh.
Call emergency services immediately.
Place in position of comfort. Usually sitting up for breathing.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Anaphylaxis Emergency Protocol
steps: Recognize: rapid onset of hives/swelling + breathing difficulty or low blood pressure.
Use epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) immediately if available — outer thigh, through clothing.
Position: sitting up if breathing difficulty, lying down with legs elevated if low blood pressure.
Antihistamines (Benadryl) are SECOND-LINE, NOT a replacement for epinephrine.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Anaphylaxis: Epinephrine Administration
steps: Recognize anaphylaxis.
Prepare the Epinephrine Auto-Injector (EpiPen).
Inject into the outer mid-thigh.
Massage the area and prepare for a second dose.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Hypothermia Treatment
steps: Get the person out of the cold and remove wet clothing.
Insulate from the ground and cover with multiple layers.
Apply warm (not hot) packs to core areas: neck, armpits, groin.
Give warm sweet drinks if conscious and alert.
Handle gently — a severely hypothermic heart is fragile.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Heat Stroke Treatment
steps: Recognize: hot/red/dry skin, confusion, temp over 104°F, loss of consciousness.
Move to shade. Remove excess clothing.
Cool aggressively: wet the entire body and fan.
Evacuate immediately. Call emergency services.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Altitude Sickness
steps: Recognize: headache, nausea, dizziness, fatigue above 8,000 ft.
Stop ascending. Rest at current altitude.
If symptoms worsen or don't improve in 24 hours: descend 1,000+ feet.
Emergency: give supplemental oxygen if available. Descend immediately.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Acute Mountain Sickness
steps: Recognize AMS: Headache, nausea, extreme fatigue, dizziness, and loss of appetite occurring above 8,000 feet.
STOP ASCENDING IMMEDIATELY.
Hydrate aggressively and rest.
If symptoms do not improve within 24 hours (or if they worsen), you MUST descend.
Never leave someone with AMS alone.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: HACE Assessment (Ataxia)
steps: High Altitude Cerebral Edema (HACE) is lethal swelling of the brain. It kills quickly.
Perform the Heel-to-Toe walk test (Tandem Gait).
If they stumble, fall over, or cannot touch heel-to-toe, they are failing the test.
Ask them simple orientation questions.
If ataxia or confusion is present, DESCEND IMMEDIATELY.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: HAPE Assessment
steps: High Altitude Pulmonary Edema (HAPE) is the lungs filling with fluid due to pressure changes.
Listen for a wet, rattling, or gurgling cough.
Check their breathing rate while resting.
Check their lips and nail beds for cyanosis.
DESCEND IMMEDIATELY.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Ear Barotrauma
steps: Recognize: sharp ear pain, muffled hearing, dizziness, possible bleeding from ear.
DO NOT insert anything into the ear canal.
Apply gentle warmth to the affected ear.
If eardrum rupture suspected: keep ear dry, no swimming, no blowing nose hard.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Acute Mountain Sickness (AMS)
steps: Symptoms at 8,000+ feet: headache that worsens, nausea, fatigue, dizziness.
STOP ascending. Rest at current altitude for 24-48 hours.
Treat headache with ibuprofen 400mg or acetaminophen.
If symptoms worsen despite rest: DESCEND immediately. 1,000+ feet minimum.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: High Altitude Cerebral Edema (HACE)
steps: Recognize: AMS symptoms PLUS ataxia (can't walk straight) and/or altered mental status.
DESCEND IMMEDIATELY. This is an emergency — carry the patient if needed.
Dexamethasone 8mg immediately, then 4mg every 6 hours if available.
Supplemental oxygen 2-4 L/min if available. Gamow bag if descent is impossible.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: High Altitude Pulmonary Edema (HAPE)
steps: Recognize: breathlessness at rest, cough (often with pink frothy sputum), extreme fatigue, cyanosis.
DESCEND AT LEAST 2,000 feet. Keep patient warm and minimize exertion.
Nifedipine 30mg slow-release if available.
Supplemental oxygen 4-6 L/min. Gamow bag as last resort.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: HAPE (High Altitude Pulmonary Edema) Protocol
steps: Recognize the symptoms of HAPE.
DESCEND IMMEDIATELY.
Minimize the patient's physical exertion during descent.
Administer OXYGEN if available, or use a Gamow Bag.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: HACE (High Altitude Cerebral Edema) Protocol
steps: Recognize the hallmark sign: Ataxia (loss of coordination).
DESCEND IMMEDIATELY.
Administer Dexamethasone if available.
Keep the patient's head elevated.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Dehydration Treatment
steps: Recognize: dark urine, dry mouth, headache, dizziness, rapid heart rate.
Give oral rehydration solution: water + salt + sugar.
Small frequent sips — do not gulp.
Rest in shade and minimize activity until rehydrated.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Pediatric Dehydration Assessment
steps: Check the fontanelle (soft spot) in infants: sunken = dehydrated.
Skin turgor test: pinch skin on back of hand or abdomen.
Check mucous membranes: dry lips and mouth, no tears when crying.
Monitor urine: dark yellow or no urine for 6+ hours = significant dehydration.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Dehydration Response
steps: Drink small amounts of fluid frequently.
Check urine color.
Use Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS).

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Dental Emergency
steps: Pick up the tooth by the crown (white part), NEVER the root.
Rinse gently with milk or saliva. Do NOT scrub.
Attempt to reinsert into the socket.
Get to a dentist within 30 minutes.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Emergency Tooth Treatment
steps: For a knocked-out tooth: pick it up by the crown, NEVER the root.
Rinse gently with milk or saliva. Do NOT scrub or use water.
Reinsert the tooth into the socket if possible. Bite on cloth to hold it.
For broken teeth: rinse mouth, apply clove oil for pain if available.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Emergency Dental Pain Relief
steps: For a lost filling: pack the cavity with softened candle wax, chewing gum, or pine resin.
For toothache: apply crushed clove (eugenol) or a piece of fresh garlic directly to the painful tooth.
For a knocked-out tooth: keep it moist in saliva (between cheek and gum) or milk. Reimplant within 30 minutes if possible.
For a broken tooth: cover sharp edges with wax to prevent tongue/cheek cuts. Take pain relief if available.
For an abscess (swelling with pus): rinse with warm salt water every 2 hours. Do NOT pop. Seek evacuation.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Field Dental Emergency
steps: Knocked-out tooth: Pick it up by the CROWN (the white part), never the root.
Attempt to reimplant the tooth into the socket immediately.
If you cannot reimplant it, store the tooth in milk, saliva, or held inside the cheek.
Lost filling or cracked tooth: Pack the cavity with sugar-free gum, dental wax, or candle wax.
Tooth abscess: Rinse with warm salt water. Take anti-inflammatory medication.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Knocked-Out Tooth Preservation
steps: Handle tooth by crown only
Reimplant within 30 minutes if possible
If can't reimplant, store properly
Seek dental care within 2 hours

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Dental Abscess Field Management
steps: Recognize: throbbing toothache, swelling of gum or face, fever, bad taste in mouth.
Rinse with warm salt water (½ tsp salt in 8oz water) every 2 hours.
Pain management: ibuprofen 400-600mg every 6 hours.
If swelling spreads to eye, neck, or floor of mouth: EMERGENCY. This can become life-threatening.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Improvised Temporary Filling
steps: Clean the cavity: rinse mouth with warm salt water, remove any debris.
Dry the tooth as much as possible.
Fill with temporary filling material, sugar-free gum, or candle wax.
Avoid chewing on that side. Replace the improvised filling daily.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Toothache Emergency Relief
steps: Clove oil (eugenol) is the strongest natural dental anesthetic — apply directly to cavity or gum.
Oil of oregano is a backup — strong antibacterial and mild anesthetic.
Ibuprofen 400mg + acetaminophen 500mg together (this combination exceeds either alone — proven in studies).
Warm salt water rinse every 2 hours.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Blister Prevention & Treatment
steps: At FIRST sign of a hot spot: STOP and address it.
Apply moleskin or athletic tape over the hot spot.
If a blister has formed, do NOT pop small blisters.
For large blisters that impede walking: drain from the edge.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Leukotape Blister Moleskin
steps: Clean and dry the affected area completely. Tape will not stick to sweaty or dirty skin.
Cut a piece of Leukotape or moleskin larger than the hot spot.
For an intact blister: Cut a hole in the center of the moleskin (a 'donut' shape) slightly larger than the blister.
Cover the entire donut pad with a solid, larger piece of Leukotape.
Change your socks.

https://www.redcross.org/get-help/how-to.name: Drowning Rescue
steps: Remove victim from water using a reaching aid.
Lay victim flat on back. Check for breathing.
If not breathing: give 5 rescue breaths first.
Begin CPR: 30 compressions, 2 breaths. Continue until breathing resumes.

https://www.redcross.org/get-help/how-to.name: Drowning Rescue Response
steps: Reach or throw — do NOT swim to a drowning person unless trained.
Once out of water: check for breathing. Begin rescue breaths FIRST.
If no pulse after 5 rescue breaths, begin standard CPR (30:2).
Even after revival, seek medical care. Secondary drowning can kill hours later.

https://www.redcross.org/get-help/how-to.name: Figure-8 Ankle Bandage
steps: Start with two anchor wraps around the ball of the foot.
Bring the bandage diagonally across the top of the foot to the outside of the ankle.
Wrap behind the ankle, then cross diagonally back across the front of the ankle to the inside bottom of the foot.
Repeat the figure-8 pattern 3-4 times, overlapping each layer by half.
Secure the end with tape, a clip, or tuck under the wraps. Check circulation: toes should stay pink and warm.

https://www.redcross.org/get-help/how-to.name: Emergency Eye Patching
steps: For objects embedded in the eye: DO NOT remove. Stabilize with a cup, cone, or ring of cloth around the object.
Patch BOTH eyes — not just the injured one. Eyes move together (consensual movement).
Use a soft pad over the closed eye, secured with tape or a bandage wrap around the head.
For chemical exposure: flush the eye with clean water for at least 20 minutes BEFORE patching.
Guide the patient — they are now effectively blind. Keep them calm and describe the environment.

https://www.redcross.org/get-help/how-to.name: Cravat Bandage Sling
steps: Fold a square cloth (shirt, bandana, etc.) diagonally to create a triangle.
Place the forearm across the triangle with the elbow at the point and the hand toward the longest edge.
Bring both ends up and tie behind the neck. Adjust so the forearm is supported across the chest.
Pin or tie the elbow point to prevent the arm from swinging.
Check circulation: fingers should stay pink, warm, and mobile. Adjust tightness if circulation is compromised.

https://www.redcross.org/get-help/how-to.name: Hands-Only CPR
steps: Confirm the person is unresponsive: tap shoulders firmly and shout 'Are you okay?'
Call for help. If alone, call emergency services first if you have a phone. Speaker mode.
Place the heel of one hand on the center of the chest (between the nipples). Stack the other hand on top.
Push HARD and FAST: compress at least 2 inches deep, at 100-120 beats per minute.
DO NOT STOP until: the person starts breathing, professional help arrives, or you are physically unable to continue.

https://www.redcross.org/get-help/how-to.name: Cold Water Immersion Response
steps: Cold shock response: The first 1-3 minutes are the most dangerous.
Do NOT try to swim immediately.
Swim failure occurs after 3-30 minutes.
Hypothermia sets in after 30+ minutes.
If in a group, huddle together.

https://www.redcross.org/get-help/how-to.name: Near-Drowning Management
steps: Remove from water. Begin rescue breathing immediately — still in water if needed.
If no pulse: begin CPR on a flat surface.
All near-drowning victims need hospital monitoring even if they seem fine.
Keep warm — immersion hypothermia is common co-morbidity.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Seizure Management
steps: Clear area around the person of hard or sharp objects.
Place something soft under their head.
Do NOT put anything in their mouth.
Time the seizure. Over 5 minutes is a medical emergency.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Sprained Ankle Field Treatment
steps: Stop walking. Remove boot and assess swelling.
Apply cold for 20 minutes to reduce swelling.
Wrap in figure-8 with elastic bandage or torn cloth.
Test weight-bearing: can you walk with tolerable pain?
Create a walking aid and plan your evacuation.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Poison Ingestion Response
steps: Identify what was ingested, how much, and when.
Do NOT induce vomiting unless specifically directed.
If conscious, rinse mouth with water. Small sips only.
Monitor breathing. Place unconscious person in recovery position.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Poisoning Emergency Response
steps: Identify the poison: what, how much, when, and how.
Do NOT induce vomiting unless specifically told to by poison control.
If poison is on skin: remove contaminated clothing, rinse with water.
If poison inhaled: move to fresh air immediately.
Monitor for shock, breathing difficulty, or seizures.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Drug Overdose Response
steps: Recognize signs: unresponsive, slow/absent breathing, blue lips or fingertips, pinpoint pupils, gurgling.
Call for emergency help immediately. Make noise, yell for help, send someone.
If person is breathing: place in recovery position (on their side).
If NOT breathing: begin rescue breathing — 1 breath every 5 seconds.
Administer naloxone (Narcan) if available: spray in one nostril.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Activated Charcoal for Poisoning
steps: Activated charcoal adsorbs many poisons and prevents them from entering the bloodstream.
Grind hardwood charcoal into the finest possible powder.
Mix 2-3 tablespoons of charcoal powder into a cup of water.
Most effective within 1 hour of ingestion.
Charcoal does NOT work against: alcohols, cyanide, heavy metals, or caustic acids/bases.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Activated Charcoal (Improvised)
steps: Make charcoal: burn hardwood until it's black but not completely ash.
Crush charcoal into fine powder.
Mix 25-50 grams of charcoal powder into 250ml of water.
Drink within 1 hour of poison ingestion for maximum effect.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Water Intoxication (Hyponatremia)
steps: Recognize the paradoxical symptoms.
Stop all pure water intake.
Start immediate, aggressive salt replacement.
Monitor for altered mental status.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Stopping a Nosebleed
steps: Sit upright and lean FORWARD slightly.
Pinch the soft part of the nose firmly for 10-15 minutes.
Apply cold compress to bridge of nose.
If bleeding persists beyond 20 minutes, seek help.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Severe Nosebleed Control
steps: Sit upright and lean FORWARD.
Pinch the soft part of the nose firmly closed using your thumb and index finger.
Breathe through your mouth.
After 15 minutes, gently release.
If bleeding does not stop after 20 minutes of firm pressure, pack the nostril with a rolled gauze strip.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Severe Nosebleed (Epistaxis) Control
steps: Sit upright, lean slightly FORWARD. Do NOT tilt head back.
Pinch the SOFT part of the nose firmly for 15 continuous minutes.
If still bleeding after 15 minutes: pack with gauze or clean cloth strips.
Apply cold compress to bridge of nose and back of neck.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Electrocution Response
steps: Do NOT touch the victim until power source is disconnected.
Once safe, check breathing and pulse immediately.
Treat entry and exit burns.
Monitor for 24 hours — delayed cardiac arrhythmia is common.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Heart Attack Recognition
steps: Recognize: crushing chest pressure, left arm/jaw pain, shortness of breath, cold sweat.
Sit person upright, knees bent, back supported.
Give aspirin if available — have them chew it.
If unresponsive, begin CPR immediately.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Stroke Recognition (FAST)
steps: FACE: Ask them to smile. Does one side droop?
ARMS: Raise both arms. Does one drift down?
SPEECH: Repeat a sentence. Is it slurred?
TIME: Note when symptoms started. Get help NOW.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Heat Stroke Emergency Treatment
steps: Move person to shade or coolest available area immediately.
Remove excess clothing. Expose skin to air.
Cool aggressively: pour water on skin, fan vigorously.
If conscious, give small sips of cool water.
Monitor mental status: confusion, seizures, unconsciousness.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Heat Illness Spectrum
steps: Heat Cramps (mild): muscle spasms during exertion. Stop activity, rest in shade, drink electrolyte water.
Heat Exhaustion (moderate): heavy sweating, weakness, nausea, headache, cool clammy skin, core temp under 104°F.
Heat Stroke (CRITICAL): core temp above 104°F, confusion, hot DRY skin, seizures, unconsciousness.
For heat stroke: cool AGGRESSIVELY. Immerse in cold water, pack ice around neck, armpits, groin.
Do NOT give fluids to a heat stroke patient who is confused or unconscious. Continue cooling until temp drops to 101°F.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Heat Stroke Rapid Cooling
steps: Recognize Heat Stroke.
Create a tarp basin.
Add ice and water.
Agitate the water.
Cool then transport.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Heat Stroke Emergency Cooling
steps: Recognize: hot DRY skin (sweating has stopped), altered mental status, core temp >104°F.
COOL BY ANY MEANS NECESSARY: immerse in cold water, pack ice in armpits/groin/neck.
If no cold water: spray/pour water on skin and fan aggressively.
Stop cooling when core temp reaches 102°F to avoid overshoot hypothermia.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Differentiating Heat Exhaustion vs Stroke
steps: Check the mental status.
Check the skin condition.
Treat Exhaustion with rest and hydration.
Treat Stroke with immediate, utterly aggressive cooling.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Asthma Attack Response
steps: Sit upright, lean slightly forward, hands on knees.
Help use inhaler: shake, exhale fully, inhale while pressing.
Coach slow breathing: 4 in through nose, 6 out pursed lips.
Blue lips or inability to speak = emergency.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Asthma Attack (No Inhaler)
steps: Sit upright — NEVER lie down during an asthma attack.
Lean forward with hands on knees (tripod position).
Pursed-lip breathing: inhale through nose 2 sec, exhale through pursed lips 4 sec — reduces air trapping.
Hot steam (boiling water, face over pot with towel) can relax bronchial muscles.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Dislocated Joint Management
steps: Do NOT try to pop the joint back in.
Immobilize in current position with splint or sling.
Apply cold compress. 20 minutes on, 20 off.
Check circulation below injury: pulse, sensation, color.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid.name: Hyperventilation Management
steps: Reassure: they are NOT dying. It feels like it, but they are safe.
Coach: 4 counts in through nose, 7 counts out through mouth.
Do NOT use a paper bag — outdated and dangerous.
If not anxious, check for other causes (DKA, asthma, heart).

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid.name: Panic Attack Response
steps: Recognize signs: racing heart, chest tightness, hyperventilation, sense of doom.
Move the person to a quiet, safe space if possible.
Guide slow, controlled breathing: inhale 4 seconds, hold 4, exhale 6.
Use the 5-4-3-2-1 grounding technique: name 5 things you see, 4 you touch, 3 you hear, 2 you smell, 1 you taste.
Reassure: 'This will pass. You are safe. Your body is having a false alarm.'

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid.name: Anxiety/Panic Attack Management in Wilderness
steps: Symptoms mimic heart attack: chest tightness, racing heart, numbness, breathing difficulty, doom feeling.
5-4-3-2-1 grounding: name 5 things you see, 4 you touch, 3 you hear, 2 you smell, 1 you taste.
Acknowledge: 'This is a panic attack.
It will pass in 20 minutes.' Physical grounding: hold ice or cold water, do push-ups, walk briskly.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Eye Injury Response
steps: Do NOT rub, press, or touch the injured eye.
For foreign objects: flush with clean water. Do NOT remove embedded objects.
Shield the injured eye with a rigid cover (cup, cardboard cone).
Cover BOTH eyes to prevent sympathetic movement.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Snow Blindness Treatment
steps: Snow blindness is a sunburn of the cornea caused by UV reflection off snow, water, or sand.
Move the patient into shade or a dark shelter.
Apply cold compresses over closed eyes.
Administer pain medication if available.
Prevention: Wear sunglasses or improvised slit-goggles made from bark, cardboard, or duct tape.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Morgan Lens Eye Irrigation
steps: Flush immediately with clean water
Pull lower lid down
Irrigate for minimum 20 minutes
Check pH if possible

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Improvised Eye Shield
steps: Do NOT remove embedded objects
Build a rigid shield
Tape shield over the eye
Patch both eyes

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Eye Foreign Body Removal
steps: DO NOT rub the eye — this embeds the particle deeper.
Pull upper lid over lower lid — lashes may sweep the particle free.
Flush with clean water from inner corner outward.
If visible on white of eye: use a wet cotton swab to gently lift off.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Spinal Immobilization
steps: Suspect spinal injury after any fall, dive, vehicle accident, or head impact.
Tell the patient: DO NOT MOVE. Stabilize the head and neck manually.
Improvise a cervical collar from rolled clothing or padding.
Do NOT move the patient unless there is immediate danger.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Field Spinal Assessment
steps: Mechanism of injury: fall from height, vehicle accident, diving, or force to head/neck = suspect spine injury.
Ask: 'Do you have any neck or back pain?' If yes — immobilize. Do not move the patient.
Test sensation: can they feel you touch their fingers and toes? Compare both sides.
Test motor: can they wiggle fingers and toes on command? Can they squeeze your hands equally?
If alert, no pain, full sensation, full motor, and no intoxication — spine can be cautiously cleared for movement.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Spinal Log Roll
steps: The log roll requires 3 people minimum: head holder + 2 body controllers.
The head holder is in charge.
Body controllers kneel beside the patient: one at shoulders/hips, one at hips/legs.
On command, roll the patient toward the controllers in ONE smooth motion.
Slide the backboard or pad under the patient, then roll them back.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Improvised Cervical Collar
steps: A cervical collar prevents flexion, extension, and rotation of the neck.
Use an SAM splint, rolled newspaper, or a folded towel wrapped inside a triangular bandage.
The collar must be tall enough to prevent the patient from looking down.
It must NOT compress the trachea or jugular veins.
A collar alone does NOT clear the spine.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Advanced Airway: Jaw Thrust
steps: Kneel at the victim's head.
Place palms on cheekbones.
Hook fingers under the jaw angle.
Lift the jaw upward.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Diabetic Emergency Response
steps: Recognize symptoms: confusion, shakiness, sweating, or unresponsiveness.
If conscious and able to swallow, give sugar immediately.
If unconscious, place in recovery position. Do NOT give food or liquid.
Look for medical ID bracelet or insulin pump.
Monitor for 30 minutes after sugar is given.

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK44.name: Crush Injury Management
steps: Do NOT remove the crushing object if person has been trapped over 15 minutes.
If safe to release: prepare for heavy bleeding before removing weight.
Give water if the person is conscious and can swallow.
Keep crushed limb below heart level after release.
Monitor for shock: rapid pulse, pale skin, confusion.

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK44.name: Crush Syndrome Management
steps: Assess entrapment duration
Start IV fluids BEFORE release if possible
Apply tourniquet above crush site before release
Monitor cardiac rhythm

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK44.name: Compartment Syndrome Recognition
steps: Suspect after fractures, crush, or tight casts
The 6 P's
Pain with passive stretch is the earliest sign
Loosen all circumferential dressings immediately

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK44.name: Avalanche Burial Recovery
steps: Dig to the airway immediately.
Clear the mouth of snow plug.
Assess pulse carefully.
Handle with extreme gentleness.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Hypothermia Field Treatment
steps: Get person out of cold environment and wind.
Remove ALL wet clothing. Replace with dry layers.
Insulate from the ground. Place padding underneath.
Apply heat to core: neck, armpits, groin.
Give warm sweet drinks if conscious and alert.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Core Temperature Assessment
steps: Touch the skin at the abdomen or armpit — these reflect core temperature better than extremities.
Shivering assessment: mild hypothermia = vigorous shivering. Severe = shivering STOPS (very dangerous sign).
Mental status: mild cold = clear thinking. Moderate = confusion, slurred speech. Severe = unconscious.
For heat: hot, DRY skin = heat stroke (emergency). Hot, WET skin = heat exhaustion (serious but less critical).
Pulse assessment: cold = slow, weak pulse. Heat stroke = rapid, bounding pulse. Both indicate core temp emergency.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Active Rewarming Techniques
steps: Insulate from the ground FIRST — ground conducts heat 25x faster than air.
Warm the core, NOT the extremities. Apply heat to the neck, armpits, and groin.
Heat sources: hot water bottles (plastic bottles filled with warm water), heated rocks wrapped in cloth, body-to-body contact.
Warm, sweet drinks if the patient is conscious and can swallow. NO alcohol.
Rewarming takes HOURS, not minutes. Monitor continuously. Severe hypothermia patients may appear dead but still be viable.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Frostbite Rewarming Protocol
steps: Do NOT rewarm frostbitten tissue if there is ANY chance it will refreeze.
Immerse the affected area in water heated to 98-102°F (37-39°C).
Keep the water temperature constant for 30-60 minutes.
EXTREME PAIN accompanies successful rewarming.
After rewarming, place sterile gauze between fingers/toes, wrap loosely, and elevate.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Severe Hypothermia Wrap
steps: Remove wet clothing.
Layer a tarp and sleeping pad.
Add sleeping bags and patient.
Wrap the outer tarp.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Frostbite Rapid Rewarming
steps: Ensure no risk of refreezing.
Prepare a warm water bath.
Immerse the frozen tissue.
Circulate and refill.
Air dry and pad.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Field Core Temperature Check
steps: Understand why oral temp fails
Use rectal or esophageal probe if available
Stage hypothermia by core temp
Document and monitor trends

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Mild Hypothermia Self-Treatment
steps: Recognize in yourself: uncontrollable shivering, fumbling hands, stumbling, confused thinking.
Get out of wind and wet — shelter is priority one.
Create insulation between yourself and the ground.
Exercise vigorously if you can — generate metabolic heat.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Severe Allergic Reaction Response
steps: Identify signs: hives, swelling, difficulty breathing, dizziness.
If EpiPen available: inject into outer thigh, hold 10 seconds.
Have person sit upright if breathing is difficult.
Remove the allergen source if possible.
Monitor for at least 4 hours — reactions can return.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Frostbite Treatment
steps: Move to warm shelter. Do NOT rub or massage affected area.
Remove wet clothing and constricting items from affected area.
Rewarm in warm (not hot) water: 98-102°F (37-39°C) for 30 minutes.
Do NOT rewarm if refreezing is possible.
Protect rewarmed tissue: wrap loosely, separate fingers/toes.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Trench Foot Prevention & Treatment
steps: Remove wet footwear and socks immediately.
Air dry feet. Do NOT rub, massage, or apply direct heat.
Elevate feet to reduce swelling.
Once dry, apply clean, dry socks loosely.
Prevent recurrence: change socks twice daily, dry feet at every opportunity.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Trench Foot (Immersion Foot) Treatment
steps: Recognize the early stages: Tingling, numbness, and 'wood-like' feet.
STOP walking and dry the feet immediately.
Elevate the feet and rewarm slowly at room temperature.
Prevent recurrence.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Frostbite: Thaw vs. Wait
steps: Assess the risk of re-freezing.
Determine evacuation capability.
Shelter-in-place scenario.
Protect thawed tissue.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Knee Injury Stabilization
steps: Immobilize the knee in the position found.
Check circulation below the knee: pulse, warmth, sensation in foot.
Apply cold to reduce swelling for 20 minutes.
Wrap with compression bandage in figure-8 pattern.
Create a walking aid: trekking pole, sturdy branch, or crutch.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Fever Management in the Field
steps: Check temperature if thermometer available. Above 101°F (38.3°C) needs treatment.
Give ibuprofen or acetaminophen if available.
Push fluids: water, broth, oral rehydration solution.
Cool the body: remove excess clothing, apply cool damp cloths.
Monitor for danger signs: stiff neck, confusion, rash, persistent vomiting.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Willow Bark Tea (Natural Aspirin)
steps: Willow bark contains salicin, which the body converts to salicylic acid (aspirin).
Peel the inner bark (cambium) from young branches.
Simmer 1-2 teaspoons of shredded inner bark in a cup of water for 10-15 minutes.
Strain and drink.
Effective for headache, muscle pain, fever, and inflammation.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Pediatric Fever Management (Field)
steps: Assess temperature: hand on forehead is crude but feel for excessive heat.
Remove excess clothing. Keep in cool shade.
Cool with lukewarm (NOT cold) water on forehead, neck, and armpits.
Maintain hydration: small frequent sips of clean water.

https://www.cdc.gov/chemical-emergencies.name: Emergency Eye Irrigation
steps: Chemical exposure: begin flushing IMMEDIATELY. Do not wait to identify the chemical.
Use clean water — pour steadily from a bottle or cup. Hold eye open with fingers.
Flush continuously for minimum 15-20 minutes for chemicals. 5 minutes for debris.
For embedded debris (metal, glass): do NOT rub. Cover with a cup or cone taped over the eye.
After flushing: cover loosely with clean cloth. Do not patch tightly. Seek medical care.

https://www.cdc.gov/chemical-emergencies.name: Dry Chemical Decontamination
steps: Do NOT use water immediately.
Brush off the powder.
Remove contaminated clothing.
Flush ONLY after brushing.

https://www.cdc.gov/chemical-emergencies.name: Wet Chemical Decontamination
steps: Protect yourself first.
Remove saturated clothing.
Irrigate with copious water.
Direct water away.

https://www.cdc.gov/chemical-emergencies.name: Tear Gas/OC Recovery
steps: Move to fresh air.
Do NOT rub eyes.
Blink rapidly and flush.
Blow nose, do not swallow.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Seizure Response
steps: Do NOT restrain the person. Do NOT put anything in their mouth.
Clear the area of hazards: move furniture, sharp objects, rocks away from the person.
Time the seizure. If it exceeds 5 minutes, this is a medical emergency.
Once convulsions stop, roll the person onto their side (recovery position).
Stay with them until fully alert. Speak calmly. Orient them: 'You had a seizure. You are safe. It's [time].'

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Seizure Management (Field)
steps: DO NOT restrain the patient or put anything in their mouth.
Clear the area of hard/sharp objects. Cushion the head.
Time the seizure. Turn on side (recovery position) after convulsions stop.
Stay with patient during the postictal (recovery) phase — they'll be confused and sleepy.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Foreign Body in Ear
steps: If a live insect is buzzing in the ear: tilt head so affected ear faces up, pour in warm oil (olive, vegetable, or mineral).
For visible objects near the canal opening: use tweezers ONLY if you can clearly see and grasp the object.
Gravity method: tilt head with affected ear down and gently pull earlobe backward and downward.
Irrigation: use warm (body temperature) water with a bulb syringe. Squirt gently along the canal wall, NOT directly at the object.
If unsuccessful, leave the object alone and seek medical help. Do NOT try repeatedly.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Fishhook Removal
steps: Assess the hook: is the barb visible through the skin (push-through possible) or deeply embedded?
String-yank method (most common): loop strong line around the hook bend, press the shank down, yank the line parallel to the skin.
Push-through method (barbless or shallow): advance the hook through the skin until the barb exits, cut off the barb, back the hook out.
Clean the wound thoroughly with clean water. The wound will look small but is a puncture — infection risk is high.
Apply antibiotic ointment if available and cover with a bandage. Watch for signs of infection.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: String-Yank Hook Removal
steps: Tie a strong piece of fishing line or braided cord in a loop.
Loop the string around the bend of the embedded hook.
With your non-dominant hand, press firmly down on the eye (shank) of the hook.
Align the string parallel to the shank of the hook, directly opposite the direction it went in.
On a count of three, give the string a single, fast, violent yank.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Stingray Barb Treatment
steps: Stingray venom is a heat-labile protein, meaning it breaks down and denatures at high temperatures.
Immerse the wound in water as hot as you can tolerate (110-115°F / 43-46°C).
Soak for 30 to 90 minutes.
Carefully attempt to remove the barb using tweezers or pliers.
Scrub the wound vigorously with soap and water afterward.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Jellyfish Sting Neutralization
steps: Rinse the sting area gently with SEAWATER, not freshwater.
Do NOT rub the area or apply ice.
Douse the area heavily with household vinegar (5% acetic acid).
Pluck any visible tentacles off the skin using tweezers or the edge of a credit card.
If the patient experiences severe shortness of breath or facial swelling, treat for anaphylaxis immediately.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Sea Urchin Spine Removal
steps: Soak the affected area in the hottest water tolerable (110-115°F) for 30-60 minutes.
Remove visible spines with tweezers.
Deeply embedded spines that do not protrude may need to be left in place.
Apply vinegar to dissolve remaining tiny spine tips.
Monitor for infection.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Black Widow Bite Treatment
steps: Identify the bite: Slight pinprick followed by intense, radiating pain.
Prepare for severe muscle cramps and rigidity.
Clean the bite site with soap and water, and apply a cool compress.
Administer pain relief if available, and keep the patient calm.
Evacuate for medical care.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Brown Recluse Bite Treatment
steps: Identify the bite: Usually painless initially, developing into a red, swollen target lesion over hours.
Elevate the limb above the heart.
Apply a cool compress or ice pack (10 minutes on, 10 minutes off).
Do not attempt to cut, lance, or suck out the venom.
Monitor for systemic signs and evacuate.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Scorpion Sting Treatment
steps: Most scorpion stings cause only localized pain similar to a bee sting.
Clean the sting site with soap and water.
Immobilize the affected limb.
Monitor for severe symptoms: muscle twitching, difficulty breathing, excessive salivation, blurred vision.
Administer pain medication and antihistamines if available.

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK44.name: Manage Tension Pneumothorax
steps: Monitor for worsening breathing.
Locate the chest seal edge.
Lift one edge upon exhalation.
Reseal the dressing.

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK44.name: Three-Sided Occlusive Dressing
steps: Find or improvise occlusive material
Place over the wound on exhalation
Leave the bottom edge open
Monitor for tension pneumo

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK44.name: Tension Pneumothorax Recognition
steps: Recognize: severe breathing difficulty, one side of chest not moving, distended neck veins.
Absent breath sounds on the affected side.
If you have a needle: decompress at the 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line.
If no equipment: maintain oxygen, elevate head, and EVACUATE immediately.

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK51.name: Nasopharyngeal Airway Insertion
steps: Select appropriate size
Lubricate the NPA
Insert bevel toward septum
Confirm airway patency

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK47.name: Oropharyngeal Airway (OPA)
steps: Confirm patient is UNCONSCIOUS
Size OPA correctly
Insert with tip pointing up
Position properly

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK43.name: Cardiac Tamponade Recognition
steps: Suspect tamponade after chest trauma
Identify Beck's Triad
Keep patient sitting upright
Evacuate to surgical care immediately

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Heat Cramps Field Treatment
steps: Stop activity and rest in shade
Sip electrolyte solution
Stretch and massage affected muscles
Do not resume activity until cramps fully resolve

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Heat Cramps
steps: Stop activity and rest in a cool place.
Drink fluids containing electrolytes.
Gently stretch and massage the cramped muscle.

https://www.wiley.com/en-us/Medical+Bota.name: Field Soap Making
steps: Make lye water: fill a container with hardwood ash, pour water through it repeatedly.
Render animal fat: heat fat scraps in water, skim off the clean tallow layer when cooled.
Heat tallow and slowly add lye water, stirring constantly.
Pour into molds (bark containers, carved wood). Cure for 2-4 weeks.

https://www.wiley.com/en-us/Medical+Bota.name: Pine Needle Tea (Vitamin C)
steps: Identify pine, spruce, or fir needles. AVOID yew (flat needles, red berries — toxic).
Harvest a handful of fresh green needles.
Steep in hot (not boiling) water for 10 minutes.
Drink 1-2 cups daily. Tastes like Christmas.

https://www.wiley.com/en-us/Medical+Bota.name: Styptic: Yarrow Poultice
steps: Identify Yarrow.
Chew or crush the fresh leaves.
Pack directly into the wound.
Utilize the alkaloid properties.

https://www.cdc.gov/drinking-water/index.name: Waterborne Disease Prevention Protocol
steps: ALWAYS treat water before drinking — no exceptions regardless of how clean it looks.
Establish a clean water station separate from washing and latrine areas.
Wash hands after latrine use and before food preparation — every single time.
Monitor for symptoms: watery diarrhea, vomiting, cramping. Begin ORS immediately.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Mild Allergic Reaction Management
steps: Identify trigger and remove it: stop eating the food, remove the stinger, move away from plants.
Antihistamine: diphenhydramine (Benadryl) 25-50mg for adults.
Cool compress on hives for comfort.
Monitor for 4+ hours for progression to anaphylaxis: throat tightening, breathing difficulty, dizziness.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Kidney Stone Pain Management (Field)
steps: Recognize: sudden severe pain in flank/back radiating to groin, nausea, bloody urine.
Hydrate aggressively: 2-3 liters per hour if possible — volume helps push the stone.
Pain management: ibuprofen 800mg (reduces ureteral spasm) combined with acetaminophen.
Hot compress on the affected flank — heat relaxes ureteral smooth muscle.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Field Constipation Management
steps: Caused by: dehydration, change in diet, stress, lack of fiber, reduced activity.
Treatment: increase water intake dramatically — 3+ liters per day.
Eat high-fiber foods: bark cambium, leafy greens, roots, berries with seeds.
Warm water first thing in morning stimulates gastric reflex.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Field Hypoglycemia Management
steps: Recognize signs of hypoglycemia.
Assess the patient's ability to swallow.
Administer rapid-acting carbohydrates (conscious patient).
Wait 15 minutes and re-test.

https://www.cdc.gov/giardia/index.html.name: Giardiasis Field Management
steps: Recognize the progression.
Hydrate aggressively.
Administer antibiotics if available.
Maintain strict hygiene.

https://www.cdc.gov/rabies/index.html.name: Rabies Exposure Protocol
steps: Identify high-risk vectors.
Scrub the wound relentlessly with SOAP and water.
Flush with a virucidal agent.
Evacuate for Post-Exposure Prophylaxis (PEP).

https://www.redcross.org/get-help/how-to.name: Survival First Aid Kit Essentials
https://www.redcross.org/get-help/how-to.body:
Minimum wilderness first aid kit:

Bleeding control:
- Tourniquet (CAT or SOFTT-W)
- Israeli bandage
- Hemostatic gauze (QuikClot)
- Roller gauze (2x)
- Medical tape

Wound care:
- Irrigation syringe

https://wms.org/.name: Triage & Treatment Priorities
https://wms.org/.body:
When someone is injured, assess in this order (MARCH):

M — Massive hemorrhage: Stop life-threatening bleeding FIRST. This kills fastest.

A — Airway: Is the airway open? Head tilt, chin lift. Clear o

https://www.aap.org/en/patient-care/disa.name: Pediatric Emergencies
https://www.aap.org/en/patient-care/disa.body:
Children are NOT small adults. Their physiology differs in critical ways that affect emergency treatment.

Key differences:
- Higher heart rate (infant: 120-160 bpm is NORMAL)
- Higher respiratory rat

https://www.aap.org/en/patient-care/disa.name: Children in Survival Situations
https://www.aap.org/en/patient-care/disa.body:
Children require different psychological management than adults.

What children fear most:
- Separation from parents/caregivers
- Loss of routine and normalcy
- Adult fear (they mirror adult emotions)

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_O.name: Improvised Medical Tools
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_O.body:
When proper medical supplies aren't available, improvisation saves lives.

Improvised tourniquet:
- Material: belt, strip of cloth, bandana (at least 1.5 inches wide)
- Windlass: stick, pen, spoon han

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK43.name: Understanding Crush Syndrome
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK43.body:
Crush syndrome is one of the most counterintuitive killers in disaster medicine. The patient seems fine while trapped — then dies within minutes of being freed.

What happens:
- Prolonged pressure on

https://www.cdc.gov/disasters/extremehea.name: Environmental Emergencies: Heat & Cold
https://www.cdc.gov/disasters/extremehea.body:
The human body operates in a narrow temperature range. Deviation in either direction can be fatal.

Heat Spectrum:
- Heat cramps: muscle spasms from salt loss. Treatment: rest, water, salt.
- Heat exh

https://www.facs.org/quality-programs/tr.name: Systematic Trauma Assessment
https://www.facs.org/quality-programs/tr.body:
In a trauma situation, systematic assessment prevents you from missing life-threatening injuries.

Primary Survey (ABCDE) — find and fix immediate killers:
- A: Airway — is it open? Clear obstructions

https://wms.org/magazine/1213/2/medicati.name: Essential Medications for Wilderness First Aid
https://wms.org/magazine/1213/2/medicati.body:
A basic medication kit can address most common wilderness medical problems.

Pain management:
- Ibuprofen (Advil): anti-inflammatory + pain relief. 400-800mg every 6-8 hours. Best for musculoskeletal

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97803959262.name: Edible Insects
steps: Target: ants, grasshoppers, crickets, grubs, termites.
Always cook insects if possible.
Remove legs, wings, and heads before eating.
Collect in quantity — you need volume for meaningful nutrition.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97803959262.name: Commonly Edible Wild Plants
steps: Dandelion: entire plant is edible — leaves, flowers, roots.
Cattail: the 'supermarket of the swamp.'
Pine: inner bark is edible, needles make vitamin C tea.
Clover: flowers and leaves are edible raw or cooked.
Plantain (broadleaf): common lawn weed, edible and medicinal.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97803959262.name: Plants to Avoid
steps: Avoid: white or yellow berries. 90% are poisonous.
Avoid: plants with milky sap (except dandelion and coconut).
Avoid: umbrella-shaped flower clusters (parsley family).
Avoid: almond/peach scent in leaves or bark.
Avoid: mushrooms unless you are an expert.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97803959262.name: Edible Seaweed Harvesting
steps: Harvest seaweed attached to rocks, not floating loose.
Rinse in fresh water if available to reduce salt.
Eat raw, dried, or add to soups. Rich in iodine and vitamins.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97803959262.name: Dandelion: Complete Edible Plant
steps: Identify: jagged leaves, hollow stem with milky sap, yellow flower.
Harvest leaves (youngest are least bitter), flowers, and roots.
Rich in vitamins A, C, K, and minerals. More nutritious than spinach.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97803959262.name: Clover Identification & Use
steps: Identify clover: three round leaflets with a pale V-shaped chevron on each. Grows in fields, lawns, and meadows.
All parts are edible: leaves, flowers, stems, and roots.
Eat flowers raw as a sweet trail snack, or steep in hot water for a mild, slightly sweet tea.
Cook leaves by boiling for 5-10 minutes to reduce the mild bitterness.
Dry flowers and leaves for long-term storage. Dried clover retains nutrition for months.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97803959262.name: Rose Hip Tea
steps: Identify rose hips: red-orange fruit at the base of wild rose flowers, appearing in late summer through winter.
Harvest hips when red and slightly soft. They sweeten after the first frost.
Split hips open and remove the seeds and fine hairs inside. The hairs are irritating if swallowed.
Crush or chop the hip flesh. Steep in hot (not boiling) water for 15-20 minutes.
Rose hips contain 20x more vitamin C than oranges by weight. Critical for preventing scurvy in long-term survival.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97803959262.name: Wild Plantain (Plantago)
steps: Identify: rosette of broad oval leaves with prominent parallel veins, growing close to the ground.
Young leaves (under 4 inches) are best raw — mild flavor, slightly bitter like spinach.
Cook larger leaves by boiling for 5-10 minutes. The parallel veins become stringy — remove them like celery strings.
Seeds: harvest the rat-tail seed spikes when brown. Rub to free seeds. Use as grain substitute.
Medicinal: crush fresh leaves and apply as a poultice to insect bites, stings, and minor wounds.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97803959262.name: Stinging Nettle Harvest
steps: Identify: tall plant with opposite, serrated, heart-shaped leaves. Covered in fine stinging hairs.
Harvest using gloves, thick cloth, or by gripping the stem firmly (the stingers fold flat under firm pressure).
Cooking neutralizes the sting completely. Boil for 2-3 minutes, steam, or sauté.
Nutritional powerhouse: more protein per weight than most vegetables, plus iron, calcium, and vitamins A/C.
Dry leaves for tea or future cooking. Dried nettles store for months and retain nutrition.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97803959262.name: Seaweed Foraging
steps: RULE: all seaweed is technically edible, but some are tough or taste bad. Focus on soft, thin varieties.
Sea lettuce (Ulva): thin, bright green sheets. Eat raw or cooked. Found worldwide in intertidal zones.
Kelp: large brown fronds with a long stipe (stem). Cook or dry. The stipe can be pickled.
Dulse (Palmaria): flat, reddish-purple fronds. Eat raw, dried, or fried. Tastes smoky/savory.
Harvest from LIVING, attached plants — not dead seaweed washed on shore. Rinse in fresh water before eating.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97803959262.name: Wild Rice Harvest
steps: Wild rice (Zizania) grows in shallow lakes, ponds, and slow streams. Tall grass 4-8 feet with grain heads in late summer.
Harvest from a canoe/raft: bend the stalks over the vessel and knock the ripe seeds into the bottom.
Dry the harvested grain in the sun for 1-2 days. Stir occasionally to prevent mold.
Parch (lightly roast) the dried grain over low heat to loosen the hulls. Stir constantly.
Hull by rubbing between hands or treading in a bark container. Winnow by tossing in the wind.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97803959262.name: Wild Root Vegetables
steps: Jerusalem artichoke (Helianthus tuberosus): follows sunflower-like tall plant. Knobby tubers at the root.
Wild carrot (Queen Anne's lace): feathery leaves, white flower cluster, hairy stem. Dig the first-year white root.
Burdock root: large, ruffled leaves, burr-producing plant. Dig the first-year taproot.
Wapato (Sagittaria): arrow-shaped leaves in shallow water. Tubers at the root in mud.
All wild root vegetables should be cooked to improve digestibility and destroy potential irritants.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97803959262.name: Stinging Nettle Foraging
steps: Identify Urtica dioica in moist, rich soil.
Harvest using heavy gloves or improvising 'tongs' from two sticks.
Neutralize the sting by boiling or crushing.
Consume as a nutrient-dense spinach substitute.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97803959262.name: Cattail Shoot Harvest (Cossack Asparagus)
steps: Locate Typha latifolia in shallow wetlands or ditches.
Grasp the inner core of leaves (the center spike) near the water line.
Peel away the tough green outer layers.
Eat raw or boil briefly.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97803959262.name: Foraging: True Morel
steps: Locate old orchards or burn sites.
Examine the cap structure.
Slice the mushroom in half lengthwise.
Verify it is NOT a False Morel (Gyromitra).

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97803959262.name: Foraging: Giant Puffball
steps: Locate large, white spherical fungi.
Slice directly down the center.
Check for toxic lookalikes.
Slice and fry.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97803959262.name: Foraging: Chicken of the Woods
steps: Locate dying or dead hardwood trees.
Examine the color.
Check the underside.
Harvest the outer edges.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97803959262.name: Three Sisters Planting
steps: Plant Corn first in a mound.
Plant Pole Beans around the corn once it's 6 inches tall.
Plant Squash/Pumpkin around the base.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97803959262.name: Plant Fiber Rope Making
steps: Harvest suitable fibers
Ret fibers if needed
Reverse wrap technique
Splice in new fibers as you go

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97803959262.name: Banana Plant Water
steps: Cut a banana plant trunk down to a 6-inch stump.
Scoop out the center of the stump to create a bowl shape.
Scoop water every 30 minutes. The stump refills 3-4 times.

https://openlibrary.org/isbn/97803959262.name: Water from Wild Fruits & Plants
steps: Identify water-rich wild fruits: berries, wild grapes, prickly pear.
Eat water-rich plants: cattail shoots, wild lettuce, clover.
Chew but do not swallow fibrous plants to extract moisture.

https://www.penguinrandomhouse.com/books.name: Canine vs Feline Tracks
steps: Check for claw marks.
Examine overall shape.
Look at the heel pad (metacarpal pad).
Draw the 'X'.

https://www.penguinrandomhouse.com/books.name: Aging Ungulate (Deer) Tracks
steps: Assess edge sharpness.
Check for moisture and debris.
Examine the 'splash' or kick-up.
Compare with your own track.

https://www.penguinrandomhouse.com/books.name: Spring-Pole Snare
steps: Select an incredibly supple sapling (the spring pole).
Create the trigger mechanism (Toggle).
Bend the sapling and set the trigger.
Set the wire noose.

https://www.penguinrandomhouse.com/books.name: Woven Fish Basket Trap
steps: Gather extremely flexible green wood.
Weave the outer cone.
Weave the inner funnel.
Insert the funnel into the open end of the basket.

https://www.skyhorsepublishing.com/97816.name: Figure-4 Deadfall
steps: Carve the upward vertical post.
Carve the horizontal bait stick.
Carve the diagonal drop-stick.
Assemble under tension.

https://www.skyhorsepublishing.com/97816.name: Paiute Deadfall
steps: Construct the Y-post and lever.
Tie the toggle.
Set the bait stick.
Bait the tip.

https://www.skyhorsepublishing.com/97816.name: Promontory Peg Snare
steps: Drive the peg and carve the notch.
Bend the sapling engine.
Set the toggle under the peg lip.
Run the snare loop.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/healthy-lifes.name: Caloric Needs in Survival
https://www.mayoclinic.org/healthy-lifes.body:
Your body needs fuel. Understanding caloric needs helps prioritize food sources.

Daily caloric needs:
- Resting in shelter: 1,200-1,500 cal
- Light activity: 2,000-2,500 cal
- Heavy activity (buildin

https://www.fao.org/edible-insects/en/.name: Insects as Survival Food
https://www.fao.org/edible-insects/en/.body:
Insects are the world's most underutilized food source. Over 2 billion people eat insects daily.

Nutrition per 100g:
- Crickets: 21g protein, 6g fat, 121 calories
- Grasshoppers: 20g protein, 6g fat,

https://nchfp.uga.edu/.name: Food Preservation Without Refrigeration
https://nchfp.uga.edu/.body:
In a survival situation, you cannot afford to waste food. Preservation methods:

Smoking (6-12 hours):
- Creates acidic surface that inhibits bacteria
- Adds antimicrobial compounds from wood smoke
-

https://nchfp.uga.edu/.name: Long-Term Food Preservation Methods
https://nchfp.uga.edu/.body:
Surplus food spoils quickly. Preservation extends your food supply for days or weeks.

Drying/Dehydration:
- Most universal method. Works on meat, fish, plants, fruits, insects.
- Cut thin strips (1/4

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK23.name: Nutritional Priorities in Survival
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK23.body:
In survival, calories matter more than balanced nutrition short-term.

Priority hierarchy:
1. Calories (energy to function)
2. Water (can't digest food without hydration)
3. Fat (most calorie-dense —

https://www.foodsafety.gov/food-safety-c.name: Food Safety Without Refrigeration
https://www.foodsafety.gov/food-safety-c.body:
Food poisoning in survival can be fatal — dehydration from vomiting depletes limited water reserves.

General rules:
- Cook ALL meat thoroughly (165°F/74°C)
- Eat food within 2 hours of cooking if not

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK53.name: Starvation Physiology: What Happens to Your Body
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK53.body:
Understanding starvation helps you prioritize and conserve energy.

Phase 1 (0-24 hours): Glycogen depletion
- Body burns stored glycogen (sugar) in liver and muscles
- Blood sugar remains normal
- Pe

https://www.poison.org/articles/plant.name: Plant Poisoning: Recognition & Treatment
https://www.poison.org/articles/plant.body:
Misidentification of wild plants is the leading cause of foraging deaths.

Dangerous plant families:
- Hemlock (Conium, Cicuta): looks like carrot/parsley. Causes respiratory paralysis. Fatal.
- Death

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Nutritional Deficiencies in Survival
https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.body:
Long-term survival requires more than just calories. Specific deficiencies create specific problems.

Vitamin C deficiency (Scurvy):
- Onset: 4-12 weeks without vitamin C
- Symptoms: bleeding gums, lo

https://www.ready.gov/nuclear-explosion.name: Nuclear Fallout Protection
steps: Get inside immediately. Deep is good.
Stay inside for at least 24-72 hours.
Seal vents and windows.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Heat Exhaustion
steps: Move to a cooler area out of direct sunlight.
Remove excess clothing and apply cool water.
Drink cool water or sports drinks slowly.
Monitor for heat stroke signs.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/first-aid/fir.name: Sunburn Treatment
steps: Get out of the sun immediately.
Cool the skin with a cool bath or damp cloth.
Apply aloe vera or moisturizing lotion.
Hydrate heavily.

https://www.cdc.gov/chemical-emergencies.name: Emergency Decontamination
steps: Brush off dry powder BEFORE applying water.
Remove contaminated clothing carefully.
Flush with lukewarm water for 15+ minutes.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Heat Stroke
steps: Call emergency services immediately.
Cool the person rapidly.
Do NOT give fluids if unconscious.

https://www.ready.gov/safety-skills.name: Gray Man Theory
steps: Dress to match the average baseline of the crowd.
Mask your movement and energy.
Remove identifying markers.

https://www.stratfor.com/.name: Antisurveillance
steps: Use shop windows as mirrors.
Make a logical turn or stop.
Do not react if confirmed.

https://www.psychologytoday.com/.name: De-escalation
steps: Show open hands (palms up).
Lower your voice and speak slowly.
Agree with their grievance (Validate).
Exit/Retreat as soon as possible.

https://www.ngdc.noaa.gov/geomag/.name: Analog Watch Compass
steps: Hold the watch horizontal. Point the HOUR HAND at the sun.
Find the midpoint between the hour hand and 12 o'clock.
That midpoint direction is SOUTH (Northern Hemisphere).

https://www.ngdc.noaa.gov/geomag/.name: Using a Magnetic Compass
steps: Hold compass level. Let needle settle.
Rotate the bezel until N aligns with the red needle.
Read your desired bearing. Point the direction-of-travel arrow that way.
Pick a distant landmark on your bearing and walk to it. Repeat.

https://www.ngdc.noaa.gov/geomag/.name: Map & Compass Navigation
steps: Orient the map: align map's north arrow with compass north.
Identify 2-3 visible terrain features on your map.
Take compass bearings to each feature.
Where the bearing lines intersect on the map is your position.

https://www.ngdc.noaa.gov/geomag/.name: Night March with Compass
steps: Set bearing on compass. Use illuminated or glow-in-dark markings.
Use Polaris or a bright star near your bearing as a walking target.
Walk slowly. Sweep feet for obstacles rather than lifting and stepping.
Stop every 100 paces to recheck bearing and assess terrain.

https://www.ngdc.noaa.gov/geomag/.name: Shadow Tip Compass Method
steps: Place a straight stick in flat ground. Mark the tip of its shadow.
Wait 15-20 minutes. Mark the new shadow tip position.
Draw a line between marks: first mark = West, second = East.

https://www.ngdc.noaa.gov/geomag/.name: Analog Watch as Compass
steps: Hold watch flat. Point the hour hand at the sun.
Northern Hemisphere: bisect the angle between the hour hand and 12 o'clock. That's south.
Southern Hemisphere: point 12 at the sun. Bisect between 12 and the hour hand. That's north.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: V: Require Assistance
steps: Select an open, high-contrast area.
Construct the letter V.
Ensure massive scale.
Maintain geometric perfection.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: X: Require Medical
steps: Select a high-contrast zone.
Construct the letter X.
Scale correctly.
Improvise with shadows.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Arrow: Proceeding This Way
steps: Determine you must abandon the site.
Construct an massive Arrow.
Leave a timestamp if possible.
Update at major terrain changes.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Autonomic Reset (Combat Breathing)
steps: Inhale deeply for 4 seconds.
Hold full lungs for 4 seconds.
Exhale slowly for 4 seconds.
Hold empty lungs for 4 seconds.
Repeat 4 cycles.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Tactical Breathing (Extended Protocol)
steps: 4-count box breathing for initial control
Transition to 4-7-8 pattern
Add belly breathing awareness
Use as a pre-performance ritual

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Cognitive Offloading
steps: Write everything down
Use the ground as a whiteboard
Create physical checklists
Delegate tasks to group members

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Stress Inoculation Training
steps: Visualize worst-case scenarios
Practice skills under artificial stress
Use self-talk scripts
Expose gradually to fear triggers

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Field Grief Management
steps: Acknowledge the loss plainly
Allow 15 minutes for emotion
Redirect to immediate needs
Create a memorial marker if possible

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Hostage Survival Psychology
steps: Stay calm in the first moments
Humanize yourself to captors
Maintain a routine
Observe and memorize

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Combat Mindset Reset
steps: Cooper Color Code: White → Yellow → Orange → Red → Black
Train yourself to live in Yellow
Practice transition drills
Avoid Black (panic freeze)

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Countering Learned Helplessness
steps: Identify helplessness thoughts
Force micro-victories
Increase task complexity gradually
Assign yourself daily objectives

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Night Watch Protocol
steps: Divide night into equal shifts
Each shift: 2 hours maximum
Use a buddy system for accountability
Brief the next watch before sleeping

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Managing Children in a Crisis
steps: Be honest but age-appropriate: 'We need to camp outside tonight. I need your help.'
Assign them age-appropriate jobs: gathering tinder, sorting supplies, water collection.
Maintain routines: meals at set times, songs before bed, morning 'briefings'.
Physical comfort is emotional comfort: keep them warm, dry, and fed first.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Long-Term Solo Isolation Survival
steps: Create and maintain a daily schedule — structure prevents psychological deterioration.
Talk out loud. Name your tools. Narrate your activities.
Keep a journal or calendar — count days, document observations, plan.
Exercise daily — it's the most effective anti-depressant in any setting.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Hostage/Captivity Survival Psychology
steps: In the first 15 minutes: comply, observe, and memorize details.
Humanize yourself: share your name, talk about your children/family.
Maintain physical fitness: exercise in confined space (isometrics, squats, push-ups).
Watch for patterns in guard schedules and look for escape opportunities.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Stockholm Syndrome Awareness
steps: Understand: emotional bonding with captors is a normal psychological survival mechanism.
Counter it: internally maintain your pre-capture identity, values, and rescue goals.
Remember: compliance is not agreement. Acting friendly is not becoming friends.
Keep a mental list of captor behaviors and routines for potential future testimony.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Group Morale Building Techniques
steps: Share a meal together daily — even if the meal is minimal.
Tell stories at night — structured group entertainment prevents isolation.
Celebrate small victories: first fire, clean water, shelter complete.
Assign everyone a role — purpose is the strongest anti-depressant.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: The Rule of Threes — Priority Framework
steps: 3 minutes without AIR: clear airway, control severe bleeding.
3 hours without SHELTER in harsh conditions: get out of wind, rain, cold, or extreme heat.
3 days without WATER: find, collect, purify water before food.
3 weeks without FOOD: food is last priority.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Controlling Panic Response — STOP Protocol
steps: S — SIT DOWN. Physical action breaks the panic loop.
T — THINK. Assess your actual situation, not your fear.
O — OBSERVE. Look around with purpose. What can you use? Where are you?
P — PLAN. Make a simple, achievable plan with 3 steps maximum.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Small Group Survival Leadership
steps: Establish leadership immediately — democratic decision-making kills in emergencies.
Communicate the plan simply. Repeat it. Ask for confirmation.
Assign specific tasks to specific people. 'Everyone help' means nobody helps.
Make decisions with available information — waiting for perfect information means waiting until death.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: The Survival Circle — Managing Group Dynamics
steps: At the start of any survival situation: gather everyone in a circle face-to-face.
Each person reports: name, any injuries, special skills, inventory of personal gear.
Establish three things: leader, immediate priorities, individual assignments.
Schedule regular circle check-ins: morning briefing, evening debrief.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Psychological Coping with Personal Injury
steps: Acknowledge the injury honestly to yourself — denial delays adaptation.
Adjust your role: if you can't walk, you can still process food, maintain fire, craft tools.
Pain management through distraction: counting, singing, detailed observation of surroundings.
Fear of being a burden is the most dangerous psychological effect of injury.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Survival Fear Inventory
steps: List every fear you have RIGHT NOW — out loud or on paper.
Categorize: REAL threat (actionable) vs IMAGINED threat (not actionable right now).
For each REAL threat: identify ONE specific action you can take.
For IMAGINED threats: acknowledge them and consciously set them aside.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: 5-4-3-2-1 Sensory Grounding
steps: 5 things you can SEE: name them out loud.
4 things you can TOUCH: describe texture.
3 things you can HEAR: identify source.
2 things you can SMELL, 1 you can TASTE.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: The SERE Pattern of Survival
steps: Stop and assess your immediate situation (S.T.O.P. methodology).
Address First Aid and immediate, life-threatening environment issues.
Determine 'Priority of Work' based on imminent threats.
Establish 'Will to Survive' mechanics.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Managing Isolation Boredom and Apathy
steps: Recognize apathy as the silent killer.
Establish a strict daily routine and schedule.
Engage in deliberate 'make-work' projects.
Utilize mental games and visualization.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: The Stockdale Paradox
steps: Acknowledge the brutal reality.
Reject false hope timelines.
Maintain absolute faith in the endgame.
Focus on the immediate next step.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Box Breathing (Autonomic Down-Regulation)
steps: Acknowledge the adrenaline dump.
Inhale deeply for 4 seconds.
Hold the full breath for 4 seconds.
Exhale completely for 4 seconds, hold empty for 4.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Z-Drag (3:1 Haul System)
steps: Build a bombproof anchor.
Attach the load.
Attach a progress capture (Prusik 1).
Attach the tractor (Prusik 2).
Thread the Z.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Breaching Interior Walls
steps: Identify standard drywall.
Find the studs.
Kick the lowest point.
Tear the gypsum.
Crawl under electrical wires.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Active Shooter Geometry
steps: Evacuate immediately (RUN).
Understand Cover vs. Concealment.
Slice the pie around corners.
Barricade deeply (HIDE).
Ambush with improvised weapons (FIGHT).

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Riot & Mob Evasion
steps: Move perpendicularly.
Avoid choke points.
Keep your chest protected.
Stay on your feet.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: One-Person Litter Drag
steps: Wrap patient in blanket/tarp
Grab the head end of the material
Maintain head-uphill orientation
Use smooth terrain

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Two-Person Seat Carry
steps: Face each other and grip wrists
Have patient sit on the seat
Stand together using a count
Walk in step

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Casualty Packaging for Transport
steps: Immobilize all known injuries first
Insulate from environment
Secure to litter with straps or cordage
Mark injuries on patient

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Helicopter LZ Marking
steps: Clear a 100x100 foot area
