Mark the center with a visible panel
Indicate wind direction
Approach from DOWNHILL side only

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Ground Signal Panel Construction
steps: Use contrasting materials
Make signals at least 10 feet long
Standard ground-to-air signals
Place on open, elevated terrain

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Urban Rubble Rescue Techniques
steps: Listen/call cycle
Mark searched areas
Shore as you go
Monitor for secondary collapse

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Confined Space Entry Protocols
steps: Test atmosphere if possible
Maintain contact with outside
Have a backup rescuer ready
Monitor for structural movement

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Simple 2:1 Rope Haul
steps: Anchor the standing end of the rope
Run rope through a pulley at the load
Haul on the free end
Add a prusik for ratcheting

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Semaphore Flag Signaling
steps: Each letter is a different arm position. Arms point like clock hands.
Learn the distress signals first: 'HELP' and 'SOS'.
Use brightly colored cloth attached to sticks for visibility.
Signal clearly with pauses between letters.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Emergency Log Raft
steps: Select 6-10 straight, dry logs of similar diameter (6-10 inches).
Lay logs parallel. Lash crosspieces at each end using square lashings.
Add a raised platform or deck from smaller sticks for keeping gear dry.
Create a steering oar or pole. Test the raft in shallow water before committing.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Improvised Snowshoe Construction
steps: Select two green branches, 4-5 feet long, flexible enough to bend into a teardrop shape.
Bend each branch into a teardrop frame, lash the overlapping ends.
Weave a platform of cordage, strips of bark, or wire across the frame.
Attach to boots with cordage — pivot point under the ball of your foot.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Travois (Drag Sled) Construction
steps: Find two strong poles 8-10 feet long.
Spread narrow ends in front (pulling end), wide apart at back.
Lash crosspieces between the poles to create a platform.
Load attaches to the platform. Pull from the narrow end, or harness to a person/animal.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Perimeter Trip-Wire Alarm System
steps: Set trip wires at ankle height across all approach paths.
Attach noise makers: cans with pebbles, shells on cord, or bells.
Set at approach choke points: gaps in brush, trail intersections, stream crossings.
Check and maintain daily — weather, wind, and animals can trigger false alarms.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Ground-to-Air Emergency Symbols
steps: V = need assistance. X = need medical help. I = need supplies.
Build from contrasting materials: dark rocks on light sand, bright fabric on dark earth.
An arrow (→) indicates direction of travel if you've moved from the signal site.
Stamp or dig letters in snow, sand, or soft earth if no materials available.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Crevasse Self-Rescue
steps: Stop falling. Catch your breath. Assess injuries.
Attach two prusik slings to the rope above you — one for foot, one for chest/harness.
Alternate: slide, weight, slide, weight — inching up the rope.
Once at the lip: use an ice axe or arm strength to roll over the edge.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Swift Water Self-Rescue
steps: Float on your BACK, feet downstream, toes up.
Use arms to ferry across the current — angled swimming toward the nearest bank.
In rapids: take breaths in troughs between waves, protect head with arms in heavy water.
Grab onto anything solid: overhanging branch, boulder, bridge pylon.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Improvised Stretcher Construction
steps: Two strong poles (7+ feet) plus a connecting surface: jackets, tarp, blanket, or poncho.
Jacket method: zip/button two jackets closed, thread poles through the sleeves.
Blanket method: lay one pole 1/3 from edge, fold excess over. Repeat with second pole.
Carry with 4 people, patient head-first uphill. Communicate: 'ready, lift, walk.'

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Improvised Flotation Devices
steps: Pants: tie legs in knots, wet, inflate by scooping air — becomes a float vest.
Plastic containers: any sealed container displaces water. Two 2-liter bottles support an adult.
Coolers, fuel cans, spare tires — all float.
Backpack floats if sealed and mostly contains clothing or gear (not rocks).

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Emergency Whistle Signal Protocol
steps: THREE blasts = universal distress signal. Repeat every minute.
A whistle carries 10x farther than a human voice and requires less energy.
Pause and listen between signal sets for response.
Improvise a whistle: acorn cap, thick grass blade between thumbs, or hollowed stick.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Adult CPR Protocol
steps: Confirm unresponsive: tap shoulders, shout. Call for help.
Check for breathing: look, listen, feel for 10 seconds maximum.
30 compressions: center of chest, 2 inches deep, hard and fast (100-120/min).
2 rescue breaths: tilt head back, lift chin, seal mouth. Watch for chest rise.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Adult Choking — Heimlich Maneuver
steps: Confirm choking: ask 'Can you breathe?' If they can't speak, cough, or breathe — act.
Stand behind the patient. Fist above navel, below ribcage.
Deliver sharp INWARD and UPWARD thrusts.
Repeat until object is expelled or patient becomes unconscious.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Rope Traverse (Tyrolean)
steps: Anchor the rope on both sides of the gap — bombproof anchors (trees, boulders, multiple points).
Tension the rope using a 3:1 or 4:1 mechanical advantage system (Z-drag).
Clip into the rope with a carabiner and harness. Cross hand-over-hand or with pulleys.
Communication between both sides: establish signals before crossing begins.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Basic Cliff/Ledge Rescue
steps: Assess: can the person climb with assistance, or do they need to be lowered?
Simple belay: secure rope to a bombproof anchor. Lower end to the patient.
Lowering: seat them in an improvised harness (Swiss seat), lower with controlled friction.
Multiple rope lengths: tie ropes together with double fisherman's knot.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Urban Building Escape (Fire/Collapse)
steps: Crawl low: smoke and heat rise. Breathable air is at floor level.
Check doors before opening: feel with back of hand. Hot door = fire behind it.
If exit blocked: seal the room. Stuff wet cloth under the door. Signal from window.
Escape from upper floors: tie sheets together for an improvised rope. Use square knots.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Ground-to-Air Vector Signaling
steps: Identify a large, open clearing devoid of overhanging canopy.
Construct a large 'V' (Require Assistance) or 'X' (Require Medical).
Use 100% contrasting materials.
Ensure symmetry.

https://irp.fas.org/doddir/usaf/afman10-.name: Improvised Flare & Pyrotechnic Setup
steps: Do not rely on a standard campfire for signaling aircraft.
Construct a 'Signal Tree'.
Prepare a reliable, instant ignition source at the exact base.
Construct 'V' formation fires.

https://armypubs.army.mil/epubs/DR_pubs/.name: Emergency Navigation Without Tools
https://armypubs.army.mil/epubs/DR_pubs/.body:
Lost without compass, map, or GPS. You can still find your way.

Daytime methods:
1. Sun position: rises east, sets west, south at noon (Northern Hemisphere)
2. Stick shadow: marks east-west line in 1

https://armypubs.army.mil/epubs/DR_pubs/.name: Reading Terrain Like a Map
https://armypubs.army.mil/epubs/DR_pubs/.body:
Terrain tells a story. Learning to read it is the oldest navigation skill.

Ridges and valleys:
- Ridges are natural travel corridors: better visibility, drier ground, fewer obstacles
- Valleys have w

https://armypubs.army.mil/epubs/DR_pubs/.name: Navigating at Night
https://armypubs.army.mil/epubs/DR_pubs/.body:
Night navigation is dangerous but sometimes necessary. Preparation is key.

Why you might need to travel at night:
- Medical emergency requiring evacuation
- Extreme heat makes daytime travel impossib

https://armypubs.army.mil/epubs/DR_pubs/.name: Night Navigation Techniques
https://armypubs.army.mil/epubs/DR_pubs/.body:
Traveling at night is sometimes necessary (desert heat avoidance, enemy evasion, emergency). It requires different skills.

Advantages of night travel:
- Cooler temperatures reduce water consumption b

https://armypubs.army.mil/epubs/DR_pubs/.name: Navigation in Dense Forest
https://armypubs.army.mil/epubs/DR_pubs/.body:
Dense forest eliminates most visual references. Navigation becomes extremely difficult.

Challenges:
- No horizon visibility for celestial navigation
- Canopy blocks sun and stars
- All directions loo

https://www.gps.gov/.name: GPS: Capabilities & Limitations
https://www.gps.gov/.body:
GPS is extraordinary but not infallible. Understand its limits.

How it works:
- 31 GPS satellites orbit Earth at 12,550 miles altitude
- Your receiver needs signals from 4+ satellites
- Calculates po

https://www.ready.gov/evacuation.name: Urban Emergency Navigation
https://www.ready.gov/evacuation.body:
When infrastructure fails — power grid down, cell network dead, GPS jammed — navigating a city becomes surprisingly difficult.

Why urban navigation fails:
- We depend entirely on street signs, GPS, a

http://www.ussartf.org/lost.htm.name: Getting Unlost: Step-by-Step Recovery
http://www.ussartf.org/lost.htm.body:
Being lost is not a failure. It's a situation to manage systematically.

STOP Protocol:
- S: Sit down. Panic causes bad decisions. Calm yourself first.
- T: Think. When did you last know your position

https://www.usgs.gov/educational-resourc.name: Map Reading Fundamentals
https://www.usgs.gov/educational-resourc.body:
A paper map is the most reliable navigation tool. It never runs out of battery.

Map essentials:
- Contour lines: connect points of equal elevation. Close together = steep. Far apart = flat.
- Scale:

https://www.gps.gov/systems/gps/performa.name: GPS Limitations & Failure Planning
https://www.gps.gov/systems/gps/performa.body:
GPS is remarkable technology. It is also unreliable in survival situations.

Why GPS fails:
- Batteries die (cold accelerates battery drain by 50%+)
- Signal blocked by deep canyons, dense forest cano

https://www.ngdc.noaa.gov/geomag/declina.name: Understanding Magnetic Declination
https://www.ngdc.noaa.gov/geomag/declina.body:
Your compass doesn't point to true north. This error can put you miles off course.

What is magnetic declination?
- True north (geographic North Pole) and magnetic north (where your compass points) ar

https://archive.org/search?query=MCRP+3-.name: Establish the Baseline
steps: Observe the environment passively.
Identify the anomaly.
Categorize the anomaly.
React preemptively.

https://archive.org/search?query=MCRP+3-.name: Cooper's Color Code
steps: Condition White: Unaware.
Condition Yellow: Relaxed Alert.
Condition Orange: Specific Alert.
Condition Red: Fight.

https://archive.org/search?query=MCRP+3-.name: Cover vs. Concealment
steps: Identify Cover.
Identify Concealment.
Do not mistake car doors for cover.
Shoot FROM cover, not over it.

https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lance.name: The Psychology of Survival
https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lance.body:
Studies of survival situations consistently show the same pattern: the #1 predictor of survival is not physical fitness, equipment, or training — it's mental attitude.

People who survive tend to:
- A

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Understanding & Defeating Panic
https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.body: Panic is not weakness — it's a neurological event. Your amygdala (threat center) hijacks your prefrontal cortex (rational brain). The result: tunnel vision, loss of fine motor skills, racing heart, in

https://www.apa.org/topics/social-psycho.name: Group Dynamics in Survival
https://www.apa.org/topics/social-psycho.body:
Groups have both advantages and severe challenges in survival.

Advantages:
- Distributed tasks (someone always watching fire, signals)
- Shared body heat
- Morale support
- More eyes for danger, food

https://www.nasa.gov/hrp/social-isolatio.name: Coping with Isolation
https://www.nasa.gov/hrp/social-isolatio.body:
Solitary survival creates unique psychological challenges that group survival does not.

Timeline of isolation effects:
- Day 1-3: Anxiety, hyper-alertness, difficulty sleeping
- Day 4-7: Loneliness,

https://www.sleepfoundation.org/sleep-de.name: Sleep and Survival
https://www.sleepfoundation.org/sleep-de.body:
Sleep is not optional. It is a survival necessity equal to water.

Cognitive decline timeline:
- 17 hours awake: equivalent to 0.05% BAC (legally impaired in many countries)
- 24 hours: equivalent to

https://www.healthychildren.org/English/.name: Children in Survival Situations
https://www.healthychildren.org/English/.body:
Children are simultaneously more vulnerable and more resilient than adults in survival.

Vulnerabilities:
- Dehydrate faster (higher surface-area-to-mass ratio)
- Hypothermia onset faster (less insula

https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/.name: Psychology of Darkness
https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/.body:
Nighttime is when most survival situations feel the most desperate. Understanding why helps you manage it.

Neurological reality:
- Amygdala activity increases 60% in darkness (threat detection center

https://www.bbc.com/future/article/20140.name: Long-Term Survival Mindset
https://www.bbc.com/future/article/20140.body:
Most survival training focuses on the first 72 hours. But some situations last weeks or months. The psychological demands change dramatically.

Short-term (1-3 days): Adrenaline-driven. Your body's st

https://hbr.org/2017/08/how-stress-affec.name: Decision Making Under Extreme Stress
https://hbr.org/2017/08/how-stress-affec.body:
Stress fundamentally changes how your brain makes decisions.

What stress does to your brain:
- Prefrontal cortex (rational thinking): shut down. Blood redirected to survival centers.
- Amygdala (fear

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK19.name: Sleep Deprivation: The Silent Killer
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK19.body:
Sleep deprivation is more immediately dangerous than food deprivation in survival.

Cognitive decline timeline:
- 24 hours: equivalent to 0.10% BAC (legally drunk). Impaired judgment, slower reactions

https://www.nasa.gov/hrp.name: Psychological Effects of Isolation
https://www.nasa.gov/hrp.body:
Solo survival presents unique psychological challenges beyond the physical.

Timeline of isolation effects:
- Days 1-3: anxiety, hypervigilance, difficulty sleeping. Normal. Manageable.
- Days 3-7: lo

https://www.ptsd.va.gov/.name: Post-Rescue Psychological Adjustment
https://www.ptsd.va.gov/.body:
Rescue is not the end of the psychological journey. Many survivors struggle more after rescue than during the event.

Common post-rescue reactions:
- Relief followed by unexpected depression
- Difficu

https://www.imo.org/.name: SOS in Morse Code
steps: SOS = 3 short, 3 long, 3 short. Any medium: light, sound, flag.
With a flashlight: quick-quick-quick, looong-looong-looong, quick-quick-quick.
Write SOS in sand, snow, or any visible surface.

https://www.imo.org/.name: Morse Code SOS
steps: SOS: three short, three long, three short.
Signal with flashlight, mirror, whistle, or tapping.
Repeat with 1-minute pauses between sets.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Ashley.name: Improvised Rescue Harness (Swiss Seat)
steps: Middle a 20-foot rope. Place the midpoint at the small of your back.
Wrap both ends around to the front, cross them, and pull between your legs from front to back.
Bring the ends up through the waist wraps on each hip.
Tie off at the front with a square knot backed with overhand safeties.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Ashley.name: Hammock Hanging System
steps: Use tree straps to protect bark
Hang at 30° angle
Use a marlin spike hitch for adjustability
Ridgeline at 83% of distance between trees

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Ashley.name: Bowline on a Bight (ABOK #1080)
steps: Double the rope to form a bight.
Tie an overhand knot with the bight, but do not pull it through completely.
Open the protruding loop and pass it completely over the entire knot.
Pull the standing parts and the two new loops to tighten.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Ashley.name: Water Bowline (ABOK #1012)
steps: Form a clove hitch on the standing part instead of a single loop.
Pass the working end up through the clove hitch.
Wrap it around the standing part and back down through the clove hitch.
Dress the knot by pulling the working end and standing part in opposite directions.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Ashley.name: Alpine Butterfly Loop (ABOK #1053)
steps: Wrap the rope around your hand three times.
Take the middle wrap and pull it slightly outward.
Pass this middle wrap over the outer wrap and then back under all the wraps.
Remove your hand and pull the two standing ends to tighten.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Ashley.name: Double Sheet Bend (ABOK #1434)
steps: Form a bight (U-shape) with the THICKER rope.
Pass the THINNER rope up through the bight.
Wrap the thinner rope around both parts of the thicker bight TWICE.
Tuck the working end of the thinner rope under its own standing part.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Ashley.name: Zeppelin Bend (Ropers Knot)
steps: Form an overhand loop (a '6' shape) with the left rope.
Form an underhand loop (a '9' shape) with the right rope.
Place the '9' loop directly ON TOP of the '6' loop.
Thread the left working end up through the center of both loops. Thread the right end down through the center.

https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/2543.name: Drownproofing (Survival Treading)
steps: Assume the resting position.
Prepare to breathe.
Exhale underwater, then press and snap.
Inhale quickly and return to rest.

https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/2543.name: Improvised Pants Flotation
steps: Remove trousers.
Tie off the legs.
Inflate the trousers.
Gather the waist.

https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/2543.name: Improvised Padlock Shim
steps: Cut an aluminum 'M' shape.
Wrap the M around the shackle.
Slide the shim downward.
Disengage the locking pawl.

https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/2543.name: Loiding a Door Latch
steps: Identify the lock type.
Insert a flexible plastic sheet.
Slide plastic downward to strike the latch.
Push inward and turn the knob.

https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/2543.name: Water Heater Extraction
steps: Locate the hot water tank.
Shut off the inlet valve.
Open a hot water faucet upstairs.
Drain from the spigot.

https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/2543.name: Toilet Tank Procurement
steps: Remove the ceramic lid.
Verify water clarity.
Dip and purify.
Do not flush the toilet.

https://www.weather.gov/safety/cold.name: Snow Cave
steps: Find a deep snowdrift or hillside with at least 6 feet of packed snow.
Dig a tunnel entrance angling upward into the drift.
Hollow out a sleeping chamber above the entrance level.
Poke a ventilation hole in the ceiling with a stick.

https://www.weather.gov/safety/cold.name: Heated Bed (Fire Bed)
steps: Dig a trench 6 inches deep, body-width and body-length.
Build a long fire in the trench and burn for 1-2 hours to heat the earth.
Rake out coals. Cover with 3-4 inches of soil, then thick bedding on top.

https://www.weather.gov/safety/cold.name: Ground Insulation Methods
steps: Gather DRY material: leaves, pine needles, grass, ferns.
Pile material at least 4-6 inches thick — it will compress.
Create a body-shaped depression in the center of the pile.
Add a top layer: cover yourself with more material for a blanket.
Check and replace material that becomes damp overnight.

https://www.energy.gov/energysaver/insul.name: Natural Insulation R-Values
https://www.energy.gov/energysaver/insul.body:
Understanding insulation helps you choose the best materials and know how much you need.

R-value measures thermal resistance. Higher = better insulation.

Natural material R-values (per inch):
- Dry

https://www.weather.gov/safety/cold-wind.name: Shelter in Extreme Wind
https://www.weather.gov/safety/cold-wind.body:
Wind is the most underestimated shelter threat. Wind chill kills faster than cold temperature alone.

Wind chill physics:
- 20°F air with 20 mph wind feels like -2°F
- Even mild temperatures become da

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Cordage Friction Saw
steps: Get a length of strong cord.
Loop around the target.
Create handles.
Saw back and forth rapidly.

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Bow Drill Complete Guide
steps: Select matched wood set
Carve spindle and notch
Create bearing block
Bow technique

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Hand Drill Complete Guide
steps: Select spindle
Prepare fireboard
Spin between palms
The difficulty is sustained friction

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Pump Drill Assembly
steps: Attach heavy flywheel to spindle
Thread cord through crossbar holes
Wrap cord around spindle by spinning crossbar
Push crossbar down repeatedly

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Stone Blade Knapping
steps: Select flint, obsidian, chert, or jasper
Use a hammerstone for initial shaping
Pressure flake for edge refinement
The conchoidal fracture is your friend

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Bone Fish Hook
steps: Select a thick bone splinter or large thorn
Carve a J-shape with a barb
Create a line attachment notch
Gorge hooks are simpler

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Deadfall Safety Techniques
steps: Always approach from the side
Use a safety stick
Test trigger sensitivity before loading weight
Mind your fingers

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Camp Latrine Construction
steps: Dig at least 200 feet from any water source
Dig 6-8 inches deep
Sprinkle dirt over deposits after each use
Mark location clearly

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Primitive Pottery (Pit Firing)
steps: Find clay: dig below topsoil near riverbanks. Clay feels smooth and sticky when wet.
Coil-build: roll clay into ropes, stack in spiraling layers to form a vessel.
Dry SLOWLY over 5-7 days. Rapid drying causes cracking.
Pit fire: dig a pit, line with fuel, place pots, cover with more fuel and a dirt layer. Burn for 4-8 hours.

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Birch Tar Production (Natural Adhesive)
steps: Collect birch bark — fresh or recently fallen, paper-thin outer bark.
Double-pot method: place bark in a sealed container (clay pot, tin can) upside down over a collection pot.
Heat the top container — the tar drips down into the collection pot.
The resulting black tar is waterproof adhesive — used for hafting tools, sealing containers, patching boats.

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Hide Glue Production
steps: Collect skin/hide scraps, tendons, and connective tissue from butchered game.
Boil scraps in water for 2-3 hours, maintaining a low simmer.
Strain the liquid through cloth. The resulting solution IS the glue.
Apply warm, clamp until dry. Reactivate by reheating with moisture.

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Multi-Pronged Fish Spear (Gig)
steps: Select a straight green hardwood pole, 6-8 feet long, 1.5 inches thick.
Split the tip into 4 prongs about 8 inches deep.
Wedge small sticks between prongs to spread them apart.
Sharpen each prong tip. Fire-harden by rotating over flames until brown.

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Field Candle Making
steps: Render fat: melt animal fat scraps in a container over low heat.
Create a wick: twisted cotton, plant fiber cordage, or a rolled strip of bark.
Pour melted fat around the wick in a container (shell, bark cup, tin can).
Bayberry, beeswax, or pine pitch can substitute for animal fat.

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Brain Tanning (Smoke-Tanned Hide)
steps: Flesh the hide: remove ALL meat and fat from the inner surface.
Remove the grain layer (epidermis) by soaking in wood ash water for 2-3 days.
Brain the hide: mash animal brains (or egg yolks + oil) into the stretched hide.
Stretch and work the hide as it dries, then smoke over a smoldering fire.

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Pump Drill Fire Starter
steps: Create the spindle: straight hardwood 12-18 inches long.
Create the flywheel: a heavy disc (stone or wood) at the bottom of the spindle.
Attach a crossbar with cord: cord wraps around upper spindle, crossbar pumps up and down.
The pumping motion maintains continuous rotation with minimal effort.

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Rawhide Production — Rigid Natural Material
steps: Flesh the hide: remove ALL meat and fat.
Soak in wood ash water for 2 days to loosen hair.
Scrape hair off with a dull blade. Stretch on a frame to dry.
Rawhide dries rigid and extremely strong.

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Natural Insect Repellent from Plants
steps: Mud: dried mud layer on skin is a physical barrier against biting insects.
Smoke: position yourself in campfire smoke — insects avoid it.
Plant repellents: crush and rub on skin — wild mint, lemon balm, yarrow, mugwort, catnip.
Preventive: sleep in smoke-filled shelter, wear long sleeves, tuck pants into socks.

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Improvised Rope Bridge (Monkey Bridge)
steps: Anchor three ropes on each side: one low (foot rope), two high (hand ropes in a V).
The foot rope should be taut with minimal sag under load.
Hand ropes attach at shoulder height, diverging to a V-shape at the far side.
Cross one person at a time. Each person clips a safety carabiner to a hand rope.

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Emergency Whistle Carving
steps: Select a straight, soft-wood branch about thumb thickness and 3 inches long.
Cut a flat notch/window 1 inch from one end, cutting about halfway through.
Carve a thin plug from the cut-away material and insert between the notch and mouthpiece.
Blow gently — adjust the plug position until you get a clear, loud tone.

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Solar Reflector Oven
steps: Create a parabolic reflector from foil, emergency blanket, or any shiny surface.
Position a dark cooking container at the focal point.
Orient directly toward the sun. Adjust position every 30 minutes as the sun moves.
Cooking times: roughly double conventional oven times.

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Charcoal Production for Filtration & Fuel
steps: Build a fire and let it burn to a thick bed of coals.
Cover the coal bed with a thick layer of dirt or ash, cutting off oxygen.
Wait 4-6 hours. Uncover carefully — the wood should be black, lightweight, and porous.
Uses: water filtration (crushed charcoal removes chemicals), fuel (burns hotter and longer than wood), tooth cleaning, wound treatment, and drawing on surfaces.

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Clay Oil Lamp
steps: Form a shallow dish from clay — pinch the rim to create a wick channel.
Create a wick from twisted cotton, plant fiber, or cattail fluff.
Dry the clay lamp before first use — rapid heating of wet clay causes cracking.
Fill with rendered animal fat or plant oil. Light the wick.

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Cast Net from Natural Cordage
steps: Weave a circular net using sheet bend knots, 3-4 feet in diameter.
Attach weights around the perimeter — stones wrapped in net loops.
Attach a retrieval line to the center of the net.
Throw: fold net over throwing arm, swing and release in a flat arc over the water.

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Refining Wild Clay
steps: Locate clay-rich soil.
Slake the clay in water.
Sieve or decant to remove impurities.
Evaporate and collect the refined clay.

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Coil Pottery Construction
steps: Create a flat, circular base.
Roll clay into uniform coils.
Score, slip, and stack the coils.
Smooth the walls.

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Pit Firing Pottery
steps: Bone-dry the pottery first.
Pre-heat the pottery slowly.
Construct the firing pit.
Burn intensely and let cool naturally.

https://primitivetechnology.wordpress.co.name: Reverse-Wrap Cordage
steps: Harvest and process suitable fiber.
Establish the central kink.
Twist ONE strand away, then cross it OVER the other.
Splice in new fibers continuously.

https://www.youtube.com/@primitivetechno.name: Atlatl (Spear Thrower) Fabrication
steps: Select a sturdy branch for the atlatl body.
Carve the spur (the hook).
Fabricate the dart.
Fletch and socket the dart.

https://www.youtube.com/@primitivetechno.name: Kylie (Hunting Boomerang)
steps: Find an elbow-shaped branch.
Carve the airfoil profile.
Tune the weighting.
Throw horizontally.

https://www.youtube.com/@primitivetechno.name: Bone Awl Fabrication
steps: Source a long-bone from a large mammal.
Score and split the bone.
Grind the point.
Temper in animal fat (optional).

https://www.youtube.com/@primitivetechno.name: Pine Pitch Adhesive
steps: Harvest crystallized pine resin.
Melt over low heat.
Add binder (crushed charcoal).
Add plasticizer (herbivore dung/fur).

https://archive.org/details/TheAshleyBoo.name: Munter-Mule Tie Off
steps: Establish the Munter Hitch.
Pinch the brake strand.
Tie the Mule Knot.
Lock it with an overhand.

https://archive.org/details/TheAshleyBoo.name: Water Knot (Ring Bend)
steps: Tie a loose overhand knot in one end.
Trace the knot with the second end.
Emerge from the other side.
Pull all four strands tight.

https://www.epa.gov/ground-water-and-dri.name: UV Light Purification (SODIS)
steps: Fill clear PET plastic bottles with clear water.
Lay bottles on dark surface in direct sunlight for 6 hours.
In cloudy weather, extend to 2 full days.

https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/.name: Purification: Rolling Boil
steps: Filter out large particulates.
Bring to a rolling, vigorous boil.
Maintain the boil.
Cool and aerate.

https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/.name: Purification: Chlorine Dioxide Drops
steps: Mix Parts A and B.
Add to 1 liter of clear water.
Wait the prescribed contact time.
Note the water temperature.

https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/.name: Purification: Solar Water Disinfection (SODIS)
steps: Select a CLEAR PET plastic bottle.
Fill with low-turbidity water.
Expose to direct, intense sunlight.
Adjust for cloud cover.

https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.name: Stages of Dehydration
https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-cond.body:
Dehydration progresses through recognizable stages:

2% body weight lost: Thirst, decreased performance, irritability. Urine is dark yellow.

5% lost: Headache, nausea, dizziness, rapid heart rate. Re
